title: ToolSense: A Diagnostic Framework for Auditing Parametric Tool Knowledge in LLMs
summary: Large language models deployed as agents over large tool catalogs face a critical tool-retrieval bottleneck. As embedding-based retrieval approaches rely on compact encoders that may under-capture specialized tool semantics, parametric tool retrieval addresses this by encoding each tool as a virtual token appended to the LLM vocabulary, fine-tuned in two stages (memorization then retrieval SFT) to use the LLM as a retriever, achieving strong performance on standard ToolBench retrieval benchmarks. Yet these benchmarks use verbose, fully-specified queries, and their evaluation applies constrained decoding that restricts outputs to valid token paths, neither reveals whether the model actually understands its tools. We introduce \textbf{ToolSense}, an open-source LLM-powered diagnostic framework that takes any tool catalog as input and automatically generates three benchmarks: a Realistic Retrieval Benchmark (RRB) with queries at three ambiguity tiers, an MCQ probing benchmark, and a QA probing benchmark. Applying ToolSense to ToolBench (~47k tools) and evaluating five parametric model training configurations reveals a knowledge-retrieval dissociation: on RRB queries, several configurations collapse by ~50-64 percentage points compared to fully-specified ToolBench benchmarks, falling below the embedding-model baseline. Additionally, despite strong retrieval performance, some models score near-random on factual probes, suggesting a knowledge-retrieval dissociation. We open-source the ToolSense framework and the ToolBench diagnostic benchmarks at https://github.com/SAP/toolsense.

AI.tldr: ToolSense is an open-source diagnostic framework that exposes a knowledge-retrieval dissociation in parametric tool-retrieval models: strong retrieval on fully specified prompts but weak understanding under realistic, ambiguous queries. It introduces three benchmarks (RRB, MCQ, QA) for any tool catalog and reveals that retrieval performance can collapse and even fall below embedding-based baselines, highlighting the gap between memorized tool semantics and actual knowledge.
AI.motivation: Current tool-retrieval for LLM agents relies on embedding-based or parametric approaches. Embeddings may miss specialized tool semantics; parametric tools encode tools as virtual tokens but evaluation on constrained benchmarks may mask true understanding. There is a need for diagnostics that probe actual knowledge of tools under realistic, ambiguous queries.
AI.method: Introduce ToolSense framework that ingests arbitrary tool catalogs and automatically constructs three benchmarks: Realistic Retrieval Benchmark (RRB) with three ambiguity tiers, an MCQ probing benchmark, and a QA probing benchmark. Apply to ToolBench (~47k tools) and assess five parametric model training configurations, analyzing both retrieval performance and knowledge-based probes to identify possible dissociations.
AI.result: RRB queries show a collapse of ~50–64 percentage points relative to fully specified benchmarks for several configurations and can fall below embedding-model baselines. Some models score near random on factual QA probes despite strong retrieval performance, indicating a knowledge-retrieval dissociation.
AI.conclusion: ToolSense and ToolBench provide open-source diagnostics to reveal gaps between retrieval capabilities and actual tool knowledge in parametric models. The work advocates more robust evaluation of tool understanding beyond constrained benchmarks and supports ongoing development of diagnostic suites for tool-using LLM systems.

title: Arbor: Tree Search as a Cognition Layer for Autonomous Agents
summary:
Arbor is a multi-agent framework that introduces structured tree search as a cognition layer for autonomous agents operating in large, stateful action spaces. Prior autonomous optimization systems operate on isolated targets with stateless evaluation. Arbor instead maintains an explicit search tree of scored hypotheses that serves as the shared working memory across agents, evolving with every measurement, treating failures as diagnostic signal that reshapes subsequent exploration, and expanding as prior successes shift the bottleneck distribution.
  We validate Arbor on full-stack LLM inference optimization, a domain where achieving peak performance has historically required coordinated effort from engineering teams across the application, framework, compiler, kernel, and hardware stack. Arbor pairs an Orchestrator agent, which drives optimization by delegating to Domain Specialists across the inference stack, with a Critic agent that safeguards stability through root-cause analysis, introspection, and measurement validation -- a checks-and-balances architecture where neither agent can unilaterally drive the system. Agent capabilities are decomposed into hard skills (domain expertise) and soft skills (coordination protocols that determine how contributions compose), enabling fully autonomous multi-day campaigns. Arbor achieves up to 193% inference throughput-latency Pareto improvement over vendor-optimized baselines, while a single agent without the harness plateaus at +33% throughput improvement and crashes irrecoverably within hours. Arbor generalizes to multiple generations of hardware platform, and run-to-run variance is within 2 percentage points demonstrating that the method is hardware-agnostic and reproducible.

AI.tldr: Arbor is a multi-agent framework that uses a structured search tree as a shared memory cognition layer for autonomous agents in large, stateful action spaces; it achieves substantial performance gains in full-stack LLM inference optimization and is robust to hardware variation, though a single naïve agent can underperform or fail.
AI.motivation: To overcome the limitations of stateless, isolated-target optimization by introducing a collaborative, diagnosable, and hardware-agnostic cognition layer that can coordinate across the inference stack over extended campaigns.
AI.method: Introduce a dual-agent governance architecture (Orchestrator and Critic) that delegate to Domain Specialists; maintain an explicit scored hypothesis tree representing shared working memory; incorporate hard (domain expertise) and soft (coordination protocols) skills; use measurement feedback, root-cause analysis, and checks-and-balances; allow failures to reshape exploration and successes to shift bottlenecks; generalize across hardware generations.
AI.result: Achieves up to 193% inference throughput–latency Pareto improvement over vendor-optimized baselines; a single agent attains +33% but can crash irrecoverably; performance is robust across hardware generations with run-to-run variance within 2 percentage points; method is hardware-agnostic and reproducible.
AI.conclusion: Structured, governance-based multi-agent cognition with an explicit search tree enables substantial, robust optimization in complex, high-dimensional systems (e.g., full-stack LLM inference), with strong cross-hardware generalization and reproducibility, suggesting applicability to other large-state optimization tasks.

title: Strategic Decision Support for AI Agents
summary: Traditionally, decision support studies how humans use machine learning models to make better decisions. In modern agentic systems, this division of roles is increasingly reversed: AI agents act on behalf of users, while humans and tools becomes support mechanisms around them. This role reversal brings reliability concerns to the forefront, since agentic errors can be consequential and agent behavior must remain aligned with human goals and constraints. Departing from the classical view of decision support, we revisit its two basic principles, the cost--value tradeoff of seeking support and the role of uncertainty quantification, in a setting where AI agents are the central actors. We propose a framework for strategic decision support for AI agents through an optimization problem that minimizes support usage subject to controlling a counterfactual missed-support error: the probability that the agent acts alone on instances where support would have materially improved its output. At the population level, we show that the optimal policy is a threshold rule on the value of support. Building on this structure, we develop an online algorithm that adaptively thresholds such a score and uses randomized exploration to control missed-support error without distributional assumptions. We further introduce a calibration-on-the-fly method that reduces unnecessary support calls online. We instantiate this framework across diverse scenarios, including information gathering, human--AI collaboration, and tool use, showing how each can be modeled through the same strategic decision-support lens. Experiments across these settings show that our method reliably controls the target error while substantially reducing support usage in practice.

AI.tldr: Reframes decision support for AI agents as a central optimization problem that minimizes unnecessary support while controlling a counterfactual missed-support error, using a population-threshold policy, online adaptive exploration, and calibration; demonstrated across information gathering, collaboration, and tool use with empirical error control and reduced support usage.
AI.motivation: As AI agents increasingly act autonomously, reliability and alignment become critical. The work addresses the cost and value of seeking support under uncertainty, proposing a distribution-free strategy to ensure agents obtain help when it meaningfully improves outputs without excessive reliance on support.
AI.method: Formulates an optimization problem that minimizes support usage subject to a bound on a counterfactual missed-support error. Proves that the optimal population policy is a threshold on the value of support. Builds an online algorithm with randomized exploration to adaptively threshold a support score, plus a calibration-on-the-fly method. Applies the framework to information gathering, human–AI collaboration, and tool use, with empirical evaluation of performance and error control.
AI.result: The analysis yields a threshold-based policy for when to seek support, and an online, distribution-free algorithm that guarantees targeted missed-support error while substantially reducing support calls. The calibration component reduces unnecessary support dependence. Across diverse scenarios, the method effectively controls the target error and lowers support usage.
AI.conclusion: Strategic decision support reframes decision making with AI agents as an optimization over when to seek support. The approach yields reliable, cost-efficient agent behavior through adaptive thresholding and on-the-fly calibration, with broad applicability to information gathering, collaboration, and tool use.

title: Pythagoras-Prover: Advancing Efficient Formal Proving via Augmented Lean Formalisation
summary: Modern Lean theorem provers achieve strong performance only with substantial training and inference compute, driven in part by scarce verified proof data and the long reasoning traces of formal proof search, making both supervised fine-tuning (SFT) and sampling expensive. We introduce Pythagoras-Prover, a compute-efficient open-source family of Lean theorem provers built for practical compute budgets. The family spans two generation paradigms: autoregressive models at 4B and 32B parameters, and a first proof-of-concept diffusion-based prover (4B) that iteratively refines Lean proofs at inference time. For training efficiency, we build a Lean-verified corpus stratified into easy, medium, and hard problems for curriculum SFT, so models acquire proof skills progressively from shorter, simpler proofs to longer, harder ones. During SFT, a dynamic proof-reasoning filtering scheme preserves informative proof traces while keeping each instance within an 8k-token context budget. We also introduce Augmented Lean Formalisation (ALF), which expands scarce verified corpora into variants of formal statements, populated via self-distillation for extra training signal without formally verifying every mutated instance. By perturbing known problems while preserving their formal character, ALF reduces reliance on any statement's surface form. Empirically, Pythagoras-Prover-4B surpasses DeepSeek-Prover-V2-671B at pass@32 on MiniF2F-Test (86.1% vs 82.4%) with ~167x fewer parameters, while Pythagoras-Prover-32B sets the open-source state of the art at 93.0% on MiniF2F-Test and solves 93 of 672 PutnamBench problems. We release MiniF2F-ALF, an ALF-mutated contamination-sensitive benchmark on which every evaluated model loses accuracy; here our 32B remains strongest and our 4B matches the prior state of the art, Goedel-Prover-V2-32B.

AI.tldr: Pythagoras-Prover introduces a compute-efficient open-source Lean prover family (4B/32B autoregressive; 4B diffusion) with curriculum SFT and ALF augmentation, achieving open-source SOTA on MiniF2F and strong Putnam performance, while enabling smaller models to outperform larger proprietary baselines.
AI.motivation: Address the high compute and data demands of training accurate Lean theorem provers, due to scarce verified proofs and long reasoning traces, by delivering scalable, open-source, compute-efficient alternatives.
AI.method: Train on a Lean-verified corpus stratified into easy/medium/hard for curriculum SFT; employ dynamic proof-reasoning filtering to fit 8k-token contexts; implement two autoregressive generators (4B and 32B) and a 4B diffusion-based prover; introduce Augmented Lean Formalisation (ALF) via self-distillation to mutate formal statements without re-verification; evaluate on MiniF2F-Test and Putnam benchmarks; release MiniF2F-ALF for contamination-sensitive benchmarking.
AI.result: Pythagoras-Prover-4B outperforms DeepSeek-Prover-V2-671B on MiniF2F-Test pass@32 (86.1% vs 82.4%) with ~167x fewer parameters; Pythagoras-Prover-32B achieves 93.0% pass@32 on MiniF2F-Test and solves 93 of 672 PutnamBench problems; ALF-mutated benchmark shows all models lose accuracy, with 32B remaining strongest and 4B matching prior state (Goedel-Prover-V2-32B).
AI.conclusion: The study demonstrates that compute-efficient, open-source Lean provers with curriculum learning and ALF augmentation can achieve competitive or superior performance relative to larger proprietary models, offering practical, scalable tools for formal verification and setting new benchmarks for open models.

title: Learning to Assist: Collaborative VLAs for Implicit Human-Robot Collaboration
summary: Human-robot collaboration (HRC) combines the complementary strengths of humans and robots to improve task efficiency. However, many existing collaborative systems rely on hand-engineered pipelines, limiting their scalability and flexibility for new tasks. In this work, we show that models trained end-to-end with imitation learning, specifically vision-language-action (VLA) models, can support collaborative manipulation, and characterize the key factors affecting their real-world performance. We evaluate two state-of-the-art models and identify a failure mode of action-chunking policies in implicit HRC, where demonstration action leakage (i.e., action chunks crossing latent task transitions) can cause premature assistive behavior. We find that this issue increases with longer execution horizons and occurs in real-world collaborative VLA systems, such as when a robot attempts to hand over a tool before the person is ready. We propose an inference-time steering method to mitigate these erroneous assistive actions while preserving policy performance. Finally, through a 16-participant user study on a long-horizon collaborative assembly task, we show that steering enables a longer execution horizon while mitigating premature assistance, leading to faster collaboration and fewer failures compared to a shorter-horizon policy.

AI.tldr: End-to-end vision-language-action (VLA) models can support collaborative manipulation, but action leakage across latent task transitions causes premature assistance; inference-time steering mitigates this while preserving policy performance, enabling longer-horizon, faster, and more reliable collaboration as shown by a 16-participant study.
AI.motivation: Address scalability and flexibility gaps in human-robot collaboration by moving from hand-engineered pipelines to end-to-end imitation-learned policies, and to understand real-world failure modes affecting performance.
AI.method: Evaluate two state-of-the-art VLA models on collaborative manipulation tasks; diagnose action-chunking/action-leakage as a failure mode during implicit HRC; develop an inference-time steering mechanism to suppress premature actions without hurting policy efficacy; conduct a 16-participant user study on a long-horizon collaborative assembly task to compare steering versus baseline with different horizons.
AI.result: Identified demonstration action leakage across latent task transitions as a failure mode that increases with longer horizons, causing premature assistive actions; proposed steering at inference time to mitigate these actions while maintaining policy performance; user study shows steering enables longer execution horizons, faster collaboration, and fewer failures compared to shorter-horizon policies.
AI.conclusion: Inference-time steering is an effective, practical remedy for end-to-end VLA-based HRC, enabling safer, more efficient long-horizon collaboration without sacrificing the benefits of end-to-end imitation learning.

title: Quickest Detection of Hallucination Onset: Delay Bounds and Learned CUSUM Statistics
summary: Token-level hallucination detectors are evaluated as classifiers, by AUC over all tokens, yet a streaming monitor is judged by its reaction time: the number of tokens that pass between the onset of a hallucination and the alarm. We formulate hallucination onset detection as a quickest change detection problem. A first-order Markov model of the latent faithful/hallucinated state, validated on RAGTruth, places the task inside classical change-point theory and yields Lorden's lower bound on detection delay: about 1.3 tokens at a false-alarm rate of 0.01. We then show that a causal recurrent labeler acts as a CUSUM with a learned increment; at a matched false-alarm rate it detects in 11-13 tokens, against 31 for a linear per-token baseline, and a controlled decomposition attributes most of this advantage to a better per-token score rather than to temporal accumulation. An information-rate optimality theorem of Donsker-Varadhan type explains the remaining order-of-magnitude gap: the learned score realizes only 1/4.5 of the divergence the features carry, a deficit that recalibration cannot remove, with the remainder a finite-horizon effect. Classification metrics conceal this delay structure; sequential analysis makes it measurable

AI.tldr: Token-level hallucination detectors evaluated as classifiers (AUC) miss temporal delay; a streaming monitor's detection latency can be characterized via quickest-change detection, yielding near-optimal bounds. A learned causal labeler behaves like a CUSUM, achieving faster detection than a linear baseline; remaining gap explained by information-rate limits and finite-horizon effects; sequential metrics reveal delay structure hidden by classification metrics.
AI.motivation: Bridge the gap between per-token AUC-based evaluation and streaming reaction-time metrics for hallucination detection; provide principled, theory-backed bounds and methods to quantify and improve detection latency in real time.
AI.method: 1) Propose a first-order Markov latent state model (faithful vs hallucinated) and validate on RAGTruth. 2) Frame onset detection as a quickest change-point problem and derive Lorden's lower bound on detection delay at a specified false-alarm rate (~1.3 tokens at FAR=0.01). 3) Implement a causal recurrent labeler that approximates a CUSUM with a learned increment. 4) Compare detection latency against a linear per-token baseline; attribute gains via per-token scoring versus temporal accumulation. 5) Prove an information-rate (Donsker-Varadhan) type bound explaining remaining gap: learned score captures about 1/4.5 of the divergence carried by features; recalibration cannot close this, finite-horizon effects remain. 6) Discuss discrepancy between classification metrics and latency, advocating sequential analysis.
AI.result: Lorden bound ~1.3 tokens at FAR 0.01; learned causal labeler detects in 11–13 tokens, vs 31 tokens for a linear baseline; most advantages come from improved per-token scoring rather than accumulation; remaining gap due to information-rate limit (only ~1/4.5 of feature divergence captured) with finite-horizon effects; sequential analysis reveals delay structures obscured by AUC.
AI.conclusion: Classification metrics conceal temporal delay; adopting sequential analysis and change-point theory yields actionable latency bounds and insights, and demonstrates substantial improvements from learned scoring, while highlighting fundamental limits on extractable information from features.

title: PersonaDrive: Human-Style Retrieval-Augmented VLA Agents for Closed-Loop Driving Simulation
summary: Closed-loop driving simulators typically populate their environments with non-ego traffic agents that behave largely the same way, produced either by rule-based traffic managers or by learned models trained toward a single behavioral mode. Recent work introduces style variation through post-hoc labels on observational data or LLM-inferred reward weights, but these signals act as proxies for what a style should reward rather than demonstrations of humans explicitly asked to drive in that style. We introduce PersonaDrive, a pipeline that conditions a vision-language-action (VLA) driving agent on retrieved demonstrations from a style-instructed human driving dataset, in which participants drive CARLA leaderboard routes under aggressive, neutral, and conservative instructions on a driver-in-the-loop rig. The pipeline has three stages: (i) offline triplet mining over per-style human driving data using a combined image-text similarity score; (ii) training a lightweight retrieval head that fuses frozen visual features with a small control encoder over per-style databases; and (iii) fine-tuning a single VLA backbone to treat retrieved context points as in-context behavioral demonstrations during waypoint prediction. At inference, the same backbone is conditioned on any style by swapping which per-style database the retrieval head queries, so selecting a style requires no per-style retraining while enabling human-style, style-diverse non-ego agents for closed-loop simulation. On Bench2Drive, PersonaDrive (no style) improves the driving score by 4.6% over SimLingo and 2.5% over HiP-AD, and under style conditioning attains the highest driving score in every style within a roughly 2% band (its weakest style surpassing the strongest baseline, DMW, by 5.4%), while average speed and acceleration rise by 18% and 25% from the conservative to the aggressive instruction.

AI.tldr: PersonaDrive uses retrieval-augmented VLA driving to condition on per-style demonstrations, enabling style-diverse, non-ego agents without per-style retraining; achieves gains on Bench2Drive, with strong style-conditioned performance.
AI.motivation: Current non-ego agents in closed-loop driving simulators show limited behavioral diversity, often using rule-based managers or single-mode learned models. Proxies like post-hoc style labels or LLM-inferred rewards do not reflect explicit human driving demonstrations. A system that can incorporate explicit human driving styles as demonstrations is needed for more realistic simulation.
AI.method: Three-stage pipeline: (i) offline triplet mining over per-style human driving data using image-text similarity; (ii) train a lightweight retrieval head that fuses frozen visual features with a small control encoder over per-style databases; (iii) fine-tune a single vision-language-action backbone to treat retrieved context points as in-context demonstrations during waypoint prediction. Inference queries a per-style database corresponding to the desired style, enabling style-conditioned non-ego agents without retraining.
AI.result: On Bench2Drive, PersonaDrive (no style) improves driving score by 4.6% vs SimLingo and 2.5% vs HiP-AD. When style-conditioned, it attains the highest driving score in every style within roughly a 2% band; its weakest style surpasses the strongest baseline (DMW) by 5.4%. Average speed and acceleration rise by 18% and 25% when moving from conservative to aggressive instructions.
AI.conclusion: Retrieval-augmented VLA driving with per-style demonstrations enables explicit human-style non-ego agents and style-diverse behavior without per-style retraining, producing consistent performance gains and more aggressive driving tendencies across styles.

title: Action-Effect Memory Pretraining for Robot Manipulation
summary: We present AEM, an Action-Effect Memory pretraining framework for robot manipulation that learns compact temporal representations from vision-action history. Unlike prior robot representation pretraining methods that mainly focus on single-frame visual encoding, AEM targets the temporal nature of manipulation, where the current observation alone is often insufficient under partial observability. AEM models manipulation as an action-driven interaction process by interleaving visual and action features and applying masked modeling to recover missing content from incomplete histories, thereby learning action-conditioned state evolution. The Mamba-encoded output of the final vision token is used as a compact history representation, serving as the global context for decoding and downstream control. This design preserves a single-vector temporal bottleneck while keeping inference efficient. We evaluate AEM with Diffusion Policy and Flow Policy. AEM consistently improves manipulation performance in both simulation and real-world settings, outperforming baselines across clean scenes, cluttered and random scenes, and non-Markovian tasks. Ablation studies further show that history-aware pretraining surpasses single-frame pretraining and direct frame stacking, while reducing inference latency and computational cost.

AI.tldr: AEM learns compact, action-conditioned temporal representations for robot manipulation by masking histories and interleaving vision-action features; final Mamba-encoded token serves as a global history bottleneck for decoding and control.
AI.motivation: Partial observability and non-Markovianities in manipulation tasks limit single-frame representations; a temporally-aware, compact history representation is needed to capture action-driven state evolution efficiently.
AI.method: Action-Effect Memory pretraining that interleaves visual and action features, applies masked modeling to recover missing history content, and uses the Mamba-encoded final vision token as a global history context for decoding and downstream control; evaluated with Diffusion Policy and Flow Policy; includes ablation studies.
AI.result: Histories-aware pretraining consistently improves manipulation performance across simulation and real-world settings (clean, cluttered, random scenes; non-Markovian tasks); reduces inference latency and computational cost compared to single-frame baselines.
AI.conclusion: History-aware pretraining with a single-vector temporal bottleneck outperforms single-frame pretraining and frame stacking, providing efficient, action-conditioned representations that enhance manipulation policies.

title: Boltzmann Attention: Learnable Ising Couplings for Cooperative Attention
summary: Attention mechanisms are central to modern sequence models, yet standard attention computes relevance primarily through individual query--key similarities. Although softmax normalization introduces competition among positions, a standard attention layer does not explicitly parameterize learnable interactions between attention decisions. This limits its ability to directly model cooperative or antagonistic co-attention structure within the attention mechanism itself. We propose Boltzmann attention, an energy-based generalization in which attention patterns are governed by an interacting Ising model. The method augments the usual data-dependent local fields with learnable pairwise couplings, allowing the model to represent inter-position correlations beyond those captured by softmax or sigmoid attention. Experiments on character-level language modeling and synthetic bracket matching show that Boltzmann attention consistently improves over standard softmax attention within a standard Transformer architecture, with the advantage becoming more pronounced as sequence length increases. A four-way ablation confirms that the improvement arises from the learnable pairwise couplings. These results suggest that explicit inter-position interactions provide a principled enhancement for attention-based sequence modeling. Moreover, the Ising formulation opens a natural path toward quantum-computing-based sampling strategies: we demonstrate that diabatic quantum annealing provides a practical training method while maintaining competitive performance with exact Boltzmann computation.

AI.tldr: Boltzmann attention generalizes softmax attention by adding learnable pairwise couplings in an Ising-model style energy, enabling inter-position interactions; it improves Transformer performance on character-level language modeling and synthetic bracket tasks, especially on longer sequences, with ablations attributing gains to the couplings; the Ising formulation also enables quantum annealing-based sampling for training.
AI.motivation: Standard attention relies on query–key similarities and softmax competition, but does not explicitly model inter-position interactions or cooperative/antagonistic co-attention. An energy-based approach with learnable pairwise couplings can capture richer dependencies among attention decisions.
AI.method: Introduce Boltzmann attention by augmenting the usual attention mechanism with an Ising-model energy over positions, incorporating learnable pairwise couplings in addition to local fields; implement within a standard Transformer; evaluate on character-level language modeling and synthetic bracket matching; perform a four-way ablation to isolate effects of pairwise couplings; explore diabatic quantum annealing as a training/sampling method.
AI.result: Boltzmann attention consistently outperforms standard softmax attention within the Transformer, with larger gains as sequence length grows; four-way ablation confirms improvements arise from learnable pairwise couplings; Ising formulation enables diabatic quantum annealing-based training while achieving performance competitive with exact Boltzmann computation.
AI.conclusion: Explicit inter-position interactions via an Ising-based Boltzmann attention provide a principled enhancement for attention-based sequence modeling; the approach opens avenues for quantum-computing-based sampling in training and highlights the value of cooperative/antagonistic co-attention in attention mechanisms.

title: "Did you lie?" Evaluating Lie Detectors across Model Scale and Belief-Verified Model Organisms
summary: Robust lie detectors for language models could enable powerful techniques for auditing, monitoring, and post-hoc investigation of model behaviour, but evaluating them requires testbeds where models verifiably believe the opposite of what they say. We show that existing trained model organisms often fail this requirement, leaving prior positive and negative detection results difficult to interpret. We address this with 13 reasoning model organisms whose hidden beliefs are verified in chain-of-thought and shown to generalise to held-out tasks, alongside Varied Deception, a prompted-lying testbed covering a broad range of lie-inducing motivations. On these testbeds we evaluate four detectors: a chain-of-thought judge, a logprob classifier, and two activation probes, including Did-You-Lie (DYL), a new method for training follow-up probes. On prompted lying, across 31 open-weight models spanning 2B to 1T parameters, all four detectors show positive scaling with model capability. However, every activation- and logprob-based detector drops sharply on our trained model organisms, with DYL retaining the most signal; only the chain-of-thought judge remains strong, with 0.82 balanced accuracy, partly as an artefact of our verification process favouring CoT-readable beliefs. Current lie detectors therefore cannot support high-confidence claims about model beliefs, and we suggest research directions that may address some of their current limitations. We release our datasets, model organisms, and trained detectors.

AI.tldr: Evaluates lie detectors for language models using 13 reasoning model organisms with verifiable beliefs and a broad prompted-lying testbed. Finds that detectors degrade on trained organisms, with chain-of-thought based methods most robust; prompts-scale with model size but high-confidence belief detection remains limited. Datasets, organisms, and detectors released.
AI.motivation: Establish robust, interpretable benchmarks for auditing model beliefs and deception; previous model organisms fail verifiable belief constraints, complicating interpretation of lie-detection results.
AI.method: Create 13 reasoning model organisms with hidden beliefs verifiable via chain-of-thought; develop Varied Deception prompted-lying testbed; evaluate four detectors (chain-of-thought judge, logprob classifier, two activation probes including Did-You-Lie); test across 31 open-weight models from 2B to 1T parameters; additionally assess performance on trained-model organisms.
AI.result: On prompted lying, detectors scale with model capability across models. However, when evaluated on trained-model organisms, activation and logprob detectors collapse, with DYL retaining the most signal; the chain-of-thought judge remains the strongest detector, achieving 0.82 balanced accuracy, partly due to verification bias toward CoT-readable beliefs.
AI.conclusion: Current lie detectors are insufficient to make high-confidence claims about model beliefs. The paper suggests directions to improve robustness and interpretability and releases datasets, organisms, and trained detectors for reproducibility.

title: Foresight: Iterative Reasoning About Clues that Matter for Navigation
summary: Open-world mapless navigation from sparse language instructions requires resolving underspecified goals and inferring which environmental cues are relevant for reaching the goal. For instance, reaching an out-of-view destination may require interpreting ramps, signs, or detours that reveal where to go or which route to take. Prior works are limited by their reliance on known navigation factors and closed-set factor categories, or identify cues before motion planning and miss plan-dependent cues. We argue that pretrained Vision-Language Models (VLMs) can discover novel instruction-relevant cues, but require adaptation to focus on which cues matter and how they should influence motion planning. We realize these ideas in Foresight, a test-time framework in which a finetuned VLM alternates between proposing image-space motion plans and critiquing them using the language goal and visual context. Subsequent plans are conditioned on prior critiques, enabling iterative motion refinement before execution. To align plan critiques and refinements with open-set behavior preferences, we learn a reward model from human feedback and use it to post-train the VLM with reinforcement learning in the plan-critique loop. In offline evaluations and 6 real-world environments, Foresight improves average task success by 37% and reduces interventions per mission by 52% relative to state-of-the-art test-time reasoning and foundation-model baselines, while running in real-time on a Jetson AGX Orin. We will release code, data, and training details to support future work on test-time reasoning for robot motion refinement. Additional videos at: https://amrl.cs.utexas.edu/foresight

AI.tldr: Vision-Language Model-based test-time reasoning loop (Foresight) for open-world, mapless navigation; iterative plan-critique with human-feedback RL; real-world success uplift and efficiency gains; real-time on Jetson AGX Orin; code/data release.
AI.motivation: Address underspecified, open-set navigation goals and reliance on known factors; enable discovery of novel, instruction-relevant cues via VLMs, and align them with motion planning.
AI.method: Finetuned VLM alternates between proposing image-space motion plans and critiquing them against language goals and visual context; plans conditioned on prior critiques; reward model learned from human feedback; post-train VLM with RL within plan-critique loop; evaluation offline and in 6 real-world environments; real-time on edge hardware.
AI.result: Average task success improved by 37%; interventions per mission reduced by 52% relative to SOTA/test-time reasoning and foundation-model baselines; runs in real-time on Jetson AGX Orin; will release code/data.
AI.conclusion: Shows viability of test-time reasoning and plan refinement using VLMs for open-set navigation; demonstrates integration of human-feedback-driven reward modeling; supports open research on aligning VLMs with motion planning in robotics.

title: ReCal: Reward Calibration for RL-based LLM Routing
summary: Large language model (LLM) routing has emerged as an effective paradigm for leveraging the complementary strengths of multiple LLMs through dynamic model and reasoning-strategy selection. Recent reinforcement learning (RL)-based routing methods further improve routing quality by optimizing routing policies from interaction feedback. However, they still struggle to provide informative and comparable learning signals under heterogeneous tasks with varying difficulty. In practice, multiple objectives (e.g., correctness, format behavior) are aggregated into a single scalar reward, leading to ambiguous credit assignment and conflicting optimization signals. Moreover, reward signals exhibit significant variability across instances, where some instances produce higher or more variable rewards, introducing optimization bias that favors trivial samples over informative ones. To address these issues, we propose \textbf{ReCal}, a \textbf{\underline{Re}}ward \textbf{\underline{Cal}}ibration framework for RL-based LLM routing. We first introduce a hierarchical reward decomposition mechanism with component-wise advantage estimation. We further propose a distribution-aware optimization strategy that calibrates optimization variability through variance-aware reweighting and per-dataset normalization. Experiments on seven datasets demonstrate that ReCal consistently improves routing performance, and training stability over baselines. Code is available at https://anonymous.4open.science/r/ReCal.

AI.tldr: ReCal introduces reward calibration for RL-based LLM routing, using hierarchical reward decomposition with component-wise advantage estimation and distribution-aware optimization (variance-aware reweighting and per-dataset normalization). It improves routing performance and training stability across seven datasets, with code released.
AI.motivation: Addresses credit assignment and optimization bias when combining multiple objectives into a single scalar reward in RL-based LLM routing, and mitigates performance variance across instances that can bias learning.
AI.method: Proposes a two-part approach: (1) hierarchical reward decomposition with component-wise advantage estimation to provide more informative credit signals; (2) distribution-aware optimization with variance-aware reweighting and per-dataset normalization to stabilize learning and reduce bias from variable rewards.
AI.result: Empirical evaluations on seven datasets show consistent improvements in routing performance and training stability compared with baselines.
AI.conclusion: ReCal effectively calibrates reward signals for RL-based LLM routing, improving effectiveness and stability; code is publicly available.

title: Stereo Vision-Based Fall Prediction and Detection using Human Pose Estimation on the AMD Kria K26 SOM
summary:
Background and Objective: Falls among elderly people can cause serious injury and reduce quality of life. Timely prediction and detection are essential to prevent harm and support well-being. We propose a portable, low-power, battery-operated, vision-based fall prediction and detection system using HPE on an AMD Kria K26 System-on-Module (SOM). The objective is a non-intrusive, privacy-preserving system for real-time fall detection.
  Methods: The system uses an Intel RealSense D455 range-sensing camera connected to the K26 SOM by USB. It captures synchronized RGB and depth frames, 640 x 480 x 3 and 640 x 480 pixels, at 60 FPS. The SOM runs a three-stage pipeline with quantized YOLOX, Anchor-to-Joint (A2J), and fall-detection models. YOLOX identifies human bounding boxes from RGB frames, then discards the RGB frames to preserve privacy. A2J uses depth frames to estimate 15 joint keypoints per person. A CNN uses selected joint coordinates (x, y, z) to classify fall activity. YOLOX was trained on CrowdHuman; A2J on ITOP, MP-3DHP, UR Fall Detection, and a custom SDSU PSG dataset; and the CNN on UR Fall Detection and SDSU PSG. The design used a single-core DPU with a serial pipeline and a dual-core DPU running YOLOX and A2J with multiple threads.
  Results: Quantized accuracy was evaluated using IoU >= 50% for YOLOX, mAP with a 10-cm rule for A2J, and classification accuracy, (TP + TN)/(TP + TN + FP + FN), for the CNN. Accuracies were 74%, 84.13%, and 75.85%. Throughput improved from 2.5 FPS for the single-threaded pipeline to 4.5 FPS for the multi-threaded version.
  Conclusion: Results demonstrate the feasibility of privacy-preserving fall detection on an AMD Kria K26 edge device. On-device HPE and fall classification runs without cloud dependency, supporting elderly monitoring and assistive healthcare. Future work will improve model accuracy and speed.

AI.tldr: A privacy-preserving, on-device, edge-based system for real-time fall prediction/detection using RGB-D sensing on AMD Kria K26 with a three-stage pipeline (YOLOX, A2J, CNN).
AI.motivation: Falls in the elderly cause injuries and quality of life reduction; need non-intrusive, real-time monitoring with privacy and no cloud dependency.
AI.method: Three-stage pipeline: YOLOX identifies human bounding boxes on RGB frames; A2J uses depth frames to estimate 15 joint keypoints; CNN classifies fall activity from selected joint coordinates. Hardware is AMD Kria K26 SOM with single-core DPU for a serial pipeline and dual-core DPU for multi-threaded operation. Training data from multiple datasets (CrowdHuman for YOLOX; ITOP, MP-3DHP, UR Fall Detection, SDSU PSG for A2J; UR Fall Detection, SDSU PSG for CNN). Privacy preserved by discarding RGB after bounding box. Real-time processing at up to 4.5 FPS (improved from 2.5 FPS in single-threaded).
AI.result: Quantized accuracy: YOLOX IoU ≥ 50% = 74%; A2J mean average precision (mAP) with a 10-cm rule = 84.13%; CNN classification accuracy = 75.85%. Throughput improved from 2.5 FPS (single-thread) to 4.5 FPS (multi-thread).
AI.conclusion: Feasibility of privacy-preserving fall detection on an AMD Kria K26 edge device demonstrated; on-device human pose estimation and fall classification without cloud dependency; supports elderly monitoring and assistive healthcare. Future work to improve accuracy and speed.

title: TrajGenAgent: A Hierarchical LLM Agent for Human Mobility Trajectory Generation
comment: 14 pages, 2 figures, 8 tables. Accepted by the 27th IEEE International Conference on Mobile Data Management (MDM 2026)
summary: Human mobility data is important for transportation, urban planning, and epidemic control, but large-scale trajectory collection is often costly and privacy-constrained, motivating realistic synthetic trajectory generation. Existing LLM-based generators typically rely on either prompt engineering, which preserves zero-shot reasoning but lacks fine-grained spatiotemporal grounding, or trajectory-level fine-tuning, which improves statistical precision but incurs substantial computational cost and may weaken general reasoning. We propose TrajGenAgent, a semantic-aware hierarchical LLM-agent framework for human mobility trajectory generation without model fine-tuning. TrajGenAgent uses a two-stage orchestrator-worker design: an LLM first synthesizes an individual- and weekday-conditioned activity chain from historical evidence via in-context learning, and a deterministic workflow then grounds each activity into a complete visit using personalized POI retrieval, distance-aware location selection, kinematics-aware travel-time propagation, and LLM-based duration estimation. To evaluate realism beyond aggregate spatiotemporal statistics, we introduce an anomaly-detection-based evaluation framework using two complementary detectors to assess behavioral and semantic plausibility. Experiments on benchmark and large-scale simulation datasets show that TrajGenAgent improves spatiotemporal fidelity, semantic coherence, and individual-specific behavioral realism over representative neural and LLM-based baselines, while avoiding parameter updates.

AI.tldr: TrajGenAgent is a non-finetuned, semantic-aware hierarchical LLM-agent that generates realistic synthetic human mobility trajectories via a two-stage orchestrator-worker framework, with anomaly-based evaluation showing improved fidelity.
AI.motivation: Addresses the need for large-scale, privacy-respecting synthetic mobility data for transportation planning, urban analysis, and epidemic control, balancing grounding quality with computational efficiency.
AI.method: Two-stage design: Stage 1 uses in-context learning in an LLM to synthesize individual- and weekday-conditioned activity chains from historical evidence. Stage 2 employs a deterministic grounding workflow to convert activities into visits via personalized POI retrieval, distance-aware location selection, kinematics-aware travel-time propagation, and LLM-based duration estimation. No parameter updates. Evaluation employs an anomaly-detection-based framework with two detectors for behavioral and semantic plausibility.
AI.result: Empirical results on benchmark and large-scale datasets show improved spatiotemporal fidelity, semantic coherence, and individual-specific realism over representative neural and LLM baselines, while avoiding parameter updates.
AI.conclusion: TrajGenAgent demonstrates effective, scalable generation of realistic mobility trajectories without model fine-tuning, with strong grounding and evaluation, suitable for privacy-preserving data generation and planning.

title: G-MAPP: GPU-accelerated Multi-Agent Planning and Perception for Reactive Motion Generation
comment: The implementation is available at: https://github.com/chart-research/g-mapp
summary: Reactive motion generation in unstructured environments remains an open challenge in robotics. Due to the computational complexity of collision-free motion generation, existing methods either generate global trajectories for static scenarios, or employ models that make conservative assumptions about the environment. This paper identifies the primary bottleneck as the runtime performance demand of planning on high-fidelity environments, and the temporal integration between the perception and planning modules. Therefore, we propose a framework that does not compromise on runtime performance and world representations for perception and planning by accelerating world modeling and vector-field based planning using the GPU. This allows us to achieve faster parallel state exploration for quasi-global trajectory planning, and tighter coupling of the perception-action loop in real-time for dynamic cluttered environments with off-the-shelf depth sensors. We quantitatively evaluate the computation-time and success rate differences for the CPU and GPU versions of our planner, and perform qualitative evaluations of our coupled framework using real-world experiments on a 7-DoF Franka Emika robot. Experimental results demonstrate that our GPU-based framework achieves up to a 5x speedup over the CPU version and successfully avoids collisions across both trivial and challenging physical world scenarios.

AI.tldr: GPU-accelerated reactive motion planning framework enabling real-time collision-free navigation in dynamic clutter, achieving up to 5x speedup over CPU while using high-fidelity world modeling.
AI.motivation: Addresses runtime bottlenecks in planning on high-fidelity environments and the tight perception–planning coupling necessary for real-time reactive motion in unstructured settings.
AI.method: GPU-accelerated world modeling and vector-field based planning to enable parallel state exploration; tight perception–action loop; real-time evaluation with off-the-shelf depth sensors on a 7-DoF Franka Emika robot.
AI.result: Demonstrates up to 5× speedup of GPU vs CPU in computation time; successful collision avoidance in both trivial and challenging real-world scenarios; qualitative and quantitative assessments of compute-time and success rate.
AI.conclusion: GPU-based acceleration enables real-time, reactive motion generation in dynamic clutter without compromising environmental representations or planning fidelity, enabling tighter perception–action coupling.

title: Representing Time Series as Structured Programs for LLM Reasoning
summary: Large language models (LLMs) have demonstrated strong reasoning and instruction-following capabilities, making them potentially powerful tools for time-series analysis. However, time series lie outside their native textual modality, raising a fundamental question: how should time series be represented so that LLMs can reason about them effectively? Existing work typically serializes raw numerical sequences or fine-tunes pre-trained LLMs on time-series data. These approaches place the burden of extracting temporal structure directly on the LLM, creating a modality mismatch that often degrades performance on long sequences and introduces substantial computational overhead. In this work, we introduce Time-Series-to-Structured-Program representation (T2SP), a deterministic, training-free method that represents a time series as a structured symbolic program. T2SP decomposes time series into trends, periods, and salient events, expressing them in a program-friendly format aligned with the textual and code-like modalities on which LLMs are natively trained. By shifting temporal-structure extraction from the model to the representation itself, T2SP enables off-the-shelf LLMs to leverage their existing reasoning capabilities for time-series understanding. We evaluate T2SP on three reasoning tasks -- editing, captioning, and question answering -- where it consistently improves performance, reduces reasoning time, and lowers failure rates compared with raw-string representations. Our results demonstrate that T2SP provides an effective interface between time series and LLMs.

AI.tldr: A deterministic, training-free method (T2SP) converts time series into a structured symbolic program to align with LLMs' textual/code modalities, enabling improved reasoning without model fine-tuning.
AI.motivation: Time series lie outside the native textual modality of LLMs, causing a modality mismatch and added computational burden when forcing the model to extract temporal structure or when serializing raw numbers. A representation that encodes temporal structure externally can leverage LLM reasoning more effectively.
AI.method: Time-Series-to-Structured-Program representation (T2SP) decomposes a time series into trends, periods, and salient events, encoding these components as a structured symbolic program suitable for textual/code-like processing by LLMs. It is deterministic and training-free, shifting temporal-structure extraction from the model to the representation.
AI.result: Across three reasoning tasks—editing, captioning, and question answering—T2SP yields consistent performance gains, reduces reasoning time, and lowers failure rates relative to raw-string representations.
AI.conclusion: T2SP provides an effective interface between time series and LLMs by offloading temporal-structure extraction to the representation, enabling off-the-shelf LLMs to leverage their reasoning capabilities for time-series understanding.

title: HairPort: In-context 3D-aware Hair Import and Transfer for Images
comment: Accepted to SIGGRAPH 2026 (Conference Papers Track). 23 pages, 15 figures, 10 tables, including supplementary material as appendices. Project page: https://deepmancer.github.io/HairPort/
summary: Transferring hairstyles between images is an important but challenging task in computer graphics, computer vision, and visual effects. It enables users to explore new looks without physically altering their hair, with applications in virtual try-on systems, augmented reality, and entertainment. Most prior works operate best under small pose gaps, and they fall short under large viewpoint and scale differences, where missing hair content must be synthesized rather than transferred. We propose HairPort, a 3D-aware hairstyle transfer framework that attempts to solve these issues by explicitly separating hair removal from transfer and enforcing geometric consistency before synthesis. We introduce a Bald Converter, which produces realistic bald versions of faces through LoRA-based in-context adaptation of FLUX.1 Kontext. To train our Bald Converter, we introduce a new dataset, Baldy, containing 6,000 paired bald and original images across diverse identities and conditions. We also use a 3D-Aware Transfer Pipeline that reconstructs and re-renders the reference hairstyle from the target viewpoint before compositing it onto the source image. Being 3D aware, our method supports large pose and scale discrepancies between the source and target. Finally, a conditional flow-matching generator synthesizes the transferred result from the bald source and geometry-aligned reference guidance. Together, our method enables accurate, pose-consistent, and identity-preserving hairstyle transfer, outperforming existing methods both qualitatively and quantitatively.

AI.tldr: HairPort is a 3D-aware hairstyle transfer framework that decouples hair removal from transfer, uses a Bald Converter trained via LoRA-based in-context adaptation on a Baldy dataset, and employs a 3D-aware pipeline to re-render reference hairstyles from the target viewpoint before synthesis with a conditional flow-matching generator; demonstrating superior performance under large pose changes and preserving identity.
AI.motivation: Current hairstyle transfer methods struggle with large pose/scale gaps and missing hair content; they often transfer content but fail to synthesize unseen regions. There is a need to separate hair removal from transfer and enforce geometric consistency to enable robust, pose-consistent transfers.
AI.method: 1) Bald Converter: LoRA-based in-context adaptation of FLUX to produce realistic bald faces. 2) Baldy dataset: 6,000 paired bald and original images for training. 3) 3D-Aware Transfer Pipeline: reconstructs and re-renders the reference hairstyle from the target viewpoint, then composites onto the source image. 4) Conditional flow-matching generator: synthesizes the transferred result from the bald source and geometry-aligned guidance.
AI.result: Claims quantitative and qualitative improvements over existing methods, with better pose robustness, realism, and identity preservation.
AI.conclusion: HairPort enables accurate, pose-consistent, and identity-preserving hairstyle transfer across large pose and scale differences by explicitly removing hair prior to transfer, enforcing geometry, and synthesizing the final appearance conditioned on geometry-guided guidance.

title: Evoflux: Inference-Time Evolution of Executable Tool Workflows for Compact Agents
summary: Compact language models (LMs) reduce cost, latency, and deployment risk for tool agents. Yet MCP-style tool use requires more than isolated function calling: an agent must discover tools from live catalogs, satisfy schemas, preserve dependencies across intermediate outputs, and ground final responses in executed evidence. Small planners often generate plausible workflow graphs that fail under tool resolution, parameter validation, dependency tracking, or execution. We argue that this failure mode is poorly handled by small-corpus distillation. A few hundred teacher traces can teach workflow format, but rarely cover the recovery behavior needed to repair failed plans over changing tool catalogs. We introduce Evoflux, an inference-time evolutionary search method that treats compact tool use as the repair of executable tool workflows. It evolves typed workflow graphs through structured edits, execution feedback, adaptive intensity, meta-guided redesign, and diversity pruning. On held-out MCP-Bench tasks spanning live MCP servers and 250 tools, Evoflux raises execution feasibility from roughly 3% to 17-24% across small planners. In contrast, SFT and SFT+DPO on the same search-mined data match, underperform, or collapse below zero-shot performance; ReAct reaches higher peaks, but with higher variance and token cost. These results show that execution-grounded search is more reliable under scarce teacher-trace budgets.

AI.tldr: Evoflux is an inference-time evolutionary search that repairs executable tool workflows to improve MCP-style tool use. It boosts execution feasibility dramatically on live MCP benchmarks by grounding planning in execution feedback, outperforming standard SFT, SFT+DPO, and ReAct under scarce labeled traces.
AI.motivation: Current MCP tool-use requires robust handling of live catalogs, schema validation, dependencies, and evidence-grounded responses. Small-corpus distillation often fails to recover from planning errors caused by changing tool catalogs; there is a need for execution-grounded repair methods that can adapt at inference time.
AI.method: Evoflux maintains typed workflow graphs and evolves them via structured edits, execution feedback loops, adaptive search intensity, meta-guided redesign, and diversity pruning. It treats tool-use as repairing workflows, using evolutionary search to repair/resize graphs based on actual tool execution results across multiple tools (up to ~250) in live MCP servers.
AI.result: On held-out MCP-Bench tasks, Evoflux increases execution feasibility from ~3% (baseline small planners) to 17–24% across various planners. In comparisons, SFT and SFT+DPO under similar data conditions perform at or below baseline, while ReAct achieves higher peaks but with higher variance and cost. The findings indicate execution-grounded search is more reliable when training data (teacher traces) are scarce.
AI.conclusion: Execution-grounded, repair-oriented search is a more reliable strategy for compact LMs in MCP-style tool use under limited supervision. Evoflux demonstrates that repairing executable workflows at inference time can significantly improve feasibility and robustness compared to traditional planning or action-reasoning baselines.

title: From Imitation to Alignment: Human-Preference Flow Policies for Long-Horizon Sidewalk Navigation
summary: Autonomous long-horizon sidewalk navigation is essential for micro-mobility applications such as robotic food delivery and assistive electronic wheelchairs. Unlike autonomous driving on the road, long-horizon sidewalk navigation requires precise maneuvering through unpredictable sidewalk terrains and pedestrians, with a lightweight perception stack as minimal as a single monocular RGB camera. While imitation learning (IL) from demonstrations offers a practical solution, the resulting autopilot policy often suffers from compounding errors, a lack of social compliance on sidewalks, and deficiencies in counterfactual reasoning to handle complex situations. To address these challenges, we introduce FlowPilot, a mapless navigation policy that achieves robust and efficient long-horizon navigation performance using only a monocular RGB camera. We first propose to use anchored flow matching as an action representation for policy pre-training on large-scale robot fleet data and to capture the diverse, complex, multimodal distribution of sidewalk navigation behaviors. To bridge the gap between imitation and alignment, we further design a human-in-the-loop preference learning scheme to tune the policy on a small amount of human intervention data. It strengthens the model's counterfactual reasoning and social compliance on sidewalks. We evaluate FlowPilot through extensive simulation and real-world experiments in diverse sidewalk environments. FlowPilot achieves 42% success rate and 66% route completion in simulation, while FlowPilot-HP further improves real-world robustness and social compliance, reducing IR by 40.0% and NIR by 52.1% relative to the base model.

AI.tldr: FlowPilot uses monocular RGB for long-horizon sidewalk navigation with anchor flow matching for policy pre-training and a human-in-the-loop preference scheme to improve counterfactual reasoning and social compliance; achieves 42% success and 66% route completion in simulation, with FlowPilot-HP improving real-world robustness and reducing error metrics.
AI.motivation: Autonomous sidewalk navigation is harder than road driving due to unpredictable terrains and pedestrians, requiring lightweight perception and robust policy learning to avoid compounding errors and social violations.
AI.method: Proposes anchored flow matching as an action representation for policy pre-training on large-scale robot fleet data to capture multimodal sidewalk behaviors; introduces a human-in-the-loop preference learning stage to fine-tune the policy on limited intervention data, enhancing counterfactual reasoning and social compliance; evaluates in simulation and real-world experiments using only a monocular RGB camera.
AI.result: In simulation, FlowPilot achieves 42% success rate and 66% route completion. FlowPilot-HP improves real-world robustness and social compliance, reducing IR by 40% and NIR by 52.1% relative to the base model.
AI.conclusion: FlowPilot demonstrates effective mapless, monocular RGB navigation for long-horizon sidewalk tasks, with the HP variant offering stronger social compliance and robustness via human-in-the-loop preference tuning.

title: Scalable anomaly detection via a univariate Christoffel function
summary: Anomaly detection plays a critical role in identifying unusual patterns across domains such as fraud detection, network intrusion, and system fault diagnosis. Recently, Christoffel function-based methods, rooted in polynomial optimization, have emerged as promising alternatives to deep learning due to their strong mathematical foundations and computational frugality. However, their practical applicability is hindered by the need to invert a matrix whose size grows exponentially with the data dimension, rendering the method intractable even for moderate-dimensional datasets. This paper addresses the dimensionality limitations of Christoffel function-based anomaly detection while preserving its key theoretical properties, i.e., the on-off support dichotomy behavior and the accurate support shape capture. We introduce UCF, a univariate Christoffel function which is based on the squared distance between the query point and the support points. Extensive experiments on the ADBench benchmark demonstrate that UCF consistently outperforms 14 state-of-the-art baselines in terms of Average Precision. By resolving the scalability bottleneck of the Christoffel Function, this work expands the toolkit of anomaly detection methods with a robust, theoretically grounded, and universally applicable approach.

AI.tldr: Introduces UCF, a scalable univariate Christoffel-based anomaly detector that preserves key theoretical properties and outperforms multiple baselines on ADBench.
AI.motivation: Christoffel-function-based anomaly detection suffers from an exponential growth in matrix size with data dimensionality, hindering practicality; a scalable approach is needed that retains the on-off support dichotomy and accurate support shape capture.
AI.method: Develops UCF by leveraging the squared distance to support points to construct a univariate Christoffel function, avoiding multivariate matrix inversions while preserving the characteristic support-related properties.
AI.result: Empirical evaluation on the ADBench benchmark shows UCF consistently outperforms 14 state-of-the-art baselines in Average Precision.
AI.conclusion: By resolving the scalability bottleneck of the Christoffel Function, the method broadens the anomaly detection toolkit with a theoretically grounded, robust, and universally applicable approach.

title: High-Fidelity Two-Step Image Generation via Teacher-Aligned End-to-End Distillation
summary: Few-step diffusion distillation has become increasingly mature for 4-8-step generation, yet pushing further to 2 steps remains challenging. In this work, we introduce Z-Image Turbo++, a high-quality 2-step image generation model distilled from the 8-step Z-Image Turbo teacher. Our method addresses the central bottlenecks of increased task difficulty and limited model capacity in 2-step generation through three simple but effective design choices tailored to this regime. First, we propose Distribution-Aligned Adversarial Learning, which uses teacher-generated images rather than external real images as real samples for GAN training, providing a more attainable and informative adversarial target. Second, we adopt Step-Decoupled Parameterization, assigning independent model parameters to the two denoising steps to better match their distinct capacity demands. Third, we perform End-to-End Training with Iterative Regularization, allowing the first step to receive gradients from final image quality while preserving a meaningful intermediate generation through an explicit step-1 loss. Together, these designs substantially narrow the quality gap between 2-step and 8-step generation in both qualitative and quantitative evaluations, highlighting the potential of carefully tailored distillation strategies for improving the quality-efficiency trade-off in few-step generation.

AI.tldr: A strong 2-step image-generation model (Z-Image Turbo++) distilled from an 8-step teacher, achieving near-8-step quality with three targeted design choices.
AI.motivation: Few-step diffusion distillation has matured to 4-8 steps; pushing to 2 steps is challenging due to task difficulty and limited model capacity; distillation is needed to close the quality gap while preserving efficiency.
AI.method: Three design choices: 1) Distribution-Aligned Adversarial Learning using teacher-generated images as GAN real samples; 2) Step-Decoupled Parameterization with independent parameters per denoising step; 3) End-to-End Training with Iterative Regularization enabling gradient flow to step 1 via an explicit step-1 loss while preserving intermediate outputs.
AI.result: Empirically narrows the quality gap between 2-step and 8-step generation in both qualitative and quantitative evaluations; demonstrates improved quality-efficiency trade-off for few-step generation.
AI.conclusion: Tailored distillation strategies can effectively transfer quality from longer-step teachers to ultra-fast 2-step generation; approach could generalize to other few-step regimes.

title: From AGI to ASI
summary: Over the last decade, building human-level artificial general intelligence has moved from far-fetched speculation to being a concrete next-decade target for many of the largest AI organisations. Achieving this goal would have profound and far-reaching impacts on human society, which raises many complex questions for the decade ahead. This report investigates how AI itself might continue to develop in a post-AGI world along the continuum of machine intelligence. The endpoint of this continuum, Universal AI, is theoretically well understood, which provides some formal grounding for the main focus of this report: the transition from human-level AGI to artificial general superintelligence, which, intuitively, can be understood as a system that is more intelligent and cognitively capable than large organisations of humans. After characterizing ASI, the report discusses four potential pathways from AGI to ASI: scaling AGI, AI paradigm shifts, recursive improvement, and ASI emerging from large-scale multi-agent collectives. The report then discusses possible frictions and bottlenecks along these pathways. Determining whether the impact of these frictions will be negligible or substantial raises a number of concrete open research questions. Due to large uncertainties for predicting ASI progress, it cannot be ruled out that AI progress might continue to accelerate over the next years. This could imply that the image of a single transformative step change, caused by the introduction of human-level AGI into our society, could be inaccurate. More apt might be the prospect of a series of transformative societal changes caused by AI-enabled progress and breakthroughs across many areas of science and technology. Preparing for this prospect requires a massively interdisciplinary endeavour of global scope and interest.

AI.tldr: A forward-looking, theory-driven exploration of the transition from AGI to artificial general superintelligence (ASI). It identifies four main pathways (scaling, paradigm shifts, recursive improvement, and multi-agent collectives), discusses bottlenecks and open research questions, and argues for broad interdisciplinary preparation given uncertain timelines and potential acceleration in AI progress.
AI.motivation: To formalize the concept of ASI, map plausible routes from AGI to ASI, and assess potential frictions and societal implications to inform policy, research priorities, and safety considerations.
AI.method: Theoretical, conceptual analysis grounded in existing AI progress literature. The paper characterizes ASI and outlines four progression pathways, then discusses bottlenecks, uncertainties, and the need for interdisciplinary, global preparation.
AI.result: Provides a structured framework for thinking about AGI-to-ASI transition, enumerates four plausible pathways, highlights potential bottlenecks and research questions, and emphasizes uncertainty and the necessity of global coordination to prepare for transformative changes.
AI.conclusion: Due to significant uncertainties and potential acceleration in AI progress, society should anticipate possible series of transformative AI-driven changes. A massively interdisciplinary, global effort is required to understand, govern, and prepare for a world where superintelligent AI could emerge and influence many domains.

title: EgoEngine: From Egocentric Human Videos to High-Fidelity Dexterous Robot Demonstrations
summary: Dexterous manipulation is limited by the cost of collecting large-scale robot demonstrations. Egocentric human videos offer a scalable source of diverse manipulation behaviors, but directly using them for robot learning requires bridging two gaps: the visual gap between human and robot observations, and the action gap between human motion and robot-executable action. We propose EgoEngine, a scalable framework for transforming egocentric human manipulation videos into high-fidelity robot data. Given an egocentric RGB video, EgoEngine produces: (i) a high-fidelity robot observation video replacing human with robot while preserving scene context and temporal alignment, and (ii) a task-aligned, executable robot action trajectory under feasibility constraints. Experiments in simulation and on real robots show that EgoEngine enables scalable conversion of human videos into robot data and, to our knowledge, demonstrates the first zero-shot visuomotor dexterous policy learning from egocentric human videos without real-robot demonstrations. Project website: https://egoengine.github.io.

AI.tldr: EgoEngine converts egocentric human manipulation videos into robot-ready data, generating a visually consistent robot observation video and executable action trajectories, enabling zero-shot visuomotor dexterous policy learning without real-robot demonstrations.
AI.motivation: Reduce reliance on expensive large-scale robot demonstrations by leveraging scalable egocentric human videos; bridge visual and action gaps between humans and robots.
AI.method: Pipeline that (i) substitutes the human with a robot in the video while preserving scene context and temporal alignment; (ii) computes feasible, task-aligned robot action trajectories; validated in simulation and on real robots.
AI.result: Shows scalable conversion of human videos to robot data; demonstrates the first zero-shot visuomotor dexterous policy learning from egocentric human videos without real-robot demonstrations.
AI.conclusion: EgoEngine enables scalable data generation for robot dexterous manipulation and supports zero-shot policy learning, potentially reducing need for real-robot demonstrations.

title: Speculative Rollback Correction for Quality-Diverse Web Agent Imitation
summary: Training interactive web agents through imitation learning from expert trajectories has emerged as a highly effective approach. However, determining the optimal timing for expert intervention presents a critical challenge in this context. Delayed intervention often leads to the accumulation of early-stage errors, pushing the page state into an irrecoverable regime. Conversely, premature or excessive intervention causes the agent to become overly reliant on expert policies, trapping the model in local optima characterized by a single, rigid trajectory. We propose Speculative Rollback Correction (SRC), a branch-level imitation framework for resettable agent environments. Instead of requesting teacher labels at every visited state or correcting only after a completed trajectory, SRC uses fixed-horizon branch review: the student executes a short speculative segment before teacher review, and the teacher localizes the first harmful deviation only when local progress breaks. Rollback preserves useful prefixes, while successful rollouts are filtered by a hard verifier and retained in a lightweight quality-diversity archive. The resulting data supports next-action supervised fine-tuning on both localized corrections and verifier-passing trajectories. On WebArena-Infinity, SRC collects 977 verifier-passing trajectories and 9,183 next-action examples; fixed-horizon review improves the recovery-versus-query tradeoff over step-level review while retaining verifier-passing solution variants. Code is available at https://github.com/LongkunHao/SRC_gui_agent.

AI.tldr: Speculative Rollback Correction (SRC) is a branch-level imitation framework for resettable agent environments that uses fixed-horizon branch review to balance timely expert intervention and autonomous learning, enhancing recovery-versus-query tradeoffs and yielding verifier-passing, diverse data for next-action fine-tuning.
AI.motivation: To resolve the timing dilemma in imitation learning for interactive web agents: delaying expert intervention causes compounding errors; excessive reliance on expert policies leads to local optima. SRC introduces speculative, localized rollback with verifier filtering to improve data efficiency and policy robustness.
AI.method: Student executes a fixed-horizon speculative segment; the teacher localizes the first harmful deviation only if local progress breaks; rollback preserves useful prefixes; successful rollouts passing a hard verifier are stored in a lightweight quality-diversity archive; next-action supervised fine-tuning uses localized corrections and verifier-passing trajectories.
AI.result: On WebArena-Infinity, SRC collected 977 verifier-passing trajectories and 9,183 next-action examples; fixed-horizon review improves the recovery-versus-query tradeoff compared to step-level review and retains verifier-passing solution variants.
AI.conclusion: SRC provides a practical, data-efficient framework for resettable agents by balancing timely corrections with autonomous exploration, enabling richer supervision signals through verifier-passing trajectories and a diverse data archive.

title: Analyzing and Improving Fine-grained Preference Optimization in Medical LVLMs
summary: Large Vision-Language Models (LVLMs) have achieved strong performance across medical imaging tasks, yet they remain prone to factual inconsistencies, poor visual grounding, and misalignment with clinically meaningful feedback. Existing post-training alignment approaches, including Direct Preference Optimization (DPO) and its variants, face three critical limitations in the medical domain: (1) sequence-level reward signals treat clinically critical tokens identically to generic filler text; (2) reliance on static supervised fine-tuning references as preferred responses introduces an off-policy distribution shift, steering optimization toward stylistic artifacts over clinical correctness; and (3) alignment objectives lack explicit visual grounding constraints, leaving models insensitive to subtle yet diagnostically decisive pathological features. Our method leverages a bidirectional token-wise KL regularizer alongside a visual-contrastive grounding objective that pairs clean and lesion-corrupted images to penalize responses generated without adequate visual evidence. Together, these components form a fine-grained, on-policy alignment framework that constructs preference pairs by minimally editing model-generated outputs, correcting only clinically erroneous spans while preserving the original linguistic style. Extensive experiments across medical imaging tasks and clinical text generation benchmarks validate the effectiveness of our approach.

AI.tldr: A fine-grained, on-policy alignment framework for Large Vision-Language Models (LVLMs) in medical imaging, combining a bidirectional token-wise KL regularizer with a visual-contrastive grounding objective to improve factual accuracy, clinical relevance, and visual grounding, addressing sequence-level reward misalignment, off-policy distribution shifts from static references, and lack of explicit grounding constraints.
AI.motivation: Current post-training alignment methods (e.g., DPO) apply sequence-level rewards that treat critical clinical tokens the same as filler text, rely on static supervised references that induce off-policy artifacts, and lack explicit visual grounding constraints, leading to misinformation and poor diagnostically grounded outputs in medical LVLMs.
AI.method: Introduce a fine-grained on-policy alignment approach comprising (1) a bidirectional token-wise KL regularizer that calibrates token-level distributions to penalize incorrect clinical spans, and (2) a visual-contrastive grounding objective pairing clean and lesion-corrupted images to penalize responses lacking adequate visual evidence. Preference pairs are constructed by minimally editing model outputs, preserving original linguistic style while correcting clinically erroneous spans.
