AI.method: Apply spectral MDP decomposition to identify transferable dynamics and structure the value function into a shared, task-agnostic core with a minimal task-specific adjustment; integrate this decomposition into SAC for multi-task learning and evaluation on in-distribution and out-of-distribution tasks.
AI.result: Demonstrates strong zero-shot performance on in-distribution tasks and rapid few-shot adaptation to out-of-distribution tasks; outperforms baselines by up to 30% on quadcopter trajectory-following tasks across ID and OOD contexts.
AI.conclusion: RepMT-SAC enables efficient knowledge sharing and robust transfer in multi-task RL by leveraging spectral dynamics, achieving strong zero-shot performance and fast adaptation, indicating promise for scalable multi-task control.

title: Improving Crash Frequency Prediction from Simulated Traffic Conflicts Using Machine Learning Based Microsimulation
summary: Traffic microsimulation combined with surrogate safety measures has increasingly been used as a proactive alternative to historical crash data for predicting crash frequency for current or planned road infrastructure designs. However, existing microsimulation-based safety studies have adopted simplified rule-based behaviour models, which reproduce traffic flow reasonably well but often fail to generate realistic conflict dynamics, limiting crash prediction accuracy. Recent advances in machine learning (ML)-based behaviour models offer a promising opportunity to potentially improve microsimulation realism and crash frequency predictions by learning human driving behaviour directly from large-scale trajectory datasets. To investigate this possibility, traffic microsimulation was conducted for five real-world signalised intersections in Leeds, UK, using both a standard rule-based model and a state-of-the-art ML model. Simulated vehicle trajectories were analysed using a two-dimensional Time-to-Collision metric to identify simulated conflicts, which were then modelled using Extreme Value Theory to predict crash frequency. Results show that conflicts from the ML model yielded crash predictions in line with the real-world crash data, whereas the rule-based model did not permit meaningful predictions, presumably due to a lack of model calibration to the specific simulated intersections. Directly using ML-generated simulated crashes to predict real-world crash frequency also yielded poor results, suggesting that while current ML models can realistically reproduce conflicts, they are not yet able to generate realistic crashes. Overall, the findings demonstrate that ML-based behaviour models are promising for improving crash prediction from simulated conflicts, without a need for location-specific model calibration, and suggest clear future directions for ML-based traffic microsimulation.

AI.tldr: ML-based driving behavior in microsimulation improves crash-frequency prediction by producing realistic conflicts and aligning with real crash data, outperforming rule-based models; direct ML-generated crashes are not yet predictive; lower need for location-specific calibration.
AI.motivation: Address the limited realism of rule-based microsimulation in generating conflict dynamics and crash predictions; leverage ML models trained on large trajectory datasets to enhance safety analysis for current or planned road designs.
AI.method: Microsimulation of five real-world signalised intersections in Leeds, UK using two models: a standard rule-based driver model and a state-of-the-art ML model. Trajectories analyzed with a 2D Time-to-Collision metric to identify conflicts. Extreme Value Theory (EVT) used to model conflicts and predict crash frequency. Additional attempt to use ML-generated crashes directly for frequency prediction.
AI.result: Conflicts from the ML model yielded crash predictions in line with observed crash data; the rule-based model did not provide meaningful predictions. Direct ML-generated crashes were not predictive of real crashes. ML models can reproduce conflicts realistically and may not require location-specific calibration.
AI.conclusion: ML-based behavior models show promise for improving crash prediction from simulated conflicts and reduce the need for location-specific calibration; further work is needed to advance ML-generated crash realism and generalize across intersections.

title: GRIP: Feedback-Guided Prompt Retrieval for Large Multimodal Models
summary:
In-Context Learning (ICL) has become a powerful mechanism for adapting Large Language Models (LLMs) to new tasks without fine-tuning. Extending this concept to Large Multimodal Models (LMMs), Multimodal In-Context Learning (M-ICL) relies on retrieving relevant examples, such as images, captions, or question-answer pairs, to guide predictions across tasks like classification, captioning, and visual question answering (VQA). Most existing approaches select in-context examples based on feature-space similarity, assuming that semantically similar samples provide the most useful context. However, our systematic analysis reveals that this assumption does not always hold: visually similar examples are not necessarily those that most effectively enhance in-context learning performance.
  To address this, we propose the Guided Retrieval of In-context Prompts (GRIP), a learnable vision-only retrieval framework that leverages feedback from LMMs to identify examples that truly improve model predictions. GRIP learns to distinguish beneficial from detrimental in-context examples through contrastive training, refining retrieval beyond pure similarity. Across three multimodal tasks, namely classification, captioning, and VQA, GRIP improves consistently over similarity-based retrieval on Qwen2.5-VL-7B, with its strongest gains in classification on Idefics2-8B. Moreover, we demonstrate that retrievers trained with feedback from one open LMM can be transferred to other models without retraining, including closed-source GPT-4o and Gemini, enabling scalable and cost-efficient deployment of M-ICL. Code will be published upon acceptance.

AI.tldr: GRIP is a vision-only learnable retriever for Multimodal In-Context Learning (M-ICL) that uses model feedback to select beneficial in-context examples, outperforming similarity-based retrieval across classification, captioning, and VQA; retrievers transfer across models, enabling scalable deployment; code to be released.
AI.motivation: In-context exemplar selection for multimodal models often relies on feature-space similarity, which does not always correlate with actual performance gains. A retrieval method aligned with downstream improvement is needed.
AI.method: Introduce GRIP, a contrastively trained, vision-only retrieval framework that uses LMM feedback to distinguish beneficial versus detrimental in-context examples. Training optimizes exemplars via contrastive objectives to maximize predictive gains, then evaluate across three multimodal tasks.
AI.result: GRIP consistently outperforms similarity-based retrieval on three multimodal tasks (classification, captioning, VQA) on Qwen2.5-VL-7B; strongest gains in classification on IdeFics2-8B. Demonstrates transferability: retrievers trained with feedback from one open LMM can be used on other models (including GPT-4o and Gemini) without retraining.
AI.conclusion: Vision-only, feedback-guided exemplar retrieval effectively improves M-ICL over similarity-based methods and is transferable across models, enabling scalable deployment. Code will be released upon acceptance.

title: Constructing Evaluation Datasets for Procedural Reasoning: Balancing Naturalness, Grounding, and Multi-Hop Coverage
comment: 10 pages, 2 numbered figures. Workshop submission to HAIL @ AIED 2026
summary:
Evaluating procedural reasoning in AI-supported learning systems requires question-answer datasets that are both learner-like and grounded in the instructional knowledge the system is expected to use. We study how TMK-based question generation strategies affect dataset quality for procedural and multi-hop reasoning.
  We compare three strategies: strict generation from Task-Method-Knowledge (TMK) models, transcript-first generation with post-hoc TMK filtering, and TMK-aware generation that combines transcripts with structured guidance. To evaluate generated items, we introduce a grounding validation framework based on closed-set evidence units extracted from TMK models. The framework measures whether answers are supported by the underlying representation, whether questions are self-contained, and whether they target multi-hop procedural reasoning.
  Across 23 instructional topics and 690 generated question-answer pairs, strict TMK generation achieves the strongest overall quality, with 96.5% grounded questions and 92.6% usable questions. Transcript-first generation produces more learner-like questions but more context-dependent or weakly grounded items, while TMK-aware generation yields high raw multi-hop coverage but lower grounding. These results show that procedural richness and natural phrasing do not guarantee representational grounding, motivating explicit representation-aware validation for evaluation datasets in AI-supported learning.

AI.tldr: Three TMK-based generation strategies for AI-supported procedural/multi-hop reasoning datasets were compared. A grounding validation framework shows strict TMK generation yields highest grounding and usability, while transcript-first yields learner-like but less grounded items, and TMK-aware yields better multi-hop coverage but reduced grounding; overall, explicit representation-aware validation is needed.
AI.motivation: To create dataset items for AI-supported learning that are both learner-like and grounded in instructional knowledge, enabling robust evaluation of procedural/multi-hop reasoning.
AI.method: Compare strict TMK generation, transcript-first generation with post-hoc TMK filtering, and TMK-aware generation combining transcripts with structured guidance; develop grounding validation framework based on TMK-derived evidence units; evaluate across 23 topics and 690 Q-A pairs.
AI.result: Strict TMK generation achieved the strongest quality: 96.5% grounded and 92.6% usable questions; transcript-first yielded more learner-like items but more context-dependent or weakly grounded; TMK-aware yielded high raw multi-hop coverage but lower grounding.
AI.conclusion: Procedural richness and natural phrasing alone do not ensure representational grounding; explicit, representation-aware validation is necessary when constructing evaluation datasets for AI-supported learning.

title: Bounding Boxes as Goals: Language-Conditioned Grasping via Neuro-Symbolic Planning
summary: For robotics to be effectively integrated into household or industrial environments, machines must adapt to natural-language prompts in real time. Although Vision-Language Models (VLMs) have enabled zero-shot generalization in robot task and motion planning (TAMP), current state-of-the-art approaches often remain computationally "heavyweight" or require extensive training on thousands of demonstrations. We present GRASP (Grounded Reasoning and Symbolic Planning), a framework designed as a step toward open-vocabulary tabletop manipulation. Our approach leverages a pretrained VLM to translate natural-language queries into neuro-symbolic goal states, grounded in the physical world via a bounding-box detection pipeline. Unlike methods that rely on fixed color lists or hard-coded coordinates, GRASP enables robots to interpret abstract spatial concepts such as "top shelf" and execute tasks without additional fine-tuning. We achieve 73.3% overall success across 90 real-robot trials at three difficulty levels, requiring no task-specific training.

AI.tldr: GRASP is a neuro-symbolic framework that uses a pretrained Vision-Language Model to map natural-language prompts to grounded, open-vocabulary, symbolic goals for tabletop manipulation, enabling abstract spatial reasoning without task-specific training and achieving 73.3% success in 90 real-robot trials.
AI.motivation: The need for real-time, open-vocabulary robot instruction in household/industrial settings; existing VLM-based methods are computationally heavy or require extensive task-specific demonstrations, hindering practical deployment.
AI.method: Grounds natural-language queries with a pretrained VLM into neuro-symbolic goal states, grounded by a bounding-box detection pipeline. Avoids fixed color lists or hard-coded coordinates and supports abstract spatial concepts (e.g., 'top shelf'), enabling zero-finetuning manipulation without task-specific data.
AI.result: Achieves 73.3% overall success over 90 real-robot trials across three difficulty levels, with no task-specific training.
AI.conclusion: GRASP represents a step toward open-vocabulary tabletop manipulation, enabling abstract spatial reasoning and real-time grounding without fine-tuning, demonstrated by real-robot success.

title: Policy-driven Conformal Prediction for Trustworthy QoT Estimation
summary: We propose Conformal QoT, a policy-driven framework that combines statistically guaranteed QoT estimation with operational decision policies, enabling reliable lightpath-feasibility predictions under domain shift and improving accuracy from 92\% to 99.6\% on open datasets.

AI.tldr: Conformal QoT is a policy-driven framework that blends statistically guaranteed QoT estimation with operational decision policies, achieving reliable lightpath-feasibility predictions under domain shift and improving accuracy from 92% to 99.6% on open datasets.
AI.motivation: To address domain shift and uncertainty in optical QoT estimation, ensuring reliable lightpath feasibility with statistical guarantees while aligning predictions with operational decisions.
AI.method: Introduce Conformal QoT, a policy-driven framework that uses conformal prediction to produce calibrated QoT estimates and couples these with operational decision policies to guide lightpath provisioning under distribution shift.
AI.result: Demonstrates improved accuracy from 92% to 99.6% on open datasets and robustness to domain shift, with statistical guarantees.
AI.conclusion: Conformal QoT yields reliable feasibility predictions by uniting guaranteed QoT estimation with policy-driven decisions, enabling improved lightpath provisioning in changing environments.

title: DIMOS: Disentangling Instance-level Moving Object Segmentation
summary: Moving instance segmentation (MIS) attracts increasing attention due to its broad applications in traffic surveillance, autonomous driving, and animal tracking. Event cameras record asynchronous brightness changes, providing high temporal resolution and dynamic range, which makes them highly sensitive to motion information. By fusing event and image features, motion cues from events can complement spatial details from images, enhancing the performance of MIS. However, current multimodal MIS methods still struggle to segment small moving instances, as event cameras often yield sparse features under limited resolution. Moreover, event features entangle appearance attributes with motion cues, which further restricts effective cross-modal fusion. To address these challenges, we first propose a dual-disentangling feature extraction framework that separates and extracts appearance and motion information within both image and event modalities, thereby improving feature density. Subsequently, a multi-granularity cross-modal alignment is introduced to align distributionally and semantically consistent features across modalities, enabling more effective fusion with rich spatial and temporal details. The experiment results demonstrate that our method achieves state-of-the-art performance in multimodal MIS, especially for small instances under challenging conditions such as fast motion and low-light settings.

AI.tldr: A dual-disentangling framework for event-image multimodal instance segmentation (MIS) with multi-granularity cross-modal alignment.
AI.motivation: Address challenges in MIS when fusing event data with images: sparse event features at limited resolution, entangled appearance and motion cues, and poor segmentation of small moving instances, especially under fast motion and low light.
AI.method: 1) Dual-disentangling feature extraction to separate appearance and motion in both image and event modalities, increasing feature density. 2) Multi-granularity cross-modal alignment to align distributions and semantics across modalities for robust fusion.
AI.result: Achieves state-of-the-art performance in multimodal MIS, with notable gains for small instances under challenging conditions (fast motion, low light).
AI.conclusion: Separating appearance and motion in both modalities and aligning features across modalities enhances fusion quality and improves segmentation of small moving instances.

title: A Tutorial on World Models and Physical AI
summary: World modeling is emerging as a central principle for building intelligent systems capable of prediction, reasoning, and decision making. A central distinction can be drawn between explicit world models, which learn structured dynamics for rollout-based reasoning and planning, and implicit world models, which encode predictive structure within scalable learned representations. These complementary paradigms provide a foundation for physical AI in domains such as robotics and autonomous driving, enabling intelligence beyond reactive control under real-world constraints. Recent foundation models further suggest a pathway toward unified systems integrating perception, prediction, and action. Despite rapid progress, major challenges remain in hierarchical reasoning, long-horizon planning, and autonomous goal formation, which are critical for advancing toward artificial general intelligence. This tutorial presents a coherent framework in which diverse world modeling approaches are unified through shared predictive structure and differentiated by how such structure is represented and exploited.

AI.tldr: A framework unifying explicit and implicit world models under shared predictive structure, highlighting progress and remaining challenges in hierarchical reasoning, long-horizon planning, and autonomous goal formation.
AI.motivation: World modeling is essential for prediction, reasoning, and decision-making in intelligent systems; integration with perception, planning, and action is needed to move toward AGI and robust real-world autonomy.
AI.method: Proposes a coherent framework that differentiates by how predictive structure is represented and exploited, unifying diverse world-modeling approaches (explicit dynamics vs. implicit predictive representations) and situating foundation models as a pathway toward unified perception-prediction-action systems.
AI.result: Conceptual synthesis and taxonomy; a tutorial framework that clarifies the relationships among world-modeling paradigms and outlines routes for hierarchical reasoning and long-horizon planning.
AI.conclusion: Although progress is rapid, major challenges remain in hierarchical reasoning, long-horizon planning, and autonomous goal formation; achieving AGI requires integrating diverse world-modeling approaches within a shared predictive framework.

title: An Embodied Simulation Platform, Benchmark, and Data-Efficient Augmentation Framework for Wet-Lab Robotics
summary: Wet-lab robots can improve the reproducibility, throughput, and safety of biomedical experiments, but scaling their learning requires customizable simulators for safe and reproducible task generation, open editable laboratory assets, and efficient pipelines that turn limited demonstrations into usable training data. We present Pipette, an embodied simulation platform, benchmark, and data-efficient augmentation framework for wet-lab robot learning. Pipette releases over 43 open-source and re-editable wet-lab assets, together with an extensible asset-building pipeline. A key component of Pipette is its simulation-based data augmentation pipeline, replaying human demonstrations in simulation, applies lighting, camera, speed, and action perturbations, and filters generated episodes with automatic task success checks, rapidly expanding usable training data from limited manual demonstrations. We further introduce an 11-task wet-lab embodied benchmark covering sample handling, culture-ware manipulation, device operation, and precision placement. With only 30 demonstrations per task, ACT achieves 65.5% average success rate, while simulation augmentation improves SmolVLA from 44.1% to 74.7% and π0 from 40.4% to 46.5%, validating the effectiveness of Pipette for data-efficient VLA training and evaluation. Pipette also supports natural-language-driven scene construction and task registration, lowering the barrier for non-expert users to define new wet-lab robotic tasks.

AI.tldr: Pipette is an embodied simulation platform for wet-lab robot learning that combines open editable assets, a simulation-based data augmentation pipeline, and a task benchmark to enable data-efficient training and evaluation with NL-driven task creation.
AI.motivation: Address reproducibility, throughput, and safety challenges in wet-lab robotics by providing customizable simulators, editable lab assets, and efficient pipelines to turn few demonstrations into usable training data.
AI.method: Pipette provides (i) 43 open-source, re-editable wet-lab assets and an asset-building pipeline; (ii) a simulation-based data augmentation pipeline that replay-demonstrates human demonstrations with perturbations in lighting, camera, speed, and actions, plus automatic task-success filtering; (iii) an 11-task bench-mark covering sample handling, culture-ware manipulation, device operation, and precision placement; (iv) NL-driven scene construction and task registration; (v) evaluation showing data-efficient improvements on wet-lab VLA tasks.
AI.result: With 30 demonstrations per task, ACT attains 65.5% average success. Simulation augmentation raises SmolVLA from 44.1% to 74.7% and π0 from 40.4% to 46.5%, demonstrating effective data-efficient VLA training and evaluation; 43 open, editable assets support reproducibility and customization.
AI.conclusion: Pipette advances data-efficient wet-lab robot learning by supplying an extensible, NL-friendly platform with reusable assets, robust simulation-based augmentation, and an embodied benchmark to enable reproducible, scalable development.

title: Dolph2Vec: Self-Supervised Representations of Dolphin Vocalizations
summary: Self-supervised learning (SSL) has opened new opportunities in bioacoustics by enabling scalable modeling of animal vocalizations without the need for expensive manual annotation. However, current SSL models in this domain prioritize broad generalization across species and are not optimized for uncovering the fine-grained structure of individual communication systems. In this work, we collect and release a novel dataset of over five years of longitudinal recordings, from five known dolphins in a semi-naturalistic marine environment, an unprecedented resource for studying dolphin communication. We adapt the Wav2Vec2.0 Baevski et al. (2020) architecture to this domain and introduce Dolph2Vec, the first large-scale, species-specific SSL model trained exclusively on this data. We benchmark our model on two biologically relevant tasks: signature whistle classification and whistle detection. Dolph2Vec significantly outperforms general-purpose baselines in both tasks. Beyond performance, we show that learned embeddings and codebook structure capture interpretable acoustic units aligned with dolphin whistle categories and possibly sub-whistle structure, enabling fine-grained analysis of communication patterns. Our findings demonstrate how SSL can serve as both a model and a scientific tool to explore hypotheses in animal communication research.

AI.tldr: A dolphin-focused self-supervised model (Dolph2Vec) trained on a multi-year dataset outperforms baselines in whistle classification/detection and reveals interpretable acoustic units for fine-grained dolphin communication.
AI.motivation: There is a gap in SSL for bioacoustics: general-purpose models underexplore fine-grained structure within a single species. A long-term dolphin dataset in a semi-natural environment enables study of individual communication systems and test hypotheses about whistle categories and sub-structure.
AI.method: Adapt Wav2Vec2.0 to dolphin vocalizations and train Dolph2Vec on five-year longitudinal data from five dolphins. Evaluate on signature whistle classification and whistle detection. Compare to general-purpose SSL baselines. Analyze embeddings and codebook for interpretable acoustic units aligned with dolphin whistle categories and potential sub-whistle structure.
AI.result: Dolph2Vec significantly outperforms general baselines on both tasks. Learned embeddings and codebook structure capture interpretable acoustic units aligned with dolphin whistle categories and possibly sub-whistle structure, enabling fine-grained analysis of communication patterns.
AI.conclusion: SSL can be a modelling tool and scientific instrument for animal communication research, enabling hypothesis testing and deeper insights into population- or individual-level vocal organization with species-specific SSL models.

title: Perceive, Interact, Reason: Building Tool-Augmented Visual Agents for Spatial Reasoning
summary: While recent vision-language models (VLMs) demonstrate strong multimodal understanding, they remain limited in spatial reasoning tasks that require active evidence acquisition and multi-step visual interaction. This limitation suggests that relying solely on implicit visual representations from vision encoders is insufficient for recovering fine-grained spatial evidence. We introduce PERception-Interaction-reason Agent (PERIA), a tool-augmented visual agent for spatial reasoning tasks across map reasoning, visual probing, and vision reconstruction. PERIA uses two lightweight tool families: vision perception tools for exposing textual, symbolic, and spatial evidence, and vision interaction tools for manipulating visual context, tracing paths, and verifying spatial relations. To train PERIA, we develop a unified recipe that combines supervised tool-use trajectory synthesis, composite rewards, and Observation-Relaxed Group-in-Group Policy Optimization (OR-GIGPO) for effective multi-tool behavior. Experiments on 13 benchmarks from 8 datasets show that PERIA-8B improves over the Qwen3-8B backbone by 10.0% on in-distribution benchmarks and 4.4% on out-of-distribution benchmarks, while outperforming previous state-of-the-art baselines of similar size by 7.0%-14.8%. It also achieves performance comparable to much larger models such as Qwen3-VL-235B-A22B-Thinking and GPT-5, demonstrating the effectiveness of PERIA in enhancing spatial reasoning capabilities.

AI.tldr: Tool-augmented visual agent PERIA enhances spatial reasoning by using perception and interaction tools with a unified training recipe, achieving strong results across multiple benchmarks, rivaling larger models.
AI.motivation: Implicit visual representations are insufficient for fine-grained spatial evidence and multi-step reasoning; explicit evidence gathering and interactive probing can improve spatial reasoning.
AI.method: Two lightweight tool families: vision perception tools exposing textual/symbolic/spatial evidence; vision interaction tools manipulating visual context, tracing paths, verifying spatial relations. Training via supervised tool-use trajectory synthesis, composite rewards, and OR-GIGPO (Observation-Relaxed Group-in-Group Policy Optimization). Evaluation across 13 benchmarks from 8 datasets.
AI.result: PERIA-8B exceeds Qwen3-8B backbone by 10.0% in-distribution and 4.4% out-of-distribution; surpasses similar-size baselines by 7.0%-14.8%; comparable to much larger models such as Qwen3-VL-235B-A22B-Thinking and GPT-5.
AI.conclusion: Tool-augmented interaction substantially improves spatial reasoning; demonstrates that curated perception/interaction tooling + OR-GIGPO training can close the gap to larger models; potential for efficient, scalable spatial reasoning in vision-language models.

title: The Containment Gap: How Deployed Agentic AI Frameworks Fail Public-Facing Safety Requirements
summary: Agentic large language model systems that autonomously invoke tools, maintain persistent memory, and execute multi-step plans are increasingly deployed in public-facing domains, including government services, healthcare triage, and financial advising. We ask whether the frameworks used to build these systems provide architectural-level structural safety guarantees. Applying six containment principles derived from a compositional model of agentic architectures, we audit three dominant frameworks (LangChain, AutoGPT, and OpenAI Agents SDK) and find no native compliance in any of them. Memory integrity, a defense against one of the most prevalent vulnerability classes, is not observed in any of the three evaluated frameworks. We validate these findings empirically: in a simulated government benefits agent built on LangChain, a single memory-poisoning write induces persistent targeted corruption across all tested seeds and backends, increasing the wrongful denial rate for targeted applicants to 88.9%. Under a complex five-factor policy, the same attack preserves aggregate accuracy while increasing targeted wrongful denials by 3.5x, rendering the corruption difficult to detect through standard monitoring. We then introduce two lightweight containment mechanisms: a memory integrity validator and a policy gate, which eliminate both attack vectors with sub-millisecond overhead (<0.2ms per call). We conclude that the current agentic framework ecosystem may not yet meet secure-by-default expectations for public-facing deployments and outline priority architectural interventions to enable trustworthy deployment in high-stakes, socially impactful applications.

AI.tldr: Agentic LLM frameworks (LangChain, AutoGPT, OpenAI Agents SDK) lack native architectural safety guarantees per six containment principles; experiments show memory integrity vulnerabilities enabling targeted, persistent memory poisoning that corrodes decision outcomes; two lightweight defenses (memory integrity validator, policy gate) can mitigate attacks with sub-millisecond overhead; implications suggest secure-by-default architectures are not yet achieved for high-stakes public deployments.
AI.motivation: Evaluate whether current agentic LLM frameworks provide architectural-level structural safety guarantees and identify latent vulnerabilities that could undermine trustworthy deployment in public-facing domains.
AI.method: Audit three dominant frameworks against six containment principles derived from a compositional model of agentic architectures; empirically validate via a simulated government benefits agent built on LangChain, featuring a memory-poisoning attack; test under a complex five-factor policy; propose and evaluate two lightweight containment mechanisms measuring performance overhead.
AI.result: No native compliance with the six containment principles observed in LangChain, AutoGPT, or OpenAI Agents SDK; memory integrity is absent across all three frameworks; memory-poisoning attack yields persistent targeted corruption across seeds/backends, driving wrongful denial rate for targeted applicants to 88.9%; under a five-factor policy, aggregate accuracy remains but targeted wrongful denials increase by 3.5x; two defenses (memory integrity validator and policy gate) eliminate attack vectors with sub-millisecond overhead (<0.2 ms per call).
AI.conclusion: The current agentic framework ecosystem does not meet secure-by-default expectations for public-facing deployments; architectural interventions focused on memory integrity and policy containment are necessary to enable trustworthy deployment in high-stakes applications.

title: Towards Reliable Sequential Object Picking in Clutter: The Runner-up Solution to RGMC 2025
comment: First, Second and Third Coauthor contributed equally to this work
summary: As a long-standing challenge in robotic manipulation, stable and efficient grasping in cluttered environments is of great importance in industrial settings. While recent studies have achieved relatively high success rates in grasping from clutter, there remain few mature solutions for more demanding tasks such as sequential object search and sorting. This work addresses sequential object picking in cluttered environments based on the Cluttered Environment Picking Benchmark (CEPB) and presents our solution to the Pick-in-Clutter track of the 10th Robotic Grasping and Manipulation Competition (RGMC) at ICRA 2025. The task poses several key challenges. First, it requires robust and collision-aware grasping with high success rates across a diverse set of objects, including both rigid and deformable ones. Second, it demands efficient search for target objects, which places stringent requirements on the decluttering and searching strategies of the solution. To address the above challenges, we design an integrated hardware-software pipeline that combines object recognition, decluttering, and multi-modal grasping. The main contributions include the hardware design of a multifunctional gripper and novel representations for object distribution and occlusion relationships in cluttered space. This pipeline enables efficient recognition, search, and sequential grasping of objects in clutter, demonstrating strong performance in both laboratory tests and competition scenarios, and ultimately achieving second place in the Pick-in-Clutter track of the RGMC 2025.

AI.tldr: Integrated hardware-software pipeline for sequential grasping in clutter, combining recognition, decluttering, and multi-modal grasping; introduces novel representations for object distribution and occlusion; uses a multifunctional gripper; achieved 2nd place in RGMC 2025 Pick-in-Clutter.
AI.motivation: Addresses the persistent challenge of stable, efficient grasping in cluttered environments, necessary for industrial manipulation tasks. Current clutter-grasping methods struggle with sequential search/sorting and robustness to diverse object types, revealing a need for integrated sensing, planning, and manipulation.
AI.method: Proposes an integrated pipeline that fuses object recognition, decluttering, and multi-modal grasping. Innovations include hardware design of a multifunctional gripper and novel representations for object distribution and occlusion relationships in clutter. The system orchestrates perception, planning, and manipulation to enable efficient recognition, search, and sequential grasping in cluttered spaces.
AI.result: The approach demonstrates strong performance in laboratory tests and competition scenarios, achieving second place in the Pick-in-Clutter track of RGMC 2025, with reported robustness across rigid and deformable objects and efficient search/decluttering.
AI.conclusion: The integrated hardware-software pipeline effectively tackles cluttered sequential grasping, showing competitive performance and potential applicability to industrial clutter manipulation tasks. The introduced gripper design and spatial representations contribute to robust recognition, search, and grasp planning under occlusion.

title: Boosting Direct Preference Optimization with Penalization
comment: Accepted at ICML 2026 Workshop on Decision-Making from Offline Datasets to Online Adaptation: Black-Box Optimization to Reinforcement Learning
summary: Offline preference optimization has become a practical substitute for reinforcement learning from human feedback, but pairwise objectives such as Direct Preference Optimization (DPO) and its variants use only the chosen and rejected responses stored in a static dataset. This leaves a useful signal unused: the response that the reference model itself would generate for the same prompt. We propose Direct Preference Optimization with Penalization (DPOP), a simple extension of DPO that augments the base preference loss with a gated penalty on reference-greedy responses. DPOP activates this penalty only when the current policy still assigns a lower likelihood to the preferred response than to the rejected response. On AlpacaEval 2.0, DPOP improves length-controlled win rate over DPO, SimPO, and AlphaDPO on both Llama-3-8b-it and Gemma-2-9b-it, achieving relative gains of 5.3\% and 4.4\% over baselines on the two models, respectively. Ablations further show that a SimNPO-style length-normalized penalty is stronger than NPO and token-level unlikelihood in this setting.

AI.tldr: DPOP extends Direct Preference Optimization by adding a gated penalty on the reference model's own greedy responses, improving length-controlled preference quality on AlpacaEval 2.0 across two models, with SimNPO-style length normalization driving the gains.
AI.motivation: To exploit the otherwise unused signal from the reference model's generated responses in offline preference optimization, addressing limitations of pairwise objectives that only consider chosen/rejected responses.
AI.method: Introduce a gated penalization term to the DPO loss that activates when the current policy assigns higher likelihood to the rejected example than to the preferred one, using a SimNPO-style length-normalized penalty; evaluate on AlpacaEval 2.0 with Llama-3-8b-it and Gemma-2-9b-it; compare against DPO, SimPO, and AlphaDPO.
AI.result: DPOP yields improvements in length-controlled win rate over baseline methods, with relative gains of 5.3% (Llama-3-8b-it) and 4.4% (Gemma-2-9b-it). Ablations indicate the length-normalized penalty (SimNPO-style) outperforms NPO and token-level unlikelihood in this setup.
AI.conclusion: A simple, effective extension to offline preference optimization that leverages the reference model's responses; the length-normalized, gated penalty is central to the observed gains, suggesting broader applicability of reference-aware penalties in offline RL with human feedback.

title: Language-Guided Abstraction for Visual Reasoning
summary: The Abstraction and Reasoning Corpus (ARC) is viewed as a critical avenue to Artificial General Intelligence (AGI), as it enables models to learn abstract transformation rules from few-shot examples and then generalize to new tasks. However, prevalent ARC methodology is either pure language or vision-only (i.e., VARC). The former depends heavily on LLMs, consuming billions of parameters. The latter often struggles to capture high-level semantics, leading to overfitting on pixel-level patterns. To bridge this gap, we propose L-VARC, a novel framework that enhances visual reasoning via a language-guided Learning Using Privileged Information (LUPI) branch. Specifically, we design a Semantic Compression Module by feeding a unified, task-agnostic prompt into DeepSeek-V3. In this way, the raw LARC (a crowd-sourced language description dataset) can be substantially refined and structured, fitting with the context length constraint of standard text encoders (e.g., CLIP). Moreover, we design a Cross-Attention Projector to align visual features with semantic embeddings, aiming to guide the training of the ARC model. Notably, the LUPI branch is taken in the training process and will be discarded during inference, thereby yielding a lightweight model with a mere 18 million parameters. Extensive experiments demonstrate that our L-VARC effectively leverages linguistic priors to boost visual reasoning and outperforms state-of-the-art. Ablation studies further confirm the contribution of the two new designs towards the L-VARC framework. The code is available at https://github.com/GZHU-DVL/L-VARC.

AI.tldr: L-VARC introduces a language-guided LUPI training framework for ARC to boost visual reasoning; uses Semantic Compression Module with DeepSeek-V3 to refine LARC into compact semantics; Cross-Attention Projector aligns visual and semantic embeddings; training-time LUPI discarded at inference, yielding 18M-parameter model; reports SOTA improvements and ablation validation; code released.
AI.motivation: ARC lacks robust integration of linguistic priors in a lightweight, generalizable visual reasoning model; bridging language-based and vision-based ARC approaches while maintaining small inference footprint.
AI.method: Propose Semantic Compression Module to convert raw LARC into condensed semantic prompts; employ Cross-Attention Projector to fuse visual features with semantic embeddings; train with LUPI branch; discard LUPI at inference; evaluate on ARC with ablations; provide code.
AI.result: Empirically outperforms SOTA on ARC tasks; ablations confirm the effectiveness of both semantic compression and cross-attention alignment; lightweight 18M-parameter model at inference.
AI.conclusion: Linguistic priors, when integrated through privileged information during training and semantic alignment during learning, can substantially enhance abstract visual reasoning in ARC without increasing inference cost; the framework offers a practical path toward more language-guided visual reasoning and potential AGI implications.

title: MLUBench: A Benchmark for Lifelong Unlearning Evaluation in MLLMs
summary: Multimodal large language models (MLLMs) are trained on massive multimodal data, making data unlearning increasingly important as data owners may request the removal of specific content. In practice, these requests often arrive sequentially over time, giving rise to the challenging problem of MLLM Lifelong Unlearning. However, most existing benchmarks are limited in scale and scope, failing to capture the complexities of MLLM lifelong unlearning. To fill this gap, we introduce the MLUBench, a large-scale and comprehensive benchmark featuring 127 entities across 9 classes under lifelong unlearning requests. We perform extensive experiments using MLUBench and reveal that existing unlearning methods suffer from severe, cumulative degradation. More critically, we further identify the unique challenge of this problem: unlike in unimodal models, MLLM lifelong unlearning is constrained by the need to preserve multimodal alignment. Continually unlearning from one modality could degrade the entire model. To alleviate this challenge, we propose LUMoE, an effective method. Experiments demonstrate that LUMoE significantly mitigates the degradation problem faced by baselines. The source code and the MLUBench dataset are open-sourced in https://github.com/lihe-maxsize/Lifelong_Unlearning_main.

AI.tldr: Introduces MLUBench, a large-scale lifelong unlearning benchmark for multimodal LLMs, and presents LUMoE, a method to mitigate cumulative unlearning degradation while preserving multimodal alignment.
AI.motivation: To address the gap where sequential data removal in multimodal LLMs can degrade model performance, especially due to alignment constraints across modalities; existing benchmarks and methods are insufficient in scale and in preserving multimodal alignment.
AI.method: Construct MLUBench with 127 entities across 9 classes and simulate lifelong unlearning requests; evaluate existing unlearning baselines; propose LUMoE architecture/strategy to enable continual unlearning with preserved multimodal alignment; release open-source code and dataset.
AI.result: Baseline unlearning methods exhibit severe cumulative degradation on MLUBench; LUMoE significantly mitigates this degradation and better preserves multimodal alignment; empirical results demonstrate effectiveness across entities/classes.
AI.conclusion: LUMoE provides a robust approach for lifelong unlearning in multimodal LLMs, addressing the degradation-prone nature of sequential data removals and preserving cross-modal alignment; MLUBench serves as a comprehensive benchmark for future work; code and data are publicly available.

title: SERF: Spatiotemporal Environment and Robot Feature Map for Long-Horizon Mobile Manipulation
summary: Long-horizon robot mobile manipulation requires continual reasoning about localization, environment changes, and task progress, all of which are challenging to infer from image observations alone. In this paper, we show that conditioning a mobile manipulation policy on a spatiotemporal feature map improves reasoning over long horizons. The map represents the environment and the articulated robot body as neural points in a shared latent space and is updated online from egocentric observations and proprioceptive state. We update the environment neural points using object-level rigid tracking and the robot neural points using forward kinematics. We use our spatiotemporal environment and robot feature (SERF) map as a state input to a vision-language-action (VLA) model by extracting map tokens from multiple reference frames and spatial scales, providing the policy with both local and global context. We demonstrate SERF on BEHAVIOR-1K, a benchmark for long-horizon mobile manipulation in household environments. Experiments show that the SERF VLA policy outperforms image-only baselines, reaches subgoals faster by following more direct trajectories, improves robustness to scene-configuration shifts, and recovers from object-drop failures.

AI.tldr: SERF: a spatiotemporal neural-point map enabling long-horizon mobile manipulation by fusing environment and robot state into tokens for a VLA policy; improves planning, robustness, and efficiency on BEHAVIOR-1K.
AI.motivation: Long-horizon manipulation requires persistent, multi-frame reasoning about localization, environment changes, and task progress. Image-only observations lack sufficient global/local context for robust planning over extended horizons.
AI.method: Introduce the SERF map comprising environment neural points updated online from egocentric observations and proprioception; track environment points via object-level rigid tracking; update robot points via forward kinematics; extract map tokens from multiple reference frames and spatial scales; feed tokens as state input to a vision-language-action (VLA) model.
AI.result: SERF-VLA policy outperforms image-only baselines, reaches subgoals faster by following more direct trajectories, is more robust to scene-configuration shifts, and recovers from object-drop failures; demonstrated on BEHAVIOR-1K benchmark for long-horizon mobile manipulation in household environments.
AI.conclusion: Conditioning the manipulation policy on the SERF spatiotemporal map improves long-horizon reasoning and manipulation performance, providing robust, efficient control in dynamically changing environments.

title: Rubric-Guided Self-Distillation: Post-Training Without Rubric Verifiers
summary: Rubrics have emerged as an alternative to RLVR in open-ended domains where a single ground-truth final answer is not available. Existing rubric-based training methods rely on an LLM verifier that scores each rollout against rubrics. This introduces substantial training-time overhead, exposes optimization to verifier-specific biases, and reduces rubric feedback to a sparse end-of-trajectory signal. We propose Rubric-Guided Self-Distillation (RGSD), a verifier-free training method in which the base policy, conditioned on the rubric, serves as the teacher for the unconditioned student. RGSD distills the rubric-conditioned teacher distribution into the student token-by-token, replacing sparse trajectory-level rewards with dense per-token learning signals and removing the LLM judge from the training loop entirely. Across Qwen-2.5 (3B, 7B) and Qwen3-Thinking (4B, 8B) models on medical and science domains, RGSD achieves rubric satisfaction comparable to judge-based GRPO while using one on-policy rollout per prompt and no training-time verifier calls. Ablations show that raw rubrics provide a stronger teacher enrichment signal than self-generated reference responses, while a stronger GRPO judge can outperform RGSD in some settings, positioning RGSD as a complementary verifier-free alternative when verifier cost or reliability is the bottleneck.

AI.tldr: Rubric-Guided Self-Distillation (RGSD) provides a verifier-free training approach that distills rubric-conditioned teacher behavior into an unconditioned student, enabling dense token-level learning signals and reducing training overhead while maintaining rubric satisfaction comparable to verifier-based methods; ablations suggest rubrics are strong signals, but a powerful verifier can outperform RGSD in some cases; RGSD is complementary when verifier cost is high.
AI.motivation: Open-ended domains lack a single ground-truth final answer; traditional rubric-based training relies on costly LLM verifiers, introduces verifier biases, and yields sparse end-of-trajectory rewards.
AI.method: Introduce RGSD where the rubric-conditioned policy serves as teacher for the unconditioned student; perform token-by-token distillation of the teacher distribution into the student; no verifier during training; one on-policy rollout per prompt; evaluated on Qwen-2.5/3-Thinking models in medical/science domains.
AI.result: RGSD achieves rubric satisfaction comparable to judge-based GRPO with minimal verifier usage; ablations show rubrics provide stronger teacher signal than self-generated references; in some settings, a strong GRPO judge can outperform RGSD.
AI.conclusion: RGSD is a viable verifier-free alternative when verifier costs or reliability are bottlenecks; it complements existing verifier-based methods and can be preferable depending on domain and resource constraints.

title: Learning Task-Aware Sampling with Shared Saliency through Density-Equalizing Mappings
summary: In image and surface-based learning tasks, convolutional features are typically extracted using receptive fields that are sampled uniformly across the entire domain. However, informative structures are rarely distributed uniformly in practice and are often concentrated in localized regions. Such phenomena are particularly common in medical imaging, where pathological changes are spatially confined. Consequently, uniform convolution allocates equal computational effort to both informative and uninformative regions, resulting in inefficient feature extraction and suboptimal utilization of model capacity. To address this issue, we propose a framework for task-adaptive sampling that dynamically redistributes computational attention according to the spatial importance of the data. Specifically, we introduce the Density-Equalizing Convolutional Neural Network (DECNN), which employs density-equalizing mappings to guide convolution through a learned density function. The density function encodes the relative importance of different regions and induces a transformation that enlarges informative areas while compressing less relevant ones. As a result, convolutional receptive fields are redistributed non-uniformly over the domain, enabling denser sampling in task-relevant regions. By coupling this importance-driven transformation with convolution, DECNN performs adaptive feature extraction that focuses computational resources on informative structures. This leads to more efficient use of model capacity, yielding a lightweight yet expressive architecture while simultaneously producing an interpretable saliency map. Experiments on image classification and craniofacial surface analysis demonstrate that DECNN achieves competitive or superior performance with fewer parameters, accurately identifies task-relevant regions, and remains robust under complex geometric variations.

AI.tldr: DECNN introduces density-equalizing convolutions that adapt sampling density to region importance, enabling non-uniform receptive fields to focus on informative regions, improving efficiency and interpretability while maintaining performance.
AI.motivation: Uniform convolution samples across the whole domain waste computational resources when informative structures are localized, a common scenario in medical imaging where pathological changes are spatially confined.
AI.method: Learned density function guides a density-equalizing transformation that enlarges informative regions and compresses less relevant ones, so convolutional receptive fields are redistributed non-uniformly. This density-aware mapping is integrated with CNNs to enable task-driven feature extraction and produce an interpretable saliency map.
AI.result: DECNN achieves competitive or superior performance with fewer parameters across image classification and craniofacial surface analysis, demonstrates robustness to geometric variations, and yields accurate, interpretable saliency maps highlighting task-relevant regions.
AI.conclusion: Task-adaptive sampling via density-equalizing convolutions yields lightweight, expressive models that focus computation on informative structures while maintaining accuracy and providing interpretability.

title: Teach-and-Repeat: Accurately Extracting Operational Knowledge from Mobile Screen Demonstrations to Empower GUI Agents
comment: 20 pages, 9 figures. Yudong Zhang and Lei Hu contributed equally to this work. Xingyu Liu, Zuojian Wang, and Zhilin Gao are corresponding authors
summary: Understanding the digital world on mobile devices is shifting from static UI perception to dynamic action comprehension. This capability enables models to convert visual state transitions into operational knowledge, defined as short natural-language sentences that describe action types, target UI elements, textual arguments, and execution orders. However, due to the highly diverse and heterogeneous UI designs across applications, existing vision-language models (VLMs) struggle to accurately infer these underlying operations. To bridge this gap, we introduce Teach VLM, a core model designed to translate mobile screen trajectories into step-wise operational knowledge by extracting and analyzing operation-related keyframes from demonstration videos. To address the scarcity of aligned training data, we develop a systematic data flywheel for scalable data acquisition. We further introduce a novel Chinese Mobile Screen Teach Benchmark for fine-grained evaluation. Building upon Teach VLM, we propose the Teach-and-Repeat paradigm, where the generated operational knowledge serves as an interpretable procedural reference to guide downstream screen-based execution agents. Extensive evaluations demonstrate that Teach VLM significantly outperforms strong VLM baselines, achieving state-of-the-art performance in operation semantics prediction. Furthermore, experiments in Android World show that our paradigm yields consistent Task Success Rate improvements for downstream agents. Together, Teach VLM and the Teach-and-Repeat paradigm offer a practical pathway from raw demonstrations to reusable task automation.

AI.tldr: Teach VLM translates mobile screen trajectories into step-by-step operational knowledge, with a data flywheel, a Chinese benchmark, and Teach-and-Repeat for executable downstream agents; achieves state-of-the-art operation semantics and improves downstream task success.
AI.motivation: Address the gap between dynamic UI understanding and executable action, overcoming UI heterogeneity and data scarcity by extracting operation-focused keyframes and providing interpretable guidance for agents.
AI.method: Build Teach VLM to map keyframes from demonstration videos into natural-language operation sentences; develop a scalable data flywheel for data collection; introduce Chinese Mobile Screen Teach Benchmark; propose Teach-and-Repeat paradigm where generated knowledge guides downstream execution agents.
AI.result: Significant improvement over strong VLM baselines in operation semantics prediction; state-of-the-art performance; consistent task success rate improvements for downstream agents in Android World.
AI.conclusion: Transforms raw demonstrations into reusable, interpretable task-automation knowledge, enabling practical pipelines from perception to action in mobile UI automation.

title: EmbodiSteer: Steering Embodiment-Agnostic Visuomotor Policies with Joint-Space Guidance for Zero-Shot Cross-Embodiment Deployment
comment: The first two authors contribute equally
summary: Scalable robot imitation learning relies on large-scale heterogeneous data from diverse robots or body-free data, making Cartesian end-effector actions a key interface for embodiment-agnostic policy learning. However, end-effector-only abstraction leaves Cartesian policies unaware of the deployed robot body, making them brittle under robot-specific constraints such as whole-body collision avoidance. To overcome this limitation, we present EmbodiSteer, a training-free framework that steers embodiment-agnostic visuomotor policies toward zero-shot, embodiment-aware deployment. EmbodiSteer keeps policy learning in Cartesian space while efficiently lifting inference-time diffusion sampling into the target robot's joint space via forward kinematics and Jacobian-based updates. With whole-body collision-aware guidance over joint trajectories after each denoising step, the arm can be steered away from collisions while preserving learned end-effector behavior. Compared with Cartesian-only execution, EmbodiSteer reduces collision rate by 46.1% and improves task success rate by 28.5% across 9 simulated robots, and further achieves 90.0% collision rate reduction and 36.7% success rate increase on two physical robots in highly constrained scenarios. Our project page is at https://frankwang67.github.io/EmbodiSteer-Page.

AI.tldr: EmbodiSteer enables zero-shot embodiment-aware deployment of Cartesian end-effector policies by lifting inference-time diffusion sampling into the robot's joint space using forward kinematics and Jacobian updates, with whole-body collision-guided steering after each denoising step; it improves safety and success across robots without retraining.
AI.motivation: End-effector Cartesian policies are embodiment-agnostic and brittle under robot-specific constraints (e.g., whole-body collisions). A training-free method that accounts for body morphology at deployment can improve safety and performance without retraining on each robot.
AI.method: Keep visuomotor policy learning in Cartesian space. At inference, lift into the target robot's joint space using forward kinematics and Jacobian-based updates to guide diffusion sampling. After each denoising step, apply whole-body collision-aware guidance to steer the arm away from collisions while preserving end-effector behavior.
AI.result: In simulation across 9 robots, collision rate reduced by 46.1% and task success increased by 28.5%. On two physical robots in highly constrained scenarios, collision rate reduced by 90.0% and task success increased by 36.7%.
AI.conclusion: EmbodiSteer enables zero-shot, embodiment-aware deployment of Cartesian policies without retraining, improving safety and task success across varied morphologies. It demonstrates practical applicability to both simulated and real robots, and suggests broader transferability with potential computational overhead from diffusion-based inference.

title: Crossing the Validation Crisis: Cross-Validation Reduces Benchmarking Variance Surprisingly Well
summary: Modern machine learning progresses through empirical work, benchmarking new methods to evaluate relative performance. However, the statistical variability inherent to evaluation - exacerbated by the stochastic nature of many algorithms - often makes performance estimation unreliable due to the limited test samples available, leading to a validation crisis in which genuine advances are difficult to discern. In this work, we show that cross-validation improves markedly confidence when evaluating and comparing learning algorithm performances. We introduce the concept of sample gain, which quantifies the virtual data augmentation achieved by using multiple cross-validation splits to reduce benchmarking variance. Experiments on both synthetic and real-world datasets (histopathologic scans and NLP fine-tuning) demonstrate that multiple splits can substantially improve the reliability and stability of performance estimates, with diminishing returns often setting in later than expected. We also introduce a procedure to dynamically early-stop cross-validation by estimating from the first few folds if subsequent folds will bring large sample gains. Our findings highlight the value of pushing cross-validation on available samples to achieve robust and reliable benchmarking.

AI.tldr: Cross-validation markedly improves reliability of ML benchmarking; introduces sample gain, enabling dynamic early stopping of CV; empirical gains with multiple splits and diminishing returns.
AI.motivation: Benchmarking variability from stochastic algorithms and limited test samples leads to unreliable performance estimates; improving evaluation reliability is crucial for identifying genuine advances.
AI.method: Define sample gain as the virtual data augmentation from multiple CV splits; evaluate on synthetic data and real-world tasks (histopathology scans, NLP fine-tuning); propose a dynamic early-stopping procedure for CV by forecasting, from early folds, whether additional folds will yield substantial gains.
AI.result: Using multiple CV splits significantly reduces benchmarking variance and improves the stability of performance estimates; gains show diminishing returns, often after more folds than expected; an early-stopping heuristic can cut computation without sacrificing reliability.
AI.conclusion: Pushing cross-validation on available samples yields robust and reliable benchmarking; dynamic CV strategies can balance accuracy and cost; practitioners should consider multiple CV splits and possibly early stopping to enhance evaluation robustness.

title: Bridging Modal Isolation in Interleaved Thinking: Supervising Modality Transitions via Stepwise Reinforcement
summary: Interleaved thinking, where a unified multimodal model alternates between textual reasoning and visual generation, has shown promise on spatial and physical tasks. However, in complex long-chain scenarios, we identify a fundamental failure mode: generated images diverge from the textual context while subsequent text ignores the visual evidence, causing the two modalities to alternate without genuinely informing each other. We term this Modal Isolation and attribute it to compounding information loss at modality boundaries. We decompose each reasoning cycle into atomic operations and define modality transition loss, quantifying cross-modal hallucination (text-to-image) and visual utilization deficit (image-to-text) at each boundary. We propose MoTiF (Modality Tiransition Fidelity), a two-stage training framework that directly optimizes these transitions: Reflective SFT trains the model to detect and recover from erroneous visual outputs; Flow-GRPO improves image generation fidelity via reinforcement learning. All training signals in MoTiF derive from transition-level fidelity rather than end-task accuracy. Across four visual puzzle benchmarks, this transition-level supervision substantially improves both cross-modal coherence and final task accuracy. The results demonstrate that effective interleaved reasoning requires explicit structural supervision at modality boundaries, not merely scaling or end-task optimization.

AI.tldr: The paper tackles modal isolation in interleaved multimodal reasoning by introducing transition-level supervision at modality boundaries. It defines modality transition loss to quantify cross-modal hallucination and visual utilization deficits, and proposes MoTiF, a two-stage training framework (Reflective SFT and Flow-GRPO) that optimizes transitions rather than end-task accuracy, improving cross-modal coherence and task performance on four visual-puzzle benchmarks.
AI.motivation: Identify a fundamental failure mode in interleaved thinking: generated images drift from textual context and text disregards visual evidence, leading to modality oscillation without mutual information exchange. This is attributed to information loss at modality boundaries, necessitating boundary-focused supervision.
AI.method: Decompose reasoning into atomic operations at modality boundaries and define two loss terms (cross-modal hallucination and visual utilization deficit). Propose MoTiF, a two-stage training framework: (1) Reflective SFT to detect/recover from erroneous visual outputs, (2) Flow-GRPO to improve image generation fidelity via reinforcement learning. Learning signals come from transition fidelity rather than end-task metrics.
AI.result: Transition-level supervision via MoTiF substantially improves cross-modal coherence and final task accuracy across four visual puzzle benchmarks, supporting the claim that explicit boundary supervision aids interleaved reasoning beyond scaling or end-task optimization.
AI.conclusion: Effective interleaved multimodal reasoning requires explicit structural supervision at modality boundaries; end-task optimization alone is insufficient.

title: GeoNatureAgent Benchmark: Benchmarking LLM Agents for Environmental Geospatial Analysis Across Frontier and Open-Weight Foundation Models
comment: Preprint. 10 pages, 8 figures. Submitted to ACM SIGSPATIAL 2026
summary: Environmental scientists spend disproportionate effort on data wrangling rather than analysis, and AI agents that automate geospatial workflows remain unvalidated: no benchmark evaluates agents operating through structured tool calling against real APIs. We introduce the GeoNatureAgent Benchmark, the first benchmark for environmental analysis agents that operate via structured tool calls to a production-style geospatial API. It comprises 93 tasks across 18 categories, covering municipality analysis, multi-turn conversation, spatial reasoning, cross-indicator synthesis, error handling and recovery, ranking, comparison, multilingual understanding, habitat analysis, and task rejection. Tasks are evaluated against an open, self-hostable API serving three environmental indicators across Spain and Portugal via sixteen tools. We evaluate seven LLMs (Claude Sonnet 4, DeepSeek V3.2, GLM-5, Gemini 2.5 Pro, Qwen3-235B, GPT-OSS-120B, Llama 4 Scout) under three temperature-1.0 seeds, reporting capability and per-case cost as orthogonal axes. We find: (1) Claude Sonnet 4 leads at 60.8% +/- 0.8%, followed by DeepSeek V3.2 at 56.3% +/- 3.1%, with no other model above 51%; (2) the cost-accuracy Pareto frontier is occupied mostly by open-weight models, with DeepSeek V3.2 offering 93% of Claude's capability at 11x lower cost ($0.011/case); (3) comparison tasks remain universally unsolved (0% on close-value comparisons), exposing systematic reasoning limits; and (4) structured tool calling against a real API is more discriminative than general-purpose GIS benchmarks, with accuracies 25-35 points lower. We further show extensibility by integrating BigEarthNet V2 land cover for Portugal alongside Spanish CO2 and erosion indicators. The benchmark, harness, and self-hostable API are publicly available.

AI.tldr: First benchmark for environmental analysis agents using structured tool calls against a production-style geospatial API; evaluates 7 LLMs across 93 tasks; shows limits in reasoning on some tasks and clear benefits of structured tool use.
AI.motivation: Address the gap where environmental scientists spend excessive effort on data wrangling and to validate agents that operate via structured tool calls against real APIs in geospatial workflows.
AI.method: 92 tasks across 18 categories (note: abstract says 93 tasks) using an open self-hostable API for three indicators across Spain and Portugal, 16 tools, and seven LLMs evaluated under three seeds; tasks include multi-turn dialogue, spatial reasoning, cross-indicator synthesis, error handling, ranking, and multilingual understanding; performance and cost are reported as orthogonal axes; also extends with BigEarthNet V2 and additional indicators.
AI.result: Claude Sonnet 4 leads at ~60.8% accuracy (±0.8%), followed by DeepSeek V3.2 at ~56.3% (±3.1%); no other model above ~51%. Open-weight models lie on the cost-accuracy Pareto frontier; DeepSeek offers ~93% of Claude's capability at ~1/11th the per-case cost; comparison tasks remain at 0% for close-value comparisons, indicating systematic reasoning limits; structured tool calling against a real API is more discriminative than general GIS benchmarks (lower accuracies by 25–35 points); the benchmark is extensible with Portugal data and new indicators like BigEarthNet V2; the benchmark, harness, and API are publicly available.
AI.conclusion: Structured tool calling against real production-style APIs provides a discriminative evaluation for environmental analysis agents and highlights current limitations in benchmarked reasoning tasks; the results inform model selection (favoring high-capability but costly models for accuracy) and emphasize the importance of open benchmarks and extensibility to new geospatial indicators.

title: Trajectory-Level Redirection Attacks on Vision-Language-Action Models
summary: Vision-language-action (VLA) policies bring natural language into closed-loop robot control, enabling robots to execute manipulation tasks directly from text instructions. The same interface gives text a recurring role in control because the prompt is reused at every replanning step, and each prompt-conditioned action changes the future observations on which the policy acts. Existing VLA attacks study adversarial prompts that elicit targeted low-level actions or make such actions persist across changing images. We identify a stronger trajectory-level failure mode: a prompt that still $\textit{appears}$ to specify the intended task but redirects the final physical outcome. We mathematically formalize this setting as $\textit{command-preserving trajectory redirection}$, a prompt-only threat model in which the attacker chooses one prompt before the episode, all policy and environment components remain fixed, and the prompt must stay close to the benign instruction while omitting target words and correction language. To find such prompts, we introduce an on-policy prompt search method that uses rollouts to discover perturbations whose closed-loop behavior tracks a target task while satisfying the command-preserving constraints. Experiments in simulation and on hardware show that near-benign prompt perturbations can redirect VLA rollouts to attacker-specified targets. These results expose a trajectory-level vulnerability in VLA instruction grounding: text that appears to preserve the intended command can still give an adversary control over the robot's final physical outcome. Project website: https://vla-redirection-attack.github.io/

AI.tldr: Identifies a trajectory-level prompt attack on VLA policies: command-preserving trajectory redirection; introduces an on-policy prompt search via rollouts to redirect final outcomes while keeping prompts near benign; validated in sim and hardware; reveals vulnerability in VLA grounding.
AI.motivation: Current VLA safety studies adversarial prompts causing targeted low-level actions, but a more insidious threat exists: prompts that appear to specify the intended task while steering the final outcome. This exposes a trajectory-level vulnerability in prompt-based control that could compromise real-world robot behavior.
AI.method: Formalize as command-preserving trajectory redirection, a prompt-only threat model where one prompt is chosen before an episode with fixed policy and environment. The prompt must stay close to the benign instruction while omitting target words and correction language. Propose an on-policy search using rollouts to find perturbations that drive closed-loop behavior toward a target task while satisfying the command-preserving constraints.
AI.result: Through simulation and hardware experiments, near-benign prompt perturbations can redirect VLA rollouts to attacker-specified targets, demonstrating a trajectory-level vulnerability in instruction grounding.
AI.conclusion: Highlights a critical vulnerability in VLA systems: text that appears to preserve the intended command can still enable adversaries to control the final physical outcome. Calls for defenses in grounding robustness, prompt-filtering, and verification to prevent such trajectory redirection.

title: Emerging Flexible Designs for Geospatial Multimodal Foundation Models
summary: Foundation models are rapidly transforming Earth observation by enabling scalable pretraining across diverse unlabeled geospatial modalities. However, their architectural diversity ranging from encoder-only to encoder-decoder and masked autoencoding paradigms makes it challenging to assess performance trade offs in a consistent manner. In this work, we present an apples-to-apples comparison of leading FM architectures designed for geospatial multimodal reasoning, with a particular focus on flexibility across varied spectral band configurations. We standardize pretraining using identical self supervised learning objectives and training datasets, and evaluate all models under consistent parameterization on the GEOBench benchmark across classification and segmentation tasks. Our results offer new insights into the design trade-offs between model flexibility, modality alignment, and downstream task performance. By highlighting architectural strengths and limitations under controlled conditions, this study provides practical guidance for building next generation geospatial foundation models capable of robust multimodal reasoning.

AI.tldr: Apples-to-apples comparison of foundation model architectures for geospatial multimodal reasoning, standardizing pretraining objectives and datasets, evaluated on GEOBench across classification and segmentation to study efficiency-flexibility trade-offs across spectral configurations.
AI.motivation: To enable fair, controlled assessment of diverse geospatial foundation model (FM) architectures (e.g., encoder-only, encoder-decoder, masked autoencoding) for multimodal geospatial reasoning, and understand how architectural choices affect cross-spectral flexibility and downstream performance.
AI.method: Pretrain multiple leading FM architectures under identical self-supervised learning objectives and training data. Evaluate all models with the same parameterization on GEOBench classification and segmentation tasks, testing across varied spectral band configurations.
AI.result: Provides insights into design trade-offs between model flexibility, modality alignment, and downstream task performance. Identifies architectural strengths and limitations under controlled conditions, offering practical guidance for building robust multimodal geospatial models.
AI.conclusion: Framework offers practical recommendations for selecting architecture based on spectral configuration and task type; emphasizes flexibility versus alignment trade-offs and sets a baseline for apples-to-apples evaluation of geospatial foundation models.

title: Magnifying What Matters: Attention-Guided Adaptive Rendering for Visual Text Comprehension
summary: Visual Text Comprehension (VTC) renders text into images for a vision-language model (VLM) to read, sidestepping LLM context-window limits and powering applications from long-page OCR to multi-page memory QA. Yet existing VTC pipelines treat rendering and layout as a fixed, content-agnostic preprocessing step and offer little mechanistic understanding of how VLMs internally process visualized text. Through a focused empirical study on VTC QA tasks, we reveal that VLMs exhibit a localization-without-utilization regime: evidence-localizing attention emerges sharply in the middle-to-late layers and is largely decoupled from answer correctness, yet simply enlarging the localized spans on the rendered page recovers a large fraction of the failures. Building on these observations, we propose AGAR (Attention-Guided Adaptive Rendering), a training-free, model-agnostic method that leverages a VLM's own middle-to-late layer attention to identify the top-K important visual patches, maps them back to word spans, and re-renders the page with those spans enlarged before re-inferring the answer. Extensive experiments across nine VTC benchmarks (short-form, long-context, and multi-page memory QA) and four VLM backbones show that AGAR (i)consistently improves off-the-shelf VLMs as a plug-and-play enhancement, (ii)composes with VLM post-training to yield further gains, and (iii)remains robust under both visual- and text-side input degradation.

AI.tldr: Training-free AGAR leverages VLM attention to identify important visual patches in visualized text, enlarges corresponding word spans, re-renders, and re-infers, yielding consistent improvements across VTC tasks and backbones.
AI.motivation: Current VTC pipelines render text as fixed, content-agnostic preprocessing and lack mechanistic understanding of how VLMs process visualized text. Observations show localization-without-utilization: attention localizes text in mid-to-late layers but doesn't drive answer; simply enlarging localized spans recovers many failures.
AI.method: Attention-Guided Adaptive Rendering (AGAR): use a VLM's middle-to-late layer attention to pick top-K patches, map to word spans, re-render with enlarged spans, and re-infer. Training-free, model-agnostic, plug-and-play; can complement post-training improvements; robust to degradation.
AI.result: Demonstrated across nine VTC benchmarks and four backbones: consistent improvements, remains effective when combined with post-training; robust under visual and text degradation.
AI.conclusion: AGAR provides a lightweight, general solution for improving VTC QA by adaptive rendering guided by model attention, achieving better QA performance without training.

title: Topical Phase Transitions in Artificial Intelligence Research: Large-Scale Evidence and an Early-Warning Signature for Emerging Topics
summary: Do research topics in artificial intelligence grow gradually, or do they advance through abrupt, detectable jumps? Analyzing 80,814 accepted main-track papers from five premier AI conferences (ACL, CVPR, ICLR, ICML, NeurIPS) spanning 2017 to 2025, we show major AI topics advance through topical phase transitions: remaining marginal for years, then surging across venues within one to three years. Large language models became the dominant cross-venue topic by 2025, diffusion models rose with comparable abruptness, and language-model methods crossed into computer vision via vision-language models, whereas reinforcement learning compounded smoothly, distinguishing genuine phase transitions from ordinary growth. This structure is our primary contribution: a large-scale, cross-venue characterization of how AI research reorganizes. We then ask whether a transition leaves a detectable footprint before it peaks. We define an early-warning signature, four publication-dynamics criteria frozen on 2017-2021 data, and evaluate it out of sample on 2023-2025 transitions, obtaining a precision of 27% and recall of 63% against a 13.5% base rate. Applied to 2025 data, the signature flags reasoning and test-time compute, agentic AI, multimodal LLMs, retrieval-augmented generation, and world models as topics to monitor over 2026-2028. The source code is also publicly available on GitHub at https://github.com/KurbanIntelligenceLab/ai-phase-transitions.

AI.tldr: The study shows AI research topics undergo phase transitions: long lulls followed by rapid adoption across venues within 1–3 years. LLMs dominate by 2025; diffusion models rise abruptly; RL grows smoothly.
