Authors propose an early-warning signature with four publication-dynamics criteria (trained on 2017–2021) and test it 2023–2025, achieving 27% precision and 63% recall (base rate 13.5%). Code is publicly available.
AI.motivation: To understand how AI research reorganizes across major venues, identify detectable precursors to topic surges, and provide a framework for forecasting and monitoring future transitions.
AI.method: Analyze 80,814 accepted main-track papers across ACL, CVPR, ICLR, ICML, NeurIPS (2017–2025). Identify topical phase transitions as abrupt cross-venue surges (1–3 years). Develop an early-warning signature based on four publication-dynamics criteria learned from 2017–2021 data. Evaluate the signature out-of-sample on 2023–2025 transitions and apply to 2025 data to flag topics for 2026–2028. Make source code available on GitHub.
AI.result: Demonstrates that major AI topics exhibit phase transitions: LLMs dominate cross-venue activity by 2025; diffusion models rise with abrupt surges; language-model methods cross into CV via vision-language. RL shows smoother growth. Early-warning signature yields 27% precision, 63% recall against a 13.5% base rate. Identifies topics to monitor (reasoning and compute, agentic AI, multimodal LLMs, retrieval-augmented generation, world models) for 2026–2028.
AI.conclusion: Provides a large-scale cross-venue map of how AI research reorganizes and introduces a transferable framework to forecast and monitor upcoming transitions, with publicly available code for replication and extension.

title: GenHOI: Contact-Aware Humanoid-Object Interaction by Imitating Generated Videos without Task-Specific Training
summary: Humanoid-Object Interaction (HOI) is a fundamental capability for humanoid robots, yet it remains challenging due to the tight coupling between dynamic balance and stable interaction with diverse objects. Existing methods often require time-consuming task-specific policy training or rely on rigid trajectory replay, which limits their ability to accommodate novel interaction scenarios. In this work, we present \textit{GenHOI}, a simple yet effective framework that enables humanoid robots to perform diverse object-interaction tasks in a zero-shot manner by directly imitating a single generated video, without task-specific training or physical demonstration data. GenHOI first reconstructs the robot-object scene in simulation and renders a first-frame image, which, together with the language command, conditions the synthesis of a task-oriented interaction video. The generated video is then analyzed to identify interaction-relevant contact events and estimate hand-object contact regions, which are encoded as object-centric geometric constraints that convert visual interaction cues into physically grounded optimization priors. Guided by these priors, the reference motion recovered from the video is refined and smoothed to resolve the scale ambiguity inherent in 2D video generation, while adapting a single reference trajectory to unseen robot-object relative poses. The optimized trajectory is finally executed by a closed-loop tracking controller. We validate the proposed framework in extensive simulation and real-world experiments across diverse object-interaction tasks, including box grasping, asymmetric bimanual chair carrying, table lifting from below, and cylindrical-object enveloping.

AI.tldr: Zero-shot humanoid object interaction via GenHOI: generate a single interaction video from a language prompt and a first frame, extract constraints, refine a reference trajectory, and execute on real robots without task-specific training.
AI.motivation: HOI tasks require stable balance and robust interaction; existing methods rely on task-specific policies or rigid replay; there is a need to generalize to novel scenarios without additional training.
AI.method: Reconstruct the scene in simulation and render a first frame; condition video synthesis on a language command; generate a task-oriented interaction video; analyze the video to identify interaction-relevant contact events and estimate hand–object contact regions; encode these as object-centric geometric constraints to convert visual cues into physically grounded optimization priors; refine and smooth the recovered reference trajectory to resolve 2D scale ambiguity and adapt to unseen robot–object poses; execute with a closed-loop tracking controller.
AI.result: Extensive simulation and real-world experiments validate GenHOI across diverse tasks, including box grasping, asymmetric bimanual chair carrying, table lifting from below, and cylindrical-object enveloping, demonstrating zero-shot capability and robust execution.
AI.conclusion: GenHOI provides a simple yet effective zero-shot framework for diverse HOI tasks by leveraging a single generated video to guide physically grounded trajectory optimization and execution, reducing reliance on task-specific training and demonstration data.

title: Viral Proteins Reveal Geometry of Protein Language Models
comment: Accepted at ICML 2026 GenBio Workshop and FM4LS Workshop. Code available at https://github.com/MisteFr/viral-proteins-plms
summary: Protein language models are trained on highly imbalanced datasets, raising the question of how they represent underrepresented biological sequences. Using viral proteins as a case study across ESM model families, we identify a dominant nativeness axis in embedding space, aligned with masked reconstruction perplexity, that orders sequences from well-modeled cellular proteins through viral proteins to shuffled and random sequences. Scaling contracts this axis unevenly across viral families. Despite this, protein language model embeddings retain viral-specific signal: viral proteins remain linearly separable beyond zero-shot perplexity and shallow sequence features. Together, these results suggest that pLM representations are structured by a general notion of nativeness while preserving information specific to distinct biological groups.

AI.tldr: A dominant nativeness axis in protein-language-model embeddings tracks sequence nativeness (cellular proteins > viral proteins > shuffled/random), aligned with masked reconstruction perplexity; scaling varies across viral families. Embeddings preserve viral-specific signals, with viral proteins remaining linearly separable beyond zero-shot perplexity, indicating both a general nativeness structure and group-specific information in pLM representations.
AI.motivation: To understand how protein language models represent underrepresented or non-native sequences in imbalanced training data, using viral proteins as a testbed across multiple ESM model families.
AI.method: Analyze embeddings from several ESM model families on viral and non-viral proteins, identify a dominant nativeness axis in embedding space that correlates with masked reconstruction perplexity, examine how this axis scales across different viral families, and test whether viral proteins retain group-specific signals such as linear separability beyond zero-shot perplexity and simple sequence features.
AI.result: A dominant nativeness axis exists in pLM embeddings and aligns with masked perplexity, ordering sequences from well-modeled cellular proteins to viral proteins to shuffled/random sequences. Scaling of this axis is uneven across viral families. Despite the global nativeness structure, viral-specific information remains: viral proteins stay linearly separable beyond zero-shot perplexity and simple sequence features.
AI.conclusion: Protein language model representations are organized around a general notion of nativeness that spans broad biological groups while still preserving signals specific to distinct groups, indicating both global structure and group-specific information in pLM embeddings.

title: Multi-Label Test-Time Adaptation with Bayesian Conditional Priors
summary: Multi-label recognition with frozen Vision-Language Models (VLMs) is brittle under distribution shift: standard zero-shot inference scores labels independently, ignoring co-occurrence structure and producing incoherent label sets where dominant concepts suppress weaker but compatible labels. We introduce Bayesian Conditional Priors (BCP) Estimation, a gradient-free test-time adaptation method that injects label dependency without tuning the backbone. BCP views zero-shot logits as a proxy for marginal posteriors under a fixed image-text likelihood and attributes shift-induced errors mainly to a mismatched label prior. For each test image, it selects a high-confidence anchor label and applies an anchor-conditioned Bayesian refinement. This update is closed-form in logit space and admits a pointwise mutual information (PMI) interpretation, explicitly promoting compatible labels and suppressing incompatible ones. BCP operates without target annotations by estimating anchor-conditioned priors online from the unlabeled test stream via lightweight second-order co-occurrence statistics, adding negligible overhead beyond a single forward pass. Across standard multi-label benchmarks and multiple CLIP backbones, BCP consistently outperforms strong TTA baselines, e.g., improving RN50 average mAP from 57.31 to 69.22 and ViT-B/16 from 62.61 to 71.79.

AI.tldr: Gradient-free test-time adaptation (BCP) for multi-label recognition with frozen Vision-Language Models (VLMs) that injects label dependencies via anchor-conditioned Bayesian refinement using online, unlabeled test streams; achieves large gains over strong TTA baselines.
AI.motivation: Zero-shot VLM logits ignore label co-occurrence and are brittle under distribution shift. There is a need to model label dependencies without changing the backbone or requiring target annotations.
AI.method: For each test image, select a high-confidence anchor label and perform an anchor-conditioned Bayesian refinement in logit space. The prior is inferred online from unlabeled test data using lightweight second-order co-occurrence statistics. Updates are closed-form and PMI-based, promoting compatible labels and suppressing incompatible ones. No target labels are required; the method is gradient-free and adds minimal overhead beyond a single forward pass.
AI.result: Across standard multi-label benchmarks and multiple CLIP backbones, BCP consistently outperforms strong TTA baselines, e.g., RN50 mAP 57.31→69.22 and ViT-B/16 62.61→71.79.
AI.conclusion: BCP provides effective, low-overhead test-time adaptation by injecting label dependencies through anchor-conditioned priors, improving robustness to distribution shift without target annotations.

title: Fantastic Scientific Agents and How to Build Them: AgentBuild for Rietveld Refinement
summary: As scientific workflows shift from deterministic executables to LLM-based agents, the development practices on offer, such as fine-tuning, reinforcement learning, and prompt-and-go, bury the scientist's judgment. We propose treating agent construction as a workflow stage and introduce AgentBuild, which builds a scientific agent from a contract the scientist authors. The contract is a version-controlled rubric, a difficulty-graded curriculum, and a curated external knowledge base. A rubric-driven judge gates a meta-optimizer coding agent that edits the agent within a declared boundary, so the build compiles the agent, not the scientist's judgment. We instantiate this for Rietveld refinement of X-ray diffraction data through GSAS-II behind MCP and A2A, where a blank-harness construction run progresses through a lithium lanthanum zirconium oxide (LLZO) signal-to-noise ladder, reaches the 4 hour scan as a frontier case, and exposes the workflow-scope limits that remain. The same rubric that rewards credible fits also scores trajectory scope, making the frontier a contract failure rather than a pattern-fitting failure. As base models evolve, re-running AgentBuild is a re-tune, not a rebuild, and the scientist's authored contract remains the durable asset.

AI.tldr: AgentBuild presents a contract-driven workflow for constructing scientific agents, using a rubric-based judge and meta-optimizer to edit agents within declared boundaries, demonstrated on Rietveld refinement of XRD data with GSAS-II. Frontier cases reveal the contract rather than data fits as the failure mode; re-running AgentBuild is a re-tune as models evolve, preserving the authored contract as a durable asset.
AI.motivation: Mitigate the erosion of scientist judgment when moving to LLM-based agents by embedding intent, safety, and reproducibility into a contract-driven build process that governs agent construction and evolution.
AI.method: Define AgentBuild as a workflow stage where a scientist authors a contract (version-controlled rubric, difficulty-graded curriculum, curated external KB). A rubric-driven judge gates a meta-optimizer coding agent that edits the agent within declared boundaries, ensuring the build outputs a compiled agent aligned with the contract. The approach is instantiated on Rietveld refinement of XRD data via GSAS-II behind MCP and A2A, using a blank-harness progression to LLZO SNR ladder, and evaluating through a frontier 4-hour scan. Frontier cases expose workflow-scope limits and cause contract failures rather than pattern-fitting failures; re-running AgentBuild corresponds to re-tuning basis models, not rebuilding the contract, preserving the contract as a durable asset.
AI.result: A working demonstration that contracts govern agent construction, reveal the limits of current workflows, and reframe frontier challenges as contract failures. The method provides a reusable framework for contract-driven governance as scientific models evolve.
AI.conclusion: Contract-driven agent construction can sustain scientist intent across evolving models; frontier conditions reveal limitations as contract failures, and re-tuning rather than rebuilding preserves the scientist-authored contract as the enduring asset.

title: Comparing Commercial Depth Sensor Accuracy for Medical Applications
summary: Depth estimation has numerous medical and surgical applications. We benchmark four depth sensors on a porcine bone specimen, a porcine belly specimen, and a silicone kidney phantom using stylus-sampled references. These objects contain several real-world challenges, including homogeneous surfaces, specular surfaces, and subsurface scattering. The comparison includes stereo, structured-light, and time-of-flight sensors at a distance of approximately 50 cm. Specifically, the Intel RealSense D405 (Intel RealSense, United States), PMD Flexx2 (pmdtechnologies, Germany), Stereolabs ZED 2i (Stereolabs, France), and Zivid 2M+ 60 (Zivid, Norway) are compared. The Zivid 2M+ 60 performed best across all objects and metrics considered in this work. The ZED ranked second for real tissue, but last on the phantom.

AI.tldr: A benchmark comparing four depth sensors (RealSense D405, PMD Flexx2, ZED 2i, Zivid 2M+ 60) on porcine bone, porcine belly, and a silicone kidney phantom at ~50 cm. Zivid 2M+ 60 provides the best overall performance across objects and metrics; ZED 2i is strong for real tissue but weaker on phantom; other sensors underperform variably depending on surface type.
AI.motivation: Evaluate depth-sensing performance in realistic surgical contexts with challenging materials (homogeneous, specular, subsurface scattering) using stylus-ground-truth references to determine robustness and accuracy across different modalities and surface types.
AI.method: Experimental benchmark at ~50 cm using four sensors (RealSense D405, PMD Flexx2, ZED 2i, Zivid 2M+ 60) across three specimens (porcine bone, porcine belly, silicone kidney phantom) with stylus-sampled references. Comparison encompasses stereo, structured-light, and time-of-flight modalities and uses standard depth accuracy/precision metrics.
AI.result: Zivid 2M+ 60 yielded the best performance across all objects and evaluation metrics. ZED 2i ranked second for real tissue but last on the phantom; the remaining sensors underperformed relative to the top performer, with performance varying by surface type.
AI.conclusion: For challenging surgical-depth sensing tasks, the Zivid 2M+ 60 is the most robust choice among the four evaluated sensors. Sensor performance is surface-dependent; practitioners should select depth-sensing modality based on target tissue and phantom characteristics, as no single sensor dominates all conditions.

title: The Mathematics of AI Winters: The mathematical Taxonomy of Paradigm Fragility in AI Winter
summary: Two major periods of reduced funding and confidence in artificial intelligence research, commonly called the first and second AI winters, are usually explained through engineering failure, commercial disappointment, and inflated expectations. This article develops a complementary thesis: that the dominant paradigms of those periods also met genuine formal barriers, including limitations of representation, optimisation, computational complexity, statistical learnability, and high-dimensional approximation. The contribution is synthetic rather than archival. We do not claim that particular theorems mechanically caused the winters; rather, we show that several central disappointments of early AI were aligned with mathematically precise bottlenecks. We analyse these bottlenecks through the perceptron impossibility results of Minsky and Papert, the complexity-theoretic hardness of exact neural-network training established by Blum and Rivest, minimax rates for nonparametric estimation in high dimension due to Stone, vanishing-gradient analyses by Hochreiter and by Bengio and collaborators, and classical statistical learning theory in the tradition of Vapnik and Chervonenkis, Valiant, and Blumer and collaborators. We then relate these barriers to the later breakthroughs that mitigated, rather than eliminated, them.

AI.tldr: The paper argues that AI winters were not only due to hype/failure but also due to formal bottlenecks in representation, optimization, complexity, learnability, and high-dimensional approximation; it synthesizes links between classic results (perceptron impossibility, training hardness, minimax rates, vanishing gradients, and VC theory) and historical disappointments, showing breakthroughs mitigated these bottlenecks rather than eliminating them.
AI.motivation: Provide a complementary theory connecting AI winters to mathematically precise barriers, beyond engineering, commercial, or social explanations.
AI.method: Synthetic/historical analysis that maps well-known formal results to historical AI setbacks, offering a conceptual synthesis rather than archival claims.
AI.result: Identifies that central disappointments during AI winters aligned with mathematically precise bottlenecks; later breakthroughs mitigated, rather than removed, these bottlenecks.
AI.conclusion: Understanding AI progress requires recognizing how formal bottlenecks shaped early disappointments and how subsequent advances mitigated them, offering a unified lens on the evolution of AI capabilities.

title: MAMVI: 3D Test-Time Adaptation via Masked Multi-View Point Clouds
summary: 3D point cloud models suffer significant performance degradation under distribution shifts caused by sensor noise, occlusions, and environmental changes. Test-time adaptation (TTA) has emerged as a practical paradigm for mitigating this issue during inference. Recently, leveraging multi-view augmentation has shown promise in improving 3D TTA performance. However, existing multi-view approaches are often constrained by sequential optimization that treats each view independently. This sequential optimization leads to substantial inference latency due to repetitive optimization steps, making real-time adaptation impractical. To address this, we propose Masked Multi-View Test-Time Adaptation (MAMVI), which replaces sequential optimization with a unified single-step adaptation. Specifically, MAMVI utilizes a hybrid masking strategy that combines fixed ratios for stability with Beta-distributed sampling for diversity. By aggregating losses across multiple views, MAMVI performs adaptation through a single backward pass based on multi-view consensus. Additionally, a confidence-based adaptive learning rate is used to dynamically adjust the adaptation intensity for each sample. Extensive experiments on ModelNet-40C, ShapeNet-C, and ScanObjectNN-C demonstrate that MAMVI achieves state-of-the-art accuracy on ShapeNet-C and ScanObjectNN-C. Moreover, it remains competitive on ModelNet-40C while delivering 4.9-8.9 times faster inference, making it highly suitable for real-time applications. Our code is available at https://github.com/Inseok-kong/MAMVI

AI.tldr: MAMVI introduces a single-step multi-view test-time adaptation for 3D point clouds, using a hybrid masking scheme and adaptive learning rate to fuse multiple views with a single backward pass, achieving state-of-the-art on ShapeNet-C/ScanObjectNN-C and substantially faster inference.
AI.motivation: 3D point clouds experience distribution shifts due to sensor noise, occlusions, and environment changes; existing multi-view TTA methods incur high latency due to view-by-view optimization, hindering real-time deployment.
AI.method: Unified single-step adaptation across multiple views using a hybrid masking strategy (fixed ratios for stability + Beta-distributed sampling for diversity), aggregated losses for multi-view consensus, single backward pass, and a confidence-based adaptive learning rate to modulate adaptation intensity per sample.
AI.result: Empirical results show state-of-the-art accuracy on ShapeNet-C and ScanObjectNN-C; competitive performance on ModelNet-40C; inference speedups of 4.9–8.9x over baselines, enabling real-time adaptation; code released.
AI.conclusion: MAMVI provides an effective, efficient multi-view TTA paradigm for 3D point clouds, delivering strong accuracy with greatly reduced latency and practical applicability for real-time systems.

title: (Human) Attention Is (Still) All You Need: Human oversight makes AI-assisted social science reliable
summary: Large language models (LLMs) are increasingly used for tasks once reserved for trained researchers, including hypothesis generation, specification choice, and drafting conclusions. We argue that the reliability of AI-assisted research depends not only on model capability, but also on how cognitive labour is structured between humans and machines. We study this problem through Human-in-the-Loop Economic Research (HLER), a decision architecture based on pre-commitment, decision sequencing, accountability, and attention allocation. In a pre-specified 2*4 factorial experiment with 280 complete research runs across four datasets, an unconstrained multi-agent baseline produced critical failures in 72% of runs. Using the same underlying model, the same agent decomposition, and identical prompts for the shared reasoning agents, HLER reduced the failure rate to 16% by imposing three architectural commitments: LLMs reason but do not execute data work, data and estimation are handled deterministically, and three human decision gates bind the workflow. Fisher's exact test rejects equality of failure rates at p<0.001. Reliability gains were largest on the least publicly represented dataset, a Qing-dynasty population register, consistent with a task-based production model with Frechet-distributed output quality. An 80-run ablation suggests that deterministic computation and human gates contribute independently, with exploratory evidence of complementarity. We interpret HLER as a research harness rather than an autonomous AI scientist: it sharply reduces failures, makes residual weaknesses more visible, and prevents unreliable claims from being advanced as publication-ready outputs.

AI.tldr: The abstract presents HLER, a human-in-the-loop research architecture that transforms AI-assisted research reliability by structuring cognitive labor. It reports large reliability gains (72% failure in unconstrained baseline vs 16% with HLER) across four datasets, using a 2x4 factorial design and ablations, and positions HLER as a research harness rather than autonomous AI.
AI.motivation: To improve reliability of AI-assisted research by not only enhancing model capability but by structuring human-machine collaboration through pre-commitment, sequencing, accountability, and attention allocation.
AI.method: A pre-specified 2x4 factorial experiment with 280 complete runs across four datasets, comparing an unconstrained multi-agent baseline to HLER. All use the same underlying model and agent decomposition; identical prompts for shared reasoning agents. HLER imposes three commitments: LLMs reason but do not execute data work; deterministic handling of data and estimation; three human decision gates. Outcomes analyzed with Fisher's exact test; an 80-run ablation explores contributions of deterministic computation and human gates.
AI.result: Baseline failure rate 72%; with HLER, failure rate 16%; p<0.001 (Fisher). Largest gains on the least publicly represented dataset (Qing-dynasty population register). An ablation (80 runs) suggests deterministic computation and human gates contribute independently with complementarity. HLER reduces failures and exposes residual weaknesses, reducing unreliable claims.
AI.conclusion: HLER acts as a research harness that sharply reduces failures, reveals residual weaknesses, and prevents unreliable claims from becoming publication-ready outputs rather than an autonomous AI scientist.

title: RoboProcessBench: Benchmarking Process-Aware Understanding in Vision-Language Robotic Manipulation
summary: Vision-language models (VLMs) are increasingly explored as visual critics, reward generators, and failure detectors in robotic manipulation. These roles implicitly require models to judge not only final task success, but also how a manipulation execution is physically and temporally progressing. However, existing evaluations fail to test whether VLMs possess fine-grained process understanding. To address this gap, we present RoboProcessBench, a benchmark for process-aware understanding in vision-language robotic manipulation. RoboProcessBench decomposes such capability into two complementary dimensions, \emph{static monitoring} and \emph{dynamic reasoning}, instantiated as 12 diagnostic question families covering phase, contact, motion, coordination, primitive-local progress, temporal order, outcome, and primitive-level transitions. Built from physically grounded execution traces, the curated benchmark corpus ProcessData contains \textasciitilde 58k question-answer pairs across 260 manipulation tasks, which is further split into ProcessData-SFT and ProcessData-Eval for post-training and evaluation purposes. Extensive evaluation of various VLMs on ProcessData-Eval reveals broad limitations across 12 diagnostic task families, suggesting current models still lack robust process-aware understanding of manipulation executions. But with ProcessData-SFT, the post-trained \textit{Qwen2.5-VL-7B} and \textit{InternVL-3-8B} exhibit consistent gains on local state, motion, progress, and primitive-aware cues. These results demonstrate that RoboProcessBench serves as both an evaluation benchmark and a learnable supervision source for developing VLMs capable of monitoring and evaluating robotic manipulation processes. Project webpage: \href{https://processbench-2026.github.io/RoboProcessBench-Web/}{https://processbench-2026.github.io}.

AI.tldr: RoboProcessBench provides a process-aware benchmark for vision-language models in robotic manipulation, introducing static monitoring and dynamic reasoning across 12 diagnostic families. It builds ProcessData (~58k QA on 260 tasks) split into ProcessData-SFT and ProcessData-Eval for post-training and evaluation, respectively. Initial results reveal broad limitations in current VLMs, while post-training with Qwen2.5-VL-7B and InternVL-3-8B yields gains on local state, motion, progress, and primitive cues, establishing a path for learning process-aware monitoring and evaluation.
AI.motivation: Existing VLM evaluations primarily assess end-task success, not fine-grained, time-evolving manipulation processes. There is a need for a benchmark that tests and teaches models to reason about intermediate states, phase transitions, contacts, and temporal order during robotic manipulation.
AI.method: RoboProcessBench decomposes process understanding into two complementary dimensions—static monitoring and dynamic reasoning—formalized as 12 diagnostic question families covering phase, contact, motion, coordination, primitive-local progress, temporal order, outcome, and primitive-level transitions. It uses physically grounded execution traces to curate ProcessData (~58k QA over 260 tasks), split into ProcessData-SFT (for post-training) and ProcessData-Eval (for evaluation). The study conducts extensive VLM evaluations and assesses post-training gains on targeted cues.
AI.result: Evaluations reveal broad limitations across the 12 diagnostic families in current VLMs. After post-training with Qwen2.5-VL-7B and InternVL-3-8B on ProcessData-SFT, models show consistent gains on local state, motion, progress, and primitive-aware cues during evaluation.
AI.conclusion: RoboProcessBench provides both an evaluation benchmark and a learnable supervision source for developing process-aware VLMs capable of monitoring and evaluating robotic manipulation processes, with a project webpage for resource access.

title: Evaluation of AutoML Frameworks for IDS under Imbalanced Data Conditions of the NSL-KDD Dataset
summary: This work investigates the impact of severe class imbalance on the performance of automated machine learning (AutoML) frameworks for multiclass network intrusion detection using the NSL-KDD dataset. Unlike previous studies that simplify the problem through binary classification or minority-class removal, we preserve the original five-class distribution, including highly underrepresented attacks such as R2L and U2R, enabling a realistic evaluation of imbalance-sensitive learning behavior. Nine open-source AutoML frameworks were analyzed under a unified and reproducible experimental protocol, considering differences in architectural design, ensemble strategies, validation procedures, hyperparameter optimization, and imbalance-handling mechanisms. The results demonstrate that frameworks incorporating ensemble learning and imbalance-aware optimization achieve better minority-class discrimination. PyCaret obtained the best overall performance, reaching 66\% macro-F1, followed by AutoGluon with 55\%, whereas frameworks lacking native balancing support exhibited significant degradation in minority-class detection capability. The analysis further shows that accuracy-oriented optimization alone is insufficient for highly imbalanced IDS scenarios, since high-weighted metrics may coexist with poor generalization on rare attack categories. As a contribution, this work establishes a standardized benchmark for AutoML-based intrusion detection under severe multiclass imbalance, highlighting current architectural limitations and the need for native integration of imbalance-aware optimization, resampling, and stratified evaluation strategies into automated learning pipelines. The source code is publicly available.

AI.tldr: AutoML frameworks can handle severe multiclass imbalance in IDS better when using ensembles and imbalance-aware optimization; PyCaret achieves the highest macro-F1 (66%), AutoGluon follows (55%), while non-balancing pipelines underperform on minority classes.
AI.motivation: To evaluate how AutoML frameworks cope with severe multiclass class imbalance in intrusion detection, preserving the original five-class distribution (including rare R2L/U2R attacks) rather than binary simplifications or minority removal; address gaps in imbalance-handling within automated learning pipelines.
AI.method: Nine open-source AutoML frameworks were assessed under a unified, reproducible protocol on the NSL-KDD dataset with five-class distribution. Analysis focused on architectural design, ensemble strategies, validation, hyperparameter optimization, and native imbalance-handling mechanisms; performance measured via macro-F1 and minority-class discrimination.
AI.result: Frameworks with ensemble approaches and imbalance-aware optimization yielded better minority-class detection. PyCaret achieved the best overall macro-F1 at 66%, followed by AutoGluon at 55%. Frameworks lacking native balancing support showed significant degradation in minority-class detection. Accuracy-focused optimization alone was insufficient for highly imbalanced IDS tasks.
AI.conclusion: Establishes a standardized AutoML-based intrusion-detection benchmark under severe multiclass imbalance, highlighting architectural limitations and the need for native integration of imbalance-aware optimization, resampling, and stratified evaluation in automated pipelines; source code is publicly available.

title: YOLO-AMC: An Improved YOLO Architecture with Attention Mechanisms for Building Crack Detection
comment: 14 pages, 8 tables, 6 figures. Expanded version of IET ICETA 2025 conference paper
summary: Crack detection plays an important role in infrastructure inspection and Structural Health Monitoring (SHM). However, cracks typically appear as thin, low-contrast structures and are easily affected by background noise, posing challenges for existing object detection models. This study proposes an improved YOLO-based architecture with integrated attention mechanisms, termed YOLO-AMC (YOLO with Attention Mechanisms for Crack Detection), to enhance automated crack detection performance. Based on YOLOv11, the original C2PSA module is removed, and multiple attention mechanisms, including Global Attention Mechanism (GAM), Residual Convolutional Block Attention Module (Res-CBAM), and Shuffle Attention (SA), are introduced into the multi-scale feature fusion layers of the Neck to strengthen cross-scale feature integration. Experimental results demonstrate that YOLO-AMC consistently outperforms baseline models YOLOv11n and YOLOv8n across multiple evaluation metrics. Among the evaluated attention modules, GAM achieves the best detection performance, obtaining mAP@0.5 = 0.9917 and mAP@0.5:0.95 = 0.9506 on the test dataset, which are higher than those of YOLOv11 (0.9833 / 0.9112) and YOLOv8 (0.9707 / 0.8921). Furthermore, while maintaining a computational complexity of 7.6 GFLOPs, the proposed model achieves 110.95 FPS on an NVIDIA RTX 4090 platform and approximately 5 FPS on a Raspberry Pi 5 edge device, demonstrating a favorable trade-off between accuracy and deployment efficiency. The implementation code for this study is available on GitHub at https://github.com/CY-Tsai24/YOLO-AMC.

AI.tldr: An improved YOLO-based crack detector (YOLO-AMC) with attention modules added to the Neck layer; GAM yields best performance, achieving near-perfect crack detection with favorable accuracy-speed trade-offs on both high-end GPUs and edge devices.
AI.motivation: Cracks are thin, low-contrast, and easily overwhelmed by background noise, making accurate detection challenging. There is a need to enhance cross-scale feature fusion and maintain deployment efficiency for SHM applications.
AI.method: Starting from YOLOv11, remove the C2PSA module and insert multiple attention mechanisms (Global Attention Mechanism, GAM; Residual CBAM; Shuffle Attention) into the Neck's multi-scale feature fusion. Compare YOLO-AMC variants using these modules against baselines YOLOv11n and YOLOv8n on a crack-detection dataset. Evaluate with mAP@0.5 and mAP@0.5:0.95; report FLOPs and FPS on RTX 4090 and Raspberry Pi 5. The best variant uses GAM in Neck.
AI.result: YOLO-AMC with GAM achieves mAP@0.5 = 0.9917 and mAP@0.5:0.95 = 0.9506 on the test set, outperforming YOLOv11 (0.9833 / 0.9112) and YOLOv8 (0.9707 / 0.8921). The model requires 7.6 GFLOPs, runs at 110.95 FPS on an NVIDIA RTX 4090, and ~5 FPS on a Raspberry Pi 5. The implementation is available on GitHub at the provided URL.
AI.conclusion: Integrating cross-scale attention in the detector’s Neck significantly improves crack-detection accuracy with a manageable computational cost, delivering a robust accuracy-efficiency trade-off suitable for SHM deployments.

title: WISE: A Long-Horizon Agent in Minecraft with Why-Which Reasoning
summary: Rapid advances have been made in developing general-purpose embodied agent in environments like Minecraft through the adoption of LLM-augmented hierarchical approaches. Despite their promise, low-level controllers often become performance bottlenecks due to repeated execution failures. We argue that a key limitation is not only the lack of episodic memory, but also the decoupling of \textit{what-where-when} memory from \textit{which-why} reasoning. To address this, we propose \textbf{WISE} (Which-Why Informed Semantic Explorer), a long-horizon agent framework with an enhanced low-level controller equipped with a Causal Event Graph that augments episodic memory with explicit causal structure linking observations to task relevance. Unlike prior work such as MrSteve, which relies on feature similarity for retrieval, WISE enables robust recall under viewpoint changes and supports opportunistic task reordering through causal reasoning. Building on this memory, we propose an Opportunistic Task Scheduler that dynamically re-prioritizes subtasks when causally relevant opportunities are detected. We further equip WISE with a multi-scale progressive exploration strategy to provide spatially comprehensive observations for downstream reasoning. Experiments show that WISE largely improves task success and efficiency on long-horizon sparse tasks, particularly in settings requiring adaptive decision-making.

AI.tldr: WISE introduces a long-horizon agent framework that augments episodic memory with a Causal Event Graph to connect observations with task relevance, enabling opportunistic task scheduling and multi-scale exploration to improve long-horizon performance in sparse tasks.
AI.motivation: Address persistent low-level controller bottlenecks in embodied agents caused by episodic memory gaps and the decoupling between what-where-when memory and why-reasoning; improve robustness to viewpoint changes and adaptability in planning.
AI.method: Integrates a Causal Event Graph (CEG) into memory to encode explicit causal links between observations and task relevance. Replaces feature-based retrieval with causal reasoning for recall. Proposes an Opportunistic Task Scheduler that reorders subtasks when causally relevant opportunities arise. Adds a multi-scale progressive exploration strategy to gather spatially comprehensive observations. Evaluates on long-horizon sparse tasks in Minecraft-like environments.
AI.result: Significant improvements in task success and efficiency for long-horizon sparse tasks, especially in scenarios requiring adaptive decisions. WISE outperforms baselines by enabling robust recall under viewpoint changes and dynamic task reordering guided by causal reasoning.
AI.conclusion: Coupling explicit causal memory with opportunistic scheduling and multi-scale exploration yields improved robustness and efficiency for long-horizon embodied agents. The approach highlights the value of integrating what-where-when memory with why-reasoning and lays groundwork for more adaptive, memory-guided planning.

title: Y-BotFrame: An Extensible Embodied Agent Framework for Quadruped Robot Assistants
summary: Quadruped robots are capable of traversing a wide range of complex terrains with high flexibility. As highly mobile ground-based intelligent platforms, they can be equipped with modules for navigation control, environmental perception, and intelligent interaction, thereby serving as real-world mobile deployment platforms for various algorithms. In this paper, we introduce Y-BotFrame, an extensible embodied platform that turns a robot into an intelligent ground assistant. Y-BotFrame integrates multimodal perception capabilities, including speech, vision, and LiDAR, and employs a large language model as the cognitive core for environmental understanding, contextual reasoning, and task planning. The system maps user natural-language instructions into executable embodied task units that can be carried out by the robot. Y-BotFrame supports natural interaction through voice commands and visual feedback, removing the need for a remote controller and enabling efficient human-robot collaboration. With a highly extensible framework, Y-BotFrame supports plug-and-play integration of new functional modules as well as modular upgrades and iterative development, offering a reference implementation for the real-world deployment of general-purpose, instruction-driven embodied agents.The supplementary video is available at https://xdei-group.github.io/Y-BotFrame/.

AI.tldr: An extensible quadruped platform (Y-BotFrame) that uses an LLM-driven embodied agent with multimodal perception to translate natural language instructions into executable tasks, enabling voice-based interaction and plug-and-play modular upgrades for real-world deployment.
AI.motivation: To enable flexible, real-world deployment of embodied agents on quadruped robots by integrating perception (speech, vision, LiDAR) and cognitive planning via an LLM, turning natural language into actionable tasks.
AI.method: Develop Y-BotFrame with multimodal sensors, an LLM-based cognitive core for environment understanding, reasoning, and planning; map natural-language instructions to executable embodied task units; support voice and visual feedback; design for plug-and-play modularity and iterative development; provide a reference implementation.
AI.result: Conceptual/system description with a supplementary video; demonstrates an extensible, instruction-driven embodied agent platform rather than presenting quantitative experimental results.
AI.conclusion: Y-BotFrame provides a flexible, extensible framework enabling natural interaction and integrated perception for real-world deployment of general-purpose embodied agents on quadruped robots.

title: Towards Provably Fair Machine Learning: Bayesian Approaches For Consistent and Transparent Predictions
summary: ML classifiers deployed in high-stakes domains produce predictions whose quality varies systematically across subgroups. For granular subgroups defined by intersections of multiple features, predictions are often inconsistent with the observed data: the model's outputs contradict the evidence available for that subgroup. This problem is exacerbated by regularisation, which improves aggregate performance by collapsing small subgroups into larger groups, disproportionately affecting demographic minorities. We define two requirements for consistent prediction: determinism (identical individuals receive identical predictions) and statistical consistency (we cannot reject, at significance level alpha, the hypothesis that the predictions for a subgroup were drawn from the Bayesian optimal target distribution inferred for that subgroup). From these requirements we derive the Fair Bayesian classifier, which enforces both across every group and subgroup simultaneously and abstains whenever no consistent deterministic prediction is possible. On three benchmark datasets (Adult, COMPAS, and Bank Marketing), standard classifiers produce statistically inconsistent predictions for a substantial proportion of subgroups. Our classifier achieves zero consistency error by construction while exceeding baseline accuracy and multicalibration on every dataset tested. Statistical consistency provides a principled foundation for prediction quality with direct implications for algorithmic fairness. Minority demographics are disproportionately concentrated in small subgroups, precisely where frequentist inference is least reliable; addressing this inference problem is therefore a necessary step toward fair ML. By enforcing Bayesian consistency at the finest resolution the data supports, the our classifier demonstrates that exhaustive subgroup fairness with principled abstention is achievable in practice.

AI.tldr: A Fair Bayesian classifier enforces determinism and statistical consistency across all groups and subgroups, abstaining when inconsistent; it achieves zero consistency error and improves accuracy and multicalibration on Adult, COMPAS, and Bank Marketing datasets.
AI.motivation: High-stakes ML predictions vary across subgroups and regularization exacerbates disparities by collapsing small subgroups. There is a need for predictions that are consistent with subgroup evidence and probabilistic targets to support fairness.
AI.method: Define two consistency requirements—determinism (identical individuals receive identical predictions) and statistical consistency (predictions for a subgroup cannot be rejected as drawn from the subgroup's Bayesian optimal target distribution). Derive the Fair Bayesian classifier that enforces both across all groups and subgroups and abstains when no consistent deterministic prediction exists.
AI.result: Standard classifiers exhibit a substantial fraction of statistically inconsistent subgroups. The proposed Fair Bayesian classifier achieves zero consistency error by construction and outperforms baselines in accuracy and multicalibration on Adult, COMPAS, and Bank Marketing datasets.
AI.conclusion: Statistical consistency provides a principled foundation for prediction quality and algorithmic fairness. Addressing inference issues in small subgroups is essential, and enforcing Bayesian consistency at the finest data-supported granularity enables exhaustive subgroup fairness with principled abstention in practice.

title: Efficient, Robust, and Anti-Collusion Fingerprinting of Image Diffusion Models
summary: Model fingerprinting, embedding user-specific identifiers (fingerprints) into generated outputs, has recently emerged as a popular solution to protect the intellectual property rights (IPR) of generative text-to-image (T2I) models and prevent unauthorized redistribution. In this work, we reveal a previously unexplored systematic vulnerability in existing generative model fingerprinting methods: they lack robustness against collusion attacks, where multiple attackers combine their models to remove or obscure the fingerprints. To address this issue, we take the first step towards a robust fingerprinting method for T2I models with anti-collusion capabilities. The proposed method encodes strings of bits, namely fingerprints, into the coefficients of a personalized normalization module (PNM) incorporated into T2I models, so that fingerprints can be reliably recovered from any generated image. To defend against collusion attacks and prevent unauthorized model redistribution, we introduce an anti-collusion mechanism based on lossless function-invariant parameter transformations. This mechanism significantly degrades the image generation quality of colluded models, making them effectively unusable. Moreover, our method allows developers to efficiently create multiple copies of fingerprinted T2I models by reparameterizing the PNM without the need for retraining. We also introduce a worst-case optimization strategy to improve robustness against model-level attacks. Our experiments demonstrate that the proposed method achieves high fidelity and robustness across multiple T2I image generation and editing tasks, with fingerprint extraction accuracy exceeding 99.5%. Compared with existing methods, our method demonstrates, for the first time, a notable proactive robustness to collusion attacks by significantly increasing the FID of colluded models.

AI.tldr: Presents a robust, collusion-resistant fingerprinting framework for text-to-image models by embedding bitstrings in a personalized normalization module (PNM) and applying lossless, function-invariant parameter transformations. It enables multi-copy model deployment via PNM reparameterization, uses worst-case optimization for robustness, and reports high fingerprint recovery (>99.5%) with degraded colluded model image quality (FID), outperforming prior methods.
AI.motivation: To fix the vulnerability of existing T2I fingerprinting schemes to collusion attacks and to provide a practical, scalable IP protection mechanism that preserves generation quality.
AI.method: Embed fingerprints into PNM coefficients; introduce anti-collusion transformations that are lossless and function-invariant; enable reparameterization of the PNM to generate multiple fingerprinted copies without retraining; adopt worst-case optimization to enhance robustness against model-level attacks; evaluate across various T2I generation/editing tasks using metrics like fingerprint recovery accuracy and FID.
AI.result: Fingerprint extraction accuracy exceeds 99.5%; colluded models show significantly higher FID, indicating degraded quality; multiple fingerprinted model copies can be efficiently produced via PNM reparameterization without retraining; robust performance demonstrated across generation/editing tasks.
AI.conclusion: Introduces the first proactive anti-collusion fingerprinting framework for T2I models, achieving high extraction fidelity, improved resistance to collusion at the cost of some generation quality in colluded configurations, and enabling scalable deployment of multiple fingerprinted copies through PNM reparameterization.

title: DailyReport: An Open-ended Benchmark for Evaluating Search Agents on Daily Search Tasks
summary: Search Agents (SAs) typically leverage large language models (LLMs) to support complex information-seeking tasks by autonomously exploring web sources and synthesizing information into comprehensive responses. For SAs evaluation, prior benchmarks mainly focus on specialized tasks that are unlikely to arise in real-world user scenarios. Moreover, their reliance on coarse task-level rubrics often limits evaluation interpretability. To bridge this gap, we introduce DailyReport, an open-ended benchmark to evaluate SA capabilities on daily search tasks. It contains 150 open-ended tasks with 3,546 associated rubrics, capturing widely discussed and timely information demands of real-world users. Each task is decomposed into subtasks and evaluated with cascade rubrics across disentangled dimensions. Through cascade performance attribution and user-centric aggregation, we derive highly interpretable scores for each dimension, along with a user preference score. Our results on 17 agentic systems show that current systems still fall short of users' expectations. To facilitate future research, our dataset and code are made publicly available at https://github.com/AGI-Eval-Official/DailyReport.

AI.tldr: Open-ended benchmark DailyReport for SA evaluation with 150 tasks and 3,546 rubrics; shows current agentic systems underperform relative to user expectations; dataset and code released publicly.
AI.motivation: Addresses the need for realistic, interpretable evaluation of search agents (SAs) beyond task-level rubrics and specialized tasks by capturing daily, real-world information demands and enabling interpretable performance attribution.
AI.method: Construct DailyReport: 150 open-ended tasks decomposed into subtasks; 3,546 rubrics organized in cascade across disentangled dimensions; cascade performance attribution and user-centric aggregation to yield interpretable scores per dimension and a user preference score; evaluations conducted on 17 agentic SAs.
AI.result: Current SA systems fall short of user expectations; the proposed cascade and aggregation scheme yields interpretable, dimension-level scores and a user preference metric; dataset and code are publicly available.
AI.conclusion: DailyReport provides a realistic, interpretable benchmark that can drive progress in SA research and reproducibility, with open data and tooling to facilitate future studies.

title: EA-WM: Event-Aware World Models with Task-Specification Grounding for Long-Horizon Manipulation
summary: Pretrained-feature world models provide a useful substrate for robot imagination, but visual or latent prediction alone does not determine whether an imagined future satisfies task-relevant events. Long-horizon manipulation requires progress signals that are relational, predicate-level, and physically grounded: whether an object has moved, whether a drawer or contact state has changed, whether a placement predicate is satisfied, and whether a candidate future is reliable enough for execution. We introduce EA-WM, an event-aware world-model framework that augments frozen visual-feature dynamics with task-specification-grounded event prediction and verification. EA-WM rolls out candidate futures in pretrained visual-feature space, decodes them into structured event states, and scores them using task-progress, semantic-consistency, physical-feasibility, and uncertainty terms. The verifier guides sampling-based planning, gates candidate actions, and, in the contact-sensitive LIBERO wine-rack setting, selects among PPOgenerated proposals. Across navigation, deformable-object, wall-constrained, and languagedescribed manipulation studies, EA-WM shows that event-aware verification can make featurespace world models more interpretable and better aligned with task progress.

AI.tldr: Event-aware world-model (EA-WM) augments pretrained feature dynamics with event-level prediction and verification to guide long-horizon manipulation. It decodes feature-space futures into structured events and scores them by task progress, semantic consistency, physical feasibility, and uncertainty, improving interpretability and alignment with task goals across diverse manipulation tasks.
AI.motivation: Visual or latent prediction alone lacks relational, predicate-level, and physically grounded progress signals needed for long-horizon planning. Task progress requires reliable event predicates (e.g., object moved, state changes, placement satisfaction) and verification to ensure candidate futures are executable.
AI.method: Freeze pretrained visual-feature dynamics and add an event-prediction and verification module grounded in task specifications. Roll out candidate futures in feature space, decode into structured event states, and score them using task-progress, semantic-consistency, physical-feasibility, and uncertainty terms. A verifier guides sampling-based planning and gates candidate actions; in LIBERO wine-rack, selects among PPO-generated proposals. Evaluated across navigation, deformable-object, wall-constrained, and language-described manipulation tasks.
AI.result: Event-aware verification yields more interpretable and task-aligned world-model predictions in feature space, improving planning robustness and selection among proposals across diverse long-horizon manipulation domains.
AI.conclusion: Incorporating event-aware verification into world models enhances their usefulness for long-horizon manipulation by tying perceptual predictions to explicit, predicate-level progress signals and feasibility checks, enabling more reliable planning.

title: Bag of Dims: Training-Free Mechanistic Interpretability via Dimension-Level Sign Patterns
summary: We show that the standard basis of transformer hidden states already provides a training-free, architecture-general feature basis. Individual dimensions encode semantic content via their signs and confidence via their magnitudes, functioning as independent binary registers. We validate this Bag of Dims framework across three model families (Qwen 3.5-4B, Gemma 3-4B, Mistral 7B) through four progressive experiments. Sign patterns alone carry predictive content: replacing all magnitudes with unity achieves 72-93% top-5 next-token accuracy through the LM head, and pure Hamming scoring without any decoder reaches 80-90% top-4096. These sign patterns organize into semantic features: using a single-token type cache (one forward pass per vocabulary token, no context), we discover 175 categories via per-dimension sign consistency (mean AUC 0.80) from 50 anchors with zero training. A trained probe adds only +0.018 AUC and converges to axis-aligned weights, confirming negligible cross-dimension structure. This structure extends to attention: all 175 categories remain discoverable in K and V projections. On the write side, static FFN weight inspection links 20% of features to individual writer neurons (>0.70 agreement; random controls: 0%), with top-200 neuron coalitions achieving >0.70 agreement on 99.9% of prototypes via majority vote. Fully unsupervised discovery (random seeds, no labels) scales to 1500 features at 100% yield and 99% sparsity across all three models, with pairwise MI of 0.0014 bits confirming low inter-dimension coupling. These results establish that the standard basis already suffices for feature reading throughout the transformer compute pathway, requiring no training, no optimization, and no GPU-days beyond a single forward pass per vocabulary token.

AI.tldr: The transformer hidden-state standard basis suffices as a training-free, architecture-general feature basis. Sign patterns encode semantic content; magnitudes encode confidence making dimensions act as independent registers. Across three families (Qwen 3.5-4B, Gemma 3-4B, Mistral 7B) and four experiments, 175 semantic categories emerge; reading features requires only a forward pass per vocab token. Trained probes add marginal gains and converge to axis-aligned weights; writer neurons and their coalitions account for write-side features; unsupervised discovery scales to 1500 features with high yield and very low inter-dimension coupling (MI ~0.0014 bits). Overall, the standard basis enables feature reading along the entire transformer compute path without training.
AI.motivation: To understand whether interpretable, high-level features exist in transformers without training, and whether the standard basis suffices as a universal feature basis across architectures. The work aims to reveal semantic structure directly from signs and magnitudes, connect it to attention and FFN writes, and demonstrate scalable, unsupervised discovery.
AI.method: Four progressive experiments across three model families (Qwen 3.5-4B, Gemma 3-4B, Mistral 7B). Analyses include: (i) sign-pattern content evaluation, using unity magnitudes; (ii) single-token type cache to discover semantic categories via per-dimension sign consistency with zero training; (iii) a trained probe to assess cross-dimension structure and axis-alignment; (iv) extension to K and V attention projections; (v) write-side analysis via static FFN weight inspection linking features to writer neurons and coalition voting; (vi) fully unsupervised discovery with random seeds yielding up to 1500 features with 100% yield and ~99% sparsity; pairwise MI computed to assess inter-dimension coupling.
AI.result: Key results include: sign patterns alone yield 72-93% top-5 accuracy; Hamming-based evaluation without decoders yields 80-90% top-4096; identification of 175 semantic categories (AUC ~0.80 with 50 anchors, zero training); trained probe adds ~0.018 AUC and finds axis-aligned weights; 20% of features map to writer neurons with >0.70 agreement; top-200 neuron coalitions achieve >0.70 agreement on 99.9% of prototypes via majority vote; unsupervised discovery scales to 1500 features with 100% yield and 99% sparsity; pairwise mutual information between features ~0.0014 bits indicating low coupling.
AI.conclusion: The standard basis of transformer hidden states already provides a training-free, architecture-general feature basis suitable for feature reading across the compute path. No training or GPU-days beyond a single token-by-token forward pass are required, enabling robust interpretability and feature discovery across attention and feed-forward components.

title: Camera and LiDAR BEV Fusion for Cooperative 3D Object Detection on TUMTraf V2X
summary: We describe a Camera and LiDAR fusion detector developed for the TUMTraf V2X cooperative 3D object detection track of the DriveX 2026 challenge. The detector fuses three roadside cameras with a fused infrastructure-plus-vehicle point cloud in a shared bird's-eye-view space and predicts boxes through a CenterPoint-style head with a generalized IoU regression loss and an IoU quality re-ranking head. Trained on the provided train and validation splits, the model reaches a 3D mAP of 0.85 on the public Codabench test split. While iterating on the system, we observed that 44 of the 50 test frames are also present in the released train (40) and validation (4) splits with their labels. We therefore conducted two additional studies to quantify how this overlap affects the final score: (1) a finetuning run that oversamples the 44 overlapping frames, reaching 0.89 mAP, and (2) a post-processing run that replaces predictions on those frames with the released ground truth, reaching 0.99 mAP (uploaded to our Codabench account for testing but not published on the leaderboard). All three configurations and their per-class results are reported.

AI.tldr: A camera-LiDAR fusion detector for DriveX 2026 TUMTraf V2X: fuses three roadside cameras with infrastructure+vehicle point cloud in BEV; CenterPoint-style head with IoU regression and IoU quality re-ranking; achieves 0.85 3D mAP on Codabench public test; detected train/test overlap (44/50 test frames) enabling two additional studies: oversampling overlapped frames to reach 0.89 mAP and post-processing GT replacement to reach 0.99 mAP; all three configurations and per-class results reported.
AI.motivation: Evaluate a multi-sensor fusion 3D object detector for V2X (roadside cameras + infrastructure+vehicle LiDAR) in a DriftX/DriveX setting and quantify how train/test frame overlap affects reported performance on Codabench.
AI.method: A BEV-based fusion detector using three roadside cameras plus fusion with a combined infrastructure+vehicle point cloud. Prediction heads follow a CenterPoint-style architecture with a generalized IoU regression loss and an IoU quality re-ranking head. Trained on provided train/validation splits; evaluated on Codabench test split. Additional studies: (1) oversampling of 44 overlapping test frames for finetuning, (2) replacing predictions on overlapping frames with ground truth during post-processing.
AI.result: 3D mAP on Codabench public test: 0.85. Finetuning with oversampling overlap: 0.89 mAP. Post-processing with GT replacement: 0.99 mAP (uploaded but not published on leaderboard). All three configurations include per-class results.
AI.conclusion: Indicates substantial performance inflation due to train/test overlap (data leakage). While the fusion architecture shows strong performance, reported gains are partly due to leakage and post-processing GT replacement; emphasizes need for strictly disjoint test sets and transparent reporting of leakage, along with robust ablations across configurations.

title: HarnessBridge: Learnable Bidirectional Controller for LLM Agent Harness
summary: Large language models are increasingly deployed as agents for long-horizon tasks, yet their performance is shaped not only by model capability and environment design, but also by the harness that mediates agent--environment interaction. Existing harnesses are largely manually engineered, making them difficult to scale as trajectories grow longer and interactions become more complex. In this work, we ask whether harness can be generated by a learnable plug-in module that can be trained in an end-to-end fashion. We introduce HarnessBridge, a lightweight learnable harness controller that parameterizes the agent--environment interface as a bidirectional projection. HarnessBridge learns two bidirectional projections: observation projection, which distills raw trajectories into compact, decision-relevant states, and action projection, which converts proposed actions into executable transitions or trajectory-grounded rejections. We train HarnessBridge on a harness supervision dataset via unified instruction tuning. On Terminal-Bench~2.0 and SWE-bench Verified, HarnessBridge matches or surpasses strong specialized harnesses while substantially reducing token usage and trajectory length, and generalizes from smaller generators to larger commercial models.

AI.tldr: Learnable HarnessBridge mediates agent–environment interactions via bidirectional projections, trained end-to-end, achieving strong performance while reducing token usage and trajectory length.
AI.motivation: Current harnesses are manually engineered and struggle to scale for long-horizon tasks; a learnable, plug-in harness could improve scalability and efficiency.
AI.method: Introduce HarnessBridge; two bidirectional projections: observation projection to extract decision-relevant states from raw trajectories; action projection to translate proposed actions into executable transitions or grounded rejections; trained with unified instruction tuning on a harness supervision dataset.
AI.result: On Terminal-Bench 2.0 and SWE-bench Verified, HarnessBridge matches or surpasses strong harnesses, while reducing token usage and trajectory length, and generalizes from small to large models.
AI.conclusion: A scalable, end-to-end trainable harness enables efficient and effective agent–environment interaction for long-horizon tasks.

title: FTP-1: A Generalist Foundation Tactile Policy Across Tactile Sensors for Contact-Rich Manipulation
summary: Despite the success of vision-based generalist robotic policies, existing tactile-based policies remain tied to fixed embodiments and sensor setups. This is because tactile signals are highly heterogeneous across hardware, making cross-sensor generalization difficult. We present FTP-1,the first generalist foundation tactile policy pretrained to acquire transferable tactile manipulation abilities across diverse sensors and embodiments. FTP-1 supports varied tactile inputs, including image-, array-, and state-based signals, by using heterogeneous encoders to project them into unified morphology-aware latent tokens that are jointly modeled by a shared tactile Transformer expert. Pretrained on around 3,000 hours of tactile manipulation data aggregated from 26 data sources, spanning human and robot demonstrations across 21 sensors, FTP-1 learns tactile skills that transfer beyond the sensors seen during pretraining. Across downstream finetuning experiments spanning 5 hardware configurations, FTP-1 improves contact-rich manipulation on seen sensor setups by +17.2% and, surprisingly, transfers to two previously unseen tactile-sensor setups, achieving a +31% gain in success rate. FTP-1 establishes the first unified foundation baseline for tactile manipulation, providing future tactile policies with a shared model-level starting point. Pretrained models, datasets, training code and more visualization at https://ftp1-policy.github.io.

AI.tldr: FTP-1 is a generalist foundation tactile policy pretrained to transfer across diverse tactile sensors/embodiments, enabling cross-sensor tactile manipulation and improving success rates, even on unseen sensors.
AI.motivation: Tactile signals are highly heterogeneous across hardware, making cross-sensor generalization difficult; existing tactile policies are tied to fixed sensor setups.
AI.method: Develop a heterogeneous-encoder framework that projects image-, array-, and state-based tactile inputs into unified morphology-aware latent tokens, which are modeled by a shared tactile Transformer expert. Pretrain on ~3,000 hours from 26 data sources across 21 sensors (human and robot demonstrations). Finetune across 5 hardware configurations, testing transfer to unseen sensors.
AI.result: FTP-1 improves contact-rich manipulation on seen sensor setups by +17.2%. It transfers to two unseen tactile-sensor setups with a +31% gain in success rate.
AI.conclusion: FTP-1 establishes the first unified foundation baseline for tactile manipulation, offering a shared model-level starting point for future tactile policies. Pretrained resources are available at the project site.

authors: Juan Pablo De la Cruz Weinstein
title: Keep Policy Gradient in Charge: Sibling-Guided Credit Distillation for Long-Horizon Tool-Use Agents
comment: 13 pages, 4 figures, 7 tables. Submitted to EMNLP 2026 Industry Track
summary:
Long-horizon tool-use reinforcement learning can learn from outcome verification, but its
  trajectory-level advantage is broadcast across many reasoning, API, and answer tokens.
  Self-distillation promises a denser signal by reusing a policy's own rollouts or a privileged
  teacher. We show, however, that direct token-level self-distillation can silently destroy tool use:
  it rehearses teacher behavior without knowing which actions the verifier rewards, so useful skills
  and harmful shortcuts are amplified together. We introduce Sibling-Guided Credit Distillation
  (SGCD), which uses distillation for credit assignment rather than as a competing actor loss.
  Dynamic sampling produces mixed successful and failed sibling rollouts; an external LLM summarizes
  their contrast into a training-only stepwise credit reference; dense teacher/student divergence
  drives credit reassignment; and bounded detached credit weights reshape GRPO token advantages. The
  deployed student sees no external LLM, sibling evidence, or oracle. Across AppWorld and
  $τ^3$-airline, SGCD improves over matched GRPO comparators: AppWorld TGC $42.9 \to 45.6$ on
  test_normal and $24.7 \to 27.0$ on test_challenge, and $τ^3$-airline pass@1 $0.583 \to 0.602$.

AI.tldr: SGCD introduces sibling-guided credit distillation to reassign credit signals for long-horizon tool-use RL, avoiding the pitfalls of token-level self-distillation, and yields consistent improvements on AppWorld and τ^3-airline.
AI.motivation: Addresses degradation from token-level self-distillation in verifier-based, long-horizon tasks by providing a signal for credit assignment rather than competing with the policy.
AI.method: Dynamic sampling of mixed-successful and failed sibling rollouts; an external LLM summarizes their contrast into a training-only stepwise credit reference; dense teacher/student divergence drives credit reassignment; bounded detached credit weights reshape GRPO token advantages; deployed student operates without external LLM or oracle.
AI.result: SGCD outperforms matched GRPO baselines: AppWorld test_normal 42.9→45.6; test_challenge 24.7→27.0; τ^3-airline pass@1 0.583→0.602.
AI.conclusion: SGCD provides a robust credit-assignment mechanism for long-horizon tool-use RL, mitigating the harms of token-level self-distillation and delivering cross-domain performance gains without exposing the deployed agent to teacher inputs.

title: Objects Before Words: Object-First Inductive Biases for Grounding Language in Child-View Video
summary: Learning grounded word meaning from natural experience requires resolving two ambiguities in infant-view recordings: when the named referent appears and where it is in a cluttered frame. In SAYCam-style data, caregiver speech is sparse and weakly synchronized with egocentric video, so single-frame contrastive pairing yields noisy positives in which the intended object is absent or entangled with distractors. We propose BabyMind, an object-first bias for child-view contrastive learning under sparse, noisy supervision. BabyMind extracts candidate object embeddings using an offline mask-based region interface, links candidates across a short utterance-centered window into lightweight object files via tracking, and aligns utterances to bags of object files with a prototype-space multiple-instance contrastive objective. Track-coherence and global-object agreement regularizers stabilize learning and transfer object-file structure into the global frame embedding used at evaluation. On SAYCam-S, BabyMind improves Labeled-S 15 forced-choice accuracy by +2.6 points over CVCL and yields consistent gains on in-vocabulary out-of-distribution benchmarks. Code is available at https://github.com/sathiiii/BabyMind.

AI.tldr: BabyMind proposes an object-first bias for child-view contrastive learning under sparse supervision, achieving +2.6 accuracy on SAYCam-S and robust OOD gains.
AI.motivation: Infant-grounded word meaning requires resolving when and where referents appear in egocentric video. SAYCam-style data have sparse, weakly synchronized caregiver speech, causing noisy positives in single-frame contrastive learning.
AI.method: Construct object embeddings via an offline mask-based region interface; track candidates across an utterance-centered window into lightweight object files; align utterances to bags of object files with a prototype-space multiple-instance learning (MIL) objective; apply track-coherence and global-object agreement regularizers to transfer object-file structure to the global frame embedding used at evaluation.
AI.result: On SAYCam-S, +2.6 points in Labeled-S 15 forced-choice accuracy over CVCL baseline; consistent gains on in-vocabulary out-of-distribution benchmarks; code available at GitHub.
AI.conclusion: An object-first bias with a MIL objective improves grounding under sparse supervision, effectively stabilizing learning and transferring object-file structure to global representations; demonstrates practical gains and viability for infant-like language grounding.

title: The Hidden Power of Scaling Factor in LoRA Optimization
summary: In Low-Rank Adaptation (LoRA), the scaling factor $α$ is often treated as a mere complement to the learning rate, yet its role in optimization remains poorly understood. In this paper, we reveal that the scaling factor $α$ and the learning rate function differently, with $α$ emerging as the dominant driver of effective optimization, delivering gains that cannot be replicated by learning rate scaling alone. Through the synergy of extensive empirical analysis and a theoretical Signal-Drift framework, we uncover three findings into LoRA's scaling mechanism: First, LoRA's spectral suppression smooths the optimization landscape, rendering standard hyperparameters overly conservative and creating an optimization gap. Second, when leveraging this smoothness to accelerate convergence, $α$ outperforms the learning rate by amplifying the task signal without increasing the drift ratio. Third, the optimal scaling factor follows a sublinear relationship with the rank, well characterized by a square-root law with an unexpectedly large coefficient, revealing the insufficient scaling of existing rank-tied heuristics. Based on these insights, we propose LoRA-$α$, a minimalist framework that restores $α$ to its principled regime, making LoRA compatible with standard small learning rates. Extensive evaluations across diverse tasks demonstrate that LoRA-$α$ consistently improves performance while streamlining hyperparameter search, unleashing the learning potential of LoRA.

AI.tldr: LoRA’s scaling factor α is a primary driver of optimization, not merely a learning-rate surrogate. α induces spectral smoothing, enables faster convergence, and follows a sublinear (square-root) rank scaling with a large coefficient, motivating a principled LoRA-α framework that improves performance and simplifies hyperparameter search.
AI.motivation: Explain why α matters beyond simple learning-rate scaling, address optimization gaps created by spectral suppression, and demonstrate that existing rank-tied heuristics underestimate the potential of α.
AI.method: Empirical analysis across diverse tasks combined with a theoretical Signal-Drift framework. The study identifies three key findings about α’s role, derives a square-root rank-scaling law with a large coefficient, and introduces LoRA-α to restore α to its principled regime.
AI.result: α outperforms learning-rate scaling by amplifying task signal without increasing drift, and the spectral smoothing effect relaxes hyperparameters. The optimal α scales sublinearly with rank (≈ sqrt(rank) with a large coefficient). LoRA-α yields consistent performance gains and reduces hyperparameter search burden across tasks.
AI.conclusion: LoRA-α reclaims a principled α regime, enabling compatibility with small learning rates and broad performance gains. The work suggests that α is a fundamental optimization lever in LoRA that was previously underutilized, and provides a framework for future exploration and simplification of hyperparameter tuning.

title: Redesigning Regularization for Effective Policy Smoothing
summary: This paper proposes a novel regularization design to effectively smooth policy functions in reinforcement learning. While regularization that enhances ``global'' Lipschitz continuity was initially considered, it has been limited to ``local'' Lipschitz continuity due to a tradeoff between smoothness and expressiveness. However, it has become apparent that the original implementation is cumbersome and does not provide sufficient smoothing, leading to a preference for simpler implementations. This stems from a discrepancy between theory and implementation, and a more appropriate implementation can expect to facilitate smoothing. Therefore, this paper identifies three reasons why the original implementation does not function adequately and provide remedies for them. This modified regularization performs well across multiple tasks and algorithms, successfully achieving smooth motion while improving control performance. Furthermore, by applying it to sim-to-real reinforcement learning for a quadruped robot, it is demonstrated that smooth motion provides robustness against sudden changes in target velocity commands.

AI.tldr: A revised regularization to smooth RL policy functions, addressing implementation gaps in global Lipschitz smoothing, identifying three failure causes and remedies; demonstrates improved smoothness and control across tasks, including sim-to-real quadruped robustness to velocity changes.
AI.motivation: Smoothing policy functions is desirable but hard: global Lipschitz approaches trade off smoothness and expressiveness; previous implementations are cumbersome and underperform, highlighting a mismatch between theory and practice.
AI.method: Diagnose three reasons the original regularization fails; propose remedies and a modified regularization design; test across multiple tasks and RL algorithms; apply to sim-to-real for a quadruped to assess robustness to velocity command disturbances.
AI.result: Modified regularization achieves smoother policies and improved control performance across tasks/algorithms; demonstrates robustness in sim-to-real quadruped to sudden velocity command changes.
AI.conclusion: The proposed regularization provides effective smoothing with better practicality, aligning theory with implementation and broad applicability in RL control tasks.

title: The Metric Picks the Winner: Evaluation Choice Flips Model Rankings for Drug-Response Prediction in Unseen Chemistry
summary:
Predicting how a cell's transcriptome responds to a drug it has never seen is a core, hard problem in computational cell biology: recent benchmarks show complex models often fail to beat trivial baselines once test compounds are held out by chemistry. We study one cell line and assay, THP-1 cells profiled by DRUG-seq, scored by the active-compound weighted MSE(wMSE) of the VCPI prediction contest.
