We propose a staged approach: dumb baselines (untreated control and mean training-compound response) that the field keeps failing to beat; non-parametric retrieval (a Tanimoto-weighted average of a held-out compound's nearest training compounds); and a fusion stage combining a frozen chemistry embedding with retrieval-support features to predict the residual over the mean, with an uncertainty head and gene programs. On the released VCPI THP-1 drug-seq data (14,026 training compounds), under a Bemis-Murcko scaffold split, the model ranking inverts depending on the metric. Under an inverse-variance per-gene proxy, a regularized linear regression on Morgan fingerprints appears to win over the deep models, retrieval, and ChemBERTa -- the textbook "simple baselines win" result. But under the contest's true active-set metric (per-(gene, compound) Mejia weights, validated against the official scorer; mean baseline 0.535 vs the organizers' 0.507 reference), that reverses: the deep models win, our fusion decoder significantly beats the linear fingerprint baseline (-0.012 wMSE, paired bootstrap p < 10^-4), and the proxy's winner becomes the worst chemistry-aware predictor. Picking the metric picks the winner -- to our knowledge the first demonstration on real held-out drug chemistry of the metric-calibration effect established largely on genetic perturbation. We release a reproducible pipeline
  wired to the official scorer that emits a valid submission over the real 1064 x 12,995 grid.

AI.tldr: Metric choice dictates which model wins in predicting drug-induced transcriptomic responses; simple baselines can outperform deep models under some metrics, but with true active-set metrics, deep models and a fusion decoder outperform; a metric-calibration effect is demonstrated on held-out drug chemistry; a reproducible pipeline is released.
AI.motivation: To understand how model performance for predicting transcriptomic responses to unseen drugs depends on the evaluation metric and to test whether sophisticated models beat simple baselines under realistic, chemistry-aware holdouts.
AI.method: Propose a staged modeling pipeline: (1) dumb baselines (untreated and mean training-compound response); (2) non-parametric retrieval using a Tanimoto-weighted average of nearest training compounds; (3) a fusion stage combining frozen chemistry embeddings with retrieval features to predict residuals with an uncertainty head and gene-program outputs. Evaluate on VCPI THP-1 DRUG-seq data (14,026 training compounds) with a Bemis-Murcko scaffold split. Compare linear Morgan-fingerprint regression, deep models, retrieval, ChemBERTa, and the fusion decoder under two metrics: an inverse-variance per-gene proxy and the contest’s per-(gene, compound) Mejia-weighted active-set metric.
AI.result: Under the proxy metric, a regularized linear regression on Morgan fingerprints wins among all methods, including deep models, retrieval, and ChemBERTa. Under the contest’s true active-set metric, deep models win; the fusion decoder significantly beats the linear baseline (−0.012 wMSE, p < 1e−4). The proxy-based winner becomes the worst chemistry-aware predictor under the true metric. The study demonstrates a metric-calibration effect: the apparent best approach depends on the evaluation metric.
AI.conclusion: Metric choice critically shapes which modeling approach is deemed best for predicting drug-induced transcriptomic responses. This is the first demonstration on real held-out drug chemistry of metric-calibration effects in genetic perturbation tasks. The authors provide a reproducible pipeline aligned with the official scorer for submission on the full dataset.

title: Diffusion Transformer World-Action Model for AV Scene Prediction
summary: Action-conditioned world models let an autonomous vehicle predict future camera scenes from its own planned controls, enabling planning and simulation without real-world rollouts, but at compact, trainable scale the futures are ambiguous and the field's standard distortion metrics actively mislead: they reward a blurry regression mean over a realistic prediction. We confront this with a compact latent world model that, given the present front-camera latent and a sequence of ego-actions, predicts future scene latents a frozen decoder renders to $256 \times 256$ frames up to 8 seconds ahead, evaluated on 150 held-out nuScenes scenes. We first benchmark where to predict: across six frozen encoders spanning four representation families, V-JEPA2 with temporal context reduces steering RMSE by 40% over the best single-frame encoder. We then train a latent Diffusion Transformer (DiT) and, through a controlled diagnosis, identify the four ingredients it needs: spatial tokens, the $x_0$ objective, residual anchoring, and sampling matched to target uncertainty. In a Stable-Diffusion-VAE encode-predict-decode pipeline we expose the central tension: distortion metrics (cosine similarity, SSIM) favor the blurry mean, masking that the diffusion model is far closer to the real frame distribution. Inception-based FID and KID reveal a clean perception-distortion frontier: diffusion attains KID 0.078 versus 0.375 for regression ($4.8\times$ better), and a deployable train-derived calibration makes this practical without test-time ground truth. The model is genuinely action-controllable (steering drives scene displacement, Spearman $ρ= 0.81$, vs $-0.18$ for regression). We trace limited single-pass motion to a shared-present anchor and engineer a compact 1.7M-parameter "jump" model that recovers full ground-truth motion magnitude ($1.02\times$ GT), where single-pass models capture less than half.

AI.tldr: A diffusion-based latent world model yields more perceptually faithful, controllable long-horizon future frame predictions for autonomous driving than regression, using a compact 1.7M-parameter jump model and strong calibration.
AI.motivation: Standard distortion metrics reward blurry regression means and fail to capture perceptual realism and controllability necessary for planning in action-conditioned world models. The work targets stable, compact prediction of future scenes to enable planning without real-world rollouts.
AI.method: Train a latent world model that predicts future scene latents from the present front-camera latent and a sequence of ego-actions, with a 256x256 decoder up to 8s ahead. Benchmark six frozen encoders (V-JEPA2 variants). Train a latent Diffusion Transformer (DiT) with spatial tokens, an x0 objective, residual anchoring, and sampling aligned to target uncertainty within a Stable-Diffusion-VAE encode–predict–decode pipeline. Evaluate with FID/KID and perception-based metrics (Spearman correlation with steering). Diagnose motion with a compact 1.7M-parameter jump model anchored to the present.
AI.result: Steering RMSE improves ~40% over the best single-frame encoder. Latent diffusion achieves KID 0.078 vs 0.375 for regression (4.8× better). Inception-based metrics show diffusion aligns better with real frames than regression. The model exhibits strong action controllability (Spearman ρ=0.81 for steering; regression is −0.18). A small 1.7M-parameter jump model recovers full motion magnitude close to GT (1.02×), outperforming single-pass models that capture less than half.
AI.conclusion: Diffusion-based latent prediction provides a cleaner perception–distortion frontier and practical controllability for action-conditioned world models in autonomous driving, addressing distortion biases and enabling efficient planning/simulation with compact models.

title: Zero-source LLM Hallucination Detection with Human-like Criteria Probing
summary: Large language models (LLMs) often hallucinate by generating factually incorrect or unfaithful content, posing significant risks to their safe use. Detecting such hallucinations is particularly challenging under the zero-source constraint, where no model internals or external references are available, and detection must rely solely on the textual query-answer pair. In this paper, we propose Human-like Criteria Probing for Hallucination Detection (HCPD), a paradigm that emulates the multi-faceted reasoning of human evaluators. Its core is a Human-like Criteria Probing (HCP) mechanism, in which a LLM agent adaptively decomposes its judgment into a weighted set of interpretable criteria and aggregates criterion-specific scores into a final truthfulness measure. To achieve this adaptive capability, we introduce a reward-based alignment scheme using only weak supervision from semantic consistency. At inference, we employ a multi-sampling aggregation strategy to ensure robust decisions while preserving full interpretability. We further provide theoretical analysis supporting the reliability of our approach. Extensive experiments show that HCPD consistently outperforms state-of-the-art baselines, offering an effective and explainable solution for zero-source hallucination detection. Code is available at https://github.com/TRISKEL10N/HCPD.

AI.tldr: Proposes HCPD, a zero-source hallucination detection framework that mimics human evaluators by decomposing judgments into weighted, interpretable criteria; uses a reward-alignment scheme with weak supervision; employs multi-sampling for robust inference; claims theoretical guarantees and superior empirical performance with explainability; code available.
AI.motivation: Hallucination in LLMs is prevalent and risky, and detecting it under zero-source constraints (no model internals or external references) is challenging. Existing methods may rely on internals or external data, lacking interpretability and robustness. A human-like, explainable, zero-source detector is needed.
AI.method: Introduce Human-like Criteria Probing (HCP) where an LLM agent adaptively decomposes its judgment into a weighted set of interpretable criteria and aggregates scores into a final truthfulness measure. Use a reward-based alignment scheme with weak supervision from semantic consistency to enable adaptation without access to internals. At inference, apply multi-sampling aggregation to improve robustness while preserving interpretability. Provide theoretical analysis for reliability.
AI.result: Extensive experiments indicate HCPD consistently outperforms state-of-the-art baselines on zero-source hallucination detection, delivering an effective and explainable solution. The authors also provide code for replication.
AI.conclusion: HCPD offers a reliable, interpretable approach to zero-source hallucination detection and shows promise for safer LLM deployment, supported by theoretical reasoning and strong empirical results.

title: Multi-Modal Multi-Agent Robotic Cognitive Alignment enabled by Non-Invasive Consumer Brain Computer Interfaces: A Proof of Concept Exploration
comment: 19 pages, 9 figures, for associated video, see https://youtu.be/0Tav-G87XGs
summary: While non-verbal behaviors and expressive movements are essential for natural human-robot interaction, existing methods often overlook a crucial element: the human's internal cognitive state. Frequently, proactive multi-agent systems can interrupt humans at inopportune moments, leading to cognitive overload and decreased task performance. This paper introduces a framework for generating "cognitively aligned" multi-agent interactions, enhancing the ability of robotic systems to contextually defer communications to the user of an agent system during moments of high human mental workload and engagement. We present the design and implementation of a closed-loop architecture that explores the interplay between autonomous task execution and real-time neurophysiological focus. Using a consumer-grade Brain-Computer Interface (BCI), our approach continuously monitors Electroencephalography (EEG) spectral band powers while a human performs an engagement-inducing task. We propose an engagement-driven pipeline where an HTTP-based signaling mechanism places a primary agent's sensory inputs and audio outputs into a holding state upon detecting high engagement. This allows secondary agents to seamlessly process complex, delegated tasks in the background. Once the human's cognitive state returns to a lower cognitive load baseline, the primary agent releases the queued agent message. Our preliminary results demonstrate the feasibility of leveraging real-time signal processing, Large Language Models (LLMs), and physical robotic embodiments to create cognitively-aware, non-intrusive multi-agent systems.

AI.tldr: A cognitively aligned multi-agent framework that defers non-urgent communications during high mental workload using EEG-based engagement detection, enabling background task delegation via LLMs and robotics.
AI.motivation: To mitigate cognitive overload and interruptions in human-robot collaboration by aligning agent communications with the user’s cognitive state, potentially improving task performance and user experience.
AI.method: Closed-loop architecture using consumer-grade EEG to monitor spectral powers during engagement tasks; an HTTP-based signaling pipeline defers a primary agent’s inputs/outputs into a holding state; secondary agents perform delegated tasks in the background; release occurs when cognitive load returns to baseline; integration with LLMs and physical robots for real-time cognition-aware interaction.
AI.result: Preliminary results indicate feasibility of real-time signal processing, executive-LMM integration, and robotic embodiment to support cognitively-aware, non-intrusive multi-agent systems; specifics (e.g., metrics, sample size) are not disclosed.
AI.conclusion: The work proposes a viable framework for cognitively-aligned HRI with deferred communications and background delegation, showing promise but requiring more rigorous validation, detailed methodology, and robust evaluation across tasks and users.

title: Individual Control Barrier Functions-Guided Diffusion Model for Safe Offline Multi-Agent Reinforcement Learning
comment: Accepted to the 23rd IFAC World Congress, 2026
summary: Offline reinforcement learning allows control policies to be learned directly from data without online interaction, making it suitable for safety-critical tasks. Recent studies have applied diffusion models to offline reinforcement learning to leverage their strong capacity for modeling complex data distributions. However, existing approaches primarily focus on single-agent settings, leaving the safety challenges in multi-agent environments largely unexplored. In this work, we propose a safe offline multi-agent reinforcement learning algorithm that embeds neural individual control barrier functions into the diffusion model to enhance safety during trajectory generation, with control policies recovered through inverse dynamics. We evaluate our algorithm across diverse benchmarks, demonstrating substantial safety improvements while maintaining competitive rewards.

AI.tldr: A safe offline multi-agent RL framework that augments diffusion-model trajectories with neural individual control barrier functions, enabling safe policy generation via inverse dynamics and achieving strong safety with competitive rewards.
AI.motivation: Safety in multi-agent offline RL is underexplored; diffusion models excel at modeling complex data but lack explicit safety guarantees in multi-agent settings. Integrating control barrier functions (ICBFs) provides principled safety constraints during trajectory generation.
AI.method: Embed neural individual control barrier functions into the diffusion model for trajectory generation in offline multi-agent RL, recover policies through inverse dynamics, and evaluate across diverse benchmarks for safety and performance.
AI.result: Demonstrates substantial safety improvements (e.g., reduced constraint violations) while maintaining competitive rewards across benchmarks.
AI.conclusion: Embedding neural ICBFs into diffusion-based offline multi-agent RL enables principled safety during trajectory generation without sacrificing performance, indicating viability for safety-critical multi-agent tasks.

title: A Machine Learning Framework for Real-Time Personalized Ergonomic Pose Analysis
summary: This paper introduces a new methodology for real-time prediction of ergonomic and non-ergonomic human poses using volumetric video data in three dimensions. Although the methodology was designed for ergonomic assessments, it can be adapted to other applications requiring real-time analysis of human posture. One aspect that makes this system stand out is its ability to analyze 3D point clouds during the assessment, enabling computation from multiple angles. This overcomes a critical limitation of cameras which provide often a fixed viewpoint, thereby restricting the data available for a thorough postural evaluation, especially when occlusions occur. The system continuously and automatically performs pose inference using the chosen perspective on the real-time streaming data; however, only the poses manually selected and labeled by the user are used to train the personalized deep learning classifier. The methodology has been refined through a case study in which RGB-D cameras captured subjects performing load-lifting tasks, enabling real-time skeletal labeling. The model was trained on this data and, following the training phase, performs inference on new streaming data in real time. This research offers a scalable and pragmatic approach for real-time ergonomic evaluation by combining state-of-the-art 3D data technologies and traditional 2D pose estimation algorithms. It addresses the increasing need for safety and health monitoring in workplace environments, marking a notable contribution to the domain.

AI.tldr: Proposes a real-time, 3D volumetric approach to ergonomic pose prediction using multi-view 3D data and personalized deep learning, enabling real-time skeletal labeling from streaming RGB-D data and overcoming fixed-view limitations.
AI.motivation: Need for occlusion-robust, real-time ergonomic monitoring in workplace settings and the ability to adapt pose analysis to other tasks while leveraging volumetric 3D data and 2D pose estimation techniques.
AI.method: Utilizes volumetric video data and 3D point cloud analysis from multiple perspectives to perform continuous pose inference on streaming data. Training is personalized: only poses selected and labeled by the user are used to train a user-specific deep learning classifier. Case study with RGB-D cameras capturing load-lifting tasks to enable real-time skeletal labeling; after training, inference runs in real time on new streaming data. Combines state-of-the-art 3D data technologies with traditional 2D pose estimation algorithms.
AI.result: Demonstrates real-time skeletal labeling and offers a scalable, pragmatic framework for real-time ergonomic evaluation, addressing safety monitoring needs in workplaces by integrating 3D data with 2D pose estimation.
AI.conclusion: The methodology provides a scalable and adaptable approach for real-time ergonomic assessment and can be extended to other applications requiring live posture analysis, with improved robustness to occlusions through multi-view 3D data.

title: MDForge: Agentic Molecular Dynamics Pipeline Design under Sparse Simulator Feedback
summary: Molecular dynamics (MD) is the canonical in-silico method for atomistic molecular science, simulating molecular behavior from first-principle physics. Designing an MD pipeline for a new system requires substantial expert knowledge: running it on even one molecule is expensive, ruling out trial-and-error. We automate this expert pipeline-design process with an LLM agent. Unlike existing MD agents that orchestrate a predefined tool set, we treat pipeline design as open-ended code generation in which the agent's behavior is reshaped online by verbal reward. Specifically, we build MDForge, an LLM agent whose in-context update rule densifies the sparse reward via a multi-agent debate among physics experts. On three SAMPL host-guest binding free-energy benchmarks, MDForge automatically designs MD pipelines competitive with human experts. Deployed on a library of unseen candidate guests, its CB[7] pipeline discovers a novel binder that wet-lab competition NMR confirms is a high-affinity, picomolar CB[7] binder. Our data and code are available at https://github.com/Zehong-Wang/MDForge.

AI.tldr: MDForge uses an LLM-driven, open-ended code-generation agent to design MD pipelines; throughput is enhanced by multi-agent debate that densifies sparse rewards; achieves competitive performance with human experts on SAMPL host-guest benchmarks and discovers a novel high-affinity CB[7] binder validated by NMR; code/data are public.
AI.motivation: Automate expert-level MD pipeline design to reduce cost, enable trial-and-error exploration, and accelerate discovery of accurate binding predictions and new binders.
AI.method: MDForge treats MD-pipeline design as open-ended code generation. An LLM agent updates its behavior via in-context, verbal rewards. A multi-agent debate among physics experts densifies sparse rewards to refine pipelines. The system is evaluated on three SAMPL host-guest free-energy benchmarks and further deployed on unseen CB[7] guest libraries.
AI.result: MDForge designs MD pipelines competitive with human experts on SAMPL benchmarks. It identifies a novel CB[7] binder in an unseen library, with NMR confirming high affinity in the picomolar range.
AI.conclusion: The approach demonstrates that open-ended, LLM-driven pipeline design with expert debate can automate MD workflow construction and enable discovery beyond training data; results suggest potential for broader automation and future integration with wet-lab validation.

title: Embedding ISO 10218 Safety Compliance in Robots via Control Barrier Functions for Human-Robot Collaboration
summary: Human-Robot Collaboration (HRC) requires strict adherence to safety standards, such as ISO 10218, to prevent harmful interactions. Standard Speed and Separation Monitoring (SSM) filters calculate safe robotic speeds based on conservative assumptions, such as constant human velocity, which prevents accurate predictions of minimum separation distances and causes unnecessary operational halts. This paper proposes a Control Barrier Function (CBF) that explicitly incorporates human acceleration data to analytically forward-predict the minimum human-robot separation distance during a worst-case robotic stopping trajectory. To guarantee safety at the control level, this predictive CBF is integrated as an inequality constraint within a Sequential Quadratic Programming (SQP) framework. Specifically, two methods are proposed: Method I, a CBF-constrained PD safety filter; and Method II, a task-scaling SQP controller that enforces a spatial tube constraint. Simulated and real-world experiments on a UR10e robot evaluate the two proposed methods against a standard industrial SSM module baseline. Results demonstrate that Method II dynamically modulates execution speed and confines spatial deviations. Compared to Method I, Method II achieves a 63\% reduction in mean trajectory error and avoids excessive evasive manoeuvres, ensuring high task throughput while complying with ISO 10218 SSM guidelines.

AI.tldr: Acceleration-aware predictive CBF integrated in SQP to improve safety and throughput in HRC; two methods (CBF PD safety filter and task-scaling SQP with a spatial tube); Method II yields 63% fewer trajectory errors vs Method I and baseline SSM.
AI.motivation: Overcome conservative SSM assumptions in ISO 10218-compliant HRC by explicitly accounting for human acceleration to predict minimum separation during worst-case robot stopping.
AI.method: Introduce a forward-plooking CBF that uses human acceleration to bound minimum human-robot separation, integrated as an inequality constraint in a Sequential Quadratic Programming (SQP) controller. Two instantiations: Method I—CBF-constrained PD safety filter; Method II—task-scaling SQP with a spatial tube constraint.
AI.result: Simulation and real-world experiments on UR10e show Method II dynamically modulates speed and confines spatial deviations, achieving a 63% reduction in mean trajectory error and avoiding excessive evasive maneuvers, while complying with ISO 10218 SSM guidelines.
AI.conclusion: Predictive acceleration-aware CBF within an SQP framework enhances safety without sacrificing throughput in HRC. Method II outperforms Method I, indicating practical viability for ISO-compliant HRC and potential extension to broader HRC settings.

title: TEDD: Robust Detection of Unstable Temporal Features
summary: When working with real-world temporal data, it is common to encounter features whose distribution is changing over time. The naive employment of Machine Learning models on this unstable data might lead to rapidly degrading performance, especially if the new distribution is much different from what was previously seen during training. In order to cope with this problem, it is critical to automatically identify features that are changing over time. With these features detected, data scientists and other practitioners will be able to mitigate the issue (for instance, by applying data transformations), deploying more robust models that retain high performance for longer periods of time. In this paper, we describe which temporal changes a feature should not suffer from, and propose TEDD, a technique to a) identify when a dataset might lead to an unstable Machine Learning model and b) automatically detect which features cause such lack of robustness. In order to achieve it, we leverage a regression model to highlight which features contribute to a good prediction of an instance's timestamp. We compare our approach to other methods in real and synthetic data, testing their detection capability on all simple change patterns. We show that our method: detects all types of basic changes, both for numerical and categorical features; can detect multivariate drifts; returns a comparable value measuring the amount of change of each feature; requires no parameter tuning; and is scalable both on number of features and instances of the dataset.

AI.tldr: TEDD detects feature drift in temporal data using a regression-based approach to identify unstable features and rank their contribution to timestamp prediction; it claims to detect all basic change types, multivariate drift, without hyperparameters, and with scalability.
AI.motivation: Real-world temporal data often exhibit distribution shifts over time, causing ML performance to degrade. Automatic detection of changing features enables timely mitigation and more robust models.
AI.method: Introduce TEDD, a regression-based technique that explains which features contribute to predicting an instance's timestamp. It identifies when a dataset may yield an unstable model and automatically detects which features drive lack of robustness. Evaluated on real and synthetic data across simple drift patterns; handles numerical and categorical features; detects multivariate drift; outputs a per-feature drift score; no parameter tuning required; scalable to many features and instances.
AI.result: Compared to other methods on real and synthetic data, TEDD detects all basic change types, including multivariate drift. It provides a per-feature measure of change that is comparable across settings, requires no parameter tuning, and scales with the dataset size.
AI.conclusion: TEDD is an effective, parameter-free, scalable method for detecting temporal feature drift, enabling practitioners to identify and mitigate unstable features to maintain model robustness over time.

title: Quality-Preserving Imperceptible Adversarial Attack on Skeleton-based Human Action Recognition
summary: Adversarial attacks on skeletal human action recognition have received significant attention. However, existing methods typically introduce noise-like perturbations that degrade motion quality post-attack, and thereby are inherently perceptible with recent advancements in S-HAR systems. We discover that this degradation stems from the gap between empirical and true risks during the optimization process of previous adversarial attacks. To address this issue, we propose an attack where adversarial motions are obtained without compromising their motion quality. To minimize the risk gap and preserve motion quality, we propose a distribution-based adversarial attack method without introducing noise-like perturbations. To faithfully evaluate the motion quality, we propose a new metric that aligns with human perception on real-world naturalness. Experiments have been conducted on the state-of-the-art S-HAR methods across two datasets, demonstrating the superiority of our method in both the attack success rate and the post-attack motion quality through qualitative and quantitative analyses. The success of our quality-preserving attack application and distribution-based method raises serious concerns about the robustness of action recognizers, highlighting the need for further enhancements in this domain.

AI.tldr: Distribution-based, quality-preserving adversarial attacks for skeletal action recognition; reduces empirical-true risk gap without noise-like perturbations; improves attack success and motion naturalness; raises robustness concerns across two datasets.
AI.motivation: Address perceptible artifacts from noise-like perturbations and the optimization risk-gap in prior attacks; enable effective adversarial examples that preserve motion quality and realism.
AI.method: Propose a distribution-based adversarial attack that avoids noise-like perturbations, minimizing the risk gap between empirical and true risks; introduces a human-perception-aligned motion naturalness metric for evaluation.
AI.result: Demonstrates superior attack success rate and preserved post-attack motion quality across state-of-the-art S-HAR methods on two datasets, supported by qualitative and quantitative analyses.
AI.conclusion: Quality-preserving, distribution-based attacks reveal robustness vulnerabilities in S-HAR systems and motivate further defense research and methodological refinements.

title: Iterating Toward Better Search: A Two-Agent Simulation Framework for Evaluating Agentic Search Architectures in E-Commerce
summary: We present a modular two-agent simulation framework for evaluating conversational shopping assistant architectures. An independent buyer agent, configured with personas, missions, and patience levels, is paired with an interchangeable responder that integrates with a real e-commerce search API. Holding the buyer constant across experiments enables controlled comparison of responder designs on identical scenarios. Using 2011 conversations across 14 persona buckets, we establish four empirical findings. First, rolling-window memory outperforms intent-extraction memory on all quality metrics while being 35% faster per query. Second, illustrating rapid evidence-driven iteration, a systematic failure analysis of a responder version enables targeted fixes that reduce failure and near-failure rates by 62% across the full dataset. Third, swapping the responder LLM backbone from Gemini~2.5 to Llama~3.3~70B costs 0.16--0.45 points despite identical architecture. Finally, we document systematic philosophical disagreement between frontier LLM judges: Gemini rewards process correctness while Claude demands concrete outcomes, despite using the same evaluation prompt.

AI.tldr: A modular two-agent simulation framework evaluates conversational shopping assistants by keeping buyer behavior constant; results show memory design matters, targeted failure analysis yields large improvements, backbone swaps incur modest costs, and evaluation is affected by judge philosophy.
AI.motivation: Enable controlled, repeatable comparison of responder architectures for conversational shopping assistants while isolating system-level effects from user behavior.
AI.method: Independent buyer agent (with configurable personas, missions, and patience) paired with an interchangeable responder connected to a real e-commerce search API. Experiments compare memory designs (rolling-window vs. intent-extraction) across 2011 conversations spanning 14 personas; perform systematic failure analysis to guide fixes; compare LLM backbones (Gemini 2.5 vs. Llama 3.3 70B) using identical architectures and prompts.
AI.result: Four findings: (1) rolling-window memory outperforms intent-extraction memory on all quality metrics and is 35% faster per query; (2) systematic failure analysis reduces failure/near-failure rates by 62% across the full dataset; (3) backbone swap costs 0.16–0.45 points despite identical architecture; (4) judges disagree philosophically: Gemini rewards process correctness while Claude emphasizes concrete outcomes, despite using the same evaluation prompt.
AI.conclusion: The framework enables controlled, rapid evaluation of conversational shopping assistants; memory strategy and targeted debugging substantially impact quality and speed; backbone choice has a modest quantitative effect; evaluation is influenced by judge philosophy, indicating need for multi-faceted assessment.

title: Proprioceptive-visual correspondence enables self-other distinction in humanoid robots
summary: Distinguishing self from others is a prerequisite for social intelligence, yet humanoid robots that increasingly share workspaces with humans still lack this ability. Here we show that a humanoid robot can learn self-other distinction from proprioceptive-visual correspondence, without any identity labels or kinematic models. Once established, this distinction bootstraps a predictive self-model that maps joint configurations to three-dimensional body occupancy, capturing how the robot's body changes with action. In multi-agent scenes involving humans or morphologically identical robots, the system reliably identifies itself, learns a 3D self-model, and supports downstream tasks including target reaching, collision-aware motion planning, and human-to-robot motion retargeting. Together, these results outline a route toward bodily self-representation in robots that act and coordinate alongside others in shared physical environments. Project page: https://euron-zc.github.io/humanoid-self-model/.

AI.tldr: A humanoid robot learns self–other distinction from proprioceptive-visual cues without identity labels or kinematic models, builds a predictive 3D self-model mapping joint configurations to body occupancy, enabling self-identification in multi-agent scenes and supporting tasks like reaching, collision-aware planning, and motion retargeting; demonstrates a route to bodily self-representation for cooperative robots.
AI.motivation: Distinguishing self from others in shared spaces is essential for social intelligence and safe collaboration; existing robots struggle with reference to self in presence of humans or morphologically identical agents.
AI.method: Self–other distinction learned via proprioceptive-visual correspondence; without identity labels or kinematic models; builds predictive self-model that maps joint configurations to 3D body occupancy; evaluated in multi-agent scenes with humans or identical robots; uses the self-model for downstream tasks (target reaching, collision-aware planning, retargeting).
AI.result: Reliable self-identification in multi-agent scenes; acquisition of a 3D self-model; improved performance on downstream tasks (reaching, planning, retargeting).
AI.conclusion: Presents a viable route to bodily self-representation enabling robots to act and coordinate with humans/robots in shared environments; future work could extend to more diverse morphologies and dynamic scenes.

title: Physics-Aware Auxiliary Losses Improve Out-of-Distribution Generalization of a GNN Synthesizability Filter
summary: Machine-learning drug-discovery pipelines increasingly rely on generative models that propose molecules far from the data used to train downstream synthesizability filters. Existing filters (SAScore, SCScore, RAscore, DeepSA) are purely statistical and degrade in exactly this out-of-distribution (OOD) regime. We ask whether cheap, closed-form physical priors, used as auxiliary supervision on a graph neural network (GNN), improve OOD generalization. We add two auxiliary losses to a GINE backbone: a topological complexity regression supervised by the Bertz index, and a strain-energy soft penalty supervised by MMFF94 force-field energy. On a 65,177-molecule corpus (HIV, Tox21, COCONUT) labeled by SAScore thresholds we reproduce a strong in-distribution baseline, then evaluate a 4-way ablation (baseline / +complexity / +strain / +both) on a single-source OOD split (train on drug-like HIV+Tox21, test on COCONUT natural products), repeated over 5 seeds with paired bootstrap confidence intervals. All three physics-aware variants give a small but statistically significant OOD improvement over the baseline (mean OOD AUC 0.9774): +complexity Delta = +0.0060 (95% CI [+0.0023, +0.0102]), +strain Delta = +0.0032 ([+0.0008, +0.0052]), +both Delta = +0.0066 ([+0.0038, +0.0093]); every interval excludes zero, and the combination is best. The variants are indistinguishable in-distribution, so the effect is visible only under OOD evaluation. We are explicit that the effects are modest, and we report a cautionary methodological finding: a single-seed version of this experiment produced a qualitatively different (non-monotone) story that did not survive multi-seed evaluation.

AI.tldr: Adding cheap physics-based priors as auxiliary losses on a GNN yields modest but statistically significant improvements in out-of-distribution synthesizability assessment (SAScore-based) for drug-like vs natural-product data; topological complexity (Bertz) and strain-energy (MMFF94) help, with best results when combined; benefits appear only OOD, not in-distribution; single-seed results can mislead.
AI.motivation: Improve OOD generalization of ML-driven synthesizability filters in drug discovery by incorporating fast, closed-form physical priors, addressing the failure of purely statistical filters under distribution shift.
AI.method: GINE-based graph neural network with two auxiliary losses: regression of Bertz topological complexity and a strain-energy penalty via MMFF94 energy. Trained on 65,177 molecules labeled by SAScore thresholds. Experimental design includes a 4-way ablation (baseline; +complexity; +strain; +both) evaluated on a single-source OOD split (train on HIV+Tox21, test on COCONUT natural products), with 5 seeds and bootstrap confidence intervals. Performance metric: AUC.
AI.result: All three physics-informed variants produced small but statistically significant improvements in OOD performance (mean OOD AUC 0.9774). Deltas: complexity +0.0060 (95% CI +[0.0023, 0.0102]); strain +0.0032 (CI +[0.0008, 0.0052]); both +0.0066 (CI +[0.0038, 0.0093]); all intervals exclude zero; combination best. In-distribution performance was indistinguishable from baseline.
AI.conclusion: Physics-informed auxiliary losses provide modest but robust improvements in OOD generalization for synthesizability scoring without sacrificing in-distribution performance. The study highlights the importance of multi-seed replication, as single-seed results can be non-monotone and misleading.

title: A Multi-Modal Framework with Cross-Subject Pseudo-Labeling and Semantic Alignment for Micro-Gesture Recognition
summary: Micro-gestures (MGs) are spontaneous and subtle body movements that frequently convey hidden human emotions. Recognizing MGs in untrimmed videos remains highly challenging due to their extremely low signal-to-noise ratio, severe long-tailed class distribution, and the inherent domain shift encountered in cross-subject evaluation scenarios. In this paper, we propose a comprehensive multi-modal framework for Track 1 of the 4th MiGA-IJCAI Challenge. To capture fine-grained representations, we design a saliency-guided multi-modal extraction pipeline integrating 68-keypoint skeleton joint coordinates, 3D heatmap volumes, and high-resolution RGB visual features. We introduce a gentle square-root smoothed weighting mechanism paired with an Orthogonal Semantic Embedding Loss to protect tail classes without compromising overall recognition capabilities. More importantly, to bridge the cross-subject generalization gap, we propose a Cross-Modal Pseudo-Labeling (CMPL) strategy for unsupervised domain adaptation, which significantly boosts single-modal robustness. A temperature-scaled soft-voting mechanism is finally utilized to alleviate overconfidence during late fusion. Extensive experiments demonstrate that our framework achieves a competitive F1-score of 68.13\%, securing the 4th place.

AI.tldr: A multi-modal framework for micro-gesture recognition in untrimmed videos using 68-keypoint skeletons, 3D heatmaps, and RGB features, with balanced learning via Square-root Smoothed Weights and Orthogonal Semantic Embedding Loss, plus Cross-Modal Pseudo-Labeling for unsupervised domain adaptation and temperature-scaled soft voting; achieves 68.13% F1 and 4th place on MiGA-IJCAI Track 1.
AI.motivation: Micro-gestures are subtle and easily overwhelmed by noise, exhibit severe long-tailed distributions, and suffer from cross-subject domain shift. A robust recognition system requires rich multi-modal representations and domain adaptation to generalize across subjects.
AI.method: A saliency-guided multi-modal extraction pipeline that fuses 68-keypoint skeleton joints, 3D heatmap volumes, and high-resolution RGB features. Introduces Square-root Smoothed Weighting to balance classes, and Orthogonal Semantic Embedding Loss to protect tail classes. Proposes Cross-Modal Pseudo-Labeling (CMPL) for unsupervised domain adaptation across subjects, and uses a temperature-scaled soft-voting mechanism for late fusion.
AI.result: Competitive F1-score of 68.13%, securing 4th place in Track 1 of the MiGA-IJCAI Challenge.
AI.conclusion: The framework demonstrates that combining multi-modal representations with targeted loss design and cross-subject adaptation yields strong performance for MG recognition in challenging, untrimmed videos. The approach highlights the importance of handling tail classes and domain shift for generalization, with room for further ablations and exploration on extra datasets.

title: MARS: Margin-Adversarial Risk-controlled Stopping for Parallel LLM Test-time Scaling
summary: Parallel test-time scaling samples many reasoning traces and majority-votes their answers, improving LLM accuracy but requiring traces to run to completion, incurring substantial computational overhead. We observe that probing partial traces at intermediate checkpoints can extract current answers without disrupting generation, revealing an evolving aggregate vote. Based on this observation, we introduce MARS, a margin-adversarial stopping rule that estimates which active traces are likely to change their answers and stops once the leader remains safe under a conservative bound on future vote movement. The rule separates two sources of uncertainty. It learns the trace-level switch probabilities that determine how much of the current margin is likely to be retained, while handling the harder question of where switching traces land through an adversarial bound calibrated from warmup traces. With true switch probabilities, MARS guarantees with high probability that the early-stopped answer matches the full-budget vote. In practice, a five-feature logistic model closely matches oracle switching behavior. Across three reasoning models and three competition-math benchmarks, MARS saves 25-47% of self-consistency tokens and 14-29% on top of DeepConf Online, a strong confidence-weighted baseline that already filters and truncates weak traces, while matching the accuracy of the corresponding full-budget baselines.

AI.tldr: MARS introduces a margin-adversarial stopping rule to dynamically stop reasoning traces during parallel self-consistency, achieving substantial computation savings while preserving accuracy.
AI.motivation: Reduce computation in parallel self-consistency for LLMs by stopping traces unlikely to change, without sacrificing the final vote accuracy.
AI.method: Uses intermediate checkpoint traces to estimate current margin and trace-switch probabilities; employs a conservative adversarial bound, calibrated from warmup traces, to bound future vote movement; trains a five-feature logistic model to approximate oracle switching behavior; provides probabilistic guarantees that early-stopped answers match full-budget votes.
AI.result: Gains 25–47% savings in self-consistency tokens and 14–29% additional savings atop DeepConf Online, while matching the accuracy of full-budget baselines across three reasoning models and three competition-math benchmarks.
AI.conclusion: MARS offers provable high-probability guarantees for early-stopped answers, delivering practical computational savings with minimal accuracy loss and broad applicability to diverse reasoning models and benchmarks.

title: WT-UMI: Tactile-based Whole-Body Manipulation via Force-Supervised Contact-Aware Planning
summary: Whole-body humanoid manipulation of bulky, deformable, and shared-load objects requires distributed contact sensing and explicit force regulation, yet most imitation policies treat contact force only implicitly. On the other hand, different demonstration sources provide complementary modalities with inherent trade-offs: human demonstrations capture natural contact forces but not robot-executable actions, while teleoperation directly records robot actions but with less natural force regulation. This paper presents \textbf{WT-UMI}, a wearable whole-body tactile interface worn by human operators or mounted on humanoids, providing accurate observations of tactile images, contact forces, and end-effector poses across both human demonstration and humanoid teleoperation modes. We introduce a force-conditioned target-pose correction module that converts measured human poses into contact-aware robot targets by learning corrections from teleoperation data. To leverage the natural force interaction in human data, we propose a force-supervised planner that predicts end-effector pose chunks and contact-force trajectories. The predicted contact force serves as the reference for a tactile-based admittance controller. Across five contact-rich tasks spanning deformable objects, bulky rigid objects, and human--humanoid collaboration, WT-UMI improves success rate and reduces contact-position tracking error over four policy baselines. Our project page is available at https://wt-umi.github.io/WTUMI/.

AI.tldr: WT-UMI is a wearable whole-body tactile interface for human demonstration and humanoid teleoperation enabling force-aware manipulation via a force-conditioned target-pose correction and a force-supervised planner, improving success rates and reducing contact-position error across five tasks.
AI.motivation: Addresses the need for distributed contact sensing and explicit force regulation in whole-body manipulation of bulky, deformable, and shared-load objects, bridging the gap between human natural force data and robot-executable actions.
AI.method: Introduces WT-UMI, a wearable interface providing tactile images, contact forces, and end-effector poses across both demonstration and teleoperation modes. Adds a force-conditioned target-pose correction module learned from teleoperation data to map human poses to contact-aware robot targets. Proposes a force-supervised planner that predicts end-effector pose chunks and contact-force trajectories, with a tactile-based admittance controller using predicted contact forces.
AI.result: Evaluated on five contact-rich tasks spanning deformable objects, bulky rigid objects, and human–humanoid collaboration. WT-UMI achieves higher success rates and lower contact-position tracking error than four baselines.
AI.conclusion: WT-UMI demonstrates effective integration of human and teleoperation data for force-aware manipulation through a wearable tactile interface and associated planning/control strategies, with a public project page for reference.

title: Physics-Informed Neural Networks for Chemotherapy Pharmacokinetics: Benchmarking the Clinical Estimator and Exposing Parameter Identifiability
summary:
Physics-Informed Neural Networks (PINNs) are an attractive tool for partial-observation problems in biology, where the governing dynamics are known but some compartments cannot be measured. Chemotherapy pharmacokinetics (PK) is a clean instance: drug concentration in plasma is routinely measured, but concentration in tissue -- which determines tumour kill and off-target toxicity -- is not. We benchmark a PINN against the standard clinical baseline (nonlinear least-squares on the analytical biexponential plasma solution, hereafter NLS) and a physics-agnostic neural baseline (a data-only MLP) on two PK problems. On the linear two-compartment problem, NLS is near-optimal; the PINN matches it to within a small constant factor while also producing the tissue curve in a single training pass, whereas the data-only MLP fails on tissue by roughly 10x. On a Michaelis-Menten extension (saturable elimination), the biexponential closed form no longer exists, so NLS is mis-specified and silently returns meaningless rate constants. The PINN instead exposes a deeper fact: the Michaelis-Menten two-compartment model is non-identifiable from plasma alone, and the PINN reports this honestly by converging to a basin with k12 -> 0. Adding two sparse tissue observations largely resolves identifiability: across five seeds the PINN recovers k21 to within 1% of truth and Vmax, Km to within one standard-deviation bar, while k12 moves in the correct direction (0.02 -> 0.82) but remains ~2 sigma below truth -- a recovery the closed-form NLS estimator cannot attempt at all, because its biexponential ansatz describes only plasma. Our claim is not that PINNs beat NLS. It is that PINNs offer a uniform recipe that ties the textbook estimator on the textbook problem, exposes structural identifiability that
  the textbook estimator hides, and absorbs heterogeneous measurements within a single loss.

AI.tldr: PINNs provide a uniform framework that matches classical estimators on linear PK problems, exposes identifiability issues in nonlinear PK with saturable elimination, and can integrate tissue data and sparse measurements where traditional NLS fails.
AI.motivation: To assess whether PINNs can reconstruct unobserved tissue concentrations in pharmacokinetics, stabilize estimations under partial observability, and reveal structural identifiability not captured by standard estimators.
AI.method: Compare a physics-informed neural network (PINN) against nonlinear least-squares (NLS) on a biexponential linear two-compartment PK model and a Michaelis–Menten extension; include a data-only MLP baseline; evaluate with and without sparse tissue observations; analyze identifiability of rate constants and PK parameters.
AI.result: In the linear case, NLS nearly optimal; PINN matches within a small factor and recovers tissue curve in one training pass; MLP fails on tissue. In the Michaelis–Menten case, NLS mis-specifies parameters due to non-existent biexponential solution; PINN reveals non-identifiability from plasma alone; with two sparse tissue observations, PINN recovers k21 and Vmax/Km accurately; k12 moves in correct direction but remains somewhat biased; NLS cannot address this due to model mis-specification.
AI.conclusion: PINNs do not necessarily beat NLS, but provide a uniform framework that (i) matches textbook estimators on textbook problems, (ii) uncovers structural identifiability issues that estimators based on closed-form plasma solutions hide, and (iii) robustly absorbs heterogeneous measurements within a single loss.

title: GeoCFNet: Geometry-Aware Confidence Field Network for Robot-Assisted Endoscopic Submucosal Dissection
summary: Advanced surgical robotics has made robot-assisted endoscopic submucosal dissection (ESD) a promising approach for the en-bloc resection of large lesions, with the potential to reduce recurrence and improve long-term outcomes. However, the technical complexity and risk of complications in ESD demand stable and precise visual guidance to maintain an accurate dissection corridor and a safe tissue margin. Dense confidence fields provide an effective representation for this purpose by describing both the preferred dissection region and its spatial transition to surrounding tissue. However, reliable confidence field estimation remains challenging in dynamic endoscopic scenes due to smoke, specular highlights, tissue deformation, weak texture, and the thin geometric structure of the target region. To address these challenges, we formulate dissection guidance as a geometry-aware confidence field estimation problem and propose GeoCFNet, a geometry-aware confidence field network built on a pretrained DINOv3 backbone. GeoCFNet integrates a Token-Differentiated Fusion module to aggregate class-token context with dense patch representations, a SegFormer decoder for confidence regression, and Geometry-Aware Spatial Regularization (GASR) to preserve spatial coherence and local geometric transitions. Experimental results show that GeoCFNet achieves RMSE 0.0480, PSNR 27.1995, SSIM 0.3397, and CC 0.2466, indicating accurate and geometrically stable confidence field estimation for robot-assisted ESD guidance.

AI.tldr: GeoCFNet: a geometry-aware confidence field network for guiding robot-assisted ESD, leveraging a DINOv3 backbone with Token-Differentiated Fusion, SegFormer decoder, and Geometry-Aware Spatial Regularization to produce stable, geometrically coherent dissection guidance fields.
AI.motivation: Provide stable, precise visual guidance for en-bloc ESD in dynamic endoscopic scenes where smoke, highlights, deformation, weak texture, and thin geometry degrade traditional confidence-field estimation.
AI.method: Use a pretrained DINOv3 backbone. Introduce Token-Differentiated Fusion (TDF) to merge class-token context with dense patch features. Employ SegFormer decoder for confidence regression. Apply Geometry-Aware Spatial Regularization (GASR) to enforce spatial coherence and preserve local geometric transitions.
AI.result: Quantitative evaluation yields RMSE 0.0480, PSNR 27.1995, SSIM 0.3397, and CC 0.2466, indicating accurate and geometrically stable confidence-field estimation for robot-assisted ESD guidance.
AI.conclusion: GeoCFNet demonstrates effective geometry-aware confidence-field estimation that can enhance dissection guidance in robot-assisted ESD; the approach shows promise for safer, more reliable navigation of dissection corridors, with potential follow-up on real-time deployment and ablation studies.

title: PRISMR: Overcoming Parse Collapse in Multimodal Listwise Ranking via Parameterized Representation Internalization
summary:
Generative listwise ranking with Large Multimodal Models (LMMs) aims to capture global list context in a single forward pass, but
  its effectiveness degrades in long-context multimodal scenarios. We identify a recurring failure mode, parse collapse, where the
  autoregressive decoder produces fluent yet incomplete rankings by silently omitting candidates and terminating early. This
  failure stems from limited context utilization rather than simple formatting mistakes, making prompt engineering and constrained
  decoding insufficient. We propose PRISMR (Parameterized Representation Internalization for Semantic Multimodal Ranking), a
  framework that replaces transient in-context list processing with parametric structural conditioning. PRISMR uses a lightweight
  hypernetwork to encode multimodal candidates in parallel and generate item-specific LoRA weights, which are synthesized into an
  instance-specific adapter for a LMM. This paradigm enables more robust internalization of list structure while preserving the
  base model. We further introduce a large-scale multimodal review-ranking benchmark for evaluation. Experiments demonstrate that
  PRISMR substantially reduces parse collapse, improves listwise ranking performance, and transfers effectively across domains and
  instruction-tuned backbones.

AI.tldr: PRISMR introduces a parametric adapter approach to long-context multimodal listwise ranking to mitigate parse collapse in LMMs, using a hypernetwork to generate item-specific LoRA weights for an instance-specific adapter, enabling robust internalization of list structure and better cross-domain performance; accompanied by a large multimodal review-ranking benchmark.
AI.motivation: Long-context generative listwise ranking with large multimodal models suffers from parse collapse where the decoder omits candidates and terminates early; simple prompt engineering or decoding constraints are insufficient.
AI.method: Train and apply a lightweight hypernetwork that encodes multimodal candidates in parallel and outputs per-item LoRA weights. These weights are synthesized into an instance-specific adapter for a base LMM, replacing transient in-context processing with parametric structural conditioning. Evaluation is conducted on a large-scale multimodal review-ranking benchmark across domains and instruction-tuned backbones.
AI.result: PRISMR substantially reduces parse collapse, improves listwise ranking performance, and transfers across domains and instruction-tuned backbones.
AI.conclusion: Parametric structural conditioning via per-item LoRA adapters provides robust internalization of list structure in LMMs, preserving the base model while enhancing long-context multimodal ranking; the benchmark demonstrates broad applicability.

title: Humor Style Drives Laughter, Topic Shapes Acceptability: Evaluating Bilingual Personal and Political Robot-Delivered AI Jokes
comment: Accepted in the 35th IEEE International Conference on Robot and Human Interactive Communication (RO-MAN 2026), Kitakyushu, Fukuoka, Japan
summary: Humor plays a central role in human social relationships, and recent advances in computational humor create new opportunities for integrating humor into human-robot interaction (HRI). While large language models (LLMs) can generate diverse forms of humor, it remains unclear how humor style, joke content, and language preference shape perceptions of robot-delivered humor in group settings. In this exploratory study, we employed a mixed factorial design in which participants evaluated AI-generated jokes delivered by a robot in a university classroom. We examined the effects of humor type (Affiliative, Self-Enhancing, Aggressive, Self-Defeating) and joke content (person-related vs. political) on perceived funniness and appropriateness, as well as preferred language. Results show that humor type significantly influences funniness, with Aggressive and Affiliative humor rated higher, while joke content primarily affects appropriateness, with person-related jokes preferred over political ones. Language preference was shaped by both joke content and participants' self-reported fluency and humor practices.

AI.tldr: A mixed-factorial study finds humor style, content, and language choice affect perceptions of robot-delivered jokes; aggression and affiliative humor yield higher funniness, person-related content yields higher appropriateness; language preference depends on content and individual factors.
AI.motivation: To understand how humor characteristics interact with group HRI and language preferences when robots use LLM-generated jokes.
AI.method: Mixed factorial experimental design; participants in a university classroom evaluated AI-generated jokes delivered by a robot; manipulated humor type (Affiliative, Self-Enhancing, Aggressive, Self-Defeating) and content (person-related vs political); outcomes measured: perceived funniness and appropriateness; language preference; also collected self-reported language fluency and humor practices.
AI.result: Humor type significantly influences funniness (Aggressive and Affiliative rated higher). Content affects appropriateness (person-related > political). Language preference influenced by content and by self-reported fluency and humor practices.
AI.conclusion: For HRI design, tailor humor style to maximize funniness; content should be chosen to maintain appropriateness; language choice should account for audience fluency and personal humor habits; results highlight trade-offs in robot humor deployment in group contexts.

title: A Zero-shot Generalized Graph Anomaly Detection Framework via Node Reconstruction
summary: Cross-domain graph anomaly detection (GAD) aims to identify abnormal nodes in unseen target graphs, showing strong potential in real-world applications with heterogeneous graph data. However, existing methods often depend on dataset-specific feature semantics and structural patterns, which limits their ability to generalize across different domains. To address this challenge, we propose AlignGAD, a zero-shot generalized graph anomaly detection framework. Our framework is built upon three key components: a Global Unification Module that aligns heterogeneous node features and normalizes graph signals in the spectral domain; a Clustering Module that constructs cluster-aware graph views to capture group-level abnormal patterns; and a Node Discrepancy Scoring Module that measures reconstruction discrepancy and aggregates anomaly evidence from different graph views. Experiments on multiple real-world datasets demonstrate the effectiveness of AlignGAD under the zero-shot GAD setting.

AI.tldr: Zero-shot generalized graph anomaly detection method AlignGAD using global feature alignment, cluster-aware views, and node-discrepancy scoring to detect anomalies across unseen target graphs.
AI.motivation: Generalization across heterogeneous graphs and cross-domain anomaly patterns; standard GAD methods rely on domain-specific semantics, hindering cross-domain performance.
AI.method: Global Unification Module aligns heterogeneous node features and normalizes graph signals in the spectral domain; Clustering Module constructs cluster-aware graph views to capture group-level anomalies; Node Discrepancy Scoring Module combines reconstruction discrepancy from multiple views to score anomalies.
AI.result: Evaluations on real-world datasets show improved anomaly detection under zero-shot GAD setting, demonstrating robustness to domain shift.
AI.conclusion: AlignGAD enables zero-shot cross-domain GAD by aligning features, exploiting cluster structure, and aggregating multi-view evidence; potential for broad applicability in heterogeneous graph data.

title: SAM-Deep-EIoU: Selective Mask Propagation for Multi-Object Tracking
summary: Multi-object tracking has a heavy-tailed difficulty distribution: most frames are easy for a lightweight base tracker, while a small fraction are intrinsically hard. Video object segmentation (VOS) models can often preserve identity through the hard frames where the base tracker fails, but they are much more expensive in compute and memory. We propose selective mask propagation, a tracking algorithm that dispatches from a base tracker to a VOS model only on windows where an assignment-uncertainty signal fires. The base tracker's output is modified only when the VOS model makes a confident prediction that contradicts the base tracker's identity assignment; weak or inconclusive predictions preserve the base output. The method is training-free, treats both the base tracker and the VOS model as black boxes, and can benefit from replacing the VOS component with a more capable model. On DanceTrack, selective mask propagation improves three different base trackers. On SportsMOT, where identity preservation is central to sports analytics, SAM3-Deep-EIoU with global track association achieves state-of-the-art performance on the benchmark with 86.8 HOTA.

AI.tldr: Introduces selective mask propagation for multi-object tracking, using a VOS model only on uncertain windows; training-free and black-box integration; improves base trackers on DanceTrack and achieves state-of-the-art HOTA on SportsMOT (86.8).
AI.motivation: Multi-object tracking exhibits a heavy-tailed difficulty distribution: most frames are easy for a lightweight base tracker, while a small fraction are intrinsically hard. VOS models can preserve identity through hard frames but are computationally expensive. A selective approach aims to combine the efficiency of base trackers with the accuracy of VOS where needed.
AI.method: A selective mask propagation mechanism dispatches from a base tracker to a VOS model only on windows where an assignment-uncertainty signal fires. The base tracker's output is updated only if the VOS model makes a confident prediction that contradicts the base identity assignment; weak or inconclusive VOS predictions leave the base output unchanged. The method is training-free, treats both components as black boxes, and is compatible with replacing the VOS component with a more capable model.
AI.result: Empirical gains on benchmarks: on DanceTrack, selective mask propagation improves three different base trackers. On SportsMOT, using SAM3-Deep-EIoU with global track association achieves state-of-the-art performance with 86.8 HOTA.
AI.conclusion: Selective mask propagation provides an efficient, model-agnostic framework that leverages VOS only when necessary to preserve identity, offering accuracy gains with lower compute and memory. It remains compatible with improved VOS models and demonstrates cross-dataset effectiveness.

title: Learning What to Remember: A Cognitively Grounded Multi-Factor Value Model for Agentic Memory
summary: Long-running LLM agents accumulate interaction histories far larger than any context window, forcing a standing decision: what to encode deeply, what to forget, and what to retrieve under a fixed memory budget. Production systems answer with semantic similarity or recency -- both mis-specified for the forgetting decision, which is made at consolidation time before the future query is known. We propose a multi-factor memory value function V(m)=\sum_i w_i f_i(m) over seven interpretable factors (emotional intensity, goal relevance, value alignment, self/user relevance, task utility, reliability, and usage history) drawn from cognitive psychology, whose weights are learned from a downstream objective by a gradient-free optimiser, and whose single scalar uniformly controls encoding depth, forget risk, and retrieval rank. We make a methodological point: on LongMemEval, scoring goal relevance against the held-out evaluation question saturates gold-evidence retention at \approx 0.98 -- this measures retrieval, not forgetting. In the realistic blind regime, a learned multi-factor value retains 0.770 \pm 0.011 of gold evidence across 479 usable cases, versus 0.657 for uniform weights, 0.518 for the best single factor, and 0.368 for recency; every paired gap's 95% bootstrap CI is above zero, and a neural network over the same factors ties the linear model. The learned weights are interpretable -- reliability, emotional intensity, and self/user relevance dominate, while query-time goal similarity is correctly down-weighted for the forgetting decision. A controlled synthetic task with planted confounds confirms the learner recovers a separating weighting (1.00 retention) where uniform weighting fails (0.62). The substrate is open-source; all experiments run on a single CPU with no API calls.

AI.tldr: A learned, multi-factor memory value function V(m)=sum_i w_i f_i(m) governs encoding/forgetting/retrieval for long-running LLMs; seven factors inspired by cognitive psychology; weights learned via gradient-free optimization; outperforms baselines on LongMemEval and in a synthetic task; open-source; CPU-only experiments.
AI.motivation: To address memory management under fixed budgets for long-running LLM agents, where forgetting decisions are made at consolidation time before future queries are known, making semantic similarity or recency alone insufficient.
AI.method: Define V(m)=sum_i w_i f_i(m) over seven interpretable factors (emotional intensity, goal relevance, value alignment, self/user relevance, task utility, reliability, usage history). Learn weights w_i with a gradient-free optimizer using a downstream objective. Evaluate on LongMemEval across 479 usable cases, compare to uniform weights and the best single-factor model; also include a controlled synthetic task with planted confounds. Report gold-evidence retention and retrieval performance, bootstrap CIs, and compare to a neural-network surrogate over the same factors. Substrate is open-source; experiments run on a single CPU with no API calls.
AI.result: Learnt weights achieve 0.770 ± 0.011 retention of gold evidence across 479 cases, versus 0.657 for uniform weights, 0.518 for the best single factor, and 0.368 for recency. Paired 95% bootstrap CIs are above zero. A neural network over the same factors ties the linear model. In blind regime, the synthetic task yields 1.00 retention with learned weights vs 0.62 with uniform. Reliability, emotional intensity, and self/user relevance dominate; goal similarity is down-weighted for forgetting decisions. The approach is open-source and CPU-based, with no API calls.
AI.conclusion: A multi-factor, interpretable memory-value function with gradient-free weight learning can effectively govern encoding depth, forgetting, and retrieval under memory constraints for long-running LLM agents, yielding meaningful gains over baselines and offering interpretability of the learned priorities. The results underscore the importance of reliability and relevance signals, and demonstrate reproducibility on CPU-only hardware.

title: See Selectively, Act Adaptively: Dual-Level Structural Decomposition for Bimanual Robot Manipulation
summary: In bimanual robotic manipulation, task-relevant visual information varies with the task stage and context, while the interaction of the two arms shifts between independent and coordinated modes, making policy learning challenging. However, existing monolithic Vision-Language-Action (VLA) policies process diverse visual inputs and interaction patterns through a single shared representation and action generation pathway, often failing to separately account for visual relevance and bimanual interaction structure. To address this issue, we propose a bimanual manipulation VLA framework based on Dual-Level Structural Decomposition. The View-Selective Visual Router dynamically adjusts wrist-view contributions to emphasize relevant visual cues, while the Interaction-Aware Action Mixture-of-Experts (MoE) decomposes action generation into coordinated and arm-wise pathways to adapt to varying bimanual interaction modes. We evaluate the proposed method on six simulated bimanual manipulation tasks in RoboTwin 2.0 and three long-horizon real-world tasks. Our model improves the overall average success rate over a monolithic baseline by 27.7% in simulation and 43.3% in real-world evaluation, while consistently outperforming single-module variants across both settings. These results demonstrate that jointly considering selective visual processing and explicit decomposition of bimanual interaction structures provides an effective inductive bias for robust bimanual manipulation.

AI.tldr: A dual-level decomposition approach with a View-Selective Visual Router and an Interaction-Aware MoE enhances bimanual manipulation by separating visual relevance from interaction structure, outperforming monolithic VLA policies in both simulation and real-world tasks.
AI.motivation: In bimanual manipulation, task-relevant visual information changes with task stage/context and inter-arm interaction shifts between independent and coordinated modes. Monolithic Vision-Language-Action (VLA) policies struggle to account for these varying visual cues and interaction patterns, limiting policy robustness.
AI.method: Introduce a dual-level structural decomposition: (1) View-Selective Visual Router dynamically weighs wrist-view contributions to emphasize task-relevant visual cues; (2) Interaction-Aware Action MoE splits action generation into coordinated and arm-wise pathways, adapting to different bimanual interaction modes. Evaluate on six simulated bimanual tasks in RoboTwin 2.0 and three long-horizon real-world tasks.
AI.result: The proposed framework yields an average improvement of 27.7% in success rate over a monolithic baseline in simulation and 43.3% in real-world tests, while consistently outperforming single-module variants across both settings.
AI.conclusion: Jointly leveraging selective visual processing and explicit decomposition of bimanual interaction structure provides a strong inductive bias for robust bimanual manipulation and demonstrates superior transfer from simulation to real-world tasks.

title: Fed-FBD: Federated Functional Block Diversification for Isolation, Privacy, and Surgical Unlearning
summary: Federated learning (FL) enables collaborative model training without sharing raw patient data, but standard approaches such as FedAvg treat each client as a black box and provide no mechanism for isolating an adversarial contributor, auditing per-client influence, or honoring a departed participant's right to be forgotten. We present Fed-FBD (Federated Functional Block Diversification), a modular federated architecture that decomposes a ResNet backbone into six functional blocks (the stem, four residual groups, and the classification head) and maintains a warehouse of N color variants, each assembled from independently tracked and contributor-stamped blocks. Fed-FBD provides three capabilities absent in FedAvg: (i) architecturally guaranteed block-level isolation, so that an adversarial or mislabelled client cannot contaminate the clean colous; (ii) privacy-by-design, where membership inference advantage is already indistinguishable from chance before any privacy mechanism is applied; and (iii) surgical machine unlearning of a departed participant's contribution at sub-second cost and without retraining. Experiments on six MedMNIST-2D datasets, PathMNIST at 224x224, and CIFAR-10 show that Fed-FBD trades a modest 0.3%-3.1% IID accuracy gap on the adequately sized datasets for these guarantees, remains within 0.8%-4.0% of FedAvg at Dirichlet alpha=1.0 on three of four datasets, and confines all six adversarial attacks we study to the poisoned client's own blocks with at most +/-0.01 AUC drift on the clean colors.

AI.tldr: Fed-FBD introduces a modular, block-decomposed federated CNN architecture that achieves block-level isolation, privacy-by-design, and fast unlearning, with only modest accuracy loss and strong robustness to adversarial attacks in medical imaging datasets.
AI.motivation: To address FedAvg's limitations—no per-client isolation, no auditing of per-client influence, and no efficient forgetfulness—in privacy-sensitive FL scenarios (e.g., medical imaging).
AI.method: Decompose a ResNet backbone into six functional blocks (stem, four residual groups, classification head). Maintain a warehouse of N color variants assembled from independently tracked, contributor-stamped blocks. Assemble color variants to form models; enable block-level isolation, privacy guarantees, and sub-second unlearning without retraining.
AI.result: Accuracy gap under IID: 0.3%–3.1% on adequately sized datasets. On Dirichlet(alpha=1.0) CIFAR-like splits, performance within 0.8%–4.0% of FedAvg on three of four datasets. All six adversarial attacks affect only the poisoned client’s blocks, with at most ±0.01 AUC drift on clean colors.
AI.conclusion: Fed-FBD delivers architecturally enforced block-level isolation, privacy-by-design, and rapid unlearning, with modest accuracy trade-offs and robust defense against adversarial contributors across multiple medical-imaging datasets.

title: TetherCache: Stabilizing Autoregressive Long-Form Video Generation with Gated Recall and Trusted Alignment
summary: Autoregressive video diffusion models provide a natural formulation for streaming and variable-length video generation by conditioning newly generated frames on previously generated content. However, extending these models to minute-level generation remains challenging: the limited KV-cache budget prevents the model from retaining the full history, while repeatedly conditioning on self-generated frames induces a context distribution shift that accumulates over time, leading to visual artifacts, quality degradation, and temporal drift. In this paper, we propose TetherCache, a training-free and plug-and-play cache management strategy for drift-resistant long video generation. TetherCache organizes the cache into sink, memory, and recent regions, and introduces two complementary mechanisms. First, GRAB (Gated Recall with Attention-Diversity Balancing) selects long-range memory frames using a gated score that combines attention-based relevance with temporal diversity, preserving informative yet diverse historical context under a fixed cache budget. Second, TAME (Trusted Alignment via Memory Editing) lightly edits newly recalled memory tokens by aligning their statistics to a trusted context distribution, reducing the pollution caused by drifted historical features. Built on Self-Forcing, TetherCache consistently improves long-video generation quality on VBench-Long across 30s, 60s, and 240s settings. In particular, for 240s generation, it substantially improves overall and semantic scores while reducing quality drift from 7.84 to 1.33, demonstrating its effectiveness for stable long-horizon autoregressive video diffusion.

AI.tldr: TetherCache is a training-free, plug-and-play cache management framework for long-horizon autoregressive video diffusion. It combines a GRAB memory selection mechanism and TAME memory editing to mitigate drift, yielding substantial quality and semantic improvements for long videos (up to 240s) on VBench-Long.
AI.motivation: Autoregressive video diffusion struggles with long sequences due to limited KV-cache preventing full historical retention and drift from self-generated frames. This leads to artifacts, quality degradation, and temporal drift over time. A training-free solution that stabilizes long-horizon generation is needed.
AI.method: TetherCache partitions the cache into sink, memory, and recent regions. GRAB (Gated Recall with Attention-Diversity Balancing) selects long-range memory frames using a gated score combining attention relevance and temporal diversity under a fixed cache budget. TAME (Trusted Alignment via Memory Editing) lightly edits recalled memory tokens by aligning their statistics to a trusted context distribution, reducing drift pollution. The approach is built on Self-Forcing and is plug-and-play (training-free).
AI.result: On VBench-Long, TetherCache yields consistent improvements for 30s, 60s, and 240s generation. At 240s, overall and semantic scores improve substantially, while quality drift decreases from 7.84 to 1.33.
AI.conclusion: TetherCache provides a stable, training-free solution for long-horizon autoregressive video diffusion, significantly reducing drift and artifacts and enabling higher-quality long video generation.

title: OpenMedQ: Broad Open Pretraining for Medical Vision-Language Models
comment: Medical Imaging with Deep Learning (MIDL) 2026, Short Paper Track
summary: We present OpenMedQ, a medical vision-language model pretrained on the broadest fully-open medical mix to date: 14 datasets totaling ~3.35M pretraining samples spanning pathology, radiology, microscopy, and text-only clinical QA.
