OpenMedQ reaches state-of-the-art BLEU-1 on PathVQA (75.9), beating Med-PaLM M variants up to 562B parameters (~80x larger), and matches the best reported VQA-MED BLEU-1 (64.5). Its vision encoder, transferred to 8 unseen medical classification benchmarks under an identical downstream recipe, obtains the highest average macro-F1 (0.757) among BiomedCLIP (0.745), PMC-CLIP (0.745), PubMedCLIP (0.746), and a from-scratch baseline (0.616). We release our code and an interactive demo is publicly available as a reproducible baseline for the community.

AI.tldr: OpenMedQ is a broad, open medical vision–language model pretrained on 14 diverse datasets (~3.35M samples) achieving state-of-the-art BLEU-1 on PathVQA and strong macro-F1 on unseen medical classification benchmarks, with code and a public demo released.
AI.motivation: Address the scarcity of fully open, large-scale medical vision–language models by aggregating diverse open medical data across pathology, radiology, microscopy, and clinical QA to enable strong performance and reproducibility.
AI.method: Pretrain on 14 datasets totaling ~3.35M samples across pathology, radiology, microscopy, and text-only clinical QA. Evaluate on PathVQA using BLEU-1; compare to Med-PaLM M variants up to 562B parameters. Transfer the vision encoder to 8 unseen medical classification benchmarks under an identical downstream recipe and compare macro-F1 with BiomedCLIP, PMC-CLIP, PubMedCLIP, and a from-scratch baseline. Release code and provide an interactive demo as a reproducible baseline.
AI.result: OpenMedQ achieves BLEU-1 of 75.9 on PathVQA, surpassing Med-PaLM M variants up to ~80x larger, and matches the best reported VQA-MED BLEU-1 of 64.5. Its vision encoder, when transferred to 8 unseen medical benchmarks, attains an average macro-F1 of 0.757, higher than BiomedCLIP (0.745), PMC-CLIP (0.745), PubMedCLIP (0.746), and a scratch baseline (0.616).
AI.conclusion: A fully open, moderately sized medical V-L model can outperform larger, closed models on core medical vision–language tasks; provides reproducible results and a publicly available demo to foster open research.

title: EMG-Based Adaptation of Anisotropic Virtual Fixtures for Robot-Assisted Surgical Resection and Dissection
summary: In this paper, we address the development of an adaptive assistance system for robot-assisted laparoscopic surgery, specifically for delicate tasks such as Resection and Dissection. Even if Virtual Fixtures offer significant advantages for guiding a surgeon's movements, conventional Virtual Fixtures are often defined by fixed geometries, lacking the flexibility to adapt to the surgical workflow or the surgeon's immediate intent. To address these limitations, we propose a novel framework for an adaptive and anisotropic virtual fixture. In addition, we introduce an intuitive control interface that modulates the fixture's geometry in real-time based on the surgeon's intent, inferred from EMG signals. This approach allows the surgeon to dynamically expand or disengage the constraint by contracting their forearm muscles, enabling seamless transitions between precise guided motion and free repositioning of the tool. Experimental results from a pilot user study, based on a standardized surgical training task, demonstrate the effectiveness of the proposed method. The system showed significant improvements in task accuracy and movement consistency, alongside a reduction in perceived cognitive load, effort, and frustration.

AI.tldr: Adaptive anisotropic virtual fixtures controlled by EMG-based intent enable flexible guidance in robot-assisted laparoscopy, improving accuracy and reducing cognitive load in a pilot study.
AI.motivation: Conventional fixed virtual fixtures limit adaptability to surgical workflow and surgeon intent, hindering seamless transition between guided and free motion.
AI.method: Develop an adaptive anisotropic virtual fixture framework and an intuitive EMG-based control interface that dynamically modulates fixture geometry in real time based on inferred surgeon intent (forearm muscle contraction). Validate with a pilot user study on a standardized surgical training task.
AI.result: Significant improvements in task accuracy and movement consistency; reductions in perceived cognitive load, effort, and frustration.
AI.conclusion: The proposed adaptive, EMG-driven VF framework enhances flexibility and user experience in robot-assisted laparoscopic tasks, enabling smooth transitions between guided precision and free repositioning; promising for broader surgical robotics deployment.

title: How Useful is Causal Invariance for Domain Adaptation in Finite-Sample Settings?
summary:
Machine learning models often degrade when they are deployed on a target distribution that differs from the source distributions they were trained on. Recent work in causality-based domain generalization has shown how shared causal structure between domains can induce invariant predictors, e.g., models on a subset of features which have stable risk across structured domain shifts. However, the extent to which such population-level causal invariances can lead to gains in finite-sample settings remains underexplored. In particular, in practice we often have access to a few labeled target samples, a setting called supervised domain adaptation (sDA). In this paper, we explore when (full or partial) causal knowledge can provably improve supervised domain adaptation.
  As a first step, we study linear regression, where full or partial causal knowledge specifies a collection of invariant or possibly invariant feature subsets, each yielding a source-trained candidate predictor. We derive matching upper and lower bounds showing that finite-sample gains are governed by the target-risk margins separating the candidates, together with the finite-source estimation error. When these margins are sufficiently large relative to $n_Q$, an adaptive aggregation procedure can match the best candidate predictor while avoiding negative transfer relative to target-only learning. On the other hand, when the margins are too small, no algorithm can reliably exploit the candidate collection to obtain faster finite-sample rates. We further connect these margins to structural shift magnitude in linear SCMs and validate the theory on real-world causal benchmarks.

AI.tldr: In supervised domain adaptation with linear regression, incorporating full or partial causal knowledge that yields invariant feature subsets allows combining multiple source-trained predictors. Finite-sample gains depend on target-risk margins between candidates and source estimation error. An adaptive aggregation can match the best candidate and avoid negative transfer when margins are large; when margins are too small, no method can surpass target-only learning. Margins relate to structural shift magnitude in linear SCMs; validated on real benchmarks.
AI.motivation: Domain shift degrades models trained on source distributions. Causality-based domain generalization seeks invariant predictors across domains by exploiting shared causal structure. The paper asks when such causal invariances provide finite-sample gains in supervised domain adaptation (sDA) with a few labeled target samples.
AI.method: Analyze linear regression under partial/full causal knowledge that defines a collection of invariant or possibly invariant feature subsets, each giving a source-trained predictor. Derive matching upper and lower bounds on finite-sample gains, governed by target-risk margins between candidates and the source estimation error. Propose an adaptive aggregation procedure over candidates that can attain the best predictor while avoiding negative transfer. Relate margins to structural shift magnitude in linear SCMs and validate theoretically and empirically on real-world causal benchmarks.
AI.result: Finite-sample gains are controlled by target-risk margins between candidate predictors and the finite-source estimation error. If margins are large relative to the target sample size n_Q, the adaptive aggregation matches the best candidate and avoids negative transfer compared to target-only learning. If margins are too small, no algorithm can reliably exploit the candidate collection to achieve faster finite-sample rates. The margins are connected to the magnitude of structural shifts in linear SCMs, and results are validated on real-world causal benchmarks.
AI.conclusion: Causal knowledge can yield finite-sample benefits in sDA, but such gains depend on the separability (margins) between candidate predictors and estimation error. The work characterizes when adaptation helps versus when it cannot provide faster rates, and links theoretical margins to SCM shift magnitudes, with empirical validation.

title: SeamEdit: A Black-Box VLM-Agnostic Pipeline for Large-Image Semantic Editing
summary: Semantic region editing for large images must satisfy two requirements at the same time: high generative quality and natural integration with surrounding content. Some related methods rely on white-box models and leave the strong generation capability of closed-source models underexplored. Directly applying closed-source models to tiled editing, however, introduces several failure modes: semantic deformation, canvas-level alignment drift, and visible seam artifacts. This paper presents SeamEdit, a training-free and model-agnostic pipeline that treats any VLM with inpainting capability as a black-box oracle. SeamEdit mitigates these issues through a five-stage post-hoc pipeline: overlay-based tile decomposition, black-box VLM inpainting, geometric and color-consistency correction, seam-risk-based multi-candidate ranking, and dynamic-programming curved seam fusion. The pipeline reduces seam visibility and supports semantic modification of arbitrary tile regions.

AI.tldr: Training-free, model-agnostic SeamEdit enables seamless, semantic editing of large images by treating any VLM with inpainting as a black box and using a five-stage tile-based workflow to reduce seams and maintain consistency.
AI.motivation: Large-image semantic editing demands high visual quality and natural integration with surrounding content. White-box methods are limited; directly applying closed-source, inpainting-capable models to tiled editing causes semantic deformation, canvas drift, and visible seams.
AI.method: Five-stage post-hoc pipeline: 1) overlay-based tile decomposition, 2) black-box VLM inpainting, 3) geometric and color-consistency correction, 4) seam-risk-based multi-candidate ranking, 5) dynamic-programming curved seam fusion.
AI.result: The pipeline reduces seam visibility and enables semantic modification within arbitrary tile regions without training, leveraging closed-source inpainting models as black-box oracles.
AI.conclusion: SeamEdit provides a training-free, model-agnostic framework for robust large-image semantic editing by effectively mitigating seams and deformations when using black-box inpainting models.

title: Multi-Modal Agents for Power Distribution Defect Detection: An Evaluation of Foundation Models
summary: The power distribution network is critical to reliable electricity delivery, yet traditional inspection methods face limitations in semantic understanding, generalization, and closed-loop automation. To address these challenges, this paper proposes a Multi-Modal Agent framework specifically for power distribution defect detection. Central to this study is the systematic evaluation of multimodal foundation models as unified cognitive engines. We rigorously assess their integrated performance across three critical capabilities: (1) Perception, where the model must accurately identify equipment and generate expert-level descriptions of defects; (2) Reasoning, where the model interprets visual findings to diagnose causes, assess severity, and plan maintenance strategies based on domain knowledge; and (3) Tool Usage, where the model acts as an autonomous operator to execute actions -- such as querying knowledge bases or generating work orders -- to achieve closed-loop maintenance. To support this evaluation, a domain-specific evaluation dataset and a comprehensive benchmark are developed. Experimental results demonstrate the strengths and limitations of current foundation models in these three dimensions, providing empirical evidence for deploying autonomous agents in high-stakes industrial environments.

AI.tldr: A Multi-Modal Agent framework for power distribution defect detection evaluates multimodal foundation models across perception, reasoning, and tool usage, using a domain-specific dataset and benchmark to assess autonomous maintenance capabilities in power systems.
AI.motivation: Address weaknesses in traditional, manual inspection—limited semantic understanding, generalization, and closed-loop automation—by leveraging unified multimodal foundation models as cognitive engines for industrial inspection.
AI.method: Develop a Multi-Modal Agent framework; systematically evaluate multimodal foundation models across three capabilities (Perception, Reasoning, Tool Usage); construct a domain-specific evaluation dataset and benchmark; conduct empirical experiments to assess performance and limitations.
AI.result: Foundation models exhibit varying strengths across perception, reasoning, and tool usage; some enable autonomous actions and integrated automation, but notable limitations remain; the dataset and benchmark provide empirical evidence guiding deployment in high-stakes industrial contexts.
AI.conclusion: Foundation-model–driven autonomous agents can support high-stakes maintenance in power distribution when paired with domain benchmarks and robust evaluation, but reliability, safety, and domain-specific constraints must be carefully managed during deployment.

title: Low cost, easily manufactured, highly flexible strain and touch sensitive fiber for robotics applications
summary: Existing stretch and touch sensors for robots are generally expensive with respect to at least one of material costs, required manufacturing equipment, or manufacturing time. We present and experimentally characterize a conductive fiber made using only inexpensive commercial off-the-shelf parts (conductive thread at $0.07/ft, silicone tubing at $0.94/ft) and tools (loop-style needle threader at $2), which can be manufactured quickly (20 cm length in 2 minutes.) We demonstrate its use as a resistive strain sensor with three applications: Triggering a grasp in a pneumatically actuated assistive finger, sensing the pose of a pneumatically actuated robotic strap, and estimating the pose of a flexible solid. We also demonstrate that it can be used as a capacitive sensor with two applications: First, as a touch sensor which triggers a commercial robot arm to move, and second, as a near-field sensor enabling the robot arm to follow a moving hand. The capacitive sensors are knitted, showcasing the high flexibility of the fiber. We discuss methods for improving manufacturing scalability and their cost trade-offs. Finally, we demonstrate a method for repairing a cut fiber.

AI.tldr: A low-cost, rapidly manufacturable conductive fiber sensor capable of resistive and capacitive sensing, demonstrated across several robotic applications, with discussion of scalability, cost trade-offs, and a repair method.
AI.motivation: To reduce cost and fabrication time of stretch and touch sensors for robotics by developing an inexpensive, quickly manufacturable conductive fiber.
AI.method: Fabricate a conductive fiber from off-the-shelf parts (conductive thread, silicone tubing, loop-style needle threader); 20 cm length produced in ~2 minutes; evaluate as a resistive strain sensor in three robotic applications and as a capacitive touch/near-field sensor; knit the capacitive sensors for flexibility; explore scalability and repair of cut fiber.
AI.result: Resistive sensing successfully triggered a pneumatically actuated assistive finger, sensed poses of a pneumatic belt/strap and a flexible solid; capacitive sensing enabled a touch-trigger for a commercial robot arm and near-field hand-following; knitted design showed high flexibility; demonstrated a fiber repair method.
AI.conclusion: The approach provides a scalable, low-cost alternative for stretch and touch sensing with rapid fabrication; scalability/cost trade-offs are discussed, and a practical repair method for cut fibers is demonstrated.

title: Forecasting Is Not Attribution: Localizing Decoder Bypass in Graph-Based Neural Marketing Mix Models
summary: Marketing mix models are used to forecast business outcomes and to attribute those outcomes to marketing channels, but these goals are not equivalent. We study a failure mode in graph-based neural MMM called attribution bypass: a high-capacity decoder can obtain low forecasting error through target autoregression, dense communication, co-movement, context, or latent memory while failing to route counterfactual sensitivity through the graph used as the attribution object. We introduce DICE-MMM as a bounded diagnostic and training framework. We do not claim that observational neural MMM identifies causal effects. Instead, DICE separates three questions often conflated in graph-based MMM: graph recovery, forecasting accuracy, and whether the trained decoder's perturbation-induced influence is graph aligned. Stage 1 trains a graph encoder with a restricted graph-mediated decoder. Stage 2 freezes the selected encoder and trains a graph-safe latent decoder whose cross-node communication must pass through the supplied graph. Decoder use is evaluated with CIG, AR-CIG, and graph-swap tests. Across controlled R/d/T swaps and an external multi-graph rawlog stress test, DICE improves stable graph recovery over CausalMMM. The experiments show that forecasting accuracy is not an attribution certificate: in a sparse-target benchmark, no-graph and full-graph decoders achieve MSE@7 around 0.004 while AR-CIG nAUPRC remains near or below zero, whereas an oracle graph reaches 0.807 +/- 0.129 at comparable MSE. Frozen graph-swap localizes the bottleneck: the same DICE-hard-trained decoder moves from nAUPRC -0.044 +/- 0.006 under learned graph inputs to 0.894 +/- 0.027 with the oracle graph. The contribution is a stress test and failure-localization framework showing that low MSE can hide attribution bypass and that the unresolved bottleneck is graph-support selection, not forecasting or decoder capacity.

AI.tldr: DICE-MMM offers a bounded diagnostic framework for graph-based neural Marketing Mix Models (MMM) to detect attribution bypass and separate graph recovery, forecasting accuracy, and attribution alignment. It reveals that low forecasting error can coexist with misaligned counterfactual attribution, and identifies graph-support selection as the bottleneck rather than model capacity.
AI.motivation: Graph-based MMMs can rely on high-capacity decoders that achieve low error via target autoregression or dense communication, bypassing the intended graph-based attribution mechanism. There is a need to disentangle (i) recovering the underlying graph, (ii) forecasting accuracy, and (iii) whether the decoder’s perturbation sensitivity actually routes through the graph. DICE-MMM provides a diagnostic/training framework and stress tests to localize where attribution fails.
AI.method: Two-stage training: Stage 1 train a graph encoder with a restricted (graph-mediated) decoder. Stage 2 freeze the encoder and train a graph-safe latent decoder whose cross-node communication must pass through the supplied graph. Decoder usage is evaluated with CIG, AR-CIG, and graph-swap tests. The approach is stressed with controlled R/d/T swaps and an external multi-graph rawlog stress test, comparing against CausalMMM and using an oracle graph as a benchmark.
AI.result: DICE improves stable graph recovery over CausalMMM across swaps and stress tests. However, forecast accuracy (low MSE) does not equal attribution validity: in sparse-target settings, decoders with no graph or full graph reach MSE~0.004 but AR-CIG remains near or below zero, whereas an oracle graph attains AR-CIG ~0.807 at similar MSE. Freezing the graph-swap reveals bottlenecks shift to graph-support selection: with oracle graph, AR-CIG jumps to ~0.894. Overall, the work shows low MSE can mask attribution bypass and that the bottleneck lies in graph-support selection rather than forecasting or decoder capacity.
AI.conclusion: DICE provides a stress-testing and failure-localization framework that disentangles graph recovery, forecasting performance, and attribution alignment in graph-based MMMs. It demonstrates that relying on low MSE as an attribution guarantee is unsafe and that improving graph-support selection is critical for trustworthy counterfactual attribution.

title: LaME: Learning to Think in Latent Space for Multimodal Embedding via Information Bottleneck
summary: Reasoning-driven universal multimodal embedding has advanced rapidly by introducing Chain-of-Thought (CoT) reasoning into the embedding pipeline. Despite the strong performance across both general and complex tasks, this paradigm suffers from two core limitations: (i) autoregressive CoT reasoning incurs high computational cost, making it impractical for low-latency retrieval; and (ii) embedding performance is heavily coupled with CoT annotation quality, making large-scale training unreliable. These raise fundamental questions: Is textual CoT the optimal form of reasoning for embedding, and can effective embedding reasoning be accomplished in latent space? To this end, we propose LaME (Latent Reasoning Multimodal Embedding), which formulates embedding-oriented latent reasoning as a weakly supervised information bottleneck. LaME employs K learnable reason tokens as a fixed-capacity bottleneck, completing all reasoning within a single forward pass. The two weak supervision signals structurally decouple contrastive from autoregressive objectives and eliminate dependence on CoT annotations, while a two-stage training pipeline ensures stable convergence. Experiments on MMEB-v2 and MRMR show that LaME achieves competitive performance, surpassing some explicit CoT-based models, while delivering 60x faster inference than explicit CoT methods and 2x faster than latent baselines with throughput comparable to discriminative embedding models. Code will be released.

AI.tldr: LaME introduces latent reasoning for multimodal embedding using K learnable reason tokens as a fixed-capacity bottleneck, enabling a single-pass, weakly supervised training without CoT annotations, achieving competitive results with significantly faster inference.
AI.motivation: Address the two main drawbacks of reasoning-driven multimodal embedding with Chain-of-Thought: high autoregressive compute cost and reliance on CoT annotations. Investigate whether textual CoT is optimal and whether effective reasoning can occur in latent space.
AI.method: LaME (Latent Reasoning Multimodal Embedding) uses K learnable reason tokens as a fixed-capacity bottleneck to perform all reasoning in a single forward pass. It employs two weak supervision signals that decouple contrastive and autoregressive objectives and removes dependence on CoT annotations. A two-stage training pipeline ensures stable convergence.
AI.result: LaME achieves competitive performance on MMEB-v2 and MRMR, surpassing some explicit CoT-based models. It delivers ~60x faster inference than explicit CoT methods and ~2x faster than latent baselines, with throughput comparable to discriminative embedding models.
AI.conclusion: Latent reasoning in a fixed-capacity token bottleneck is viable for efficient multimodal embedding, enabling fast, CoT-free or CoT-annotation-free training with competitive accuracy. Code release is planned.

title: A Mathematical Forum Platform for Collaborative Problem Solving and Dataset Generation for AI Reasoning
summary: Sharing mathematical content in online forums remains a significant friction point for students and educators: writing raw LATEX is error-prone, standalone optical character recognition tools require platform switching, and current forum software offers no integrated path from a photograph of a formula to a rendered post. We present a unified system that eliminates this friction by embedding an image to LATEX conversion pipeline directly inside a forum posting interface. A user uploads or captures an image of a mathematical expression; the system routes it through the Mathpix OCR API, detects whether the returned output is LATEX or plain text containing inline math, applies the appropriate delimiter normalisation, and renders a live preview in either LATEX or Markdown mode before the post is committed to the database. The architecture is organized in three loosely coupled layers: image processing, rendering, and storage, and supports both desktop and mobile clients. A provisional US patent application has been filed covering the core methods. We describe the full system design, each component in detail, the data schema, and the key technical innovations, and we position the work against existing standalone tools and forum platforms to demonstrate the practical gap it closes. Beyond immediate usability, we argue that a deployed platform of this kind constitutes a continuously growing, community-validated dataset of mathematical problems and step-by-step solutions, a resource that can be used to train and benchmark AI systems for accurate mathematical reasoning

AI.tldr: An end-to-end image-to-LaTeX pipeline is integrated into a forum posting interface, enabling live rendering of math expressions from images via the Mathpix OCR API, with a three-layer architecture (image processing, rendering, storage), mobile/desktop support, and a patent-disclosed system that also builds a community-driven math dataset for AI benchmarking.
AI.motivation: To eliminate friction in sharing mathematical content online, by eliminating manual LaTeX entry and isolating OCR/formatting from forum workflows, thereby enabling seamless, accurate rendering of math from photos as posts.
AI.method: Image is uploaded or captured and sent to the Mathpix OCR API. The system detects whether the output is LaTeX or plain text with inline math, applies delimiter normalization, and renders a live preview in LaTeX or Markdown before posting. Architecture comprises three loosely coupled layers (image processing, rendering, storage) and supports desktop/mobile clients. A provisional US patent covers core methods. The paper describes system design, data schema, and innovations, and positions the work against existing tools.
AI.result: The work presents a complete end-to-end system design rather than a conventional experiment, including live previews, data schema, and a patentable method. It argues that such a platform closes practical gaps in current forums and creates a growing dataset of mathematical problems and solutions suitable for training/benchmarking AI systems.
AI.conclusion: This integrated platform reduces friction in mathematical communication on forums, with potential to seed a large, community-driven dataset for AI reasoning in mathematics; it also highlights architectural choices (three-layer design) and patent considerations as key contributions.

title: Real-Time Execution with Autoregressive Policies
summary: Real-time execution, enabled by asynchronous inference that ensures both smooth action trajectories and fast reactivity, is critical for realistic deployments of large-scale Vision-Language-Action models. However, recent work on real-time execution primarily focuses on variants of diffusion policies, even though it is more critical for autoregressive policies given their slower rollout speed in synchronous inference. In contrast, we demonstrate that autoregressive policies can achieve real-time execution by adjusting the tokenization horizon and applying constrained decoding, thereby guaranteeing strict latency bounds that enable multi-trajectory decoding to maximize performance. Across simulated and real-world environments, we find that the autoregressive policy consistently outperforms its equivalent-level flow-matching policy counterpart while achieving significantly improved task completion speeds from synchronous inference. Coupled with the inherent advantages of autoregressive policies, such as faster convergence and better generalizability in instruction-following, these results confirm that autoregressive policies can remain a competitive policy type supporting real-time execution.

AI.tldr: Autoregressive policies can achieve real-time execution for Vision-Language-Action models by adjusting tokenization horizon and using constrained decoding, outperforming diffusion/flow-matching approaches in latency and speed while enabling multi-trajectory decoding.
AI.motivation: Real-time execution is crucial for deploying large-scale Vision-Language-Action models. Autoregressive policies suffer from slower rollout under synchronous inference, and most real-time work focuses on diffusion-based policies, leaving a gap for latency-bound autoregressive methods.
AI.method: Tune tokenization horizon and apply constrained decoding to autoregressive policies to guarantee strict latency bounds. Compare against equivalent-level flow-matching policies. Evaluate across simulated and real-world environments, focusing on latency, action speed, convergence, and generalization, and enable multi-trajectory decoding.
AI.result: Autoregressive policies consistently outperform their flow-matching counterparts under real-time constraints, achieving faster task completion and strict latency guarantees that support multi-trajectory decoding. These findings hold in both simulated and real-world settings, with autoregressive policies also showing faster convergence and better generalization in instruction-following tasks.
AI.conclusion: Autoregressive policies remain a competitive choice for real-time execution in large-scale Vision-Language-Action systems. Practical strategies like tokenization horizon tuning and constrained decoding can deliver latency guarantees without sacrificing performance, enabling efficient multi-trajectory decoding.

title: M*: A Modular, Extensible, Serving System for Multimodal Models
summary: We are entering a new era of composite model architectures that integrate diverse components such as vision encoders, language backbones, diffusion and flow heads, audio codecs, action generators, and world-model predictors. Such architectures underpin a broad class of multimodal models, including unified multimodal models, omni models, speech-language models, vision-language-action policies, and world models. However, existing model serving frameworks were built on narrow assumptions about model structure, making them ill-suited to accommodate this new architectural diversity. Here we present M*, a universal serving system for efficient serving of composite AI models. M* represents models as dataflow graphs, processing requests spanning diverse modalities and tasks as traversals over these graphs. The core insight is a modular abstraction that supports arbitrary composition of model components, flexible placement onto a physical cluster, and model-agnostic optimizations within a distributed runtime. We call this abstraction the Walk Graph and show how it can concisely capture composite models from a broad range of families. We instantiate M* on representative models and find that it achieves, on average, 20% lower end-to-end latency than vLLM-Omni for text-to-image workloads on BAGEL, while delivering up to 2.9x lower real-time factor and 2.7x higher throughput for text-to-speech workloads on Qwen3-Omni. M* also outperforms the V-JEPA 2-AC rollout baseline for robotic planning by up to 12.5x. Thus, our work paves the road towards more efficient serving of complex models with minimal developer effort.

AI.tldr: M* presents a universal, modular serving system that represents composite AI models as Walk Graphs (dataflow graphs), enabling flexible placement, composition, and optimizations across diverse modalities.
AI.motivation: To accommodate rapidly evolving composite AI architectures (vision, language, diffusion, audio, world models) and the limitations of traditional single-model serving frameworks.
AI.method: Introduce Walk Graph as a modular dataflow abstraction; implement M* runtime for distributed, model-agnostic serving with arbitrary component placement and optimizations; instantiate on representative composite models and benchmark against baselines.
AI.result: M* achieves ~20% lower end-to-end latency than vLLM-Omni for text-to-image on BAGEL; up to 2.9× lower real-time factor and 2.7× higher throughput for text-to-speech on Qwen3-Omni; up to 12.5× improvement over V-JEPA 2-AC for robotic planning.
AI.conclusion: Walk Graph enables efficient, flexible serving of complex composite AI models with minimal developer effort, advancing scalable deployment for diverse AI systems.

title: Unified MRI Brain Image Translation via Hierarchical Tumor Structure Comparison
summary:
Multi-modal MRI brain image translation via available modalities holds significant practical importance in modern medicine, providing robust support for early diagnosis, treatment planning, and outcome assessment of diseases. For this purpose, it is important to ensure the fidelity of the tumor regions after translation. However, existing brain image translation methods ignore the structure information of different tumor regions, which could assist translation models in enhancing the quality and clinical applicability of the translated images.
  In this work, we propose a novel translation model called HTSCGAN, which is a unified multi-modal brain image translation generative adversarial model integrating the structural information within tumor regions with the aim of improving the quality of brain image translation. Specifically, the generator employs three Patch Contrast Module (PCM) with different patch sizes to capture the hierarchical structural information of the tumor regions. In addition, a pretrained Patch Classifier (PC) and a pretrained Structure-Aware Encoder (SAE) are employed to derive the generated image containing the same tumor region structure as the ground truth image via patch classification loss and tumor perceptual loss, respectively. The experiments on BraTS2020 and BraTS2021 demonstrate strong performance of our model in both translation tasks and down stream segmentation tasks, highlighting its effectiveness in enhancing the quality and clinical relevance of the translated brain images. Our code is available at https://anonymous.4open.science/r/HTSCGAN.

AI.tldr: HTSCGAN: a multi-modal brain MRI translation model that preserves tumor-region structure using Patch Contrast Modules, a Patch Classifier, and a Structure-Aware Encoder to improve translation fidelity and downstream segmentation.
AI.motivation: Current brain image translation methods often ignore structure information of tumor regions, risking fidelity loss in clinically critical areas and limiting downstream analyses.
AI.method: A unified GAN-based translator with three Patch Contrast Modules (PCM) of different patch sizes to capture hierarchical tumor-structure. It uses a pretrained Patch Classifier (PC) and a pretrained Structure-Aware Encoder (SAE) to enforce structure-consistent translation via patch-classification loss and a tumor perceptual loss. Evaluated on BraTS2020/2021.
AI.result: Reported strong performance in both translation quality and downstream segmentation tasks on BraTS2020/2021; code made available.
AI.conclusion: Integrating tumor-region structural information via PCM, PC, and SAE improves tumor-region fidelity and clinical relevance of multi-modal brain MRI translations, with demonstrated benefits on BraTS datasets.

title: Structured Testbench Generation for LLM-Driven HDL Design and Verification-Oriented Data Curation
summary: Automated testbench generation has become a critical bottleneck in large language model (LLM)-driven Register Transfer Level (RTL) workflows, where large numbers of candidate designs must be verified rapidly and reliably. Existing prompt-based approaches treat testbench generation as unconstrained code synthesis, yielding stochastic outputs with high token cost, low reproducibility, and insufficient coverage. To address this gap, we present STG, a Structured Testbench Generation framework that exploits the inherent structure of hardware designs to generate deterministic testbenches. As a direct verification tool, STG runs 720x faster than an iterative LLM-based testbench generation flow and higher rate of successful compilation, achieves higher coverage, and reduces false-pass verdicts on incorrect DUTs. STG also helps identify errors in RTL generation benchmarks by exposing faulty benchmark testbenches. As a data curation engine, it is 11x faster than LLM-based filtering on a single CPU core with 127x less energy, and the resulting distilled models provide state-of-the-art performance in our multi-benchmark evaluation. As a test-time scaling oracle, it reduces node count by 14-47\%. Our models are available at https://huggingface.co/collections/AS-SiliconMind/siliconmind-v12.

AI.tldr: STG introduces a Structured Testbench Generation framework for RTL verification, delivering deterministic testbenches and large gains in speed, coverage, and resource efficiency over conventional LLM-driven approaches.
AI.motivation: LLM-driven testbench generation suffers from unconstrained code synthesis, stochastic outputs, high token costs, poor reproducibility, and insufficient coverage, creating a bottleneck in large-scale RTL workflows.
AI.method: Leverages hardware design structure to generate deterministic testbenches; functions as direct verification tool, data curation engine, and test-time scaling oracle; provides faster, higher-coverage, lower-energy outputs; identifies benchmark errors.
AI.result: Achieves 720x speedup vs iterative LLM-based generation; higher compilation success rate; improved coverage; fewer false positives on incorrect DUTs; detects faulty benchmark testbenches; 11x faster data filtering with 127x less energy; distilled models achieve state-of-the-art performance across multiple benchmarks; reduces node count by 14-47%.
AI.conclusion: STG delivers a scalable, deterministic, and efficient RTL testbench framework that outperforms LLM-only approaches, enabling faster verification, better coverage, and lower resource usage, with publicly available models.

title: GeoHAT: Geometry-Adaptive Hybrid Action Transformer for Mobile Manipulation
summary: Whole-body mobile manipulation requires coordinating mobile base and manipulator under shifting viewpoints, posing challenges in geometric perception and action generation. Current policies either rely on 2D features or sparse 3D representations that lack dense spatial structure, and typically encode arm and base within one action vector that ignores their distinct control demands. Moreover, existing dense fusion strategies risk corrupting pretrained representations under noisy depth while incurring heavy computational overhead. We present GeoHAT, an end-to-end diffusion-based framework built on a simple principle: geometry should be injected only where reliable and attended to only where needed. GeoHAT employs a lightweight Fourier spatial encoder that maps dense per-pixel 3D coordinates into geometric tokens without an additional 3D vision backbone. These tokens are then selectively injected into vision foundation model features through per-token gated fusion modulated by depth validity, preserving the semantic prior while enriching spatial understanding. For action generation, a Hybrid Whole-Body Action Decoder decomposes arm and base into distinct subspaces and lets each action modality attend to its task-relevant visual context through sparse cross-attention, while causal temporal modeling captures intra-timestep coordination and inter-timestep dependencies. Experiments on the ManiSkill-HAB simulation benchmark demonstrate that GeoHAT achieves a 79.3% mean success rate, surpassing the strongest baseline by 23.7%. Furthermore, real-world experiments on diverse tasks also confirm consistent improvements over all baselines.

AI.tldr: GeoHAT offers a diffusion-based, geometry-aware policy for whole-body manipulation that selectively injects 3D geometry into a vision foundation model and uses a modular, task-separated action decoder with sparse cross-attention, achieving state-of-the-art performance on ManiSkill-HAB and real-world tasks.
AI.motivation: Address the mismatch between perception and action in whole-body manipulation under shifting viewpoints by injecting geometry only where reliable and separating control demands of base and arm to reduce interference and computation.
AI.method: A Fourier spatial encoder converts dense per-pixel 3D coordinates into geometric tokens. These tokens are selectively fused into vision foundation model features via per-token gating modulated by depth validity. Action generation uses a Hybrid Whole-Body Action Decoder that decomposes arm and base into separate subspaces with sparse cross-attention to their task-relevant visual context, plus causal temporal modeling for intra- and inter-timestep coordination.
AI.result: On ManiSkill-HAB, GeoHAT attains 79.3% mean success, outperforming the strongest baseline by 23.7%. Real-world experiments show consistent improvements over baselines across diverse tasks.
AI.conclusion: Selective geometry injection and modular action decomposition enable robust, efficient whole-body manipulation across changing viewpoints, leveraging diffusion-based policy learning and avoiding heavy, noisy fusion of depth information.

title: Two-Layer Linear Auto-Regressive Models Estimate Latent States
summary: Auto-regressive models have emerged as powerful tools for sequential data, from language to video. Understanding how and why these models learn latent representations remains an open theoretical question. In this work, we demonstrate that when trained by empirical risk minimization on data from partially observed linear dynamical systems, two-layer linear auto-regressive models naturally learn to approximate Kalman filtering. In particular, we show that the learned hidden representation coincides, up to a similarity transformation, with the state estimates produced by the optimal (Kalman) filter, even though the model has no explicit knowledge of the underlying dynamics or state. The result follows from three main insights. First, we establish that the Kalman filter is well approximated by an auto-regressive model with bounded truncation error. Second, we show that despite non-convexity, the two-layer optimization landscape is benign, i.e., all stationary points are either strict saddles or global minima. Finally, as our main contributions, we provide finite-sample guarantees on prediction error, parameter estimation error, and latent state recovery. Numerical simulations support the theoretical results and demonstrate that the latent representations of auto-regressive models recover state estimates.

AI.tldr: Two-layer linear auto-regressive models trained on data from partially observed linear dynamical systems learn latent representations that align with Kalman filter state estimates (up to a similarity transform), with provable guarantees.
AI.motivation: Explain why autoregressive models learn meaningful latent representations in dynamical settings and establish theoretical guarantees for their state-estimation capabilities.
AI.method: Analyze ERM-trained two-layer linear AR models on data from partially observed linear dynamical systems. Prove that Kalman filtering is well-approximated by such ARs with bounded truncation error; show the optimization landscape is benign (all stationary points are strict saddles or global minima); derive finite-sample guarantees for prediction error, parameter estimation, and latent state recovery.
AI.result: Demonstrate that the learned hidden representation coincides (up to a similarity transform) with Kalman filter state estimates; ARs provide finite-sample guarantees and exhibit a favorable optimization landscape; numerical simulations corroborate latent state recovery.
AI.conclusion: AR models can effectively recover latent state estimates in partially observed dynamical systems, with both theoretical guarantees and empirical support, highlighting a bridge between ERM-trained ARs and classical state estimation.

title: PP-OCRv6: From 1.5M to 34.5M Parameters, Surpassing Billion-Scale VLMs on OCR Tasks
summary: Vision-Language Models (VLMs) have achieved impressive results on general vision-language tasks, yet they suffer from hallucination, imprecise localization, and prohibitive computational cost when applied to dedicated OCR scenarios. This paper presents PP-OCRv6, a lightweight OCR system that combines architectural innovation with data-centric optimization. PP-OCRv6 redesigns the backbone, detection neck, and recognition neck around a unified MetaFormer-style building block with structural reparameterization, decoupling spatial token mixing from channel mixing and supporting both tasks through task-specific stride configurations. Three model tiers (medium, small, tiny) share the same block primitives, covering deployment scenarios from server to edge. On our in-house benchmarks, PP-OCRv6_medium achieves 83.2% recognition accuracy and 86.2% detection Hmean, outperforming PP-OCRv5_server by +5.1% and +4.6% respectively while surpassing Qwen3-VL-235B, GPT-5.5, and Gemini-3.1-Pro with orders of magnitude fewer parameters. The tiny tier achieves 3.9$\times$ faster inference than PP-OCRv5_mobile on Intel Xeon CPU while maintaining comparable accuracy.

AI.tldr: PP-OCRv6 is a lightweight OCR system that unifies backbone, detection neck, and recognition neck around a MetaFormer-style block with structural reparameterization, enabling server-to-edge deployment with higher accuracy and significantly fewer parameters; the tiny version offers substantially faster inference with similar accuracy.
AI.motivation: OCR systems face high computational cost and fragmentation of architectures; there is a need for compact, accurate models that can operate efficiently on edge devices while maintaining strong recognition and localization performance.
AI.method: Redesign of backbone, detection neck, and recognition neck around a unified MetaFormer-style building block with structural reparameterization; decouples spatial token mixing from channel mixing; employs task-specific stride configurations; three model tiers (medium, small, tiny) sharing the same block primitives.
AI.result: On in-house benchmarks, PP-OCRv6_medium achieves 83.2% recognition accuracy and 86.2% detection Hmean, outperforming PP-OCRv5_server by +5.1% and +4.6% respectively; also surpasses Qwen3-VL-235B, GPT-5.5, Gemini-3.1-Pro with orders of magnitude fewer parameters. The tiny tier achieves 3.9× faster inference than PP-OCRv5_mobile on Intel Xeon CPUs with comparable accuracy.
AI.conclusion: PP-OCRv6 delivers a lightweight, high-performance OCR system achieved through architectural unification and data-centric optimization, enabling deployment from server to edge with substantial efficiency gains while maintaining strong accuracy.

title: APCyc: Property-Informed Design of Cyclic Peptides via Automated Cyclization
comment: Accepted at the 32nd ACM SIGKDD Conference on Knowledge Discovery and Data Mining (KDD 2026)
summary: Cyclic peptides represent a promising class of therapeutic compounds in modern drug discovery, often offering improved stability and binding affinity. However, the de novo design of cyclic peptides remains challenging because methods must identify pocket-adaptive cyclization patterns and linkage sites while simultaneously controlling drug-relevant properties. This challenge is particularly pronounced for recent generative models trained predominantly on linear peptide data, which may fail to capture cyclization-specific constraints. To address the limitation, we introduce APCyc, a target-aware de novo cyclic peptide generation framework that explicitly models cyclization and jointly optimizes multiple essential physicochemical properties. By using an expanded residue vocabulary and explicitly encoding cyclization-site and linkage-type information, APCyc learns cyclization-aware representations and leverages Bayesian posterior guidance to steer sampling toward cyclic peptides satisfying multiple property objectives. Experimental results demonstrate that our model learns target-dependent cyclization preferences, and enables effective and controllable multi-property optimization for cyclic peptide design. The source code of this paper is available at https://github.com/HKUSTGZ-ML4Health-Lab/APCyc.

AI.tldr: APCyc: a target-aware de novo cyclic peptide generator that explicitly models cyclization and optimizes multiple properties with Bayesian guidance.
AI.motivation: Cyclic peptides pose cyclization-specific constraints; existing generative models trained on linear peptides may fail to capture these constraints, hindering de novo cyclic peptide design.
AI.method: Expand the residue vocabulary; explicitly encode cyclization-site and linkage-type information; learn cyclization-aware representations; apply Bayesian posterior guidance to steer sampling toward cyclic peptides that satisfy multiple properties.
AI.result: Demonstrates target-dependent cyclization preferences and effective controllable multi-property optimization for cyclic peptide design; source code available at the provided repository.
AI.conclusion: APCyc enables explicit cyclization modeling and multi-property optimization for target-aware cyclic peptide design, addressing limitations of models trained on linear peptides.

title: GIVE: Grounding Human Gestures in Vision-Language-Action Models
summary: Human communication is inherently multimodal, where language is often accompanied by non-verbal cues such as gestures to convey intentions. However, current Vision-Language-Action (VLA) models treat robotic manipulation as a pure text-driven task, overlooking the important role of gestures in Human-Robot Interaction (HRI). This often leads to inaccurate intent grounding and unreliable manipulation when language instructions are ambiguous or underspecified. To address this challenge, we propose GIVE (Gesture Intent via Visual-Semantic Enhancement), an effective approach that enhances pre-trained VLA models with human gesture understanding without architectural modifications. Specifically, GIVE incorporates gesture information through two complementary pathways: a visual pathway that overlays hand skeletons and fingertip rays onto robot observations for explicit object grounding, and a semantic pathway that generates high-level descriptions of human gestures and task instructions for robust intent grounding. By jointly leveraging visual and semantic guidance, GIVE enables VLA policies to better associate gestures with manipulation behaviors and adapt to dynamic interaction intents. In real-world HRI experiments, GIVE substantially outperforms the baseline, improving target object recognition accuracy by 40% and overall task success rate by 80%, while demonstrating strong robustness and generalization to unseen spatial layouts and diverse participants.

AI.tldr: GIVE enhances pre-trained Vision-Language-Action models with gesture understanding via dual pathways to improve HRI grounding and manipulation without changing architecture.
AI.motivation: Gestures provide critical disambiguation in human-robot dialogue; traditional VLA models rely largely on language, missing non-verbal cues.
AI.method: Two complementary pathways: a visual pathway overlays hand skeletons and fingertip rays onto robot observations for explicit object grounding; a semantic pathway generates high-level descriptions of human gestures and task instructions; integrates with pre-trained VLA models without architectural changes, jointly leveraging visual and semantic guidance.
AI.result: Real-world HRI experiments show 40% improvement in target object recognition accuracy and 80% increase in overall task success, with robustness to unseen spatial layouts and diverse participants.
AI.conclusion: Joint visual-semantic gesture grounding enhances intent understanding and manipulation in HRI; method is effective, robust, and generalizable; future work could explore broader gesture vocabularies and efficiency improvements.

title: LLM-Powered Personalized Glycemic Assessment in Type 2 Diabetes with Wearable Sensor Data
comment: The 14th IEEE International Conference on Healthcare Informatics, 2026
summary:
Type 2 Diabetes (T2D) poses an increasing global health threat, demanding effective glycemic assessment to support personalized and improved diabetes care. Wearable sensors such as continuous glucose monitors (CGM) and fitness trackers offer many valuable insights for glycemic assessment. However, effectively analyzing these data requires integration with essential individual-level context. Existing methods are often based on traditional machine learning (ML) and rely primarily on historical blood glucose measurements and overlook personalized information, which limits their performance across diverse diabetes populations. Recent advances in large language models (LLMs) have demonstrated their ability to integrate diverse data modalities while modeling sequential dependencies, motivating the exploration of their potential for personalized glycemic assessment.
  In this paper, we propose GlyLLM, an LLM-powered framework for modeling CGM-based glycemic dynamics through the integration of wearable sensor data and structured metadata. GlyLLM can leverage the extensive prior knowledge of pre-trained LLMs and achieve sensor-text semantic abstraction at decision time. Experiments on two related tasks on the AI-READI dataset demonstrate that our model outperforms traditional ML methods by an average of 13.66\% in Root Mean Squared Error (RMSE) for glucose forecasting and 13.08\% in Area Under the Receiver Operating Characteristic (AUROC) for diabetes categorization. Additionally, our ablation study shows that diabetes surveys and biometric tests are more critical than other health information for glycemic assessment. Our work presents a promising step toward harnessing the power of LLMs to advance personalized glycemic assessment in T2D care.

AI.tldr: GlyLLM uses a large language model to fuse CGM wearables and structured metadata for personalized glycemic assessment, achieving notable RMSE and AUROC gains over traditional ML on AI-READI data; ablations highlight the importance of diabetes surveys and biometric tests.
AI.motivation: Need for personalized, accurate glycemic assessment across diverse T2D populations. Wearable sensors offer rich data but require integration with individual context. LLMs offer multimodal integration and sequential modeling, potentially improving performance over traditional ML that relies mainly on historical glucose.
AI.method: GlyLLM is an LLM-powered framework that models CGM-based glycemic dynamics by integrating wearable sensor data with structured metadata. It leverages pre-trained LLM knowledge and performs sensor-text semantic abstraction at decision time, enabling multimodal data fusion for forecasting and classification tasks.
AI.result: On the AI-READI dataset, GlyLLM outperforms traditional ML by an average of 13.66% in RMSE for glucose forecasting and 13.08% in AUROC for diabetes categorization. Ablation suggests diabetes surveys and biometric tests are more impactful than other health information for glycemic assessment.
AI.conclusion: LLMs can effectively harness multimodal wearable and metadata data to advance personalized glycemic assessment in T2D care. While promising, further validation on diverse datasets and transparent methodological details are needed to assess generalizability and clinical utility.

title: Fully Distributed Multi-View 3D Tracking in Real-Time
summary: Multi-camera tracking with overlapping fields of view typically relies on centralized fusion, which creates computational bottlenecks that prevent deployment at scale. We present MV3DT, a fully distributed framework for real-time multi-view 3D tracking that achieves accurate identity propagation and occlusion recovery through peer-to-peer coordination, eliminating the need for central aggregation. Each camera node executes a lightweight modular pipeline comprising monocular 3D perception, distributed multi-view association, and collaborative fusion via lightweight messaging. MV3DT achieves 94.3% IDF1 and 93.3% MOTA on WILDTRACK, competitive with state-of-the-art centralized methods, while demonstrating superior scalability by sustaining 30 FPS on 100 cameras with less than 10 ms inter-camera latency and only 2.2% communication overhead. MV3DT operates in a zero-shot regime given camera calibrations, requiring no scene-specific learning and making it directly deployable in new environments. These results establish MV3DT as a practical solution for real-time multi-view tracking in large-scale overlapping camera networks.

AI.tldr: Distributed, peer-to-peer multi-view 3D tracking (MV3DT) that matches centralized methods in accuracy while scaling to many cameras with low latency, zero-shot deployment.
AI.motivation: Centralized fusion in multi-camera tracking creates computational bottlenecks and scalability limits; need scalable, real-time tracking without central aggregation.
AI.method: Per-camera modular pipeline: monocular 3D perception, distributed multi-view association, and collaborative fusion via lightweight messaging, all peer-to-peer. Operates in a zero-shot regime with camera calibrations and no scene-specific learning.
AI.result: Achieves 94.3% IDF1 and 93.3% MOTA on WILDTRACK; sustains 30 FPS on 100 cameras with <10 ms inter-camera latency and 2.2% communication overhead; competitive with state-of-the-art centralized methods.
AI.conclusion: MV3DT provides a practical, scalable, real-time solution for multi-view tracking in large-scale overlapping camera networks without centralized aggregation.

title: The Illusion of Multi-Agent Advantage
summary: Prevailing wisdom posits that Multi-Agent Systems (MAS) are superior to Single-Agent Systems (SAS), citing advantages like context protection, parallel processing and distributed decision-making. However, empirical support for this claim relies primarily on comparisons with SAS baselines using benchmarks that prioritize isolated reasoning tasks, which do not adequately assess these advantages. Focusing on automatically generated MAS that are designed for enhanced generalizability over manually-designed counterparts, we perform a rigorous, systematic evaluation against SAS, specifically Chain-of-Thought with Self-Consistency (CoT-SC). Across traditional reasoning datasets and tasks with interactive multi-step workflows (e.g., BrowseComp-Plus), we demonstrate that automatic MAS consistently underperform CoT-SC despite being up to 10x more expensive. To isolate these failures from limitations inherent to task structure, we introduce a diagnostic synthetic dataset tailored for MAS featuring explicit task decomposition, context separation and parallelization potential. We show that expert-architected MAS consistently outperforms automatically generated architectures in both raw performance and cost-efficiency on this dataset, demonstrating that existing evaluation frameworks mask critical architectural gaps and inefficiencies of complex MAS by failing to account for the marginal utility of increased computational cost. Critically, systematic deconstruction of the generated MAS architectures reveals that current automated design paradigms produce architectural bloat that prioritizes superficial complexity which does not translate into functional utility, exposing a fundamental misalignment with multi-agent principles.

AI.tldr: Automatic MAS underperform CoT-SC baselines on reasoning tasks and are cost-inefficient, with architecturally bloated designs; expert-architected MAS outperform automated MAS on a diagnostic synthetic dataset, revealing gaps in current automated design paradigms and questioning MAS superiority claims.
AI.motivation: Reassess the claimed advantages of multi-agent systems (MAS) over single-agent systems (SAS) by focusing on automatically generated (generalizable) MAS and evaluating them against strong SAS baselines, to identify whether increased parallelization, context protection, and distributed decision-making translate into practical gains when cost is considered.
AI.method: Systematic evaluation across traditional reasoning datasets and interactive multi-step tasks (e.g., BrowseComp-Plus) comparing automatically generated MAS to Chain-of-Thought with Self-Consistency (CoT-SC). Introduces a diagnostic synthetic dataset emphasizing explicit task decomposition, context separation, and parallelization potential. Performs architectural analysis to distinguish expert-designed MAS from automatically generated designs, assessing raw performance and cost-efficiency.
AI.result: Automatic MAS consistently underperform CoT-SC, despite up to 10x higher computational cost. Expert-architected MAS outperform automated designs on the diagnostic dataset in both raw performance and cost-efficiency. Automated design paradigms produce architectural bloat with superficial complexity that does not translate into utility, revealing misalignment with multi-agent principles and masking architectural gaps by neglecting marginal utility of computation.
AI.conclusion: Current evaluation frameworks and benchmarks may mask essential architectural limitations of MAS by not accounting for the marginal utility of additional computation. There is a need for better evaluation that aligns with core multi-agent principles, discourages unnecessary architectural bloat, and emphasizes cost-effective collaboration and decomposition strategies.

title: NavWAM: A Navigation World Action Model for Goal-Conditioned Visual Navigation
summary: Goal-conditioned visual navigation requires a robot to act under partial observability by anticipating how its motion will change the future egocentric view and whether that change brings it closer to the goal. Navigation world models provide such visual foresight, but they remain prediction modules that require an external planner to convert predicted futures into closed-loop control. We propose Navigation World Action Model (NavWAM), a diffusion-transformer policy that turns navigation world-model prediction into executable action by representing future observations, goal-progress values, and action chunks in a shared latent sequence. By learning future prediction jointly with the action and value targets that determine closed-loop behavior, NavWAM makes visual foresight directly usable for robot control. We build NavWAM through simulation pretraining and real-robot adaptation, and evaluate it on image-goal navigation against planning-based world models and a representative direct navigation policy. Across offline benchmarks and closed-loop real-robot deployment, NavWAM improves over planning-based world-model baselines in our evaluations while using the default policy mode without CEM-style action search. Project page: https://dachii-azm.github.io/navwam/

AI.tldr: NavWAM is a diffusion-transformer policy that converts navigation world-model predictions into executable actions by learning future observations, goal progress, and action chunks in a shared latent sequence, enabling direct closed-loop control without external planners.
AI.motivation: Address partial observability in goal-conditioned visual navigation and the gap between predictive world models and executable control; standard world models require external planning to act on predicted futures.
AI.method: Introduce Navigation World Action Model (NavWAM): a diffusion-transformer policy that encodes future observations, goal-progress, and action chunks into a shared latent sequence. Train future prediction jointly with action and value targets to produce controllable behavior. Pretrain in simulation and adapt to real robots; evaluate against planning-based world-models and direct policies without using CEM-style search.
AI.result: NavWAM achieves better performance than planning-based world-model baselines in both offline benchmarks and real-robot closed-loop deployment, using the default policy mode without action-search methods like CEM.
AI.conclusion: Learning a unified latent representation that connects world-model predictions to executable control enables visual foresight to be directly usable for navigation, reducing reliance on external planners and improving performance.

title: A Stabilized Path-Space Approach to Diffusion-Based Posterior Sampling
summary: Diffusion models provide expressive data-driven priors for Bayesian inverse problems, but many diffusion posterior samplers rely on heuristic guidance approximations that can fail for nonlinear operators and multimodal posteriors. In this work, we develop a stabilized path-space framework for diffusion-based posterior sampling. Starting from a base diffusion process whose terminal marginal represents the prior, we define a likelihood-weighted target measure on trajectories and cast posterior sampling as learning a controlled stochastic process whose path measure matches this target. This formulation connects diffusion posterior sampling to stochastic optimal control while preserving the Bayesian structure needed for uncertainty quantification. We introduce a time reparameterization that makes the path-space control problem well posed by removing the bias induced by the unknown initial value function, without auxiliary training. We then learn the control via a trust-region path-space optimization method with log-variance objectives. The path-space perspective also unifies our learned control approach with existing guidance-based samplers, quantifies the sampling error induced by approximate controls, and yields importance sampling corrections for asymptotically exact posterior expectations. We evaluate the proposed framework on a suite of benchmark inverse problems with analytically characterized or high-quality reference posteriors, enabling principled assessment of sampling accuracy and uncertainty quantification. These experiments provide insight into the behavior of diffusion-based posterior samplers and demonstrate improved accuracy and robustness over leading approaches.

AI.tldr: A stabilized, Bayesian-consistent diffusion posterior sampler via path-space control and time reparameterization, with trust-region learning and importance sampling corrections, improving accuracy and robustness on inverse problems.
AI.motivation: Diffusion-model priors in Bayesian inverse problems rely on heuristic guidance that can fail with nonlinear operators or multimodal posteriors. A framework that preserves Bayesian structure and provides uncertainty quantification is needed.
AI.method: Define a likelihood-weighted target over diffusion trajectories and learn a controlled SDE whose path measure matches this target. Introduce time reparameterization to remove bias from unknown initial value. Optimize the control via a trust-region path-space method with log-variance objectives. Connect to existing guidance-based samplers and provide importance sampling corrections for exact posterior expectations.
AI.result: Empirical evaluation on benchmark inverse problems with analytic or high-quality reference posteriors showing improved accuracy and robustness over leading approaches; provides principled assessment of sampling accuracy and uncertainty quantification, and insights into diffusion-based sampler behavior.
AI.conclusion: The path-space framework yields stable, principled diffusion-based posterior sampling that preserves Bayesian uncertainty quantification, unifies control- and guidance-based approaches, and enhances performance on nonlinear and multimodal inverse problems.

title: Cascade Classification of Dermoscopic Images of Skin Neoplasms with Controllable Sensitivity and External Clinical Validation
summary:
Purpose. To compare deep learning architectures and classification schemes for dermoscopic images of skin neoplasms and assess their generalization on transfer from open international datasets to independent clinical datasets of Russian practice.
  Methods. Four architectures (ViT-B/16, Swin-S, ConvNeXt-S, EfficientNetV2-S) were compared in three schemes: binary (malignant/benign), single-stage four-class (benign, MEL, SCC, BCC), and a two-stage cascade (binary triage, then three-class differentiation MEL/SCC/BCC). All models used ImageNet-pretrained weights and a single augmentation protocol on aggregated open ISIC Archive data, and were evaluated on an internal held-out sample and two clinical datasets (Melanoscope AI mobile system; Sechenov University).
  Results. Internally the binary stage attains ROC-AUC 0.952-0.966; on Sechenov University it drops to 0.797-0.893, sensitivity to 0.53-0.67, and ECE rises from 0.02 to 0.27-0.39 with underestimation of malignancy, quantifying a generalization gap in ranking and calibration. Paired tests confirm one inter-architecture result on clinical data: the deficit of ViT-B/16 at the binary stage (p<0.05); at the differentiation stage no architecture has a proven advantage. The cascade raises macro F1 over single-stage four-class classification for most architectures, but significantly only for ViT-B/16, by recovering malignant lesions assigned to the dominant benign class. On ISIC MILK10k, direct 11-class classification yields mean-class sensitivity 0.525.
  Conclusion. A tunable triage threshold gives sensitivity control not attainable in standard single-stage (argmax) classification and better reproduces clinical differential-diagnosis logic. The persistent generalization gap mandates external clinical validation and recalibration before deployment.

AI.tldr: Four architectures evaluated across three schemes for dermoscopic skin neoplasms; generalization gap observed when transferring from open ISIC data to clinical Russian datasets; a two-stage cascade with a tunable triage threshold improves macro F1 for some architectures (notably ViT-B/16); external validation and recalibration required.
AI.motivation: To quantify generalization and compare architectures and classification schemes for dermoscopic lesion classification, ensuring clinical applicability beyond open datasets.
AI.method: Train four architectures (ViT-B/16, Swin-S, ConvNeXt-S, EfficientNetV2-S) with ImageNet-pretrained weights on aggregated ISIC Archive data using a single augmentation protocol; evaluate on internal held-out sample and two clinical datasets (Melanoscope AI and Sechenov University) under three schemes: binary, four-class, two-stage cascade; measure ROC-AUC, sensitivity, ECE, macro F1; statistical tests for architecture differences; MILK10k 11-class analysis.
AI.result: Internal binary ROC-AUC 0.952–0.966; clinical Sechenov ROC-AUC 0.797–0.893 with sensitivity 0.53–0.67 and ECE 0.27–0.39 indicating calibration issues; ViT-B/16 deficit at binary stage (p<0.05); cascade increases macro F1 mainly for ViT-B/16; MILK10k 11-class mean sensitivity 0.525.
AI.conclusion: A tunable triage threshold enables controlled sensitivity not available with argmax; generalization gap remains, requiring external validation and recalibration before deployment.

title: Otters++: A Time-to-first-spike Based Energy Efficient Optical Spiking Transformer
summary: Spiking neural networks (SNNs) are promising for energy-efficient inference, and time-to-first-spike (TTFS) coding is especially attractive because each neuron fires at most once. In practice, however, this benefit is often reduced by the cost of computing a temporal decay term and multiplying it by the synaptic weight. We address this issue by turning a physical hardware "bug," the natural signal decay in optoelectronic devices, into the main computation of TTFS, named Otters++. Specifically, we use the measured decay of a custom In$_2$O$_3$ optoelectronic synapse to directly realize the TTFS temporal term, removing the need for explicit digital decay computation. To scale this idea to Transformer models, we establish a layer-wise functional equivalence between the Otters++ and a quantized neural network (QNN), and develop a hybrid training method that uses device-faithful SNN computation in the forward pass and QNN straight-through gradients through the equivalent QNN path in the backward pass, together with model distillation. This avoids differentiation through discrete first-spike events and reduces the over-sparsity problem in direct TTFS-SNN training. We further make training aware of measured device noise by sampling run-to-run variation, and refine the system-level energy model by accounting for device sharing and multi-hop communication. On GLUE dataset, Otters++ improves the average score to 84.17\% while maintaining a clear energy advantage over prior spiking Transformer baselines. These results show that physically grounded TTFS computing can be efficient, trainable, and robust under realistic hardware effects.

AI.tldr: Otters++ uses the natural decay of optoelectronic synapses to implement TTFS computation in spiking neural networks, removing explicit digital decay. It scales to Transformers via a layer-wise equivalence to a QNN and a hybrid training scheme: forward device-faithful SNN with backward QNN straight-through gradients and distillation. It accounts for hardware noise and system-level energy in a realistic model. On GLUE, it achieves 84.17% average with energy advantages over prior TTFS baselines, demonstrating efficient, trainable, hardware-aware TTFS computing.
AI.motivation: TTFS SNNs offer potential energy efficiency by firing at most once per neuron, but practical implementations incur costs for temporal decay computation and weight-dependent decay multiplications. The work aims to leverage a hardware 'bug'—natural decay in optoelectronic devices—as the TTFS term, enabling energy-efficient, trainable TTFS computation and scalable transformer models.
AI.method: 1) Use measured decay in In2O3 optoelectronic synapses to realize the TTFS temporal term directly, removing separate decay computations. 2) Establish a layer-wise functional equivalence between Otters++ and a quantized neural network (QNN) to scale to Transformer models. 3) Develop a hybrid training method: forward pass uses device-faithful SNN computation; backward pass uses straight-through gradients through the equivalent QNN path, plus model distillation. 4) Introduce noise-aware training by sampling run-to-run device variation; refine system-level energy model considering device sharing and multi-hop communication.
AI.result: Otters++ achieves an average GLUE score of 84.17% with a clear energy advantage over prior spiking Transformer baselines.
AI.conclusion: Physically grounded TTFS computing via optical decay can be energy-efficient, trainable, and robust to realistic hardware effects, enabling scalable TTFS-enabled transformers.

title: SPARC: Reliable Spatial Annotations from Robot Demonstrations at Scale
summary: This work introduces Spatial Annotations from Robot Demonstrations with Reliability Calibration (SPARC), a risk-aware framework that automatically labels robot demonstrations with structured spatial annotations and assigns each annotation a reliability score. Structured spatial annotations, such as bounding boxes, object trajectories, and manipulation phase labels, benefit a broad range of robotics applications from training grounded robot policies and embodied foundation models to motion planning and hierarchical task composition. Existing automated pipelines generate such annotations at scale but provide no reliable quality signal: detector confidence is poorly calibrated for annotation correctness, forcing a choice between accepting noisy labels or discarding useful samples. In contrast to existing automated pipelines, SPARC leverages the spatio-temporal structure inherent to robot tasks to generate a reliability signal, reducing noisy labels and retaining more useful samples. We further introduce Interaction-Aware Bench (IA-Bench), a benchmark that measures model accuracy in grounding the locations of interacted objects in robot demonstrations. On 1.7k human-annotated demonstrations spanning diverse embodiments and scenarios, SPARC significantly outperforms detection-only baselines in localization accuracy while retaining three times more samples at high-precision operating points. Our experiments demonstrate that models finetuned on our annotations achieve state-of-the-art results on object-grounding and pointing benchmarks among similarly sized models, while remaining competitive on broader spatial-reasoning suites without manually verified or annotated training data. Furthermore, policies trained on SPARC-generated annotations outperform baselines in cluttered, visually ambiguous real-world scenes. Code, data, and models are available at intuitive-robots.github.io/sparc-labeling.

AI.tldr: SPARC introduces reliability-calibrated, structured spatial annotations from robot demonstrations and a risk-aware labeling framework, plus IA-Bench for evaluation; outperforms detection-only baselines and enables high-sample-efficiency, state-of-the-art grounding in similarly sized models; scalable without manual labels.
AI.motivation: Automated annotation pipelines provide noisy labels due to poor detector calibration. There is a need for a reliability signal that leverages spatio-temporal structure to improve label quality and data efficiency for grounding and manipulation tasks.
AI.method: SPARC uses spatial annotations (e.g., bounding boxes, trajectories, manipulation phases) labeled with reliability scores derived from the spatio-temporal context of robot demonstrations. It incorporates a risk-aware labeling calibration to retain useful samples while reducing noise. IA-Bench is introduced to quantify grounding accuracy of interacted objects in demonstrations.
AI.result: On 1.7k human-annotated demonstrations, SPARC outperforms detection-only baselines in localization accuracy and retains ~3x more samples at high-precision operating points. Finetuned models achieve state-of-the-art results on object-grounding and pointing benchmarks among similarly sized models and are competitive on broader spatial-reasoning tasks without manual verification data. Policies trained on SPARC annotations perform better in cluttered, visually ambiguous real-world scenes.
AI.conclusion: SPARC provides calibrated, scalable spatial annotations that improve grounding, policy learning, and hierarchical task execution in robotics, with IA-Bench offering a robust evaluation benchmark; no manually annotated training data is required beyond the 1.7k demonstrations used for benchmarking.

title: Out-of-Distribution (OOD) Detectors for Open-Set RF Fingerprinting
summary: Radio-frequency (RF) fingerprinting systems must operate in open-world environments where signals from unknown transmitters and temporal drift introduce distribution shift at test time. Out-of-distribution (OOD) detection provides a natural framework for this problem, yet its application to RF fingerprinting (RFF) remains limited.
