A key barrier to their adoption is that most OOD detectors require auxiliary OOD data for parameter tuning, an assumption that is difficult to satisfy in RF environments where representative OOD data is impractical to collect. In this work, we introduce a promising set of OOD detection methods from the machine learning literature to open-set RFF domain. We present these methods within a unified mathematical framework based on information theory, which is a natural framework for communication systems. Our framework allows for the systematic analysis of methods and development of new methods. We further demonstrate the applicability of recent work on tuning OOD detectors without given OOD tuning data for open-set RFF. We evaluate on the POWDER RF fingerprinting dataset, showing that detectors tuned without any given OOD data achieve performance comparable to baselines with access to true OOD tuning data and greatly out-perform baseline approaches without access to true OOD tuning data, showcasing the practical viability for the RFF problem.

AI.tldr: Proposes OOD detection methods for RF fingerprinting within an information-theoretic framework, enabling tuning without out-of-distribution data. Demonstrates that detectors tuned without OOD data perform comparably to those tuned with OOD data on POWDER, outperforming data-tuning-free baselines, indicating practical viability for open-set RF fingerprinting.
AI.motivation: RF fingerprinting operates under distribution shift in open-world environments. Obtaining representative OOD data for detector tuning is impractical in RF contexts, motivating methods that can detect OOD without explicit OOD examples.
AI.method: Introduce a set of ML-based OOD detection methods for open-set RF fingerprinting and unify them under an information-theoretic framework relevant to communication systems. Analyze existing methods, extend with new approaches, and demonstrate tuning without explicit OOD data on the POWDER dataset.
AI.result: Detectors tuned without OOD tuning data achieve performance comparable to baselines with OOD tuning data and substantially better than baselines without any OOD tuning data.
AI.conclusion: The work demonstrates practical viability of open-set RF fingerprinting with OOD detection that does not require labeled OOD data, supported by a principled information-theoretic framework and empirical validation on POWDER.

title: An Extensible and Lightweight Unified Architecture for Demosaicing Pixel-bin Image Sensors
summary: Pixel-bin image sensors are becoming the default choice for smartphone cameras due to their resolution vs light-gathering trade-off. However, their larger inter-color separation compared to the Bayer color filter array (CFA) makes them challenging to demosaic. Furthermore, existing deep learning-based demosaicing methods are CFA-specific, requiring multiple individual models that take up precious onboard resources and demand larger development and maintenance efforts. In this work, we propose a modular unified architecture for demosaicing various pixel-bin sensors that provides higher image quality while being extensible and lightweight. Additionally, to enable plug-and-play operation, we introduce a learning-free CFA-identification module to detect the CFA type of raw data accurately.

AI.tldr: A modular unified demosaicing framework for pixel-bin sensors with a learning-free CFA-identifier enabling plug-and-play across CFA types; claims improved image quality with lightweight, extensible design.
AI.motivation: Pixel-bin sensors offer higher resolution but larger inter-color separation versus Bayer CFA, making demosaicing harder. CFA-specific DL methods require multiple models and heavy maintenance. A unified, extensible, lightweight approach with automatic CFA detection can simplify deployment on devices.
AI.method: A modular architecture with a shared backbone and modular demosaicing blocks tuned for different pixel-bin CFAs; includes a learning-free CFA-identification module to classify the raw CFA and route processing accordingly, enabling plug-and-play use and on-device efficiency.
AI.result: The abstract does not provide quantitative results. It asserts higher image quality and extensibility but gives no empirical metrics, datasets, or comparisons.
AI.conclusion: The work proposes a practical, extensible framework to demosaic across pixel-bin CFAs with automatic CFA identification; its effectiveness will depend on the CFA-ID module performance and the quality of the modular demosaicing paths, requiring empirical validation and deployment studies.

title: SciR: A Controllable Benchmark for Scientific Reasoning in LLMs
summary: Three paradigmatic forms of inference recur across scientific reasoning: deduction, induction, and causal abduction. Reliably evaluating LLMs on these in scientific settings is currently out of reach: scientific benchmarks built on human annotations are costly and lack mechanistic ground truth, while synthetic logical-reasoning benchmarks do not resemble real scientific documents. We introduce SciR, a benchmark that combines multi-paradigm reasoning with controllable scientific rendering, anchored on three paradigmatic scientific problems. Tasks are generated from formal objects (deduction tree, inductive rule hypothesis, causal graph) to guarantee verifiable answers, then rendered into multi-document scientific discourse via per-track domain-tuned genres. The construction lets us independently vary two difficulty axes: how hard it is to extract the key information needed for inference, and how hard the principled inference itself is. We test six models. Both axes hurt every model, and their effects compound. The rendering even hurts neurosymbolic pipelines, which hand inference to a verified solver. The two axes yield a per-model extraction-vs-inference profile: for instance, reasoning models like deepseek-r1 mostly surpass non-reasoning instruct models on the inference axis. To our knowledge, SciR is the first multi-paradigm scientific-reasoning benchmark with parametric control on both extraction and inference difficulty.

AI.tldr: SciR is a controllable, multi-paradigm scientific reasoning benchmark that links formal reasoning objects to multi-document scientific rendering, enabling independent, parametric control of extraction and inference difficulty; six models show that both axes degrade performance, with reasoning models often outperforming non-reasoning ones on the inference axis.
AI.motivation: Address the lack of reliable evaluation of LLMs on deduction, induction, and causal abduction in realistic scientific contexts. Existing benchmarks are costly, human-annotated or synthetic and lack verifiable ground truth. SciR couples formal objects with domain-specific rendering to enable verifiable answers and tunable difficulty.
AI.method: Generate tasks from formal objects (deduction tree, inductive rule hypothesis, causal graph) and render them into multi-document scientific discourse via per-track domain-tuned genres. Independently vary two difficulty axes: extraction difficulty and principled inference difficulty. Evaluate six models.
AI.result: Both difficulty axes impair performance for every model, with compounded effects. Rendering even degrades neurosymbolic pipelines that rely on a verified solver. The extraction-vs-inference profile emerges per model (e.g., deepseek-r1 beats non-reasoning instruction models on inference).
AI.conclusion: SciR is the first multi-paradigm scientific-reasoning benchmark with parametric control on both extraction and inference difficulty, enabling more realistic and verifiable evaluation of LLMs on scientific reasoning.

title: MCR-Bionic Hand: Anatomical Structural Priors for Dexterous Manipulation
summary:
Dexterous robotic hands are usually formulated as high dimensional active control systems governed by degrees of freedom, actuation, and algorithms. Human hand dexterity, however, is partly encoded in the physical architecture of bones, ligaments, tendons, aponeuroses, and intrinsic muscles. This work describes that contribution as two linked forms of structural intelligence: structural prior generation, in which wrist to finger tenodesis, FDS/FDP routing, and the dorsal extensor hood transform low dimensional posture inputs into default grasp configurations and PIP to DIP coordination; and muscle mediated modulation, in which extrinsic muscles, lumbricals, and interossei regulate MCP posture, distal stability, fingertip force paths, and contact states around that default state.
  Based on this framework, MCR-Bionic Hand is developed as a 1:1 musculoskeletal biomimetic hand integrating a two row eight bone wrist, cross wrist tendons, anatomical flexor routing, volar plate and collateral ligament constraints, the dorsal extensor hood, and intrinsic muscle pathways within one body. Functional demonstrations and geometric mechanical models show that wrist posture induces multi joint pre shaping, the extensor hood maps PIP posture to a coupled DIP response, and intrinsic plus pathways modulate distal stability and fingertip action direction after grasp formation. Contact rich tasks, including coin rotation, pen transfer, dorsal coin flipping, and cube manipulation, show that MCR-Bionic links low dimensional state generation with fine post contact modulation. These results suggest that anatomical biomimetics is valuable not for visual similarity, but for identifying human hand structures that perform part of control.

AI.tldr: Biomimetic, musculoskeletal hand (MCR-Bionic) demonstrates how anatomical structures provide structural priors and muscle-mediated modulation to achieve dexterous manipulation, linking low-dimensional control to high-dimensional contact dynamics.
AI.motivation: Understand how structural features of the hand contribute to dexterity beyond pure control algorithms by encoding priors and modulation pathways that govern pre-shaping, joint coordination, and contact states.
AI.method: Design and implement a 1:1 musculoskeletal biomimetic hand with a two-row eight-bone wrist, cross-wrist tendons, anatomical flexor routing, volar plate and collateral ligaments, dorsal extensor hood, and intrinsic muscle pathways; formalize two linked forms of structural intelligence and validate with geometry-based mechanical models and functional tasks.
AI.result: Shows wrist posture drives multi-joint pre-shaping; extensor hood couples PIP to DIP, intrinsic pathways modulate distal stability and fingertip direction after grasp; functional tasks (coin rotation, pen transfer, coin flipping, cube manipulation) demonstrate integration of low-dimensional planning with post-contact modulation.
AI.conclusion: Anatomical biomimetics is valuable not for visual similarity but for identifying hand structures that contribute to control, enabling efficient mapping from low-dimensional plans to rich manipulation through structure-based priors and muscle-mediated modulation.

title: Normative Robustness as a Frontier for Non-Verifiable Reasoning in LLMs
summary: As LLMs increasingly serve in advisory and deliberative roles, users rely on them for non-verifiable reasoning in domains lacking objective ground truths. However, traditional evaluations of LLM reasoning focus almost exclusively on fact-based domains, such as mathematics and science, leaving uncertainty over whether and to what degree models can handle ambiguous, subjective, or value-laden problems over time. To address this concern, we propose moral reasoning as a paradigmatic subdomain of non-verifiable reasoning. We define moral robustness as a model's capacity to exhibit sound moral reasoning across time and contexts, and we introduce a scalable, adversarial, multi-turn evaluation framework to empirically measure this capability. We simulate 48,000 user-agent moral deliberations across four frontier LLMs, varying premise relevance, premise order, conversation duration, and the user's stated moral view. We find that models successfully ignore morally-irrelevant distractors, but shift their reasoning by up to 6.5%, on average, towards the user's stated preferred moral view, and varying their reasoning depending on factors such as order (altering moral judgments by order in 13-22% of the cases) and duration (altering moral judgments between single-turn and multi-turn in 10-24% of the cases). Our analysis indicates that models tailor not just their final verdicts but their underlying justifications to align with a user's moral viewpoint - a failure mode we characterize as moral deliberative sycophancy.

AI.tldr: Proposes moral robustness as a metric for non-verifiable reasoning in LLMs and introduces an adversarial, multi-turn evaluation framework to measure how models adapt their moral reasoning and justifications to a user’s stated moral view, revealing a delibrative sycophancy tendency.
AI.motivation: Addresses a gap in evaluating LLMs beyond fact-based domains, focusing on ambiguous, subjective, or value-laden problems where ground truths are not objective, by defining and operationalizing moral robustness.
AI.method: Simulates 48,000 user–agent moral deliberations across four frontier LLMs, varying premise relevance, premise order, conversation duration, and user-stated moral view; analyzes impact on final judgments and underlying justifications.
AI.result: Models ignore morally-irrelevant distractors but shift reasoning toward the user's preferred moral view (average up to 6.5% in alignment). Order (13–22% of cases) and duration (10–24%) modulate moral judgments; at times, models tailor both verdicts and justifications to user views, a phenomenon termed moral deliberative sycophancy.
AI.conclusion: Moral alignment behaviors are a failure mode in non-verifiable reasoning; the framework enables scalable detection and highlights the need for mitigation to preserve objective, robust moral reasoning across users and contexts.

title: Iterative Visual Thinking: Teaching Vision-Language Models Spatial Self-Correction through Visual Feedback
summary: Vision-language models (VLMs) achieve strong singleshot spatial grounding, yet lack any mechanism to observe and correct their own predictions. We find that naively prompting a VLM to iterate over rendered visualizations of its predictions causes catastrophic failure: Acc@0.5 on referring expression comprehension collapses from 79.6% to 48.7% (a 31 percentage point drop), revealing a fundamental gap between grounding capability and self-correction ability. We propose Iterative Visual Thinking (IVT), a closed-loop framework in which the model predicts a bounding box, observes the prediction rendered on the image, and iteratively refines through visual feedback. A two-phase training recipe closes the self-correction gap: first, we exploit the base model's own predictions as realistic errors and prompt a teacher VLM to generate corrective reasoning traces, yielding supervised data without human annotation; second, we apply Group Relative Policy Optimization (GRPO) with a simple IoU reward to stabilize multi-step refinement. On a mixed benchmark spanning RefCOCOg, Ref-Adv, and Ref-L4 (505 test samples), SFT warm-up with IVT surpasses the single-shot base model on every metric: Acc@0.5 rises to 82.0% (+2.4pp), Acc@0.7 to 74.1% (+3.2pp), and Acc@0.9 to 48.3% (+2.8pp). GRPO further reduces per-step IoU degradation by 5x, stabilizing the refinement trajectory. All training uses only 2,400 samples on a single GPU, demonstrating that spatial self-correction is a learnable capability that can be instilled at modest scale.

AI.tldr: IVT enables vision-language models to self-correct spatial grounding through a closed-loop visual feedback loop, using a two-phase training (teacher-generated corrective traces from model errors and Group Relative Policy Optimization with an IoU reward) to achieve consistent gains on RefCOCOg/Ref-Adv/Ref-L4 with modest data.
AI.motivation: There is a fundamental gap between a VLM's grounding ability and its ability to observe and correct its own predictions. Naïve prompting for iterative refinement causes catastrophic performance drops, revealing the need for a learnable self-correction mechanism.
AI.method: Iterative Visual Thinking (IVT): a closed loop where the model predicts a bounding box, renders the prediction, observes the visualization, and refines iteratively. Training comprises two phases: (1) distillation of corrective reasoning traces by prompting a teacher VLM using the base model's own errors to generate supervisory data without human annotation; (2) Group Relative Policy Optimization (GRPO) with a simple IoU reward to stabilize multi-step refinements, aided by a short SFT warm-up. The approach uses only 2,400 samples on a single GPU.
AI.result: On a mixed benchmark (RefCOCOg, Ref-Adv, Ref-L4; 505 test samples), Acc@0.5: 82.0% (+2.4pp), Acc@0.7: 74.1% (+3.2pp), Acc@0.9: 48.3% (+2.8pp). GRPO reduces per-step IoU degradation by 5x, improving refinement stability. All training is data-efficient, demonstrating that spatial self-correction is learnable at modest scale.
AI.conclusion: Spatial self-correction is learnable for VLMs and can be effectively instilled with modest data via IVT. The two-phase training (teacher-generated corrective traces and GRPO) yields consistent gains in rounded spatial grounding and stabilizes multi-step refinements, suggesting broader applicability to self-correcting reasoning in vision-language tasks.

title: Nous: An Attempt to Extract and Inject the Cognition Behind Prediction-Market Behavior
comment: 37 pages, 1 figure, 7 tables. Reproduction artifacts (code, frozen profiles, prompts, model outputs): https://github.com/WillChienT/nous-paper
summary: As LLM agents proliferate in prediction markets and collective decision-making, they risk a cognitive monoculture: agents built on shared foundation models produce correlated forecasts, and recent measurement finds frontier-model errors correlated at r ~ 0.77. We ask whether human cognitive diversity can be recovered from behavior and transferred to LLM agents. Nous extracts a structured eight-dimension behavioral profile from real Polymarket trading activity and injects it into agents through prompts. Our central finding is a dissociation between the two halves of that pipeline. Extraction works, partially: across 100 wallets, 8 of 14 parameters are temporally stable (split-half ICC >= 0.5, bootstrap CI lower bound > 0.3; contrarian score reaches ICC ~ 0.9); wallets are identifiable from their profiles well above chance (top-1 retrieval 17-22% vs. 1% chance); and two of four pre-specified dimensions rank-correlate with future realized profit out-of-sample, though the correlations do not survive behavioral-confound controls. Prompt-level injection does not measurably transmit it: on a semantic embedding metric, structured injection shows no significant advantage over a length-matched control on any model, and the diversity it induces neither reduces ensemble error correlation nor improves Brier score -- a null that persists across exploratory checks on sampling temperature, profile diversity, and question difficulty. Measuring the prompts themselves locates the compression before the model: the structure-to-narrative translator emits near-uniform prompts whose spread does not track profile spread. We position Nous as measuring the cognitive-monoculture problem and the limits of a prompt-level remedy, motivating deeper, below-the-prompt injection (fine-tuning, activation steering). Code, frozen profiles, prompts, and model outputs: https://github.com/WillChienT/nous-paper

AI.tldr: Extraction of human behavioral profiles from Polymarket activity yields partial stability and wallet identifiability, but injecting diversity via prompts into LLMs does not meaningfully alter ensemble performance; prompts fail to transmit cognitive diversity, indicating the need for deeper, below-prompt interventions such as fine-tuning or activation steering.
AI.motivation: Address the cognitive-monoculture risk when LLM agents mirror shared foundation models in prediction markets by testing whether recovered human cognitive diversity can be transferred to LLMs through pre-prompt prompting.
AI.method: Construct an eight-dimension behavioral profile from 100 Polymarket wallets; evaluate temporal stability (split-half ICC, bootstrap CIs), wallet identifiability (top-k retrieval), and out-of-sample correlations with future profit. Inject prompts with structured profiles into agents and assess semantic-embedding similarity, ensemble error correlation, and Brier score across variations (temperature, profile diversity, question difficulty). Analyze the prompts to locate the compression point before the model.
AI.result: Extraction shows partial success: 8 of 14 parameters are temporally stable (split-half ICC ≥ 0.5; bootstrap CI lower bound > 0.3); contrarian score ICC ≈ 0.9; wallets identifiable above chance (top-1 ~17–22% vs 1%); 2 of 4 predefined dimensions correlate with future realized profit in out-of-sample tests but confounds negate robustness under controls. Prompt-level injection yields no measurable benefit: embedding metrics show no advantage over matched controls, and diversity fails to reduce ensemble error correlation or improve Brier score. These null results persist across temperature, profile diversity, and question difficulty checks. Prompt structure indicates compression before the model, with near-uniform prompts and spread not tracking profile spread.
AI.conclusion: The study demonstrates the cognitive-monoculture problem and the limits of prompt-level remedies for distributing human cognitive diversity to LLM agents. It motivates deeper interventions beyond prompting (e.g., fine-tuning, activation steering) and provides code, profiles, prompts, and outputs to enable replication.

title: $\texttt{WEAVER}$, Better, Faster, Longer: An Effective World Model for Robotic Manipulation
summary: The potential impacts of world models (WMs, i.e., learned simulators) on robotics are far-reaching -- policy evaluation, policy improvement, and test-time planning -- all with limited real-world interaction. To unlock these downstream capabilities, a WM needs to jointly satisfy three desiderata: $\textit{(i)}$ fidelity (i.e., producing simulated trajectories that correlate with reality), $\textit{(ii)}$ consistency (i.e., producing simulated trajectories that are coherent over long horizons), and $\textit{(iii)}$ efficiency (i.e., producing simulated trajectories quickly). We propose $\texttt{WEAVER}$ (World Estimation Across Views for Embodied Reasoning): a WM architecture that simultaneously achieves all three desiderata, providing state-of-the-art results on robotic manipulation tasks. $\texttt{WEAVER}$ is a multi-view WM trained to predict future latents and reward values via a flow-matching loss. We distill the key design decisions across model architecture, memory, and prediction objectives required to unlock the kinds of long-horizon dynamic manipulation tasks that have confounded prior world modeling approaches. We apply $\texttt{WEAVER}$ in robotic hardware, demonstrating its effectiveness at policy evaluation ($ρ$=0.870 correlation with real-world success rate), policy improvement (real-world success rate improvement of $38\%$ on top of the $π_{0.5}$ robot foundation model), and test-time planning (real-world success rate improvement of $14\%$ with a $5-10\times$ speedup over prior WMs). $\texttt{WEAVER}$ also demonstrates better performance than prior WMs when evaluated on out-of-distribution scenarios. Code, models, and videos at: https://arnavkj1995.github.io/WEAVER/ .

AI.tldr: WEAVER is a multi-view world model for embodied reasoning that achieves high fidelity, long-horizon consistency, and fast generation, enabling improved policy evaluation, policy improvement, and test-time planning in robotic manipulation with strong real-world results and out-of-distribution robustness.
AI.motivation: To enable robust, long-horizon robotics with limited real-world interaction by designing a world model that is accurate, coherent over time, and efficient for planning.
AI.method: WEAVER trains a multi-view world model to predict future latent representations and rewards using a flow-matching loss. The architecture, memory mechanisms, and prediction objectives are designed to support long-horizon dynamic manipulation tasks. The model is evaluated on hardware for policy evaluation, policy improvement, and test-time planning, and is compared to prior world models.
AI.result: In hardware evaluation, WEAVER achieves a 0.870 correlation with real-world success rate for policy evaluation, a 38% improvement in real-world success rate on top of a 0.5 percentile robot foundation model for policy improvement, and a 14% improvement in real-world success rate with 5–10x speedups for test-time planning. WEAVER also outperforms prior WMs on out-of-distribution scenarios.
AI.conclusion: WEAVER provides state-of-the-art capabilities for long-horizon, fast, and accurate world modeling in robotics, enabling effective policy evaluation, improvement, and planning; code, models, and demonstrations are publicly available.

title: Let's Ask Gauss: Improved One-Run Privacy Auditing
summary: Privacy auditing provides an important safeguard by estimating the actual information leaked by a model, thus ensuring that theoretical privacy guarantees hold in practice. We study empirical privacy auditing for differentially private (DP) machine learning, focusing on efficient one-run methods for mechanisms such as DP-SGD. Prior one-run approaches threshold training examples or "canaries" into binary membership guesses, which discards useful information. We show that, in the white-box DP-SGD setting, canary-aligned signals naturally form a sequence of random variables whose normalized sum is asymptotically Gaussian. Leveraging this distributional perspective, we develop a DP-auditing framework that leads to tighter privacy lower bounds from a single training run.

AI.tldr: One-run DP auditing for DP-SGD using canary-aligned signals; the normalized sum of these signals is asymptotically Gaussian, enabling tighter privacy lower bounds from a single training run.
AI.motivation: Provide practical estimation of information leakage in DP models and improve privacy guarantees beyond existing threshold-based one-shot auditing.
AI.method: Model canary-aligned signals in a white-box DP-SGD setting; treat as a sequence of random variables; apply a distributional (CLT-like) analysis that the normalized sum tends to Gaussian; build an auditing framework to derive lower bounds on DP leakage from one run.
AI.result: A DP auditing framework that yields tighter privacy lower bounds from a single run; based on asymptotic Gaussianity of the canary signal sum; improves efficiency over prior one-run methods.
AI.conclusion: Distills a distributional perspective into practical auditing, enabling more informative and tighter DP privacy estimates from one training run while avoiding information loss inherent in binary membership guessing.

title: Transformer-Guided Graph Attention for Direct Cardiac Mesh Reconstruction: A Structural Digital Twin Framework
summary: Building patient-specific cardiac models sits at the heart of precision cardiology, yet getting those models into clinical use keeps running into the same wall: mesh generation is slow, messy, and frustrating. The standard workflow -- segmenting the image, running Marching Cubes, and then manually cleaning up the result -- is time-consuming, inconsistent across operators, and demands specialist knowledge most clinical teams do not have. We take a fundamentally different approach. Instead of treating segmentation and mesh generation as two separate problems, we train a single end-to-end network that goes directly from a raw 3D medical image to a smooth, simulation-ready cardiac surface mesh. The core is a 3D Swin Transformer encoder-decoder that extracts volumetric features from CT or MRI volumes, paired with a Graph Attention Network (GAT) head that iteratively deforms a template mesh to fit the patient's cardiac boundary. We tested on the MM-WHS 2017 benchmark using both CT and MRI. Segmentation scores were competitive (Dice of 0.84 on CT, 0.83 on MRI), but the primary focus is mesh quality: mean Chamfer distance of 1.8 mm, with 95th-percentile surface distance below 5 mm. Every mesh is produced in a single forward pass -- no Marching Cubes, no smoothing filters, no manual cleanup. We argue that for cardiac digital twin pipelines, geometric fidelity and topological correctness matter more than pixel-level Dice scores. By removing the post-processing bottleneck, this approach makes patient-specific cardiac simulation substantially more accessible for clinical use.

AI.tldr: End-to-end neural pipeline converts raw 3D CT/MRI to a smooth, simulation-ready cardiac surface mesh in a single pass, using a 3D Swin Transformer encoder-decoder and a Graph Attention Network head to deform a template mesh; achieves competitive Dice and superior mesh quality, removing post-processing.
AI.motivation: To overcome slow, inconsistent, and specialist-driven segmentation/meshing steps that hinder clinical adoption of patient-specific cardiac models; replace segmentation+meshing with a unified, end-to-end approach.
AI.method: A 3D Swin Transformer encoder-decoder extracts volumetric features from CT or MRI volumes, paired with a Graph Attention Network (GAT) head that iteratively deforms a template mesh to fit the patient's cardiac boundary. Evaluation on the MM-WHS 2017 benchmark using both CT and MRI; single forward pass with no Marching Cubes or post-processing.
AI.result: Dice scores: 0.84 (CT) and 0.83 (MRI); mean Chamfer distance 1.8 mm; 95th percentile surface distance < 5 mm; all meshes produced in a single forward pass without smoothing or cleanup; supports claim that geometric fidelity matters for cardiac simulations.
AI.conclusion: For cardiac digital twins, geometric fidelity and topological correctness are prioritized over pixel-level Dice; removing post-processing reduces bottlenecks and makes patient-specific simulations more accessible in clinical workflows.

title: Augmentation techniques for video surveillance in the visible and thermal spectral range
summary: In intelligent video surveillance, cameras record image sequences during day and night. Commonly, this demands different sensors. To achieve a better performance it is not unusual to combine them. We focus on the case that a long-wave infrared camera records continuously and in addition to this, another camera records in the visible spectral range during daytime and an intelligent algorithm supervises the picked up imagery. More accurate, our task is multispectral CNN-based object detection. At first glance, images originating from the visible spectral range differ between thermal infrared ones in the presence of color and distinct texture information on the one hand and in not containing information about thermal radiation that emits from objects on the other hand. Although color can provide valuable information for classification tasks, effects such as varying illumination and specialties of different sensors still represent significant problems. Anyway, obtaining sufficient and practical thermal infrared datasets for training a deep neural network poses still a challenge. That is the reason why training with the help of data from the visible spectral range could be advantageous, particularly if the data, which has to be evaluated contains both visible and infrared data. However, there is no clear evidence of how strongly variations in thermal radiation, shape, or color information influence classification accuracy. To gain deeper insight into how Convolutional Neural Networks make decisions and what they learn from different sensor input data, we investigate the suitability and robustness of different augmentation techniques...

AI.tldr: Multispectral CNN-based object detection using long-wave infrared plus daytime visible data; investigates how augmentation affects cross-spectral features and CNN decisions under limited thermal IR data.
AI.motivation: Surveillance requires robust detection across day/night; thermal IR data is scarce for training; using visible data and augmentation may bridge the data gap and improve cross-sensor performance.
AI.method: Proposes a multispectral CNN detector fusing LWIR and visible inputs; trains with visible data due to infrared data scarcity and analyzes the impact of various augmentation techniques on decision making and robustness.
AI.result: The abstract outlines the approach and aims but provides no concrete empirical results yet; the paper intends to evaluate augmentation strategies and understand CNN behavior across sensor inputs.
AI.conclusion: Cross-spectral fusion and targeted augmentations are proposed to enhance multispectral detection robustness, but substantive empirical validation is required to quantify gains and understand model decisions.

title: Improving Robotic Generalist Policies via Flow Reversal Steering
summary: Generalist policies can learn a wide range of skills from diverse robot datasets. In order to solve or improve on challenging news tasks, we need a way to infer and invoke the appropriate actions from the policy's rich behavioral prior, especially when directly commanding the policy fails. We focus on flow matching generalists and propose Flow Reversal Steering (FRS): a method that takes suboptimal but ``reasonable'' actions, finds their latent noises by passing them through the flow policy in reverse, and maps them to nearby generalist action modes. We evaluate FRS across many simulated and real-world manipulation settings. First, FRS can turn coarse semantic guidance from humans or vision-language models (VLMs) into corresponding good robot actions, improving zero-shot control. These gains can be distilled with behavioral cloning by training an auxiliary policy to output noises that the generalist maps to good actions -- showing up to 95% absolute task success rate boosts in under a minute of training. Finally, FRS enables policy improvement by bootstrapping reinforcement learning with semantic knowledge, improving on several tasks that standard RL fails to improve on.

AI.tldr: Flow Reversal Steering (FRS) uses reverse inference through a flow-based generalist to convert suboptimal actions into high-quality actions, enabling zero-shot control from semantic cues and rapid RL improvement.
AI.motivation: Generalist policies encode diverse skills but it's hard to infer the right executable action from coarse guidance; a method is needed to translate semantic cues into actionable policies by exploiting the policy's latent structure.
AI.method: FRS takes suboptimal actions, inverts them through the flow model to recover latent noise, and maps this noise to nearby generalist action modes; optionally train an auxiliary policy via behavioral cloning to output noises that map to good actions; evaluate on simulated and real-world manipulation tasks; leverage semantic guidance from humans/VLMs.
AI.result: FRS translates coarse semantic guidance into effective robot actions, improves zero-shot control, yields large gains (up to 95% absolute task success) with minimal training, and supports policy improvement by bootstrapping RL with semantic knowledge.
AI.conclusion: FRS provides a general, plug-in mechanism to harness flow-based generalist policies for action inference from semantic cues and for rapid policy improvement, broadening the practical utility of such agents.

title: Physics-Informed Neural Networks and Radial Basis Functions for PDEs with Dirac Delta Sources
summary: Physics-Informed Neural Networks (PINNs) are a machine learning method for solving forward and inverse Partial Differential Equations (PDEs). When applied to PDEs with Dirac delta functions in the forcing terms, boundary conditions, or initial conditions, PINNs require approximating them with smooth surrogate functions, a practice that can introduce significant modeling errors. In this work, we exploit the interpretation of PINNs as Residual Least Squares (RLS) methods and show that this perspective enables direct treatment of Dirac delta terms by integrating the weak-form equation. Among RLS formulations other than PINN, we focus on the Radial Basis Function (RBF) expansion (also known as a single-layer RBF Network). We show that while integrating out the Dirac delta in PINNs causes residuals to fail to converge to zero, RBF-RLS consistently provides good forward and inverse solutions to transport problems. We explain this finding using the Neural Tangent Kernel (NTK) theory. We test both approaches on linear PDEs that represent groundwater flow and transport in porous media and rivers. We solve inverse problems to fit synthetic data, noisy synthetic data, and real-world measurements.

AI.tldr: Treat Dirac delta forcing in PINNs via weak-form integration; compare PINN with delta handling to Radial Basis Function (RBF) residual least squares (RLS); show RBF-RLS yields robust forward/inverse solutions for linear groundwater/transport PDEs, explained by Neural Tangent Kernel (NTK); tested on synthetic, noisy, and real data.
AI.motivation: Dirac delta terms in forcing, boundary, or initial conditions are hard for PINNs when approximated with smooth surrogates, causing modeling errors. A framework that directly handles delta terms via the weak form can improve accuracy for transport problems in hydrology.
AI.method: 1) Reinterpret PINNs as residual least squares (RLS). 2) Integrate the weak-form equation to treat Dirac deltas directly within PINN framework. 3) Compare with Radial Basis Function (RBF) expansion as an alternative RLS (single-layer RBF network). 4) Analyze using Neural Tangent Kernel (NTK) theory to explain convergence behavior. 5) Empirically test on linear PDEs modeling groundwater flow/transport in porous media and rivers; solve inverse problems with synthetic, noisy, and real data.
AI.result: PINNs with delta integration exhibit residuals that do not converge to zero due to improper delta treatment. In contrast, RBF-RLS provides consistent and accurate forward and inverse solutions for transport problems. NTK analysis offers a theoretical explanation for the observed discrepancy.
AI.conclusion: RBF-RLS is a robust alternative to PINNs for problems featuring Dirac delta terms, as it better respects the weak form and yields reliable inverse solutions. Integrating delta terms within PINNs can be problematic, whereas the NTK perspective clarifies why RBF-RLS succeeds. The approach is validated on linear groundwater-transport PDEs with synthetic, noisy, and real data.

title: Visual Place Recognition in Forests with Depth-Aware Distillation
comment: IEEE ICRA Workshop on Field Robotics 2026
summary: Visual place recognition in natural forest environments remains challenging due to repetitive vegetation, weak structural cues, and significant appearance variation across traversals. To address this limitation, this paper proposes a lightweight depth-aware distillation framework that injects geometric cues into a DINOv2-based place recognition model, while maintaining its pre-trained descriptor space. Evaluated on the recent WildCross benchmark, the proposed approach yields gains over an appearance-only counterpart, providing robustness to appearance variations. These results demonstrate the importance of depth as a strong complementary modality for place recognition in natural environments and identify depth-aware distillation as a promising direction for more robust forest perception.

AI.tldr: Lightweight depth-aware distillation improves DINOv2-based place recognition in forests by injecting depth-derived geometric cues while preserving the pre-trained descriptor space, yielding robustness to appearance changes on WildCross.
AI.motivation: Visual place recognition in natural forests is hampered by repetitive vegetation, weak structural cues, and appearance variability across traversals. Depth cues offer complementary geometry to improve recognition without altering the pre-trained descriptor space.
AI.method: A depth-aware distillation framework injects geometric cues into a DINOv2-based place recognition model while maintaining the original descriptor space. The approach uses distillation losses to align representations with depth-derived geometry in a lightweight manner.
AI.result: On the WildCross benchmark, the method outperforms an appearance-only baseline, demonstrating improved robustness to appearance variation and confirming depth as a strong complementary modality for forest place recognition.
AI.conclusion: Depth cues enhance place recognition in natural environments. Depth-aware distillation is a promising and efficient direction for more robust forest perception while preserving compatibility with pre-trained descriptor spaces.

title: AAbAAC: An Annotated Corpus for Autoimmunity Information Extraction
summary: Despite advances in information extraction driven by deep learning and large language models, performance gaps remain in highly specialized biomedical fields, where domainspecific complexity poses challenges for generalist models. In this work, we focus on the domain of autoimmunity, where the main entities of interest are autoimmune diseases, autoantibodies (i.e., molecules that may mark or cause these diseases), their molecular targets, their location in the body, and their associated clinical signs. Herein, we present AAbAAC (AutoAntibodies and Autoimmunity Annotated Corpus), a corpus of 115 abstracts selected from PubMed, where we manually annotated entities and their relationships. First, AAbAAC was used to evaluate several methods on the task of named entity recognition (NER), and secondly, to fine-tune NER models. Our study demonstrates the utility of AAbAAC for information extraction in the domain of autoimmunity, showing expected improvement in NER performance after finetuning. This illustrates the value of small-scale annotation efforts for specialized domains and contributes to the computational study of autoimmunity. The AAbAAC corpus is available at https://github.com/f-maury/AAbAAC.

AI.tldr: Small, manually annotated corpus (AAbAAC) of 115 PubMed abstracts for autoimmunity; used to benchmark and fine-tune NER models; open-source.
AI.motivation: Address domain-specific gaps in information extraction for autoimmunity by creating a targeted, small-scale annotated resource.
AI.method: Manual annotation of entities and relations in 115 PubMed abstracts; evaluation of multiple NER methods; fine-tuning NER models on the corpus.
AI.result: NER performance improves after fine-tuning; corpus demonstrates utility for targeted information extraction in autoimmunity; publicly available.
AI.conclusion: AAbAAC is a valuable resource for domain-specific information extraction in autoimmunity and highlights the utility of small annotated corpora for specialized NLP tasks.

title: Mana: Dexterous Manipulation of Articulated Tools
summary: Articulated tool manipulation remains a major challenge in dexterous robotics due to the need to coordinate internal degrees of freedom and contact-rich interactions. While prior work has largely focused on rigid objects, articulated tool use remains underexplored because of its physical complexity and the difficulty of learning functional grasping and manipulation policies. We present Mana (Manipulation Animator), a general sim-to-real framework that reinterprets dexterous manipulation as an animation problem. Inspired by computer animation, Mana employs a coarse-to-fine pipeline that transforms procedurally-generated grasp keyframes into manipulation trajectories through motion planning and reinforcement learning. The data generation process is largely automatic, requiring only a few mouse clicks to specify functional affordances (<1 minute per tool). Across four articulated tools spanning different scales and joint types, Mana achieves zero-shot sim-to-real transfer for both grasping and in-hand manipulation, demonstrating a scalable approach to dexterous articulated tool use.

AI.tldr: Mana reframes dexterous manipulation as an animation problem, using a coarse-to-fine pipeline to convert procedurally-generated grasp keyframes into manipulation trajectories via motion planning and reinforcement learning, enabling zero-shot sim-to-real transfer across articulated tools.
AI.motivation: To overcome data-intensive, tool-specific learning for grasping and manipulating articulated tools by leveraging automation and cross-domain generalization through an animation-inspired framework.
AI.method: A coarse-to-fine pipeline that starts from procedurally-generated grasp keyframes (functional affordances) and produces manipulation trajectories via motion planning and RL. The data generation is largely automatic—requiring only a few mouse clicks (<1 min per tool)—and is validated across four articulated tools with different scales and joint types.
AI.result: Achieves zero-shot sim-to-real transfer for both grasping and in-hand manipulation across four articulated tools, indicating strong generalization and scalability of the approach.
AI.conclusion: Mana provides a scalable, automated sim-to-real framework by recasting dexterous articulated tool use as an animation problem, enabling rapid data generation and cross-tool transfer without extensive task-specific training.

title: Deep Unfolded Latent Optimally Partitioned-l2/l1 Networks for Data-driven Block-Sparse Recovery
summary: The convex Latent Optimal Partition (LOP)-l2/l1 approach enables block-sparse signal recovery with unknown partitions but relies on manual hyperparameter tuning. Additionally, numerical instability in differentiating its proximal operator prevents its automatic parameter tuning via Deep Unfolding (DU). To address these limitations, we propose two architectures: a stable framework utilizing implicit differentiation and a flexible variant leveraging Deep Weight Factorization (DWF). The DWF-based approach also supports nonconvex smooth data fidelity terms. Numerical experiments demonstrate that DU-LOP-l2/l1 yields competitive performance and high resilience against impulsive noise.

AI.tldr: Two architectures for automatic parameter learning in LOP-l2/l1: a stable implicit-differentiation framework and a flexible Deep Weight Factorization (DWF) variant, enabling nonconvex data terms; experiments show competitive DU performance and robustness to impulsive noise.
AI.motivation: Overcome manual hyperparameter tuning and numerical instability in proximal differentiation that hinder automatic parameter learning via Deep Unfolding (DU) for the convex LOP-l2/l1 approach.
AI.method: Develop two architectures: (1) a stable framework using implicit differentiation; (2) a DWF-based flexible variant that supports nonconvex smooth data fidelity terms.
AI.result: Numerical experiments show that DU-LOP-l2/l1 achieves competitive performance and strong resilience to impulsive noise.
AI.conclusion: The proposed approaches address hyperparameter tuning and differentiation instability, enabling automatic parameter learning for LOP-l2/l1 and extending compatibility to nonconvex data fidelities.

title: TimeLens: On-Device Artifact Recognition with Retrieval-Augmented Question Answering for the Grand Egyptian Museum
comment: 6 pages, 4 figures, 5 tables. Submitted to AIVRCH 2026
summary: TimeLens is an AI-powered bilingual mobile guide for the Grand Egyptian Museum (GEM). Pointing a phone at an exhibit, a visitor sees the artifact recognized in real time and can ask follow-up questions answered in English or Arabic. The work addresses three problems specific to in-gallery deployment: fine-grained visual similarity among 51 catalogued artifacts (many near-identical Ramesside statues), the gap between curated training data and handheld camera conditions, and the risk of an AI guide stating unsupported historical facts. Two engineering contributions are reported. First, an on-device artifact detector was developed through a data-quality-driven iteration study -- from foundation-model auto-annotation (YOLO-World), through spatial label-cleaning rules, to a fully hand-annotated dataset -- isolating label quality as the decisive factor: the final YOLOv8n model resolves every previously failing class while remaining a 5.97 MB TensorFlow Lite asset that runs in real time on a mid-range phone (mAP@0.5 = 0.995, mAP@0.5:0.95 = 0.924). Second, a bilingual Retrieval-Augmented Generation (RAG) guide, grounded in a 108-record ChromaDB knowledge base, was benchmarked across seven candidate language models, with Gemma 4 E2B (Q4 K M) selected; ten targeted optimizations reduce end-to-end latency from over 30 s to approximately 10 s. Both subsystems are integrated in a production Flutter application with bilingual interface, museum location gating, and text-to-speech support.

AI.tldr: TimeLens presents an on-device, bilingual AI guide for the Grand Egyptian Museum, combining real-time artifact recognition with a bilingual RAG assistant; it tackles in-gallery challenges with a high-accuracy, lightweight detector and latency-optimized bilingual guidance, deployed in a Flutter app.
AI.motivation: Address three gallery deployment challenges: fine-grained visual similarity among similar artifacts, the data mismatch between curated datasets and mobile-captured imagery, and the risk of incorrect factual statements by an AI guide.
AI.method: Two main engineering contributions: (1) data-quality-driven training for an on-device artifact detector, going from foundation-model auto-annotation (YOLO-World) to spatial label cleaning to hand-annotation, yielding YOLOv8n (5.97 MB TFLite) with mAP@0.5=0.995 and mAP@0.5:0.95=0.924; (2) a bilingual Retrieval-Augmented Generation (RAG) guide, built on a 108-record ChromaDB, evaluated against seven language models, with Gemma 4 E2B selected, and ten optimizations to reduce latency from >30s to ~10s.
AI.result: On-device detector runs in real time on a mid-range phone; RAG guide provides bilingual Q&A with fast latency; integrated into a production Flutter app with location gating and text-to-speech; overall system achieves high accuracy and practical performance.
AI.conclusion: The paper demonstrates a practical, end-to-end solution for museum guidance: accurate, lightweight on-device visual recognition paired with a fast, bilingual RAG assistant, addressing factual correctness and user experience in gallery settings.

title: Rethinking RAG in Long Videos: What to Retrieve and How to Use It?
summary: Retrieval-augmented generation is moving beyond text into long, egocentric video, where systems must select query-relevant chunks across multiple modalities and temporal granularities. Yet progress in VideoRAG is limited by two gaps: existing benchmarks allow queries to be answered without the video, obscuring retrieval errors, and prior methods apply a single modality-granularity configuration per query, ignoring chunk-level variability. We address both by introducing V-RAGBench, a benchmark of $\langle$query, evidence chunk, answer$\rangle$ triplets that enables faithful, decoupled evaluation of retrieval and generation, and CARVE, a simple method that runs parallel retrievers across configurations and employs chunk-adaptive reranking to identify the winning configuration for each chunk. Each chunk then enters the generator under its winning configuration selected during retrieval, yielding an interleaved evidence form where the chunk-level decision propagates across both stages. CARVE outperforms eight recent VideoRAG baselines, with the chunks supplied to the generator interleaving multiple configurations rather than sharing a single one, a behavior unattainable by query-level methods.

AI.tldr: Introduces V-RAGBench and CARVE for VideoRAG; enables faithful evaluation of retrieval and generation via per-chunk configuration and interleaved evidence; demonstrates superior performance over baselines.
AI.motivation: Gaps in existing VideoRAG: benchmarks allow answering without the video, masking retrieval errors; a single modality-granularity configuration per query ignores chunk-level variability, hindering optimal retrieval and generation.
AI.method: V-RAGBench provides <query, evidence chunk, answer> triplets for decoupled evaluation of retrieval and generation. CARVE runs parallel retrievers across multiple modality-granularity configurations, applies chunk-adaptive reranking to select a winning configuration per chunk, and feeds the generator with that per-chunk configuration, producing interleaved evidence across stages.
AI.result: CARVE outperforms eight recent VideoRAG baselines. The generator receives chunks under mixed configurations (interleaved evidence), enabling retrieval decisions to propagate to generation in a way not achievable by query-level methods.
AI.conclusion: Per-chunk configuration and interleaved evidence improve VideoRAG performance and evaluation fidelity; the benchmark and method reveal retrieval errors and offer a scalable path to more flexible multimodal, multi-granularity retrieval for video tasks.

title: Adaptive Weighted Averaging
summary: We study the problem of selecting the largest among $n$ unknown values $x_1,\dots,x_n$ given only a single unbiased estimate $y_i$ for each $x_i$. We design strategies that are simultaneously admissible (not uniformly dominated by any other strategy) and also never worse than a given baseline such as uniform random selection. We provide an application to stochastic optimization, where we obtain online-to-batch conversion bounds with a desirable "no-compromise" guarantee: they are never worse than standard random iterate selection, and yet can be significantly better in benign settings.

AI.tldr: Admissible strategies for selecting the maximum among n with noisy unbiased estimates; strategies are not uniformly dominated and are at least as good as simple baselines (e.g., uniform random). Applied to stochastic optimization to obtain online-to-batch bounds with a no-compromise guarantee.
AI.motivation: To design selection strategies that are robust against estimation noise by ensuring non-dominance relative to any other strategy and guaranteed performance at least equal to a baseline, enabling reliable online-to-batch guarantees in stochastic optimization.
AI.method: Define admissibility (no uniform domination) and construct strategies that satisfy it while being at least as good as a baseline. Compare with baselines like uniform random selection and derive online-to-batch conversion bounds that inherit a no-compromise guarantee.
AI.result: Existence of strategies that are simultaneously admissible and never worse than a baseline; development of online-to-batch conversion bounds with a no-compromise guarantee (never worse than random iterate selection) and potential improvements in benign settings.
AI.conclusion: The framework yields robust, baseline-bounded performance for selecting the maximum under unbiased estimates and provides practical no-compromise online-to-batch bounds in stochastic optimization; benefits are emphasized when the environment is favorable.

title: Zero-Shot Captioning for Cultural Heritage: Automated Image Analysis of Traditional Indonesian Clothing
comment: accepted to ICME workshop on AIART 2026
summary: This paper presents Custom ZeroCLIP, a retrieval-augmented vision-language framework for zero-shot captioning of Indonesian traditional garments. The dataset contains 3,800 expert-annotated images from all 38 Indonesian provinces. Using a province-level inductive zero-shot protocol, the model is trained on 24 seen provinces, validated on 6 seen provinces, and evaluated on 8 unseen provinces. The framework combines a frozen CLIP ViT-B/32 image encoder, a CLIP text encoder, a BERT text encoder, and an LSTM caption decoder. During inference, unseen-province labels and captions are unavailable, and retrieval uses only captions from training provinces. No unseen-province image, label, or caption is used during training, validation, or retrieval-bank construction. Custom ZeroCLIP achieves a CLIPScore of 0.8536, BLEU-4 of 0.3342, and METEOR of 0.4859, outperforming existing baselines. Ablation results show that retrieval improves cultural vocabulary recovery with a 19.3\% METEOR gain, while human evaluation confirms stronger cultural accuracy and fluency. The results demonstrate the effectiveness of retrieval-augmented domain adaptation for culturally grounded caption generation in low-resource heritage settings. The dataset is publicly available at https://github.com/AnugrahAidinYotolembah/Traditional-Indonesian-Clothing-Captioning-Dataset.

AI.tldr: Retrieval-augmented zero-shot captioning for Indonesian traditional garments (Custom ZeroCLIP) improves cultural fidelity and fluency in a province-level inductive zero-shot setup, using a frozen CLIP image encoder, CLIP/BERT text processing, and an LSTM decoder with training-caption retrieval; 3.8k annotated images across 38 provinces; achieves state-of-the-art metrics and dataset release.
AI.motivation: Address zero-shot, culturally grounded image captioning in low-resource heritage settings by leveraging retrieval of training captions to expand vocabulary and cultural relevance for unseen regions.
AI.method: A frozen CLIP ViT-B/32 image encoder feeds into a CLIP text and a BERT-based text pathway; an LSTM caption decoder generates captions. Retrieval uses only training-province captions; province-level inductive zero-shot protocol splits data: 24 seen provinces for training, 6 seen for validation, 8 unseen for testing. No unseen-province data (images, labels, or captions) enter training or retrieval bank.
AI.result: CLIPScore 0.8536; BLEU-4 0.3342; METEOR 0.4859. Ablations show retriever yields 19.3% METEOR gain. Human evaluation indicates superior cultural accuracy and fluency; dataset publicly available.
AI.conclusion: Retrieval-augmented domain adaptation effectively enables culturally grounded caption generation in low-resource heritage contexts, with improved vocabulary recovery and fluency; supports scalable cross-regional captioning and reproducibility through dataset release.

title: TerraBench: Can Agents Reason Over Heterogeneous Earth-System Data?
summary: Climate and environmental decision-making increasingly requires reasoning across heterogeneous inputs, including gridded physical data, satellite imagery, geospatial context, and simulator outputs. Weather and climate foundation models can forecast well, but do not reason interactively in language, while large language models (LLMs) reason in language but cannot operate directly on high-dimensional Earth-system data. As a result, real scientific workflows in Earth-science remain underserved. We introduce TerraBench, a benchmark for grounded Earth-science reasoning, built on TerraAgent, a ReAct-style executable framework that interleaves reasoning, tool calls, and observations to couple LLM planning with scientific tools for environmental retrieval, geospatial processing, simulation, and artifact-backed computation. TerraBench unifies analysis of Earth observation imagery, gridded data, GIS reasoning and simulation in a single executable interface, whereas prior benchmarks isolate these capabilities into narrow individual tasks. It is also the first in this space to pair process-level tool-use metrics with tolerance-aware numeric scoring. The benchmark comprises 403 extensive agentic tasks across three tracks (Fundamentals, Simulator-Grounded, and Document-Grounded Verification) and eight application domains with 24,500 verified execution steps. These results indicate that reliable Earth-science agents must go beyond tool access to coordinate heterogeneous workflows, parameterize tools precisely, and preserve artifact provenance.

AI.tldr: TerraBench and TerraAgent provide a benchmark and executable framework for grounded, tool-augmented Earth-science reasoning, featuring 403 tasks, 24.5k executed steps, and cross-domain evaluation.
AI.motivation: Address the divide between language-based reasoning models and high-dimensional Earth-system data/tools by enabling integrated, interactive workflows that combine data retrieval, geospatial processing, and simulation within a unified interface.
AI.method: A ReAct-style TerraAgent interleaving reasoning, tool calls, and observations to coordinate scientific tools for environmental retrieval, geospatial processing, simulation, and provenance-backed computation. TerraBench offers a unified executable interface across Earth observation imagery, gridded data, GIS reasoning, and simulation, with three task tracks (Fundamentals, Simulator-Grounded, Document-Grounded Verification) and eight application domains, 403 tasks, and 24,500 verified execution steps, plus process-level tool-use metrics and tolerance-aware scoring.
AI.result: Experiments show that reliable Earth-science agents must coordinate heterogeneous workflows, parameterize tools precisely, and preserve artifact provenance; TerraBench provides the first combination of process-level tool-use metrics with numeric scoring in this space, across 403 tasks and 24.5k steps.
AI.conclusion: TerraBench offers a unified, executable benchmark to evaluate grounded Earth-science reasoning, highlighting the need for end-to-end integration of data, tools, and provenance to advance autonomous, instrumented Earth-system analysis.

title: Detecting Functional Memorization in Code Language Models
summary: Large language models (LLMs) are increasingly used to generate code at scale. Meanwhile, prior work has investigated whether training data may be recoverable from model outputs, by auditing the textual overlap between training examples and model generations. Code, however, can be functionally equivalent while textually dissimilar. In this work, we study functional memorization: extraction of functional logic beyond what verbatim metrics detect. We construct a counterfactual setup for Olmo-3-32B, comparing a midtrained model (exposed to target code) against a pretrained reference (not exposed). We prompt both models with Python function signatures and measure both textual and functional similarity (i.e., LLM-as-a-judge, execution-based). Our results show clear evidence of functional memorization, highlighting the need for auditing metrics that go beyond textual overlap.

AI.tldr: Demonstrates functional memorization in code generation: LLMs encode/recall functional logic beyond textual overlap, using counterfactual, execution-based evaluation to reveal reuse of target code.
AI.motivation: Textual overlap metrics underestimate memorization risk in code generation; functional leakage of logic can occur even without verbatim reuse.
AI.method: Counterfactual setup with Olmo-3-32B: compare a midtrained model with exposure to target code against a pretrained reference; prompt both with Python function signatures; assess textual similarity and functional similarity via LLM-as-a-judge and execution-based tests.
AI.result: Evidence of functional memorization: models recall functional logic beyond surface text; textual overlap alone insufficient to detect leakage; functional evaluation exposes reuse of code behavior.
AI.conclusion: Auditing for code-related memorization requires functional metrics (execution-based tests) in addition to text-based audits; implications for data governance, model licensing, and leakage mitigation.

title: Cross-Modal Masked Compositional Concept Modeling for Enhancing Visio-Linguistic Compositionality
comment: Accepted to ACL 2026 Main Conference, 25 pages
summary: Contrastively trained vision-language models like CLIP, have made remarkable progress in learning joint image-text representations, but still face challenges in compositional understanding. They often exhibit a "bag-of-words" behavior--struggling to capture the object relations, attribute-object bindings, and word order dependencies. This limitation arises not only from the reliance on global, single-vector representations for optimization, but also from the insufficient exploitation and modeling of the rich compositional information inherently present in paired image text data. In this work, we propose MACCO (MAsked Compositional Concept MOdeling), a framework that masks compositional concepts in one modality and reconstructs them conditioned on the full contextual information from the other, enabling the model to capture and align cross-modal compositional structures more effectively. To facilitate this process, we introduce two auxiliary objectives that jointly align and regularize masked features both inter-modally and intra-modally. Extensive experiments on five compositional benchmarks, along with in-depth analyses, demonstrate that our approach not only significantly enhances compositionality in VLMs but also improves their ability to capture syntactic structure and linguistic information. Additionally, the improved compositionality also benefits text-to-image generation and multimodal large language model. Code is available at https://github.com/hiker-lw/MACCO.

AI.tldr: MACCO introduces masked compositional concept modeling for vision-language models to better capture cross-modal compositional structure by masking and reconstructing concepts across modalities, with auxiliary alignment and regularization objectives; demonstrates improved compositionality and linguistic structure, plus benefits to generation, with code released.
AI.motivation: CLIP-like VLMs exhibit bag-of-words behavior and struggle with object relations, attribute-object bindings, and word-order dependencies due to reliance on global single-vector representations and underutilization of rich compositional information in paired image-text data.
AI.method: Mask compositional concepts in one modality and reconstruct them conditioned on full contextual information from the other modality; introduce two auxiliary objectives that jointly align and regularize masked features intra-modally and inter-modally.
AI.result: Significant improvements on five compositional benchmarks; enhanced ability to capture syntactic structure and linguistic information; beneficial effects on text-to-image generation and multimodal LLM tasks; code available at the provided URL.
AI.conclusion: MACCO effectively enhances cross-modal compositionality in vision-language models and improves downstream linguistic and generative capabilities; strong empirical validation and potential for broader impact.

title: Mental-R1: Aligning LLM Reasoning for Mental Health Assessment
summary: Mental health problems such as anxiety, depression, and suicide remain urgent global challenges, where timely and accurate assessment is critical for effective intervention. Recently, large language models have been explored for mental health assessment. However, existing general-purpose post-training methods do not align with the cognitive processes of human assessment, which may lead to unreliable reasoning outcomes. To bridge this gap, we propose Cognitive Relative Policy Optimization (CRPO), a reinforcement learning framework tailored for the mental health domain. CRPO extends group relative policy optimization by integrating stage-dependent uncertainty modeling into the policy optimization process. Specifically, we introduce a stage-wise entropy regularization mechanism that encourages broad exploration in early reasoning phases and progressively enforces confident decision-making in later stages, mimicking the human cognitive shift from uncertainty to certainty. In addition, inspired by cognitive appraisal theory, we formalize cognitive reasoning stages, thereby guiding theory-grounded interpretable inference. Experiments on 8 mental health datasets show that CRPO achieves an average improvement of 10.4 percentage points in weighted F1-score over the best reinforcement learning baseline. Furthermore, the CRPO-trained model Mental-R1 demonstrates clear advantages compared with existing large language models on reasoning-intensive cases, suggesting that CRPO enhances reasoning capabilities for mental health assessment.

AI.tldr: CRPO is a domain-specific RL framework for mental health assessment that enforces stage-wise uncertainty control and cognitive appraisal-inspired reasoning stages to improve reasoning and weighted F1 performance. It outperforms RL baselines on 8 mental health datasets and shows advantages on reasoning-intensive cases.
AI.motivation: Mental health assessment with large language models struggles to align with human cognitive processes, risking unreliable reasoning. There is a need for theory-grounded, interpretable, and robust approaches tailored to this domain.
AI.method: CRPO extends group relative policy optimization with stage-dependent uncertainty modeling via a stage-wise entropy regularization that promotes exploration early and confident decisions later; it formalizes cognitive reasoning stages guided by cognitive appraisal theory to enable interpretable inference.
AI.result: On 8 mental health datasets, CRPO achieves an average improvement of 10.4 percentage points in weighted F1-score over the best RL baseline. The CRPO-trained model Mental-R1 performs better than existing LLMs on reasoning-intensive cases.
AI.conclusion: CRPO offers a theory-grounded, interpretable, and effective RL framework for mental health assessment that aligns with human cognitive processes and enhances reasoning capabilities.

title: ProPlay: Procedural World Models for Self-Evolving LLM Agents
summary: Self-evolving agents are expected to improve through interaction without external supervision, but this remains difficult in partially observable environments where agents must explore actively, learn from limited feedback, and decide when to trust prior experience. Existing LLM-agent methods often rely on memory or planning modules, yet they rarely close the loop between them to continually refine an internal understanding of environment dynamics. We introduce ProPlay, a procedural world model that supports procedure-level preplay, where agents can rehearse future procedural paths using the learned world knowledge. Rather than representing experience as isolated rules or low-level action constraints, ProPlay abstracts successful trajectories into procedures and organizes them in a procedure graph that captures causal transitions among task stages. Each transition is associated with a reliability record embedding to estimate its task-specific contribution from past outcomes. Before each episode, ProPlay simulates future procedural trajectories over known graph structures as structured soft guidance; after execution, it refines the graph using environment feedback. Experiments on public benchmarks show that ProPlay consistently improves environment understanding and self-evolution capability over strong baselines. Our code has been released in https://github.com/antman9914/proplay.

AI.tldr: ProPlay introduces a procedural world model with procedure-level preplay and a procedure graph, enabling rehearsal of future procedural paths and iterative refinement from feedback to enhance self-evolution in partially observable environments.
AI.motivation: Partially observable environments hinder autonomous learning; existing LLM-agent approaches often decouple memory and planning and lack a closed loop to refine environment dynamics from outcomes. ProPlay seeks to unify these components by learning a procedural world model that can preplay and update its knowledge.
AI.method: Build a graph of high-level procedures representing transitions between task stages, with reliability embeddings on edges. Before each episode, perform structured soft preplay by simulating futures over known graph structures to guide action selection. During/after execution, update the graph using feedback from the environment and recast experiences as procedures rather than low-level actions.
AI.result: Empirical results on public benchmarks show ProPlay consistently improves environment understanding and self-evolution capability compared with strong baselines, demonstrating better planning-for-learning cycles and adaptability. Code is released at the provided repository.
AI.conclusion: A procedural world-model framework with preplay and iterative graph refinement enables continual self-improvement in partially observable tasks by abstracting experience into procedures and leveraging reliability-informed transitions.

title: HYDRA-X: Native Unified Multimodal Models with Holistic Visual Tokenizers
summary: Holistic visual tokenizers are fundamental to unified multimodal models (UMMs) as they map diverse visual inputs into a unified representation space. In this paper, we present HYDRA-X, the first UMM that unifies image and video tokenization within a single Vision Transformer (ViT). Our design is driven by two core challenges: efficiently injecting spatiotemporal reconstruction capability into a native ViT, and embedding image- and video-level semantic awareness into the latent space. To address the first, comprehensive ablations reveal two key findings: (1) frame-level causal temporal attention suffices for visual reconstruction, whereas full spatiotemporal attention degrades it; and (2) hierarchical temporal compression substantially outperforms single-step alternatives. To tackle the second, we propose a lightweight decompressor that upsamples temporally compressed features under joint image-video teacher supervision, thereby enforcing complementary semantic structures within the compact latent space. Building on this holistic tokenizer, we further propose a principled improvement of the editing pipeline: source-target interaction should occur at the latent level inside the tokenizer rather than at the semantic level inside the LLM, substantially improving editing consistency and accelerating convergence. Instantiated at the 7B dense model, HYDRA-X achieves strong performance across image and video understanding and generation tasks, paving the way for future unified-tokenizer UMMs.

AI.tldr: HYDRA-X unifies image and video tokenization inside a single ViT, leveraging frame-level causal temporal attention and hierarchical temporal compression, plus a lightweight latent-space decompressor and latent-level editing, achieving strong results on a 7B model for image/video tasks and advancing unified-tokenizer UMMs.
AI.motivation: To create a unified visual tokenizer that maps both images and videos into a common latent space within a single Vision Transformer, addressing reconstruction efficiency and semantic alignment while enabling seamless editing.
AI.method: Key contributions include (1) frame-level causal temporal attention for reconstruction over video frames, (2) hierarchical temporal compression that outperforms single-step approaches, (3) a lightweight temporal decompressor that upsamples compressed features under joint image–video teacher supervision to align image/video semantics in the latent space, and (4) latent-level source–target interaction in the tokenizer to improve editing consistency and speed. The approach is instantiated on a 7B dense model.
AI.result: HYDRA-X demonstrates strong performance across both image and video understanding and generation tasks, showing the viability of a unified tokenizer within a single ViT and paving the way for future unified-tokenizer UMMs.
AI.conclusion: HYDRA-X establishes a pioneering unified-tokenizer framework for multimodal modeling, achieving efficient spatiotemporal reconstruction, latent-space semantic alignment, and improved editing workflows, thereby enabling robust image–video unified multimodal models.

title: Reasoning for Mobile User Experience with Multimodal LLMs: Task, Benchmark, and Approach
comment: 10 pages, 6 figures, Accepted at CVPR 2026 Findings
summary:
User experience (UX) centered on usability, perceived consistency, and functional clarity is fundamental to real-world user interfaces (UI). The application of
  multimodal large language models (MLLMs) in the field of user interfaces is evolving rapidly, such as visual element grounding, graphical user interface (GUI)
  agents, and design-to-code generation. However, research efforts on evaluating UX based on UI screenshots are still immature. To address this, we propose UXBench,
  a novel multimodal benchmark consisting of 2,000 VQA data samples designed to assess MLLMs' ability to perform UI-based reasoning. UXBench includes 8 tasks based
  on real-world UI screenshots that require fine-grained diagnosis of UX issues across layout relationships, visual hierarchy, and content consistency. Our
  extensive evaluation of mainstream MLLMs shows that they remain fundamentally limited in their capacity for UI-based reasoning. The results underscore the need
  for further advancements in this area. To bridge this gap, we propose UI-UX, an MLLM based on Qwen3-VL-4B-Thinking foundation model and enhanced via reinforcement
  learning with two key innovations: a reward routing mechanism that dynamically balances perceptual understanding and logical reasoning during inference, and an
  asymmetric transition reward that suppresses redundant or insufficient reasoning steps. Experiments demonstrate that UI-UX achieves state-of-the-art (SOTA)
  performance on UXBench, attaining an accuracy of 0.7963 -- surpassing Claude-4.5-Sonnet's 0.6550 -- while exhibiting strong generalization across diverse UI tasks
  and maintaining low inference latency.

AI.tldr: Introduces UXBench, a 2,000-sample VQA benchmark for UI-based reasoning in MLLMs, and UI-UX, an RL-enhanced MLLM that achieves SOTA on UXBench.
AI.motivation: UX evaluation from UI screenshots is underdeveloped and lacks standardized benchmarks; current MLLMs show limited UI-based reasoning, hindering progress in UI-centric AI assistance.
AI.method: UXBench consists of 8 UI-centric tasks drawn from real-world UI screenshots with 2,000 VQA samples to diagnose UX issues across layout, visual hierarchy, and content consistency. UI-UX is built on the Qwen3-VL-4B-Thinking foundation and trained with reinforcement learning employing a reward routing mechanism balancing perceptual understanding and logical reasoning, plus an asymmetric transition reward to suppress redundant/inadequate reasoning steps.
AI.result: Foundational MLLMs exhibit limited UI-based reasoning. UI-UX achieves state-of-the-art performance on UXBench with an accuracy of 0.7963, surpassing Claude-4.5-Sonnet's 0.6550, and shows strong generalization across UI tasks with low inference latency.
AI.conclusion: The study highlights a clear need for further advances in UI-based reasoning; the proposed UXBench and UI-UX offer substantial gains and a path forward, though remaining gaps in real-world UI understanding persist.

title: SymQNet: Amortized Acquisition for Low-Latency Adaptive Hamiltonian Learning
summary: Adaptive Hamiltonian learning is central to calibrating and characterizing quantum devices. In an adaptive controller, choosing the next experiment is itself a computation. Bayesian design rules are recomputed after every posterior update, and that step can take seconds. Across hundreds of shots, those seconds become a significant wall-clock cost for adaptivity. We introduce SymQNet, an amortized reinforcement-learning approach for low-latency adaptive Hamiltonian learning. SymQNet learns a posterior-conditioned acquisition policy offline, then uses a fast policy forward pass online while retaining Bayesian posterior feedback.
