On transverse-field Ising benchmarks, SymQNet substantially reduces acquisition latency relative to bounded Fisher-information search and bounded two-step Bayesian active learning by disagreement (BALD). At five qubits, it reduces acquisition-only decision latency by $47.1\times$ and $72.6\times$ relative to these online baselines; at twelve qubits, full simulated steps take $1.02$ s for SymQNet versus $13.27$ s for bounded two-step BALD. Overall, we show that learned acquisition can make adaptive Hamiltonian learning practical for repeated low-latency workloads.

AI.tldr: Amortized RL (SymQNet) learns a posterior-conditioned acquisition policy offline to enable fast online decisions in adaptive Hamiltonian learning, achieving large latency reductions on Ising benchmarks and enabling practical low-latency adaptivity.
AI.motivation: Bayesian design in adaptive experiments incurs expensive recomputation after each posterior update; wall-clock latency limits scalability.
AI.method: Offline training of a policy conditioned on the current Bayesian posterior; online fast forward pass with posterior feedback; evaluated on transverse-field Ising model benchmarks; comparison with bounded Fisher-information search and BALD.
AI.result: Acquisition latency reduced by factors up to ~47x-72x at five qubits; full steps ~1.02 s vs 13.27 s at twelve qubits; overall demonstrates practicality of low-latency adaptive learning.
AI.conclusion: Learned acquisition can render adaptive Hamiltonian learning feasible for repeated low-latency workloads.

title: DuET: Dual Expert Trajectories for Diffusion Image Editing
summary: Recent diffusion editors perform diverse instruction-based edits while conditioning on the source image at every denoising step. Yet persistent source-image conditioning can limit how fully an edit is executed and how natural the result appears, especially when the target scene diverges substantially from the input. We introduce DuET (Dual Expert Trajectories), a training-free inference method that temporarily relaxes source-image conditioning by transitioning through a text-to-image phase before returning to edit mode, allowing the denoising trajectory to move toward the target distribution while retaining the structural benefits of image-conditioned editing. Without modifying model weights or increasing sampling cost, DuET consistently improves instruction relevance, semantic fidelity, and perceptual quality across diverse models and benchmarks. In some cases, these gains come with a modest reduction in source-image preservation, revealing a predictable trade-off between source preservation and edit fidelity.

AI.tldr: DuET is a training-free inference method that temporarily relaxes source-image conditioning by inserting a text-to-image phase during denoising, allowing the edit trajectory to better reach the target distribution while preserving structure; it improves instruction relevance, semantic fidelity, and perceptual quality with a predictable trade-off: slight loss of source preservation in some cases.
AI.motivation: Persistent source-image conditioning in diffusion-based editors can hinder substantial edits that diverge from the input image; there is a need for inference-time strategies to steer the denoising process toward the target distribution without retraining or increasing cost.
AI.method: During inference, DuET temporarily introduces a text-to-image phase to relax source conditioning, then returns to edit mode, effectively creating dual expert trajectories (text-guided and image-guided) without modifying model weights or sampling cost; the approach aims to steer the denoising path toward the target distribution while retaining image-conditioned structure.
AI.result: Across diverse models and benchmarks, DuET consistently improves instruction relevance, semantic fidelity, and perceptual quality of edits; in some cases, gains come with a modest reduction in source-image preservation, indicating a predictable trade-off between source preservation and edit fidelity.
AI.conclusion: DuET offers a training-free, low-cost mechanism to enhance editing quality in diffusion-based editors by decoupling and re-aligning the denoising trajectory through a temporary text-to-image phase, with broadly positive effects and manageable trade-offs.

title: Under What Conditions Can a Machine Become Genuinely Creative?
summary: Recent AI systems can generate texts, software architectures, hypotheses, designs, and scientific workflows that appear creative. This paper asks under what conditions a machine can become genuinely creative, and how human agency can be preserved within shared cognitive and creative environments. It develops a requirement framework derived from Designics, the science of meaning-bearing intentional change. The paper argues that genuine machine creativity should not be defined by output novelty, current performance, or transient architecture alone. Instead, creativity is understood as the structural transformation of incomplete situations through recursive intervention dynamics. On this view, it depends on ten requirements: environment representation, scoped perception, conflict identification, intervention capability, consequence observation, knowledge and environment update, rescoping, local-to-global unfolding, value-based scoping, and human-AI co-living. These are organized through the three laws of Designics: perception, conflict, and capability. The paper illustrates the computational tractability of these requirements through selected cyber-physical and cyber-biological studies, including recursive element extraction, autonomous mesh generation, and neurophysiological and workload analysis. It then treats open-ended systems, automated discovery frameworks, self-modifying agents, foundation models, and agentic workflows as pressure cases: they demonstrate powerful generative means but do not by themselves establish genuine machine creativity. Finally, the paper argues that proactive AI ethics is internal to genuine machine creativity rather than an after-the-fact filter. Value-based scoping and human-AI co-living must shape how creative machines perceive environments, identify conflicts, select interventions, observe consequences, update knowledge, and rescope future action.

AI.tldr: A Designics-based framework proposes genuine machine creativity as recursive, environment-aware intervention dynamics, not just novelty or capability, with ten requirements organized under three laws, demonstrated via cyber-physical/biological studies and pressure cases; ethics is intrinsic to creativity and human-AI co-living is essential.
AI.motivation: To redefine machine creativity beyond output novelty, ensuring human agency and ethical alignment within ongoing, meaning-bearing, and potentially self-modifying intelligent systems.
AI.method: Introduce a ten‑requirement framework (environment representation, scoped perception, conflict identification, intervention capability, consequence observation, knowledge/environment update, rescoping, local-to-global unfolding, value-based scoping, human‑AI co-living) organized by three laws of Designics (perception, conflict, capability); illustrate with selected cyber-physical and cyber-biological studies; assess open-ended systems and foundation models as pressure cases; argue for internalization of proactive AI ethics.
AI.result: A conceptual framework for genuine machine creativity with demonstrated computational tractability in select domains; open-ended systems and agents can generate power but do not alone ensure genuine creativity; ethics is integral and must guide perception, conflict handling, and action in creative loops.
AI.conclusion: Genuine machine creativity emerges from structural transformation of incomplete situations via recursive intervention, governed by environment-aware perception, conflict processing, and capability development, with humans and AI co-living as an essential, value-guided component.

title: Scale Buys Interpolation, Structure Buys a Horizon: Certified Predictability for Equivariant World Models
summary:
Scale buys interpolation; structure buys a certified horizon. A world model's average error says nothing about whether a particular prediction can be trusted, or for how long. For equivariant latent world models we give a computable, multi-step certificate of the predictable horizon: $T$-step rollout error is provably constant over each symmetry orbit (Theorem A) and stratified channel-by-channel by the predictor's Lyapunov spectrum, $T_j(ε)\sim\log(1/ε)/λ_j$. The horizon is two-sided -- a matching lower bound makes approximate equivariance provably horizon-limited -- and the certificate is exclusive to structure: orbit-constant error characterizes equivariance, so no non-equivariant model has it at any scale. Empirically, on 40-D Lorenz-96 only a $\mathbb{Z}_N$-equivariant network recovers the full Lyapunov spectrum ($R^2{=}0.98$); dense and recurrent baselines fail.
  Because the spectrum is faithful, the certificate acts, a priori: under a fixed sensing budget a $c\times$-inflated certificate provably needs $c\times$ the budget, and the equivariant certificate meets a budget its inflated dense counterpart cannot -- with zero calibration data. The same read-out, unchanged, audits public pretrained world models training-free: TD-MPC2 checkpoints land on the certificate's own scope taxonomy -- calibrated where strongly expansive (ratio 0.94-1.02), optimistic where weakly expansive, correctly abstaining where contracting -- a map a deployed monitor replicates cell-by-cell, out-of-sample. Across the official 1M-317M multitask ladder, calibration does not improve with parameters. On V-JEPA 2-AC (1B, real robot data) the measured cross-check correctly overrides an over-promising tangent spectrum -- the cross-validated audit, not the raw number, is the deployable object. Scale buys interpolation, not a calibrated horizon.

AI.tldr: Equivariant latent world models admit a computable, symmetry-aware horizon certificate: the T-step rollout error is orbit-constant and stratified by the predictor's Lyapunov spectrum, yielding two-sided horizon bounds and a certificate exclusive to equivariance. Empirically validated on Lorenz-96 and large pretrained models, with calibration-free cross-checks that monitor deployed systems.
AI.motivation: To provide reliable, computable guarantees for multi-step predictions from world models by exploiting symmetry (equivariance) and dynamical-system theory (Lyapunov spectrum), addressing the gap where average forecast error does not reveal trustworthiness or horizon of validity.
AI.method: Formulate a T-step rollout error bound that is constant along symmetry orbits for equivariant latent predictors. Use Lyapunov spectrum to stratify error: T_j(ε) ~ log(1/ε)/λ_j. Prove a two-sided horizon bound and show the error certificate is exclusive to equivariant structure (orbit-constant error). Validate on 40-D Lorenz-96 showing recovery of full Lyapunov spectrum only with a Z_N-equivariant network. Apply the certificate to large pretrained models and real-robot data without extra calibration, using cross-check audits (training-free) to assess deployment-readiness.
AI.result: Theorem A establishes orbit-constant T-step rollout error for equivariant models and a spectrum-based horizon scaling. The horizon is provably two-sided with lower bounds. Empirically, a Z_N-equivariant network recovers the full Lyapunov spectrum on 40-D Lorenz-96 (R^2=0.98); dense/recurrent baselines fail. Calibration-free audits align with certificate across large model suites (1M-317M multitask ladder, TD-MPC2, V-JEPA 2-AC). The certificate remains exclusive to equivariance; non-equivariant models lack the orbit-constant error property. Scale improves interpolation but does not inherently extend a calibrated horizon; cross-validated audits are the deployable proxy.
AI.conclusion: Equivariant structure provides a computable horizon certificate for world-model predictions, enabling reliable deployment through calibration-free audits. Scaling models enhances interpolation capacity without guaranteeing horizon calibration, underscoring the primacy of symmetry-aware guarantees for trustworthy prediction in complex dynamics.

title: CLARITree: Cholesky and Lookahead Accelerations for Regression with Interpretable Piecewise Linear Trees
summary: Regression trees are among the most interpretable yet expressive model classes in machine learning. Historically, greedy induction has been the dominant approach for constructing well-performing regression trees. While optimal methods based on dynamic programming and branch-and-bound exist, they are computationally prohibitive for general linear regression trees, despite often achieving substantially better performance than greedy approaches. Recent work has shown that specialized lookahead strategies can dramatically improve runtime while maintaining near-optimal performance, primarily in classification settings. In this work, we develop a novel algorithm for near-optimal, sparse, piecewise linear regression trees that combines a lookahead-style search strategy with efficient rank-one Cholesky updates of the Gram matrix. We demonstrate, both theoretically and empirically, that our method achieves a favorable trade-off between computational efficiency, predictive accuracy, and sparsity, and scales significantly better than the current state of the art.

AI.tldr: Near-optimal, sparse, piecewise linear regression trees achieved via a lookahead search combined with rank-one Cholesky updates, offering a favorable accuracy–efficiency–sparsity trade-off.
AI.motivation: Greedy regression-tree induction is fast but often suboptimal; exact dynamic-programming/branch-and-bound methods for general PLRTs are computationally prohibitive. There is a need for scalable algorithms that approach optimal performance while enforcing sparsity.
AI.method: Introduce a lookahead-style search for PLRTs together with efficient rank-one Cholesky updates of the Gram matrix to enable fast, sparse, interpretable models.
AI.result: The approach attains near-optimal accuracy with significant gains in sparsity and computational efficiency, scaling more favorably than current state-of-the-art methods, with theoretical guarantees and empirical validation.
AI.conclusion: The proposed algorithm delivers a practical, scalable framework for near-optimal, sparse, piecewise linear regression trees, balancing accuracy, sparsity, and efficiency.

title: ReFree: Towards Realistic Co-Speech Video Generation via Reward-Free RL and Multilevel Speech Guidance
summary: Speech-driven talking character animation seeks to generate life-like portrait videos that convey natural conversation behavior, aligning facial motion with spoken audio. Although recent advances in video generation have substantially improved realism in video-based animation, achieving both accurate lip articulation and expressive behavior remains challenging. Existing approaches typically trade off precise phoneme-to-lip synchronization against dynamic facial expressions and head motion, yielding animations that are either accurate yet rigid, or expressive but poorly synchronized. We address this challenge by proposing ReFree-S2V, a flow-matching speech-to-portrait animation framework that builds upon a pretrained video generation model to achieve fine-grained speech articulation and high-level expressive cues in speech-driven portrait animation. This model introduces a multi-level speech representation capturing phonetic and prosodic information at both local and global granularities. These representations are selectively injected into transformer blocks via learnable level selectors, enabling both accurate lip synchronization and natural expressive motion. To achieve natural head movements, we further introduce a novel reward-free reinforcement learning scheme into flow-matching training to discourage perceptually implausible motion without relying on handcrafted synchronization metrics or reward models, or the high cost of human preference annotation. Extensive experiments demonstrate that ReFree-S2V achieves state-of-the-art performance, significantly outperforming existing methods in both quantitative lip-sync accuracy and qualitative human evaluations of naturalness and expressivity.

AI.tldr: Proposes ReFree-S2V, a flow-matching speech-to-portrait animation framework that uses multi-level speech representations injected via learnable level selectors into a pretrained video generator, plus a reward-free RL scheme for natural head motion; achieves state-of-the-art lip-sync and expressivity.
AI.motivation: Existing methods trade lip-sync precision with expressive motion; there is a need for simultaneous accurate articulation and natural expressive behavior without handcrafted metrics or costly human preference data.
AI.method: Flow-matching training on a pretrained video generation model; introduces multi-level speech representations capturing local phonetic and global prosodic information; selectively injects these representations into transformer blocks via learnable level selectors; incorporates a reward-free reinforcement learning scheme to discourage perceptually implausible motion without requiring explicit reward models or human annotations.
AI.result: Extensive experiments demonstrate state-of-the-art performance, with significant improvements in quantitative lip-sync accuracy and qualitative evaluations of naturalness and expressivity compared to existing methods.
AI.conclusion: ReFree-S2V effectively integrates multi-level speech cues and a reward-free RL approach to achieve accurate lip articulation and natural expressive dynamics in speech-driven portrait animation, validating the proposed flow-matching framework.

title: ARMOR-MAD: Adaptive Routing for Heterogeneous Multi-Agent Debate in Large Language Model Reasoning
summary: Multi-agent debate (MAD) can improve large language model reasoning, but fixed debate pipelines often waste computation and can amplify correlated errors among similar agents. We propose ARMOR-MAD, a training-free heterogeneous MAD framework that treats debate as conditional computation. ARMOR-MAD combines three components: Pre-debate Agreement Routing (PAR) decides whether independently generated Round-0 answers require debate; Early Agreement Stopping Evaluator (EASE) stops debate after convergence; and Semantic Outlier Detection (SOD) down-weights abnormal final answers during aggregation. Across MATH Level 5, GSM8K, MMLU, and MMLU-Pro, ARMOR-MAD consistently improves over fixed-round heterogeneous debate with the same model pool, reaching 65.5\%, 96.5\%, 90.0\%, and 81.5\% accuracy, respectively. The results suggest that genuine model heterogeneity and agreement-based control are both important for making MAD more accurate and efficient.

AI.tldr: ARMOR-MAD is a training-free heterogeneous multi-agent debate framework that uses conditional computation to improve reasoning. It introduces three components—PAR, EASE, and SOD—to route, stop, and down-weight results—achieving strong accuracy gains across MATH Level 5, GSM8K, MMLU, and MMLU-Pro with the same model pool.
AI.motivation: Fixed, uniform debate pipelines waste computation and can propagate correlated errors among similar agents. Harnessing genuine model heterogeneity and agreement-based control can make MAD both more accurate and efficient.
AI.method: Three components: (1) Pre-debate Agreement Routing (PAR) decides whether Round-0 answers require debate; (2) Early Agreement Stopping Evaluator (EASE) stops debate after convergence; (3) Semantic Outlier Detection (SOD) down-weights abnormal final answers during aggregation. The framework is training-free and relies on a heterogeneous pool of models; debate is treated as conditional computation with an agreement-based control loop.
AI.result: On MATH Level 5, GSM8K, MMLU, and MMLU-Pro, ARMOR-MAD achieves 65.5%, 96.5%, 90.0%, and 81.5% accuracy respectively, consistently outperforming fixed-round heterogeneous debate with the same model pool.
AI.conclusion: Genuine model heterogeneity plus agreement-based routing and stopping are key to improving MAD performance and efficiency; ARMOR-MAD demonstrates that training-free, conditional computation can yield substantial accuracy gains with heterogeneous debaters.

title: TimeROME-DLM: Temporal Causal Tracing and Low-Rank Inference-Time Knowledge Editing for Masked Diffusion Language Models
summary: Masked diffusion language models (MDLMs) such as LLaDA now rival autoregressive (AR) LLMs, but every existing knowledge-editing and unlearning method (ROME, MEMIT, etc.) targets AR transformers and either makes assumptions that fail under iterative denoising, or requires gradient updates whose backward-pass activations cost tens of GB of extra VRAM and which collapse MDLMs at standard learning rates. We introduce TimeROME-DLM, the first training-free, gradient-free, inference-time knowledge-editing framework for MDLMs. It couples two components: a Temporal Indirect Effect (TIE) causal-tracing protocol that identifies, for each fact, the coordinate whose intervention most strongly drives the object prediction at later denoising steps; and a closed-form, low-rank residual edit memory that aggregates subject keys and target deltas across all forget facts and applies a single ridge-regularised update at that coordinate at every diffusion forward, with sparsification to limit utility spillover. Backbone weights stay frozen; only three hyperparameters (alpha, lambda, q) are tuned on a small validation split. On TOFU forget01 with TOFU-finetuned LLaDA-8B-Base, TimeROME-DLM cuts forget-set log-probability by roughly 83 nats. The same configuration transfers to LLaDA-8B-Instruct, Dream-7B, MMaDA-8B, DiffuLLaMA-7B, and LLaDA-MoE-1.4B. It keeps retain-set log-probability nearly flat (within ~1 nat at the utility-safe operating point) across 50 sequentially inserted facts, delivers a four- to fourteen-fold wall-clock speedup with zero additional VRAM over the strongest converged training-time baseline, and scales sub-linearly to 400 facts. TimeROME-DLM closes the locate-then-edit gap between AR LLMs and MDLMs at a fraction of the computational cost.

AI.tldr: TimeROME-DLM is a training-free, inference-time knowledge-editing method for masked diffusion language models (MDLMs) that uses a causal-tracing-based coordinate update and a low-rank residual memory to edit facts without gradient updates, delivering large forget-set edits with little impact on retain-set performance, significant speedups, and good scaling across models.
AI.motivation: MDLMs are competitive with autoregressive LLMs, but existing knowledge-editing/unlearning methods target AR transformers and rely on gradient-based updates or assumptions that break under iterative denoising. A training-free, gradient-free, inference-time edit mechanism for MDLMs is needed.
AI.method: Two-part framework: (1) Temporal Indirect Effect (TIE) causal tracing to identify, for each fact, the coordinate whose intervention most strongly drives the target at later denoising steps; (2) a closed-form, low-rank residual edit memory that aggregates subject keys and target deltas for forget facts and applies a single ridge-regularised update at the identified coordinate during each diffusion forward pass, with sparsification to limit spillover. Backbone weights are frozen; only three hyperparameters (alpha, lambda, q) are tuned on a small validation split.
AI.result: On TOFU forget01 with TOFU-finetuned LLaDA-8B-Base, TimeROME-DLM reduces forget-set log-probability by ~83 nats. The approach transfers to LLaDA-8B-Instruct, Dream-7B, MMaDA-8B, DiffuLLaMA-7B, and LLaDA-MoE-1.4B. Retain-set log-probability remains nearly flat (within ~1 nat) over 50 sequential edits. It yields 4x to 14x wall-clock speedup with zero additional VRAM, and scales sub-linearly to 400 facts.
AI.conclusion: TimeROME-DLM closes the locate-then-edit gap between AR LLMs and MDLMs at a fraction of the computational cost, enabling effective, scalable, training-free editing for MDLMs.

title: MagPlus: Bridging Micro-to-Regular Facial Expressions through Learnable Magnification
summary: Facial micro-expressions are subtle and short-lived facial movements that provide important cues about genuine human emotions. However, modeling and generating them remains difficult because annotated micro-expression data is limited and the underlying facial motions are extremely weak. Existing micro-expression generation methods therefore often suffer from limited quality, weak robustness, and poor generalization. We propose MagPlus, a transferable micro-expression processing pipeline that connects micro-expression analysis with standard facial animation models. Instead of training a dedicated generator from scratch, MagPlus learns to magnify subtle facial motions into the range of regular facial expressions, transforming micro-expressions into signals that are compatible with existing facial expression processing models. The magnified sequence is then used by a standard facial expression model for tasks such as transfer and synthesis. A complementary DeMagPlus module then restores the generated motion back to realistic micro-expression intensity levels while preserving the synthesized dynamics. We evaluate the framework using four facial animation models: FOMM, FSRT, MetaPortrait, and EmoPortraits. None of these models are trained on micro-expression data. Experiments show that MagPlus-DeMagPlus enables pretrained macro-expression models to generate more realistic micro-expression motion without retraining the backbones.

AI.tldr: MagPlus-DeMagPlus offers a plug-and-play approach to micro-expression generation by magnifying micro-expressions into macro-range signals that pretrained macro-expression models can process, then demagnifying the output back to realistic micro-expression intensities, enabling transfer/synthesis without retraining backbones.
AI.motivation: Address the data scarcity and weak signal problem in micro-expressions by leveraging existing macro-expression models instead of training new micro-expression generators from scratch.
AI.method: A two-stage pipeline: (1) Magnify subtle micro-expressions into the range of regular facial expressions to be compatible with standard facial expression models; (2) Use a standard macro-expression model for transfer/synthesis; (3) Apply DeMagPlus to restore the generated motion to micro-expression intensity while preserving dynamics. Evaluated across four macro-trained models (FOMM, FSRT, MetaPortrait, EmoPortraits).
AI.result: Experiments show MagPlus-DeMagPlus enables pretrained macro-expression models to produce more realistic micro-expression motion without retraining backbones, improving quality/robustness/generalization relative to micro-expression-specific training.
AI.conclusion: The framework provides a transferable, plug-and-play solution to micro-expression generation by bridging micro and macro facial models, mitigating data constraints and enabling broader applicability of existing backbones. Further work would clarify quantitative fidelity, boundaries of magnification, and potential artifacts.

title: A Minimal Model of Bounded Trade-Off Screening in Multi-Attribute Choice
comment: 3 pages, 1 figure, accepted as extended abstract at Annual Conference on Cognitive Computational Neuroscience 2026
summary: Human decision-making often involves choosing between multi-attribute alternatives, yet classical models assume fully compensatory utility aggregation despite evidence that people reject options with poor performance on critical attributes. We propose a bounded trade-off reasoning framework in which decisions are governed by a screening process that evaluates the balance between gains and losses across attributes. The model introduces a trade-off tolerance parameter that controls acceptable imbalance and can vary across contexts. Through simulation, we show that this mechanism produces preference patterns that differ from standard utility-based models and captures context-dependent variation in trade-off behavior. These results establish bounded trade-off screening as a plausible computational mechanism for multi-attribute choice and generate testable predictions for future behavioral studies.

AI.tldr: Introduces bounded trade-off screening for multi-attribute choice with a trade-off tolerance parameter; yields non-compensatory, context-sensitive decisions.
AI.motivation: Classic fully compensatory utility is often violated in real decisions; need a computational mechanism to explain non-compensatory screening and context effects.
AI.method: Simulation study implementing a bounded screening process where gains and losses are weighed across attributes with a tolerance parameter; compare with standard utility models and explore context variation.
AI.result: Produces preference patterns that diverge from utility-based predictions; captures context-dependent trade-off behavior; offers testable predictions for experiments.
AI.conclusion: Bounded trade-off screening is a plausible mechanism for multi-attribute choice and a basis for further empirical validation.

title: PolyFlow: Safe and Efficient Polytope-Constrained Flow Matching with Constraint Embedding and Projection-free Update
summary: While flow-based generative models have demonstrated strong performance across a wide range of domains, deploying them in safety-critical physical systems remains challenging due to strict constraint requirements. Existing approaches typically enforce safety through post-hoc corrections, which incur substantial computational overhead and may distort the learned distribution. We propose PolyFlow, a polytope-constrained flow matching framework that embeds constraints directly into the model and flow dynamics. PolyFlow introduces a discrete-time flow formulation and a projection-free architecture, which eliminate the discretization error and guarantee strict satisfaction of arbitrary polyhedral constraints, without the need for expensive iterative solvers. Experimental results show that PolyFlow achieves zero constraint violation while maintaining high distributional fidelity across a range of planning and control tasks. Compared to state-of-the-art constrained generation baselines, PolyFlow significantly reduces inference latency and demonstrates a favorable trade-off between safety, efficiency, and generative quality. Code is available on https://github.com/MJianM/PolyFlow.

AI.tldr: PolyFlow: a discrete-time, projection-free flow matching framework that enforces polyhedral constraints directly, achieving zero constraint violations with high fidelity and improved efficiency.
AI.motivation: Safety-critical physical systems require hard, guaranteed constraint satisfaction. Post-hoc corrections are costly, can distort distributions, and may violate safety in real-time.
AI.method: Develop a polytope-constrained flow matching model with a discrete-time flow formulation and a projection-free architecture that enforces arbitrary polyhedral constraints within the model and its dynamics, eliminating discretization error without iterative solvers.
AI.result: Zero constraint violation across tasks, high distributional fidelity, reduced inference latency, favorable safety-efficiency-quality trade-offs compared to constrained generation baselines.
AI.conclusion: PolyFlow provides provable, efficient enforcement of polyhedral constraints in flow-based models, enabling safe and fast deployment in safety-critical domains.

title: Interpretable Factor Decomposition for Decision Intelligence in Large-Scale Financial Markets: Evidence from China's A-Share Market
summary: We present an interpretable machine learning pipeline to decompose Cross-Sectional Equity Return Predictability into auditable factor contribution. We apply an XGBoost model with TreeSHAP attribution and conduct stress testing on 3632 Chinese A-share stocks from 2009 until 2019. Using 60-month, rolling windows over 55 months of out-of-sample data, XGBoost obtains a mean AUC of 0.547 and +2.38%/month (Newey-West t = 5.94; Annualized Sharpe 2.23) long-short spread for the top vs bottom quintiles. This alpha is persistent after adjusting for the Carhart four-factor model (+2.31%/month; t = 7.48). SHAP Decomposition indicates that behavioral signals (turnover and momentum) account for 58.2% of predictive attribution compared to 10.7% for valuation ratios, on average, across 55 industry groups. Ablation analysis serves to cross-validate this ranking and provides evidence that SHAP and ablation diverge in a manner that highlights feature substitutability structure that is largely invisible to either method used in isolation.

AI.tldr: An interpretable ML pipeline using XGBoost with TreeSHAP attribution decomposes cross-sectional equity return predictability into auditable factor contributions; behavioral signals dominate predictive attribution; alpha persists after Carhart four-factor adjustment; ablation reveals feature substitutability.
AI.motivation: To understand drivers of cross-sectional equity return predictability with interpretable, auditable factor contributions; quantify the relative importance of behavioral signals versus valuations; assess robustness and feature substitutability via ablation.
AI.method: 60-month rolling windows on 55 months of out-of-sample data for 3,632 Chinese A-share stocks (2009–2019). Train XGBoost, apply TreeSHAP attribution to decompose predictions by feature groups. Conduct stress tests, construct long-short portfolios, and compare against the Carhart four-factor model. Perform ablation analysis across 55 industry groups to examine feature substitutability and validate attribution.
AI.result: Mean AUC of 0.547; +2.38%/month long-short spread (t-statistic via Newey-West = 5.94); annualized Sharpe = 2.23. Alpha remains after Carhart four-factor adjustment: +2.31%/month (t = 7.48). SHAP decomposition assigns 58.2% of predictive attribution to behavioral signals (turnover and momentum) and 10.7% to valuation ratios, across 55 industry groups. Ablation analysis corroborates the ranking and reveals divergences between SHAP and ablation due to feature substitutability.
AI.conclusion: The predictive power is largely driven by behavioral signals rather than valuations. The combination of SHAP attribution and ablation provides a consistent, auditable decomposition, with persistent alpha beyond standard factor models, while also highlighting substitutability structures among features.

title: Masked and Predictive Self-Supervised Foundation Models for 3D Brain MRI
summary: Self-supervised foundation models have shown strong promise in medical imaging. However, existing MRI foundation-model studies have primarily emphasized segmentation and dense prediction tasks, while systematic investigation of self-supervised foundation models for MRI-based disease detection remains limited. In this work, we investigate two major self-supervised pretraining paradigms for MRI-based disease detection: reconstruction-based learning via Masked Autoencoders (MAE) and predictive representation learning via Joint Embedding Predictive Architectures (JEPA). We study the role of auxiliary objectives by introducing a novel spectral-domain reconstruction loss for MAE to enhance sensitivity to fine-grained anatomical structure, and by integrating variance--covariance regularization (VCR) within our JEPA framework to encourage decorrelated latent representations. Our models are pretrained on heterogeneous single-contrast MRI volumes in a contrast-agnostic setting, without modality concatenation. Across five downstream disease detection tasks, our results highlight the importance of self-supervised objective design for medical foundation model pretraining, demonstrating that the downstream benefit of each objective is determined by its relevance to the task's structure. Specifically, spectral regularization yields the largest improvements when the downstream discriminative signal is characterized by strong high-frequency anatomical structures, while covariance regularization is most beneficial when discriminative information spans multiple decorrelated feature dimensions. MAE with spectral-domain supervision consistently achieves superior downstream performance for MRI-based disease detection. These findings suggest that self-supervised objectives in medical imaging encode specific biases, and their downstream benefit is fundamentally conditioned on the task's structure.

AI.tldr: Self-supervised MRI pretraining with MAE and JEPA shows that auxiliary losses (spectral-domain MAE, VCR in JEPA) encode task-specific biases; spectral-domain MAE best for high-frequency anatomical signals, while VCR helps when discriminative info is multi-dimensional and decorrelated. downstream gains depend on task structure.
AI.motivation: Address the gap that MRI foundation-model work emphasizes segmentation; systematically study self-supervised pretraining for MRI-based disease detection and how auxiliary objectives affect transfer to five downstream disease-detection tasks.
AI.method: Pretrain on heterogeneous single-contrast MRI volumes in a contrast-agnostic setting (no modality concatenation). Compare MAE (reconstruction) with spectral-domain supervision and JEPA (predictive representation) with covariance regularization (VCR). Evaluate on five disease-detection tasks.
AI.result: Spectral-domain regularization yields the largest gains for downstream signals with strong high-frequency anatomy; covariance regularization helps when discriminative information spans multiple decorrelated features. Overall, MAE with spectral-domain supervision achieves superior performance for MRI-based disease detection.
AI.conclusion: Self-supervised objectives embed task-specific biases; the downstream benefit of an objective is conditioned on the task structure. Objective design should align with the discriminative structure of the target disease detection task.

title: Hallucination in Medical Imaging AI: A Cross-Modality Analytical Framework for Taxonomy, Detection, and Mitigation under Regulatory Constraints
summary: AI systems are being deployed across medical imaging faster than their failure modes are understood. At this point in time, the failure of greatest clinical concern is hallucination: clinically plausible but factually incorrect outputs, including fabricated anatomical structures, missed findings, incorrect laterality, and invented measurements in generated reports, with direct consequences, for example, for biopsy decisions, staging, and treatment planning. This structured narrative synthesizes peer-reviewed studies, benchmark datasets, and FDA regulatory guidance across five imaging modalities to produce a cross-modality analysis of hallucination taxonomy, etiology, detection, and mitigation. Specifically, we address three questions in this study: (1) how can existing taxonomies be unified across modalities?, (2) how do medical-specialized foundation models hallucinate less than general-purpose ones?, and (3) which mitigation strategies are effective and compatible with FDA lifecycle oversight? We note that three taxonomic frameworks together cover the imaging pipeline in a way no single framework does alone. We also highlight that general-purpose foundation models outperform medical-specialized models on hallucination-specific benchmarks, indicating that narrow domain fine-tuning can introduce overfitting-induced confabulation. At the same time, the oversight of radiologists remains essential; for instance, a very high percentage of of AI-generated flags required expert correction before clinical use. Physics-informed architectural constraints, Chain-of-Thought prompting, and human-in-the-loop safeguards each address different failure modes and is effective when combined. All findings are mapped to the FDA's Total Product Lifecycle and Predetermined Change Control Plan frameworks, which treat hallucination management as a lifecycle obligation rather than a pre-deployment checklist.

AI.tldr: Cross-modality analysis of AI hallucinations in medical imaging, proposing unified taxonomies, comparing model types, and outlining mitigation strategies aligned with FDA lifecycles.
AI.motivation: Hallucinations in medical imaging can cause clinically incorrect conclusions and treatment decisions. A unified taxonomy across modalities, understanding of etiology, and regulator-aligned mitigation are needed to safely deploy AI in healthcare.
AI.method: Synthesize peer-reviewed studies, benchmark datasets, and FDA regulatory guidance across five imaging modalities to analyze hallucination taxonomy, etiology, detection, and mitigation. Evaluate contrast between general-purpose and medical-specialized foundation models and assess mitigation strategies, mapping findings to FDA Total Product Lifecycle and Predetermined Change Control Plan.
AI.result: Three taxonomic frameworks together cover the imaging pipeline and can be unified across modalities. General-purpose foundation models hallucinate less than medical-specialized ones, suggesting overfitting from narrow fine-tuning. Radiologist oversight remains essential, with many AI-generated flags requiring expert correction before clinical use. Physics-informed architectural constraints, Chain-of-Thought prompting, and human-in-the-loop safeguards address different failure modes and are effective when combined. Findings are mapped to FDA lifecycle frameworks, treating hallucination management as a lifecycle obligation rather than a pre-deployment checklist.
AI.conclusion: Hallucination management in medical imaging AI requires an integrated lifecycle approach. Unifying taxonomies, selecting robust model families, and combining mitigation strategies—all within FDA Total Product Lifecycle oversight—are necessary to ensure clinically safe deployment.

title: Heterogeneous LiDAR Early Fusion and Learned Re-Ranking Strategy for Robust Long-Term Place Recognition in Unstructured Environments
summary: Robust localization in unstructured environments, such as agricultural fields, is a critical challenge for autonomous systems. LiDAR sensors provide detailed 3D information about the environment and are invariant to lighting conditions. For this reason, LiDAR-based place recognition methods have gained significant attention. In this paper, we propose MinkUNeXt-VINE++, a novel approach that combines early fusion of heterogeneous LiDAR data from two sensors (Livox Mid-360 and Velodyne VLP-16) and a learned re-ranking strategy in inference time. This fusion leverages the strengths of each sensor to provide a more comprehensive representation of the environment. Additionally, the re-ranking approach is particularly important in repetitive environments, such as vineyards, as finding true positives is a major challenge. We evaluated our approach using the TEMPO-VINE dataset, which provides heterogeneous LiDAR data in vineyard environments across different phenological stages. Our results demonstrate that MinkUNeXt-VINE++ significantly improves place recognition performance compared to single-sensor approaches and state-of-the-art methods. MinkUNeXt-VINE++ achieves a 20% improvement in the Recall@1 metric compared to single-sensor approaches, and +30% including re-ranking. The code of our method is publicly available for reproduction.

AI.tldr: MinkUNeXt-VINE++ fuses data from two LiDAR sensors via early fusion and uses a learned re-ranking module at inference to boost place recognition in vineyards; achieves ~20% Recall@1 gain over single-sensor baselines, ~30% with re-ranking; code is released.
AI.motivation: Robust, sensor-agnostic localization in unstructured agricultural fields. LiDAR provides reliable 3D structure independent of lighting. Vineyards present repetitive patterns, causing false positives; evaluating on TEMPO-VINE validates performance across phenological stages.
AI.method: Early fusion of heterogeneous LiDAR data from Livox Mid-360 and Velodyne VLP-16 to form a comprehensive representation, followed by a learned re-ranking stage during inference to refine candidate matches, particularly in repetitive vineyard environments.
AI.result: Significant gains in place recognition: 20% improvement in Recall@1 over single-sensor approaches and 30% improvement when including the re-ranking component, with state-of-the-art performance in TEMPO-VINE corpus.
AI.conclusion: Multi-sensor early fusion combined with a learned re-ranking strategy yields robust place recognition in challenging, repetitive agricultural settings, validated on TEMPO-VINE and with code publicly available for replication.

title: Multimodal Graph Negative Learning
summary: Multimodal attributed graphs (MAGs) integrate graph topology with heterogeneous modality attributes, such as text and images, thereby enabling richer modeling of complex relational systems. However, such expressiveness also makes learning on MAGs depend on multiple semantic sources, including structural topology, textual and visual attributes, each of which can be regarded as a branch for node representation. Node-level branch semantic imbalance arises when these branches differ across nodes in semantic informativeness and reliability: a branch that provides discriminative semantics for one node may mislead another due to bias in modality quality or structural context. Existing methods often mitigate such heterogeneity through cross-branch agreement or alignment, implicitly treating the dominant prediction as reliable supervision. When the dominant branch is biased, forced imitation may propagate its bias to other branches and suppress original semantics that are useful for classification. We propose GraphMNL, a graph-aware multimodal negative learning framework that addresses this issue by using Negative Learning as cross-branch guidance. Instead of forcing inferior branches to imitate a teacher prediction, the model teaches them which classes a node is unlikely to belong to. GraphMNL builds a branch library, identifies dominant and inferior branches via graph-aware reliability arbitration, gates unstable transfer, and applies target-preserving negative learning over non-target classes. This design decouples target supervision from branch guidance so that supervised losses learn the correct class, while Negative Learning suppresses unlikely alternatives when branch agreement is unreliable. Through the comprehensive experimental evaluation, GraphMNL achieves the best performance on Grocery datasets with 72.47% accuracy and 76.60 F1 score on Reddit M datasets.

AI.tldr: GraphMNL uses graph-aware negative learning to handle branch semantic imbalance in multimodal attributed graphs (MAGs) by building a branch library, identifying dominant vs inferior branches, gating unstable transfer, and performing target-preserving negative learning over non-target classes, achieving state-of-the-art results on Grocery and Reddit M datasets.
AI.motivation: Node-level semantic imbalance across modalities and structural branches in MAGs causes biased supervision if dominant (or higher-quality) branches mislead other branches; existing cross-branch alignment methods force imitation, propagating bias.
AI.method: Propose GraphMNL: construct a branch library; use graph-aware reliability arbitration to label branches as dominant or inferior; gate unstable transfer across branches; apply target-preserving Negative Learning on non-target classes to suppress unlikely predictions; decouple target supervision from branch guidance.
AI.result: Comprehensive experiments show GraphMNL achieves top performance with 72.47% accuracy on Grocery datasets and 76.60 F1 on Reddit M datasets, outperforming baselines.
AI.conclusion: GraphMNL effectively mitigates branch bias in MAGs by leveraging negative learning as cross-branch guidance, enabling robust node classification without over-reliance on a possibly biased dominant branch.

title: OR-Action: Multi-Role Video Understanding with Fine-Grained Actions
summary: Fine-grained understanding of operating room (OR) activity could enable workflow-aware assistance, yet remains difficult due to clutter, occlusions, and limited sensing. The prevailing approach to model this environment is scene graphs as an interpretable representation of OR interactions. Converting their frame-wise relational predictions into temporally extended, fine-grained actions however, is challenging without explicit temporal modeling. To enable a principled temporal evaluation of current OR understanding methods, we introduce the first action-centric benchmark built on a publicly available ego-exocentric OR dataset by defining a fine-grained, multi-role action taxonomy and generating dense action segments via distillation from ground-truth scene graph state changes. Experiments on this benchmark show that current scene graph prediction methods struggle to model temporal structure, even when adding explicit modeling through Graph Neural Networks. We therefore introduce a vision-only temporal model that outperforms graph-based methods significantly when using all available egocentric video as input. Building on this model we also introduce a novel multi- to single-view feature alignment strategy that improves single-view performance on multi-role action recognition, mitigating the need for extensive egocentric video capture. Benchmark and code will be released upon acceptance.

AI.tldr: Introduces an action-centric benchmark for OR activity understanding, highlighting temporal modeling gaps in scene-graph methods and proposing a vision-only temporal model with multi-view alignment that yields strong performance and reduces need for multi-view data; benchmark and code to be released.
AI.motivation: To enable principled temporal evaluation of OR understanding and address the gap between frame-level scene graphs and dense, fine-grained, temporally extended actions in cluttered OR videos.
AI.method: Define a fine-grained, multi-role action taxonomy; distill dense action segments from ground-truth scene graph state changes; develop a vision-only temporal model; propose a multi- to single-view feature alignment strategy; evaluate on ego-exocentric OR data.
AI.result: Graph-based scene graph predictions struggle to capture temporal structure, even with GNNs; the vision-only temporal model outperforms graph-based approaches when using full egocentric video; alignment improves single-view performance on multi-role action recognition.
AI.conclusion: Presents the first action-centric benchmark for OR understanding; demonstrates temporal modeling necessity; offers a strong vision-only baseline and a practical multi-view alignment approach; will release benchmark and code on acceptance.

title: LLM-as-an-Investigator: Evidence-First Reasoning for Robust Interactive Problem Diagnosis
summary: Large language models (LLMs) are increasingly used as interactive assistants for technical problem solving. However, when users provide incomplete descriptions or plausible but unverified explanations, LLMs may prematurely align with these assumptions and propose solutions before collecting sufficient evidence. We refer to this behavior as user-driven sycophancy: the tendency of an LLM to reinforce a user-provided hypothesis instead of testing alternative explanations. This paper introduces LLM-as-an-Investigator, an evidence-first agentic AI methodology for robust problem diagnosis. The approach is implemented through a Solution Investigator Agent, which estimates the ambiguity of an initial problem description, generates candidate hypotheses, asks targeted clarification questions, and updates hypothesis probabilities after each answer. Rather than producing an immediate response, the agent continues the investigation until the evidence makes one candidate explanation stronger than the alternatives. To evaluate the approach, we build a benchmark from solved technical forum threads in mechanical, electrical, and hydraulic domains. We use a three-agent evaluation pipeline in which a Problem-Solution Extractor Agent converts solved threads into structured cases, a Ground-Truth Evaluator Agent simulates the user while hiding the known solution, and the tested assistant attempts to recover the solution through dialogue. The experiments compare standard assistants, reasoning-oriented LLMs, and the proposed investigator-based model across LLM backbones. In addition to diagnostic accuracy, we analyze how standard assistants follow misleading user hypotheses in diagnostic cases. The results show that the proposed approach identifies the problem more accurately than direct prompting and reasoning-only baselines, while its evidence-first protocol helps reduce user-induced conversational bias.

AI.tldr: Proposes an evidence-first agent (Solution Investigator Agent) to mitigate user-driven sycophancy in LLM problem solving; evaluated via a three-agent benchmark showing improved diagnostic accuracy and reduced bias across domains and backbones.
AI.motivation: LLMs often align with user-provided, incomplete or plausible-but-unverified explanations, risking premature/wrong solutions. An evidence-first approach aims to robustly test hypotheses before answering.
AI.method: Introduce Solution Investigator Agent that estimates problem ambiguity, generates hypotheses, asks targeted clarifying questions, and updates hypothesis probabilities after each response. The agent delays final answers until evidence favors a single explanation. Evaluation uses a three-agent pipeline: Problem-Solution Extractor Agent converts solved threads into structured cases; Ground-Truth Evaluator Agent simulates the user and hides the solution; the tested assistant attempts to recover the solution via dialogue. Benchmark drawn from solved technical forum threads in mechanical, electrical, and hydraulic domains; tested across multiple LLM backbones.
AI.result: The evidence-first investigator approach identifies the problem more accurately than direct prompting and reasoning-only baselines and reduces user-induced conversational bias.
AI.conclusion: An evidence-first, hypothesis-testing protocol yields more robust problem diagnosis, is effective across domains, and generalizes to different LLM backbones.

title: MaskWAM: Unifying Mask Prompting and Prediction for World-Action Models
summary: World Action Models (WAMs) present a promising paradigm for robotic control via video prediction. However, current WAMs suffer from fundamental spatial bottlenecks: standard text inputs introduce referential ambiguity in cluttered scenes, while unstructured RGB predictions lack semantic grounding and remain biased by task-irrelevant backgrounds. To overcome these limitations, we introduce MaskWAM, an object-centric world-action model. By jointly integrating masks as both explicit inputs and predictions via a unified Mixture of Transformers (MoT), MaskWAM unlocks robust policy generalization. This design provides two key benefits: (1) predicting future masks yields object-centric semantic supervision that suppresses visual noise, significantly enhancing even standard text-conditioned WAMs; and (2) coupling this predictive supervision with first-frame visual prompts, such as target object masks, establishes a precise spatial anchor that substantially reduces language ambiguity. Crucially, as WAMs are inherently vision-driven architectures, direct mask conditioning yields substantially stronger guidance than text alone, establishing a precise and robust paradigm for manipulating unseen objects. Evaluations on LIBERO, RoboTwin, and real-world tasks demonstrate that MaskWAM significantly outperforms baselines in both language-clear and language-ambiguous tasks.

AI.tldr: MaskWAM introduces an object-centric world-action model that uses masks as both inputs and predictions within a Mixture of Transformers to address spatial bottlenecks in WAMs. This yields improved policy generalization and stronger grounding, especially in language-ambiguous tasks.
AI.motivation: Standard WAMs struggle with referential ambiguity in cluttered scenes when using text inputs and with lack of semantic grounding in unstructured RGB predictions. There is a need for precise spatial and semantic supervision to improve generalization to unseen objects and reduce language ambiguity.
AI.method: Propose MaskWAM, integrating masks as explicit inputs and as predicted outputs via a unified Mixture of Transformers. Use future mask prediction for semantic supervision and couple with first-frame visual prompts (target masks) to provide a spatial anchor, enabling robust object-centric control.
AI.result: Empirically, MaskWAM outperforms baselines on LIBERO, RoboTwin, and real-world tasks for both language-clear and language-ambiguous settings, indicating stronger guidance and generalization.
AI.conclusion: MaskWAM establishes that mask-conditioned, object-centric supervision provides stronger grounding and spatial precision than text alone in WAMs, enabling improved manipulation of unseen objects and robust policy generalization.

title: SMGFM: Spectral Multimodal Graph Pretraining for Multimodal-Attributed Graphs
summary: Multimodal-attributed graphs (MAGs) couple graph topology with node semantics from text, images, and other modalities. Traditional graph learning contextualizes node semantics by coupling topology with node features. However, this coupling design becomes troublesome in MAGs, where structure-induced and modality-intrinsic semantics may contribute differently to downstream tasks. Structure-induced semantics promote relational consistency through smooth topological variation, whereas modality-intrinsic semantics often encode local, fine-grained distinctions that should not be uniformly smoothed or aligned. Therefore, the key challenge is to identify semantic roles before cross-modal fusion. To this end, we leverage graph-frequency variation as a prior, where low-frequency components capture topology-consistent semantics and high-frequency components preserve modality-specific semantics. Based on this intuition, we propose SMGFM, a spectral multimodal graph pretraining framework that decomposes each modality-specific node signal into graph-frequency bands and assigns band-level semantic roles before cross-modal interaction. Concretely, SMGFM constructs frequency-resolved modality tokens with scalable Chebyshev filters, estimates their coupling reliability through topology-conditioned routing, and performs band-modality interaction before fusion. Its frequency-routed objectives align smooth consensus routes while preserving modality-specific routes, mitigating spatial-domain entanglement and uniform cross-modal alignment. Extensive experiments conducted on the MAG datasets demonstrate that SMGFM achieves state-of-the-art performance across graph-level and modality-level tasks.

AI.tldr: SMGFM introduces a spectral multimodal graph pretraining framework that decomposes modality signals into graph-frequency bands to identify semantic roles before cross-modal fusion, using Chebyshev filters and topology-conditioned routing to achieve state-of-the-art MAG performance.
AI.motivation: In multimodal-attributed graphs (MAGs), structure-induced semantics and modality-intrinsic semantics contribute differently to tasks. Uniformly coupling topology with all modalities risks over-smoothing or misalignment; graph-frequency variation can serve as a prior to distinguish semantic roles and guide fusion.
AI.method: Decompose each modality's node signal into graph-frequency bands. Construct frequency-resolved modality tokens via scalable Chebyshev filters. Estimate coupling reliability with topology-conditioned routing. Perform band-modality interaction before fusion. Use frequency-routed objectives to align smooth consensus routes while preserving modality-specific routes, mitigating spatial-domain entanglement and uniform cross-modal alignment.
AI.result: Empirically achieves state-of-the-art performance on MAG datasets for both graph-level and modality-level tasks, across extensive experiments.
AI.conclusion: Frequency-based spectral decomposition enables effective semantic role identification prior to fusion, reducing entanglement and enabling selective cross-modal interaction, leading to superior MAG pretraining performance.

title: Dual-Domain Equivariant Generative Adversarial Network for Multimodal CT-PET Synthesis
comment: 4 pages, 3 figures, 1 table, 2026 IEEE 23rd International Symposium on Biomedical Imaging (ISBI)
summary: We present a Dual-Domain Equivariant Generative Adversarial Network (DDE-GAN) for multimodal CT-PET image synthesis. Traditional GAN-based approaches often operate solely in the spatial domain and ignore geometric consistency, resulting in limited structural fidelity. DDE-GAN addresses these challenges by jointly learning from both spatial and frequency (Fourier) domains, capturing complementary anatomical and spectral information. Furthermore, rotational equivariance embedded in the physics of the CT and PET measurements are integrated into the loss of both the generator and discriminator to ensure consistent responses under rotations, improving anatomical accuracy. A hierarchical dual-domain training strategy enforces intra- and inter-domain consistency through multi-stage loss functions. Evaluated on the HECKTOR 2022 CT-PET dataset, DDE-GAN achieves superior synthesis quality over baseline models for CT-PET image synthesis. The results demonstrate that combining dual-domain learning with geometric equivariance substantially enhances multimodal image synthesis accuracy and robustness, enabling practical applications in PET completion and data augmentation.

AI.tldr: A dual-domain GAN with rotationally equivariant losses for CT-PET synthesis, integrating spatial and Fourier domains with hierarchical multi-stage losses, evaluated on HECKTOR 2022, achieving superior results.
AI.motivation: Improve structural fidelity and robustness in multimodal CT-PET synthesis by leveraging complementary spatial and spectral information while enforcing geometric consistency under rotations, addressing the limitations of spatial-domain GANs.
AI.method: DDE-GAN jointly learns in spatial and Fourier domains; embeds rotational equivariance into losses for both generator and discriminator; employs hierarchical dual-domain training with multi-stage losses to enforce intra- and inter-domain consistency.
AI.result: Reported superior synthesis quality over baseline models on CT-PET synthesis tasks, with improved anatomical accuracy and robustness; demonstrated potential for PET completion and data augmentation.
AI.conclusion: Dual-domain learning with geometric equivariance substantially enhances multimodal image synthesis accuracy and robustness, enabling practical applications, though metric details and generalization require further validation.

title: Brick: Spatial Capability Routing for the Mixture-of-Models (MoM) Paradigm
summary: Defining query difficulty is one of the hardest problems in deployment engineering. Existing LLM routers rely on surface features such as domain labels, keywords, and token count, ignoring the within-domain variance that actually determines model success. Frontier models cost ten to one hundred times more than local open-weight models, so at production scale even small per-request savings become a direct cloud-bill lever. We present Brick, a multimodal router that scores each model on six capability dimensions, combines this with a per-query difficulty estimate, and dispatches via a cost-penalized geometric rule. A continuous preference knob lets operators slide between max-quality and max-saving profiles at deploy time. On a benchmark of 5,504 queries, Brick at max-quality reaches 76.98% accuracy, beating the best single model (75.02%) and all tested routers. At a neutral cost-quality profile, Brick achieves 74.11% accuracy at 4.71x lower cost than always using the strongest model. At min-cost, it cuts cost 22.15x with 11.85 points accuracy loss. Median latency drops from 51.2s to 22.8s.

AI.tldr: Brick is a multimodal router that scores models on six capabilities plus per-query difficulty and uses a cost-aware rule to dispatch queries, achieving higher accuracy and lower cost/latency than baselines.
AI.motivation: Defining query difficulty and reducing production costs for LLM routing. Frontier models are costly; existing routers rely on surface features and ignore within-domain variance that drives success.
AI.method: Introduce Brick: a multimodal router that (i) scores each model on six capability dimensions, (ii) estimates per-query difficulty, and (iii) dispatches via a cost-penalized geometric decision rule, with a continuous deploy-time preference knob for max-quality vs max-saving.
AI.result: On a benchmark of 5,504 queries, Brick at max-quality achieves 76.98% accuracy (best single model 75.02%); neutral profile yields 74.11% accuracy at 4.71x lower cost; min-cost yields 22.15x cost reduction with 11.85 points accuracy loss; median latency drops from 51.2s to 22.8s.
AI.conclusion: A cost-aware, multi-criteria routing approach that accounts for within-domain variance and per-query difficulty can surpass best single models and existing routers while delivering substantial cost and latency savings.

title: Multi-Bitwidth Quantization for LLMs Using Additive Codebooks
summary: As large language models (LLMs) are increasingly deployed across heterogeneous hardware with varying resource constraints, the ability to adaptively manage the trade-off between performance and efficiency without retraining is critical. We propose Drop-by-Drop, a novel multi-bitwidth post-training quantization framework that enables inference-time precision control over LLM weights from a single trained model. Our method is theoretically grounded in information theory and successive refinement. We establish that LLM weights, which commonly follow a Gaussian distribution, can be optimally reconstructed with increasing fidelity as additional bits are incorporated, under a weighted mean squared error distortion motivated by LLM loss functions. To realize this in practice, Drop-by-Drop incorporates Matryoshka-style supervision into the loss function, exploiting the structure of additive codebooks. Drop-by-Drop produces a single model where ordered subsets of codebooks yield accurate partial reconstructions at each precision level. This approach significantly reduces storage and memory overhead by allowing a single checkpoint to serve multiple bitwidths, while maintaining competitive perplexity and accuracy across major architectures, such as Qwen, LLaMA, Gemma, and Mistral.

AI.tldr: Single-model, multi-bitwidth post-training quantization (PTQ) enables adaptive precision at inference without retraining by using additive codebooks and Matryoshka-style supervision; achieves multi-bitwidth reconstructions from one checkpoint with competitive accuracy across Qwen, LLaMA, Gemma, and Mistral while reducing storage.
AI.motivation: Need to deploy LLMs on diverse hardware with varying resource constraints without retraining, enabling efficient inference and reduced storage/memory footprint.
AI.method: Drop-by-Drop uses multi-bitwidth PTQ grounded in information theory and successive refinement; models weights (assumed Gaussian) are optimally reconstructed with increasing fidelity as more bits are added, using a weighted MSE distortion aligned with LLM loss. Implements Matryoshka-style supervision over additive codebooks, creating ordered subsets that yield accurate reconstructions at each precision level from a single trained model.
AI.result: The approach reduces storage/memory overhead by allowing a single checkpoint to serve multiple bitwidths, while maintaining competitive perplexity and accuracy across major architectures (Qwen, LLaMA, Gemma, Mistral).
AI.conclusion: Demonstrates a scalable, theoretically grounded method for adaptive precision management in LLMs without retraining, enabling efficient deployment across heterogeneous hardware with preserved performance.

title: JointEdit3D: Feed-Forward 3D Scene Editing in a Unified Latent Space
summary: Existing 3D scene editing methods typically rely on per-scene optimization over explicit 3D representations or cascaded edit-and-reconstruct pipelines, resulting in high test-time cost, limited 3D awareness, and structural inconsistencies. To couple appearance synthesis and geometry prediction during editing, we build on a unified RGB-geometry reconstruction-generation latent space and adapt it to feed-forward 3D scene editing. The resulting framework, \textbf{JointEdit3D}, performs asymmetric latent inpainting by observing only a single edited RGB reference latent and generating the remaining RGB views and edited geometry latent under source-scene anchoring. JointEdit3D introduces a dedicated SceneAnchor Branch to inject source-scene structure without forcing direct copying, and adopts edit/background-aware losses to balance edited-region fidelity with unedited-content preservation. To address the lack of paired resources for standardized 3D scene editing evaluation, we introduce SceneEdit3D-15K, a dataset with 15K paired editing samples and renderer-provided 3D annotations, together with SceneEdit3D-Bench, a curated 100-sample benchmark. Experiments show that JointEdit3D improves edited-region quality and 3D structural completeness over prior baselines while maintaining competitive background preservation.

AI.tldr: JointEdit3D enables efficient, feed-forward 3D scene editing by learning a unified RGB-geometry latent space, with a SceneAnchor Branch and asymmetric latent inpainting, plus SceneEdit3D-15K/SceneEdit3D-Bench datasets; it achieves better edited-region fidelity and 3D structure with competitive background preservation compared to baselines.
AI.motivation: To overcome high test-time costs, limited 3D awareness, and structural inconsistencies of existing per-scene optimization or cascade edit-and-reconstruct pipelines; to couple appearance and geometry in editing and to provide standardized evaluation resources for 3D scene editing.
AI.method: Leverages a unified RGB-geometry reconstruction-generation latent space and adapts it for feed-forward 3D scene editing. Performs asymmetric latent inpainting using a single edited RGB reference latent to generate remaining RGB views and edited geometry latent anchored to the source scene. Introduces a SceneAnchor Branch to inject source-scene structure without direct copying, and uses edit/background-aware losses to balance edited-region fidelity with unedited-content preservation. Introduces SceneEdit3D-15K (15K paired samples with 3D annotations) and SceneEdit3D-Bench (100-sample benchmark) for evaluation.
AI.result: Empirical results show JointEdit3D improves edited-region quality and 3D structural completeness over prior baselines while maintaining competitive background preservation.
AI.conclusion: JointEdit3D provides a performant, coherent feed-forward 3D editing framework with improved fidelity and structure; the accompanying SceneEdit3D datasets enable standardized evaluation and benchmarking for future work.

title: EPIG: Emotion-Based Prompting for Personalised Image Generation
comment: Submitted to arXiv. 20 pages, 4 figures. Work on emotion-based prompt engineering for text-to-image diffusion models with applications in personalized image generation
summary: Text-to-image diffusion models have achieved impressive results in synthesizing high-quality images from natural language prompts. However, commonly used prompting strategies remain relatively generic, limiting the model's ability to accurately express emotional intent and nuanced affective attributes. This work proposes EPIG, a method that enhances emotional expressiveness at the prompt level prior to image generation. Grounded in psychologically informed emotion representations (valence-arousal) and leveraging structured, role-aware prompt enrichment, EPIG enriches emotion-related components of prompts without modifying or retraining the image generation backbone. The resulting emotion-aware prompts guide the generative process toward more emotionally coherent visual outputs, with particular effectiveness in controlling arousal. EPIG is lightweight, training-free, and well suited for resource-constrained and personalized image generation scenarios. Experimental results on a benchmark of 10 diverse prompts show that EPIG reduces mean arousal error compared to strong baselines, including naive insertion and LLM-based prompt expansion, with reductions of 14% and 12%, respectively. These improvements are statistically significant. EPIG also preserves valence alignment and semantic consistency, as measured by CLIPScore and supported by ablation studies. The effect is more pronounced on prompts containing explicit subjects such as humans, children, or animals, where the reduction reaches 17%, highlighting the subject-sensitive behavior of the proposed method.

AI.tldr: EPIG is a training-free prompt enrichment method that uses valence-arousal emotion representations to enhance emotional expressiveness in text-to-image diffusion models, improving arousal control and preserving valence alignment with semantic consistency; gains are strongest for prompts about explicit subjects (up to ~17%), with 14%/12% reductions in arousal error versus strong baselines.
AI.motivation: Prompting strategies in diffusion models are too generic to capture nuanced affective intent. Grounding prompts in psychological emotion representations (valence-arousal) offers a lightweight way to control emotional attributes (especially arousal) without retraining the model, enabling personalized and resource-constrained generation.
AI.method: EPIG performs emotion-aware prompt enrichment at the prompt level before image generation by grounding prompts in valence-arousal representations and applying structured, role-aware enrichment. No changes to the diffusion backbone. Evaluation on a benchmark of 10 prompts against baselines (naive insertion, LLM-based prompt expansion). Metrics include mean arousal error, CLIPScore (valence alignment/semantic consistency), and ablation studies to verify the contribution of components.
AI.result: EPIG reduces mean arousal error by 14% compared with naive insertion and by 12% compared with LLM-based expansion. Improvements are statistically significant. Valence alignment and semantic consistency are preserved as evidenced by CLIPScore; ablations confirm component contributions. The effect is stronger on prompts with explicit subjects (humans, children, animals), with reductions up to 17%.
AI.conclusion: EPIG is lightweight and training-free, suitable for resource-constrained and personalized image generation. It enhances emotional expressiveness at the prompt level without modifying the generation backbone, achieving more emotionally coherent outputs while maintaining semantic fidelity, particularly for subject-centric prompts.

title: LongSpike: Fractional Order Spiking State Space Models for Efficient Long Sequence Learning
summary: Spiking Neural Networks (SNNs) are well-regarded for their biological plausibility and energy efficiency in processing sequential data. However, dominant SNN architectures typically rely on first-order Ordinary Differential Equations (ODEs) to govern neuronal state transitions. This first-order assumption imposes a "memoryless" bottleneck, limiting the model's capacity to capture the complex, long-range dependencies inherent in long-sequence tasks. In this work, we propose LongSpike, a novel SNN framework that integrates fractional-order State-Space Modeling, or f-SSM, from control theory into the spiking domain. By extending traditional integer-order SSMs to the fractional-calculus regime, LongSpike enables the hierarchical integration of neuronal dynamics with long-memory kernels. To mitigate the computational overhead and parallelization challenges typically associated with fractional operators, we leverage a state-space formulation that supports efficient, parallel training. Empirical evaluations on challenging benchmarks, including Long Range Arena (LRA), large-scale WikiText-103, and Speech Commands, demonstrate that LongSpike outperforms state-of-the-art SNNs in accuracy while preserving sparse synaptic computation. The code is available at https://github.com/xinruihe389-commits/LongSpike.

AI.tldr: LongSpike introduces fractional-order state-space modeling into spiking neural networks to capture long-range dependencies, achieving higher accuracy on long-sequence benchmarks while maintaining sparse computation.
AI.motivation: Traditional first-order SNNs rely on memoryless dynamics, hindering the modeling of long-range dependencies. Fractional calculus provides long-memory kernels; combining it with efficient state-space formulations could improve performance without sacrificing efficiency.
AI.method: Integrate fractional-order state-space modeling into SNNs (f-SSM), enabling hierarchical, long-memory neuronal dynamics. Use a state-space formulation that allows efficient, parallel training and mitigates the overhead of fractional operators. Evaluate on Long Range Arena, WikiText-103, and Speech Commands, comparing to state-of-the-art SNNs.
AI.result: LongSpike outperforms state-of-the-art SNNs in accuracy on challenging long-sequence benchmarks (LRA, WikiText-103, Speech Commands) while preserving sparse synaptic computation.
AI.conclusion: Fractional-order SSMs enable effective long-memory dynamics in SNNs, yielding superior accuracy for long-range tasks without compromising sparsity or training efficiency.

title: Dual-Constrained Diffusion Image Compression for Operational Rate-Distortion-Perception Optimization
summary: The rate-distortion-perception (RDP) trade-off extends classical rate--distortion theory by imposing a distributional constraint on reconstructions, providing a unified framework for neural image compression that jointly governs fidelity and perceptual realism. While prior work achieves near-optimal rate--perception trade-offs, practical frameworks explicitly realizing the full RDP surface remain scarce, primarily due to the difficulty of introducing common randomness at the decoder. We propose DCIC (Dual-Constrained Diffusion Image Compression), which integrates a learned codec with a diffusion-based decoder governed by joint distortion and idempotence constraints. The distortion constraint bounds reconstruction fidelity relative to the base codec output; the idempotence constraint -- requiring that re-encoding the restored image recovers the base codec reconstruction -- serves as a tractable surrogate for the distributional perception requirement. Together, they steer the reverse denoising process via iterative optimization with consistent noise injection, realizing common randomness without additional rate overhead. At fixed rate, dual attenuation factors $(K_D, K_P)$ jointly navigate the Pareto frontier of the distortion-perception plane, enabling continuously adjustable fidelity-realism trade-offs from a single bitstream. DCIC$_{RD}$ ($K_P{=}0$) and DCIC$_{RP}$ ($K_D{=}0$) arise as boundary curves, with DCIC$_{RDP}$ ($K_D = K_P=1$) realizing the optimal interior operating point.
