Experiments on CelebA-HQ, CLIC2020, and ImageNet-1K across CNN, Transformer, and hybrid architectures confirm that DCIC$_{RDP}$ achieves superior BD-PSNR over all perceptual codecs, while DCIC$_{RP}$ matches dedicated perception-oriented methods in BD-FID, validating the practical value of full RDP surface navigation.

AI.tldr: Proposes DCIC, a dual-constrained diffusion-based image compression framework that navigates the rate-distortion-perception (RDP) surface at a fixed bitrate by jointly optimizing a learned codec and a diffusion-based decoder with distortion and idempotence constraints to realize common randomness without extra rate overhead.
AI.motivation: Extend rate-distortion-perception theory into a practical framework that can realize the full RDP surface. The main challenge is implementing common randomness at the decoder; existing methods achieve partial trade-offs but lack a unified, adjustable system.
AI.method: Integrate a learned codec with a diffusion-based decoder governed by two constraints: a distortion constraint (fidelity relative to the base codec output) and an idempotence constraint (re-encoding the restored image yields the base reconstruction). Use iterative denoising with consistent noise injection to guide the reverse process, thereby achieving common randomness without additional rate. Introduce dual attenuation factors (K_D, K_P) to navigate the RDP surface at fixed rate; boundary curves DCIC_RD (K_P=0), DCIC_RP (K_D=0), and interior DCIC_RDP (K_D=K_P=1).
AI.result: Empirical evaluation on CelebA-HQ, CLIC2020, and ImageNet-1K across CNN, Transformer, and hybrid architectures shows DCIC_RDP achieves superior BD-PSNR relative to perceptual codecs, and DCIC_RP matches dedicated perception-oriented methods in BD-FID, validating practical RDP surface navigation.
AI.conclusion: DCIC enables practical full-RDP navigation with a single bitstream, offering continuously adjustable fidelity-realism trade-offs; the dual-constraint diffusion approach effectively leverages common randomness to realize the perceptual component without rate overhead.

title: Multi-Field Hybrid Retrieval-Augmented Generation for Maritime Accident Root Cause Analysis
summary: Maritime accident adjudication reports contain critical tribunal findings for root cause analysis (RCA), yet retrieving relevant precedents and drafting consistent reports from decades of records remains labor-intensive. This paper proposes a multi-field hybrid retrieval-augmented generation (RAG) framework for automated maritime RCA, utilizing a comprehensive dataset of 13,329 Korea Maritime Safety Tribunal (KMST) reports (1971-2025). We transform raw adjudications into a structured knowledge base of "incident cards", indexing three distinct fields-Summary, Causes, and Disposition-alongside a hierarchical L1/L2 cause taxonomy. Our retrieval strategy employs a field-aware hybrid approach, fusing sparse and dense rankings via Reciprocal Rank Fusion (RRF). Given the lack of large-scale expert relevance labels, we evaluate retrieval performance using ceiling-normalized recall and nDCG based on a metadata-derived proxy relevance score. Experimental results demonstrate that our proposed retrieval significantly outperforms baseline methods, improving NormRecall@100 from 0.18 to 0.55. Furthermore, grounding the generator on the retrieved precedents enhances RCA generation quality over an LLM-only baseline, increasing the LLM-as-a-judge score from 3.34 to 3.72. These findings suggest that field-aware RAG can substantially streamline maritime safety investigation workflows by enabling faster precedent search and more consistent, evidence-based RCA drafting.

AI.tldr: A field-aware RAG framework for automated maritime RCA using incident cards (Summary, Causes, Disposition) and a hierarchical L1/L2 taxonomy. It fuses sparse and dense retrieval with Reciprocal Rank Fusion, evaluated on 13,329 KMST reports, achieving substantial gains in retrieval and generation quality over baselines.
AI.motivation: To alleviate labor-intensive retrieval of precedents and inconsistent RCA drafting from decades of records by building a structured knowledge base and integrating retrieval with generation for evidence-based outputs.
AI.method: Transform adjudications into incident cards with fields Summary, Causes, Disposition, plus an L1/L2 cause taxonomy. Use a field-aware hybrid retrieval (sparse+dense) with Reciprocal Rank Fusion. Evaluate using ceiling-normalized recall and nDCG with a proxy relevance score due to lack of expert labels. Ground the generator on retrieved precedents (RAG) and compare to an LLM-only baseline.
AI.result: NormRecall@100 improves from 0.18 to 0.55 due to field-aware retrieval. LLM-as-a-judge score improves from 3.34 to 3.72 when grounded on retrieved precedents, indicating better RCA generation quality.
AI.conclusion: Field-aware RAG can streamline maritime safety investigations by enabling faster precedent search and more consistent, evidence-based RCA drafting; the approach shows promise for knowledge-rich, archival domains.

title: PolicyGuard: Towards Test-time and Step-level Adversary Defense for Reinforcement Learning Agent
summary: While real-world applications of reinforcement learning (RL) are becoming increasingly popular, the security of RL systems deserve more attention and exploration. In particular, recent work has revealed that RL agents are vulnerable to backdoor attacks, where a victim agent behaves normally under standard conditions but executes malicious actions when a specific trigger is activated. Existing backdoor defenses for RL either require access to the agent's internal parameters, operate only at the model or trajectory level, or are limited to specific attack types. To ensure the security of RL agents, we propose \texttt{PolicyGuard}, a \textit{test-time step-level} backdoor defense which leverages Gaussian Process (GP) posterior variance and adapts pseudo trajectories to enable uncertainty computation for individual time step. Besides, we also provide theoretical foundations to explain the efficacy of GP posterior variance. Extensive experiments across seven RL games demonstrate that PolicyGuard achieves state-of-the-art detection performance in most cases, with average AUROC of 0.856 for perturbation-based attacks and 0.859 for adversary-agent attacks.

AI.tldr: PolicyGuard provides a test-time, step-level defense against RL backdoors using Gaussian Process posterior variance to measure per-step uncertainty and adapt pseudo trajectories, achieving near-state-of-the-art detection (AUROC ~0.856–0.859) across seven games.
AI.motivation: Backdoor attacks pose security risks for RL; existing defenses either rely on internal parameters, operate only at model/trajectory level, or handle limited attack types. A step-level, parameter-free (at inference) defense can detect malicious action triggers by monitoring per-step uncertainty.
AI.method: Introduce PolicyGuard, which computes per-time-step uncertainty via GP posterior variance by augmenting trajectories with pseudo steps to enable per-step uncertainty estimation at test time, and provides theoretical justification for GP variance as a security signal.
AI.result: Empirical evaluation on seven RL games shows high detection performance, with average AUROC of 0.856 for perturbation-based attacks and 0.859 for adversary-agent attacks, indicating strong effectiveness in detecting backdoors.
AI.conclusion: PolicyGuard is a strong, test-time, step-level defense against RL backdoors, leveraging GP-based uncertainty and pseudo-trajectory augmentation to enable per-step detection with formal backing and broad empirical validation.

title: MoVerse: Real-Time Video World Modeling with Panoramic Gaussian Scaffold
summary: We present MoVerse, a real-time video world model that creates an interactively navigable scene from a single narrow-field-of-view image. This setting is challenging because the input observes only a small fraction of the environment, while interactive roaming requires a complete surrounding world, persistent geometry, controllable camera motion, and temporally coherent high-fidelity observations. MoVerse addresses this problem by separating world construction from observation rendering. It first expands the input into a gravity-aligned 360$^\circ$ panorama with topology-aware diffusion, closing the missing field of view before 3D reasoning. It then lifts the panorama into a persistent 3D Gaussian scaffold using panoramic geometry-aware residual prediction, yielding a dense and directly renderable spatial memory. Finally, a Gaussian-conditioned video renderer translates scaffold renderings along user-specified camera trajectories into photorealistic video. To make this renderer practical for interaction, we train a bidirectional diffusion teacher for high-quality conditional rendering and distill it into a causal autoregressive student for bounded-latency streaming. This design combines the controllability and long-range consistency of explicit 3D representations with the perceptual quality of generative video models. MoVerse supports real-time scene roaming at 8~FPS on a single NVIDIA RTX~4090 GPU, demonstrating a practical path toward single-image world creation with interactive video output.

AI.tldr: MoVerse constructs an interactive 3D scene and photorealistic video from a single narrow-view image via a three-stage pipeline: panorama expansion, 3D Gaussian scaffold, and diffusion-driven video rendering, enabling real-time roaming.
AI.motivation: The core challenge is to recover a complete, persistent world and temporally coherent high-fidelity observations from a single narrow field-of-view image for interactive navigation.
AI.method: (1) Expand input into a gravity-aligned 360 panorama using topology-aware diffusion; (2) lift panorama into a persistent 3D Gaussian scaffold with panoramic geometry-aware residual prediction; (3) render via a Gaussian-conditioned video renderer that maps scaffold renderings along user camera trajectories into photorealistic video. A bidirectional diffusion teacher guides high-quality conditional rendering and is distilled into a causal autoregressive student for bounded latency.
AI.result: Real-time roaming at 8 FPS on an RTX 4090, with a dense, directly renderable 3D memory and photorealistic video output; demonstrates feasibility of single-image world creation with interactive video.
AI.conclusion: The approach merges the controllability and long-range consistency of explicit 3D representations with the perceptual quality of generative video models, offering a practical path to single-image world creation with real-time interactive video.

title: MOSAIC: Modality-Specific Adaptation for Incremental Continual Learning in Parkinson's Disease Gait Assessment
summary: Gait-based Parkinson's disease assessment increasingly relies on heterogeneous sensors, but clinical systems rarely collect all modalities simultaneously. New sensors may arrive through device upgrades, protocol changes, or multi-center deployment, while historical patient data are often unavailable because of privacy and storage constraints. This modality-incremental setting faces three challenges: unreliable cross-modal distillation, modality-specific statistical shifts, and reduced plasticity after preservation. We propose MOSAIC, a compact continual learning framework. First, we identify the Toxic Teacher phenomenon and introduce Modality-Specific Warm-Up to stabilize newly learned modality representations before distillation. Second, we propose a statistics-decoupled MSBN architecture that isolates sensor statistics while maintaining a shared semantic backbone. Third, we design a curriculum-guided repulsive objective for Plasticity Recovery, preserving legacy knowledge while recovering modality-specific capacity. Experiments on three multimodal Parkinson's gait datasets show that MOSAIC improves final performance and mitigates forgetting. Project code is available at: https://github.com/minlinzeng/MOSAIC_Modality-Specific-Adaptation-for-Incremental-Continual-Learning-in-PD-Gait-Assessment.git

AI.tldr: MOSAIC is a compact continual learning framework for modality-incremental gait-based PD assessment that stabilizes new sensor modalities, decouples statistics, and preserves old knowledge via a curriculum-guided repulsive objective.
AI.motivation: Clinical PD gait analysis uses heterogeneous sensors; when new modalities arrive and historical data are unavailable, models suffer from unreliable cross-modal distillation, modality-specific shifts, and reduced plasticity.
AI.method: Three components: (1) Toxic Teacher identification and Modality-Specific Warm-Up to stabilize representations before distillation; (2) a statistics-decoupled MSBN architecture that isolates sensor statistics while sharing a semantic backbone; (3) a curriculum-guided repulsive objective for Plasticity Recovery to preserve legacy knowledge while recovering modality-specific capacity.
AI.result: Experiments on three multimodal Parkinson's gait datasets show improved final performance and reduced forgetting, compared to baselines; code is released at the provided GitHub URL.
AI.conclusion: MOSAIC effectively addresses cross-modal distillation instability, modality-specific statistical shifts, and limited plasticity in modality-incremental PD gait assessment, offering robust performance and potential applicability to other modality-incremental learning problems.

title: Selecting Samples on Graphs: A Unified Dataset Pruning Framework for Lossless Training Acceleration
summary:
The rapid growth of modern training datasets has significantly increased computational cost, motivating dataset pruning~(DP) methods which retain only a subset of informative samples to reduce training cost.
  Existing pruning criteria typically rely on either intrinsic signals that assess samples independently or extrinsic signals that promote diversity via pairwise relations.
  While effective in their own specific regimes, each captures only one aspect of sample utility and lacks robustness across different pruning ratios or data distribution.
  In this work, we present a unified graph-based DP framework.
  By modeling the dataset as a weighted graph, where node weights encode intrinsic value and edge weights encode extrinsic value, DP can be cast as a Maximum Weight Clique Problem (MWCP).
  Although MWCP is NP-hard, its structure admits a principled greedy solution based on sample-wise marginal gains.
  Under a few mild conditions, we further prove that this unified objective enjoys a formal approximation guarantee, which applies to a broad family of importance metrics and provides practical design guidelines.
  Extensive experiments show that our method outperforms existing DP methods while substantially reducing training cost, reducing training time by over 40\% without sacrificing accuracy on ImageNet-1k with ResNet-50.

AI.tldr: Unified graph-based dataset pruning (DP) converts DP into a Maximum Weight Clique Problem (MWCP) and solves it greedily with approximation guarantees, achieving substantial training-time reductions while preserving accuracy on ImageNet-1k with ResNet-50.
AI.motivation: As datasets grow, training cost escalates. Existing pruning criteria capture intrinsic signals or pairwise extrinsic signals but fail to be robust across pruning ratios and data distributions. A unified approach aims to combine these signals.
AI.method: Model the dataset as a weighted graph: node weights encode intrinsic sample value; edge weights encode extrinsic (diversity) value. Cast DP as MWCP. Although NP-hard, apply a principled greedy algorithm using sample-wise marginal gains. Prove approximation guarantees under mild conditions for a broad family of importance metrics and extract practical design guidelines.
AI.result: Empirical evaluation shows the method outperforms existing DP methods, reducing training time by over 40% without sacrificing accuracy on ImageNet-1k using ResNet-50.
AI.conclusion: The unified objective provides robustness across pruning ratios and data distributions, backed by theoretical guarantees and practical guidelines, enabling substantial computational savings in DP.

title: SmartFont: Dynamic Condition Allocation for Few-Shot Font Generation
summary: Few-shot font generation simultaneously requires global structural completeness and fine-grained local style fidelity. Existing methods usually either rely on global content-style modeling, which is robust but imperfectly disentangled, or emphasize component/local modeling, which captures fine details but relies heavily on local priors and reference coverage. We argue that the key challenge is not merely to learn purer conditions, but to organize complementary yet biased global and local conditions through multi-level allocation during generation. To this end, we propose SmartFont, a diffusion-based few-shot font generation framework that combines global content-style generation with weakly supervised local corrective experts. The local branch performs semantic-spatial allocation by learning expert-wise local concepts and semantically meaningful spatial maps under weak component supervision, enabling fine-grained correction without requiring explicit component-conditioned inference. On top of this, a denoising-state condition allocation module adaptively weights global content, global style, and local corrective feature across timesteps and injection blocks. Extensive experiments show that SmartFont achieves better global-local balance, improves glyph quality and local detail fidelity.

AI.tldr: SmartFont introduces a diffusion-based few-shot font generator that fuses global content/style with weakly supervised local experts and adaptive conditioning to balance global structure and local detail, improving glyph quality.
AI.motivation: Existing approaches trade global robustness for local fidelity; the key problem is effectively coordinating biased global and local conditions across generation through multi-level allocation.
AI.method: A diffusion-based framework with a global content-style generator and a local corrective expert branch trained under weak component supervision to learn semantic-spatial local concepts and maps; a denoising-state condition allocation module weighting global content, global style, and local corrections across timesteps and injection blocks.
AI.result: Empirical results show improved global-local balance, higher glyph quality, and better local detail fidelity compared to baselines.
AI.conclusion: SmartFont demonstrates the benefit of integrating weakly supervised local experts with adaptive multi-level conditioning in few-shot font generation.

title: From Verdict to Process: Agentic Reinforcement Learning for Multi-Stage Fact Verification
summary: Recent approaches combining Large Language Models (LLMs) with retrieval-augmented reasoning have shown promise for automated fact verification. To process complex claims, these verification pipelines typically execute multi-stage workflows that coordinate tightly coupled modules, including claim decomposition, evidence gathering, and verdict prediction. However, existing methods optimize individual stages in isolation or rely on fixed heuristics, which limits adaptive coordination among stages and can lead to suboptimal outcomes. In this work, we propose ProFact, an agentic reinforcement learning framework for end-to-end optimization of multi-stage fact verification trajectories. ProFact trains a unified policy to coordinate claim decomposition, evidence seeking, answer generation, and verdict prediction. To address the sparse and delayed supervision provided by final veracity labels, ProFact introduces process-aware rewards that provide stage-level learning signals throughout the verification process. Empirical evaluation shows that ProFact consistently outperforms strong baselines in both verification performance and inference efficiency. These results highlight the effectiveness of process-aware trajectory optimization for multi-stage fact verification.

AI.tldr: ProFact uses agentic reinforcement learning to end-to-end optimize multi-stage fact verification, with process-aware rewards, improving verification performance and efficiency.
AI.motivation: Current multi-stage fact verification pipelines optimize stages in isolation, lack adaptive coordination, and rely on fixed heuristics, leading to suboptimal and inefficient outcomes.
AI.method: ProFact trains a unified policy to coordinate claim decomposition, evidence seeking, answer generation, and verdict prediction within an agentic RL framework and introduces process-aware rewards to provide stage-level supervision throughout verification.
AI.result: Empirically, ProFact outperforms strong baselines in both verification accuracy and inference efficiency across evaluated tasks.
AI.conclusion: Process-aware trajectory optimization is effective for coordinating multi-stage fact verification and yields tangible gains in performance and efficiency.

title: Where Computation Lives Inside TabPFN: Causal Localisation of Attention Head Function
summary: We present the first causal mechanistic analysis of a tabular foundation model, investigating how TabPFN 2.5's feature wise attention heads distribute computation across layers. Using activation patching, ablation, and attention entropy across two synthetic regression datasets, we find clear temporal specialisation: one head's causal necessity dominates that of the others by 2 to 5 times at peak layer, with its dominant layer shifting across tasks of different complexity, while the remaining heads exhibit symmetric late layer profiles. Attention entropy and patching provide convergent evidence for the computationally active layers of the dominant head. We additionally investigate inference time steerability via contrastive activation steering, which fails to transfer across samples. We attribute this result to TabPFN's in context learning mechanism, which encodes task structure through context dependent attention rather than the stable parametric directions that make steering tractable in language models.

AI.tldr: A causal mechanistic study of TabPFN 2.5 reveals a dominant attention head driving computation with clear temporal specialization across layers; inference-time steerability via activation steering fails due to in-context learning, implying task structure is encoded in context-dependent attention rather than stable parameters.
AI.motivation: To uncover how tabular foundation models allocate computation across layers via feature-wise attention, linking interpretability and controllability in TabPFN-type architectures.
AI.method: Activation patching, ablation, and attention entropy analyses conducted on two synthetic regression datasets to identify causal necessity per head and layer; evaluation of inference-time steerability using contrastive activation steering to assess transferability across samples.
AI.result: One attention head shows causal dominance by 2–5x at its peak layer; the dominant layer shifts with task complexity; remaining heads exhibit symmetric late-layer activity. Attention entropy and patching converge on the active layers of the dominant head. Contrastive activation steering fails to transfer across samples.
AI.conclusion: Task structure is encoded via context-dependent attention (in-context learning) rather than stable parametric directions, limiting steerability; this has implications for interpretability and controllability in tabular foundation models and motivates strategies that consider dynamic, task-conditioned attention rather than fixed directions.

title: Person Identification from Contextual Motion
summary: We consider the problem of identifying people based on their motion styles. We present a generative model describing the action instance creation process and derive a probabilistic identity inference scheme for two common person identification scenarios motivated by the surveillance and authentication applications. We introduce a novel, \emph{interactive}, scenario for person identification from motion patterns. To this end, we formalize the identification process in the context of a sequential message exchange session between the subject and the system. The subject's behavior is modeled using a probabilistic generative model inspired by the Human Information Processing (HIP) paradigm. At each stage, the system presents a visual stimulus (a cue) to the subject and records their motion response. The cue is selected so as to maximize the mutual information of the expected response and the subject's identity. Once recorded, the response is used to update the a posteriori probability over possible subjects' identities. The process terminates once a sufficient classification confidence level is reached. To the best of our knowledge, this is the first time person identification is addressed in such interactive setting. We report high recognition rates on five publicly available datasets and our own novel dataset consisting of 4,476 recordings of 22 test subjects responding to 15 cues.

AI.tldr: Generative, interactive motion-based person identification using cues selected to maximize mutual information; introduces HIP-inspired probabilistic model and sequential identification with stopping criteria; reports strong results on five public datasets and a new large dataset.
AI.motivation: Identify individuals by motion styles for surveillance and authentication; introduce an interactive identification paradigm to actively elicit informative responses.
AI.method: A probabilistic generative model of action instance creation; identity inference via posterior updating; an interactive loop where each trial presents a cue, records the motion response, and selects cues to maximize expected mutual information; stop when confidence is sufficient.
AI.result: High recognition rates reported on five public datasets and a novel dataset (4,476 recordings, 22 subjects, 15 cues).
AI.conclusion: First demonstration of interactive motion-based person identification with information-theoretic cue design; shows feasibility and effectiveness across datasets.

title: ERTS: Adversarial Robustness Testing of Ethical AI via Semantic Perturbation in a Bounded Consequence Space
summary: As AI systems are deployed in high-stakes ethical contexts such as healthcare triage, autonomous vehicle control, and employment screening, formal methods for evaluating their robustness against adversarial manipulation of ethical reasoning remain underdeveloped. This paper introduces the Ethical Robustness Testing System (ERTS), a closed-pipeline framework that: (1) encodes ethical dilemmas into a 22-dimensional Ethical Consequence Space (ECS) grounded in established ethical theory; (2) applies 17 semantic perturbation functions subject to 6 validity constraint classes including a novel semantic coherence constraint; (3) measures decision deviation via a 4-component Ethical Instability Index (EII); and (4) produces domain-adaptive pre-deployment robustness assessment verdicts. We evaluate 4 structured baseline models and 2 production LLMs (Gemini 2.0 Flash and Llama 3.2) across 50 ethical scenarios spanning 8 deployment domains, generating 1,500 adversarial test cases. Results demonstrate that only 33% of models achieve assessment clearance, with the local Llama-3.2 model proving particularly vulnerable to fairness corruption and information degradation attacks (ERS = 0.737). To the best of our knowledge, no existing framework combines a bounded ethical consequence space, semantic coherence constraints, and domain-adaptive assessment in a single adversarial testing pipeline.

AI.tldr: Introduces the Ethical Robustness Testing System (ERTS), a closed-pipeline framework for adversarial testing of AI ethical reasoning. It encodes dilemmas into a bounded 22-dimensional Ethical Consequence Space (ECS), applies 17 semantic perturbations under 6 validity constraints including a semantic coherence constraint, and assesses decisions with a 4-component Ethical Instability Index (EII), yielding domain-adaptive robustness verdicts. Evaluated on 4 structured baselines and 2 production LLMs (Gemini 2.0 Flash and Llama 3.2) across 50 scenarios (8 domains) with 1,500 adversarial tests; only 33% of models clear assessment, with Llama-3.2 showing particular vulnerability (ERS ≈ 0.737).
AI.motivation: Formal methods for evaluating robustness of AI ethical reasoning in high-stakes contexts are underdeveloped. There is a need for a bounded ethical representation, coherence-aware perturbations, and domain-adaptive assessment to enable pre-deployment robustness evaluation against adversarial manipulation.
AI.method: Encode ethical dilemmas into a bounded 22-dimensional Ethical Consequence Space (ECS) grounded in ethical theory; apply 17 semantic perturbation functions constrained by 6 validity classes, including a novel semantic coherence constraint; quantify decision deviation with a 4-component Ethical Instability Index (EII); generate domain-adaptive pre-deployment robustness verdicts; evaluate 4 structured baselines and 2 production LLMs across 50 scenarios (8 domains), yielding 1,500 adversarial test cases.
AI.result: Empirical results show only 33% clearance across models. The local Llama-3.2 model exhibits notable vulnerability to fairness corruption and information degradation attacks, with an ERS score of 0.737 in the reported evaluation.
AI.conclusion: ERTS represents the first integrated framework combining a bounded ethical consequence space, semantic coherence constraints, and domain-adaptive assessment within a single adversarial testing pipeline; it highlights gaps in current models’ ethical robustness and provides a concrete methodology for rigorous pre-deployment testing.

title: LoRA-Muon: Spectral Steepest Descent on the Low-Rank Manifold
summary: Low-Rank Adaptation (LoRA) significantly reduces compute and memory costs for finetuning Deep Learning models but is often harder to tune than dense training: when using factor-wise optimizers such as AdamW, it is sensitive to initialization choices, its optimal learning rates transfer poorly across ranks, and it often fails to beat dense baselines. We derive LoRA-Muon by applying the Muon optimizer's spectral steepest-descent rule to the low-rank setting. Along with our split weight-decay rule, our main claim is that LoRA-Muon is a good low-rank proxy for full-rank Muon and Shampoo-family optimizers. Its optimal learning rates transfer across rank, width, depth, and factor-rescaling. In our compute-matched TinyShakespeare study, a rank-$2$ proxy recovers the dense best tested learning rate, and a rank-$32$ LoRA-Muon run attains lower mean validation loss than the dense baseline in the seed-averaged sweep. We further show that the Spectron optimizer depends on arbitrary factor scaling, so it would likely be a poor fit when finetuning starts from badly imbalanced factors, and that LoRA-RITE's simplified QR-coordinate core implements the same spectral update. LoRA-Muon computes that update without QR-decomposition and avoids storing second moments, making it more accelerator-friendly and memory-efficient.

AI.tldr: LoRA-Muon extends Low-Rank Adaptation by applying Muon's spectral steepest-descent update to the low-rank setting, yielding cross-rank transferable learning rates and memory-efficient updates that rival full-rank optimizers.
AI.motivation: LoRA reduces compute and memory in finetuning but is sensitive to initialization and hyperparameters; a low-rank proxy with stable, transferable learning rates and efficiency is needed.
AI.method: Apply Muon optimizer's spectral steepest-descent rule to LoRA updates; introduce split weight-decay; compare against Shampoo-family; derive a QR-free update analogous to LoRA-RITE; implement memory-efficient update avoiding second-moment storage; experimental validation on compute-matched TinyShakespeare; analyze factor-scaling effects (Spectron).
AI.result: Rank-2 LoRA-Muon recovers the dense best tested learning rate; Rank-32 LoRA-Muon attains lower mean validation loss than the dense baseline in seed-averaged sweep; learning rates transfer across rank, width, depth, and factor-rescaling; Spectron depends on arbitrary factor scaling and may perform poorly with imbalanced factors; LoRA-RITE implements the same spectral update via a simplified QR-coordinate core; LoRA-Muon computes updates without QR-decomposition and avoids storing second moments, improving accelerator-friendliness and memory efficiency.
AI.conclusion: LoRA-Muon provides a strong low-rank proxy for full-rank Muon and Shampoo-family optimizers, with transferable learning rates and memory-efficient updates, addressing hyperparameter sensitivity and efficiency issues in low-rank finetuning.

title: VietFashion: Benchmarking Sketch-Text Composed Image Retrieval for Cultural Outfits
summary: Cultural garments pose a unique challenge for visual retrieval systems, as their identity often depends on subtle structural and symbolic details that are poorly captured by standard AI models. We introduce VietFashion, a new benchmark for sketch-text composed image retrieval centered on the Ao Dai, a traditional Vietnamese garment. VietFashion enables designers and researchers to retrieve culturally meaningful outfits using a combination of hand-drawn sketches, which convey garment structure, and textual descriptions, which encode cultural semantics. The dataset is initialized with 650 sketches and expanded using generative models to produce over 21,000 photorealistic images with aligned captions. Textual prompts that describe detailed outfit attributes, which are extracted from fashion magazines to ensure authenticity and diversity. To better reflect the inherent ambiguity of design intent, VietFashion adopts a multi-target retrieval setting, where a single query may correspond to multiple valid results. We establish standardized evaluation protocols and benchmark state-of-the-art composed image retrieval methods. Experimental results reveal significant performance gaps in modeling fine-grained cultural semantics and multi-modal composition, positioning VietFashion as a challenging benchmark for fine-grained fashion retrieval. The dataset is publicly available at: https://hng0303.github.io/VietFashion.

AI.tldr: VietFashion is a benchmark for sketch-text composed image retrieval focusing on the Ao Dai, introducing a multi-modal, multi-target dataset to study fine-grained cultural semantics in fashion retrieval; it reveals gaps in current models and establishes a public benchmark.
AI.motivation: To address the challenge of retrieving culturally meaningful garments where identity depends on subtle structural and symbolic cues. Existing models underperform on fine-grained, multi-modal fashion retrieval, especially for culturally specific attire.
AI.method: Introduce VietFashion: a dataset built from 650 hand-drawn sketches expanded via generative models to over 21k photorealistic images with aligned captions. Text prompts describe detailed attributes from fashion magazines. Adopt a multi-target retrieval setting where one query may map to multiple valid results. Standard evaluation protocols and benchmarks for state-of-the-art composed image retrieval methods.
AI.result: Experiments show significant performance gaps in modeling fine-grained cultural semantics and multi-modal composition, indicating VietFashion is a challenging benchmark for fine-grained fashion retrieval.
AI.conclusion: VietFashion provides a valuable, publicly available resource for researching sketch-text composed image retrieval in cultural fashion, promoting advances in models that can handle fine-grained semantics and multi-modal composition for traditional garments.

title: Physics-Guided Spatiotemporal Learning for Coastal Wave Peak Period Estimation from Video
summary: Wave parameters in the nearshore are crucial for coastal engineering, shoreline protection, marine hazard assessment, and coastal management for climate resilience. Traditional monitoring systems like buoys and radar platforms offer accurate monitoring but can have high installation and maintenance expenses and limited spatial coverage. Passive ocean monitoring using video has been achieved by leveraging deep learning, however, many methods are not physically interpretable, feasible, and validated for oceanography. In thiswork, a Physics-Guided Deep Spatiotemporal Learning Framework for direct estimation of nearshore wave peak periods from passive coastal video stream is proposed. The framework combines automated temporal-variance based region-of-interest detection, multi-stage Sim-to-Real transfer learning, and physics-informed regularization to enhance the predictive accuracy and physical consistency. A variety of spatiotemporal architectures were assessed, such as transformer-based and recurrent-convolutional ones, alongside synthetic pretraining,silver-label adaptation, and expert fine-tuning. The results show that transformer-based architectures outperformed in terms of the accuracy of the instantaneous prediction, while lightweight recurrent-convolutional architectures achieved higher temporal stability and operational oceanographic skill. Ablation studies also demonstrated the benefits of physics-guided regularization in terms of trend-following consistency, and physically implausible predictions. Explainability auditing also helped to focus attention in hydrodynamically active surf-zone regions and showed good agreement with the physically derived wave propagation behavior. In general, the proposed framework shows the promise of physics-guided video-based deep learning systems for long-term coastal wave monitoring that are cost-efficient and operationally feasible.

AI.tldr: A physics-guided, sim-to-real, deep spatiotemporal learning framework using passive coastal video to estimate nearshore wave peak periods. Transformer architectures optimize instantaneous predictions while lightweight RCNNs improve temporal stability; physics-informed regularization enhances physical consistency; explainability auditing aligns model attention with hydrodynamic behavior; framework promises cost-efficient, operational long-term coastal monitoring.
AI.motivation: Address the need for reliable, long-term nearshore wave parameter monitoring for coastal engineering and climate resilience. Traditional sensors are expensive and sparse; pure data-driven video methods often lack physical interpretability and validation in oceanography.
AI.method: Automated temporal-variance-based ROI detection; multi-stage Sim-to-Real transfer learning; physics-informed regularization; evaluation across transformer-based and recurrent-convolutional architectures; synthetic pretraining, silver-label adaptation, expert fine-tuning; explainability auditing to validate physical plausibility and focus on surf-zone dynamics.
AI.result: Transformers achieve higher accuracy for instantaneous wave-peak predictions; lightweight recurrent-convolutional networks attain greater temporal stability and operational oceanographic skill; physics-guided regularization improves trend-following consistency and reduces physically implausible outputs; explainability audits show attention aligns with hydrodynamically active regions and wave propagation behavior.
AI.conclusion: The proposed physics-guided video-based DL framework demonstrates strong potential for long-term, cost-efficient coastal wave monitoring, combining interpretability, physical consistency, and practical performance across architectures.

title: Order Is Not Control
summary: AI alignment, interpretability, steering, and neural perturbation studies identify order-inducing objects. We argue that order is not control. Control requires a receiver-gated response law: a denominator-indexed operator mapping material state, action/drive, bath, and receiver state to response displacement, sinks, effort, and basin projection. We identify it across biological, LLM, adapter, and stochastic-operator panels. The laws are local: an intervention can be admitted, saturated, sign-changing, leaky, or overdriven depending on medium, bath, receiver state, action port, and comparator. Control is assigned when finite effort moves a target or outcome-readout class under the same denominator while damage, null/evasive, invalid format, overdrive, and unnecessary effort stay bounded. Mouse ALM, C. elegans, and zebrafish panels provide physical response-operator evidence while excluding coordinate identity and controller conclusions. LLM panels show generated-output response laws: across four material conditions, response vectors are predictable at 72.8-73.7% component-sign accuracy, rising to 84.3-84.8% on nonzero components; held-out observers predict system-effect and target/oracle families at 93.6% and 91.7% accuracy. Constitution-conditioned adapters reshape susceptibility as prepared media, and stochastic-operator panels separate measured opportunity from deployable action policies. This gives a driven-dissipative response-system account at the mesoscopic control level: drives act through prepared media, baths, and receivers, producing admitted movement, impedance, sinks, or overdrive. The evidence supports local admitted control and measurable stochastic response operators, while leaving deployable pre-generation control, hidden/logit causal sufficiency, biological-to-LLM coordinate identity, and literal thermodynamic quantities outside scope.

AI.tldr: A cross-domain framework distinguishes order-inducing phenomena from true control via a receiver-gated response law implemented as a denominator-indexed operator. The work aggregates biology (mouse ALM, C. elegans, zebrafish) and AI (LLMs, adapters, stochastic-operators) to propose a mesoscopic driven-dissipative model of control, supported by quantitative predictions but with scope limitations (pre-generation control, hidden causal sufficiency, and physical thermodynamics left out).
AI.motivation: To challenge the conflation of observed order with controllability by formalizing a receiver-gated, locality-based control law that can operate across biological and artificial systems and to test its applicability using multi-modal panels.
AI.method: Synthesize cross-domain evidence from biological systems (ALM, C. elegans, zebrafish), LLMs, adapters, and stochastic-operator experiments. Define a denominator-indexed, local response operator mapping states, drives, baths, and receiver states to response displacement, sinks, and other outcomes. Assess predictability of response vectors, target/oracle predictions, and system effects across conditions, with held-out data.
AI.result: Evidence shows locally admitted control-like behavior with measurable stochastic response operators. In LLM experiments, component-sign accuracy ~72.8–73.7% (84.3–84.8% for nonzero components); held-out observers predict system-effect and target/oracle families at ~93.6% and ~91.7% accuracy. Constitution-conditioned adapters alter susceptibility; stochastic-operator panels separate opportunity from deployable policies. Overall, a driven-dissipative, mesoscopic control account emerges, though deployable pre-generation control, hidden/logit sufficiency, biological-to-LLM coordinate identity, and literal thermodynamic quantities remain outside scope.
AI.conclusion: The data support local admitted control and measurable stochastic response operators, providing a unifying, mesoscopic framework for control across domains. However, several aspects—pre-generation control, causal sufficiency, coordinate identity, and physical quantities—are not addressed, indicating boundaries of the current theory and directions for future work.

title: OmniDirector: General Multi-Shot Camera Cloning without Cross-Paired Data
summary: Cloning camera motion from reference videos is an important task in video generation, as videos provide intuitive and precise control. Existing methods either directly use parametric representations that fail to handle multi-shot generation or synthesize cross-paired data, which suffer from data scarcity, resulting in poor performance in complicated camera motion cloning. To address these issues, we introduce a general camera motion representation that encodes cameras as grid motion videos. This camera grid represents the camera parameters visually and supports the integration of diverse trajectories for multi-shot video generation. Building upon this, we propose OmniDirector, a unified framework trained on a million-scale camera grid-video pairs that coordinates characters, actions, and cameras to provide director-level control for multimodal diffusion transformers. Furthermore, we design a novel hierarchical prompt expansion agent that harmoniously integrates different control signals by systematically describing camera motion and visual content through understanding signal relationships. Extensive experiments demonstrate the superior performance and outstanding controllability of our framework. Project page: https://ymlinfeng.github.io/OmniDirector.github.io/

AI.tldr: Proposes a camera grid video representation for multi-shot video generation and introduces OmniDirector, a large-scale, director-level multimodal diffusion framework with a hierarchical prompt expansion agent to integrate camera motion with characters and actions.
AI.motivation: Current camera motion cloning struggles with multi-shot generation using parametric representations and suffers from data scarcity when using cross-paired data, limiting performance on complex camera motions.
AI.method: Encode cameras as grid motion videos (camera grid) to visually represent camera parameters and support diverse trajectories; train OmniDirector on a million-scale camera grid-video dataset to coordinate characters, actions, and cameras for multimodal diffusion; develop a hierarchical prompt expansion agent to harmonize control signals by describing camera motion and visual content and modeling their relationships.
AI.result: Extensive experiments demonstrate superior performance and strong controllability in multimodal video generation with integrated camera motion control.
AI.conclusion: The camera grid representation enables robust, scalable multi-shot video generation, and the OmniDirector framework provides director-level control for multimodal diffusion models; the hierarchical prompt expansion agent enhances integration of diverse control signals.

title: ReSum: Synergizing LLM Reasoning and Summarization with Reinforcement Learning
comment: 24 pages, including 13 pages of main text and 11 pages of appendix
summary: Reinforcement Learning with Verifiable Rewards (RLVR) is a central technique for improving long-horizon reasoning in Large Language Models (LLMs). However, existing RLVR methods often encourage unnecessarily long reasoning rollouts, which can degrade reasoning coherence and exhaust the available context budget. Existing approaches to long-context organization often depend on external mechanisms to organize rollouts, rather than enabling the model to manage its own reasoning trajectory. To address this limitation, we propose ReSum, a novel RLVR framework that enables LLMs to compress and organize their reasoning trajectories through self-summarization. Our pilot studies show that self-summarization stabilizes generation by lowering token-level entropy, and that introducing a ``summarization'' phrase can substantially mitigate errors propagated from an incorrect rollout prefix. Motivated by these findings, ReSum adopts a summarization-aware adaptive rollout mechanism that contrastively evaluates whether self-summarization benefits the ongoing reasoning process. Specifically, when the model spontaneously triggers self-summarization, ReSum masks the summarization phrase to create a contrastive branch; for non-summarization positions, it instead randomly injects the phrase to create a matched branch. We further design a summarization-aware advantage to enable finer-grained comparison between contrastive rollout trajectories. Extensive experiments show that ReSum improves performance at an average of 4\% while reducing rollout length by 18.6\%.

AI.tldr: ReSum uses self-summarization to compress and organize LLM reasoning in RLVR, employing a summarization-aware, contrastive rollout to reduce length and improve coherence, achieving ~4% gain and 18.6% shorter rollouts.
AI.motivation: Long-horizon RLVR can induce excessive, incoherent reasoning and exhaust context; current methods rely on external organization; models should manage their own reasoning trajectory.
AI.method: Introduce self-summarization with summarization phrase; adaptive rollout that contrastively evaluates branches: masking summarization phrase when self-summarization occurs, or injecting phrase to create a matched branch; include summarization-aware advantage for finer comparison.
AI.result: Empirical evaluation shows average performance improvement around 4% and rollout length reduction of 18.6%; self-summarization lowers token entropy and mitigates errors from incorrect rollout prefixes.
AI.conclusion: ReSum demonstrates that self-directed summarization and contrastive rollout management can improve long-horizon reasoning efficiency and stability in LLMs.

title: Is Spurious Correlation Removal Always Learnable?
summary: Invariant learning can fail even when the invariant structure is statistically identifiable. We show a conditional computational barrier: under a black-box samplable supervised sparse recovery primitive motivated by average-case sparse-recovery reductions, there exist \emph{samplable} multi-environment instances with a one-dimensional predictive invariant subspace ($k=1$) that are learnable with polynomial samples by exhaustive search, while any polynomial-time constant-accuracy recovery algorithm would contradict the primitive. We further quantify environment diversity by a separation parameter $γ$, which controls identifiability and the curvature of invariance objectives. Under sufficient diversity and local Gaussian regularity, the minimax risk is $\mathbb{E}[\dist(\hat{V},V_{\mathrm{inv}})^2]=Θ(k(d-k)/(n|\mathcal{E}|))$, and under label-induced shifts a phase transition occurs at $n^*\propto k(d-k)/(|\mathcal{E}|γ^2)$ with refined estimation error scaling proportional to $1/γ^2$. Synthetic and real datasets illustrate the predicted gaps and transitions and motivate simple diversity diagnostics.

AI.tldr: In multi-environment invariant learning, there is a computational barrier: samplable instances with a 1-D invariant subspace are learnable by exhaustive search with polynomial samples, while any polynomial-time constant-accuracy recovery would contradict the primitive; risk scales as Θ(k(d−k)/(n|E|)); phase transition in sample complexity for label-induced shifts at n* ∝ k(d−k)/(|E| γ^2).
AI.motivation: To understand why invariant learning can fail despite statistical identifiability; characterize computational-statistical tradeoffs induced by environment diversity; quantify how diversity (γ) and curvature affect identifiability, sample complexity, and risk.
AI.method: Theoretical setup based on a black-box samplable supervised sparse-recovery primitive; average-case reductions; derivation of minimax risk under local Gaussian regularity; analysis of diversity parameter γ; characterization of phase transition; synthetic/real-data experiments for validation and diagnostics.
AI.result: Demonstrates conditional computational barrier: polynomial-time algorithms with constant accuracy would contradict the primitive; minimax risk scaling Θ(k(d−k)/(n|E|)); phase transition threshold n* ∝ k(d−k)/(|E| γ^2) with error scaling ∼ 1/γ^2; empirical evidence of gaps and transitions; proposed diversity diagnostics.
AI.conclusion: Diversity of environments is essential; statistical identifiability is insufficient to guarantee learnability; provide practical diagnostics to assess diversity and anticipate gaps; results unify computational-statistical perspective in invariant learning.

title: VISA: VLM-Guided Instance Semantic Auditing for 3D Occupancy World Models
summary: Semantic 3D occupancy provides a voxelized world state for autonomous driving and robot decision making, but object and rare-class errors can affect free-space interpretation, collision checking, and temporal state propagation. We show that a common VLM strategy, aligning 3D voxel or object features with crop-caption embeddings, improves text-space similarity without reliably improving closed-set occupancy mIoU. Motivated by this mismatch, we propose VISA, a training-time semantic auditing approach for existing occupancy world models. VISA queries an offline VLM on a representative crop of each physical object instance, obtains a structured audit with class hypotheses, plausible confusions, reliability, attributes, and evidence, and propagates it along the object track. The audit is grounded to matched 3D object voxels and distilled into semantic logits through reliability-weighted taxonomy, attribute-factor, and scene-level audit graph losses, while inference remains unchanged and requires no VLM. On nuScenes, averaged across three runs, VISA improves OccWorld from 19.06 to 20.05 mIoU and GaussianWorld from 21.36 to 21.91 mIoU; on GaussianWorld, object mIoU improves from 18.18 to 19.16 and rare-class mIoU from 15.60 to 16.79. These results suggest that VLMs are better suited to closed-set occupancy as reliability-aware semantic auditors than as generic caption-embedding targets.

AI.tldr: VLM-based alignment of 3D occupancy to text space does not reliably improve occupancy metrics; authors introduce VISA, a training-time semantic auditing method that converts VLM outputs into reliability-weighted semantic logits. This yields consistent improvements in 3D occupancy mIoU (and rare-class mIoU) without changing inference.
AI.motivation: There is a misalignment between 3D occupancy semantics and typical VLM caption-space objectives, which can harm free-space interpretation and rare-class handling. A reliability-aware auditing mechanism is needed to leverage VLMs during training without incurring inference-time VLM costs.
AI.method: VISA queries an offline VLM on representative object-crops to build a structured audit (class hypotheses, confusions, reliability, attributes, evidence) linked to 3D voxels. This audit is distilled into semantic logits via three losses (reliability-weighted taxonomy, attribute-factor, scene-level audit graph). Inference remains unchanged and does not require a VLM.
AI.result: On nuScenes (three runs): OccWorld improves 19.06→20.05 mIoU; GaussianWorld 21.36→21.91 mIoU. For GaussianWorld: object mIoU 18.18→19.16; rare-class mIoU 15.60→16.79.
AI.conclusion: VLMs are more effective as reliability-aware semantic auditors for closed-set occupancy than as generic caption-embedding targets; VISA provides training-time semantic auditing that improves occupancy performance without additional inference-time cost.

title: Can I Buy Your KV Cache?
summary: Right now, across the world, AI agents are repeating the same absurd act: to read one document, they each recompute it from scratch. Every agent re-runs prefill, the most compute-intensive step a large model takes, over identical text, only to rebuild a key-value (KV) cache identical to the one the agent before it just built. The same answer, computed a million times. We make a proposal that is almost offensively simple: compute it once. Let a publisher precompute a document's KV cache, and let every other agent buy the right to load it and skip prefill. It works, and it is token-exact: loading a precomputed KV and continuing matches prefilling from scratch (24/24 greedy tokens, and at the logits level), with no accuracy cost. On Qwen3-4B, reuse is 9-50x cheaper in compute than prefill, and the gap widens with length (prefill's attention scales with L^2), so a single reuse already pays it back. Then the part that matters: where the KV lives. Shipping it fails, because KV is nearly incompressible, so per-load egress costs more than the prefill it saves. Hosting it provider-side, exactly as production prompt-caching works, removes egress entirely. The size of the prize is set by our measured compute saving: serving one hot 3774-token document to 80M agents costs ~$1.5M to re-prefill but only ~$0.03M of reuse compute (49.7x less). The 0.1x cache-read tariff APIs charge passes a 10x discount to users while sitting inside this measured envelope, so the 10x is a floor that the measured ~50x compute saving clears, and the gap to the physical ~50x is provider margin: millions of dollars per popular document. We frame the resulting agent-native prefill CDN and leave lossless KV compression and a cross-party payment layer as the open problems.

AI.tldr: Precompute and host document KV caches to avoid per-agent full prefill; observed 9–50x compute savings on Qwen3-4B; real-world economics hinge on hosting (egress costs) and a cross-party payment layer; the approach yields large potential margins but requires practical deployment (KV compression, caching policy, and incentive schemes).
AI.motivation: Reduce redundant compute and latency in multi-agent LM usage by avoiding repeated prefill for identical prompts/text, addressing the quadratic attention cost of prefill with long inputs.
AI.method: Publishers precompute a document's KV cache; agents load the precomputed KV instead of performing prefill; results shown to be token-exact to scratch prefill; evaluated on Qwen3-4B with metrics comparing compute cost of reuse vs prefill; analysis of hosting vs egress for KV data; proposal of an agent-native prefill CDN and a cross-party payment layer as open problems.
AI.result: Token-exact reuse: loading a precomputed KV matches scratch prefill for 24/24 greedy tokens and logits; compute savings of 9–50x depending on length; for a 3774-token doc to 80M agents, reuse compute ~$0.03M vs prefill ~$1.5M (≈49.7x cheaper). KV data is near-incompressible, making per-load egress cost high; hosting KV on provider side eliminates egress; 0.1x cache-read tariffs provide a floor that still accommodates a ~50x potential overall savings considering provider margins.
AI.conclusion: The potential economic gains from agent-native prefill caching are substantial, but practical deployment hinges on enabling lossless KV compression, efficient host delivery, and a cross-party payment mechanism; the paper frames a concrete path toward a scalable prefill CDN for LMs and highlights the principal technical and economic open problems.

title: Circuit Synchronization Precedes Generalization: Causal Evidence from Fourier Structure in Grokking Transformers
summary: Grokking -- where a transformer on modular arithmetic suddenly transitions from near-chance to near-perfect validation accuracy -- is attributed to a Fourier circuit, but its timing, causal structure, and controllability remain poorly understood. We introduce the Frequency Synchronization Degree (FSD), a normalised, permutation-tested metric for Fourier circuit synchronisation requiring no prior circuit knowledge. Across nine modular addition configurations (primes p in {53, 71, 97, 113, 131}, three seeds), FSD synchronises 500-3,000 steps before grokking (mean lead +1,722 steps; all nine positive, sign-test p~0.004), and precedes a restricted-logit loss baseline (Nanda et al.'s excluded loss) in all nine cases, making it the earliest available predictor. We provide direct causal evidence that the inter-phase gap is a regularisation phenomenon: forking training at the FSD-ceiling step and varying weight decay lambda produces strictly monotone earlier grokking, with Delta_t proportional to 1/lambda. This law replicates across three primes (p in {53,97,131}; R^2=1.00 and R^2=0.99 for two clean cases), captured as Delta_t ~ C/lambda, consistent with (1/lambda)*log(||W_mem||/tau). Architecture ablations show an attention-only model groks with a strong FSD precursor; an MLP-only model never groks; a single-layer model's FSD lags, confirming the precursor is a multi-block circuit property.

AI.tldr: Frequency Synchronization Degree (FSD) as an early predictor of grokking in transformer models on modular arithmetic tasks, with causal evidence linking inter-phase gap regularisation to generalisation timing; architecture ablations show multi-block circuits necessary for grokking.
AI.motivation: Understand the timing, causal structure, and controllability of grokking in neural transformers and identify an early, model-agnostic predictor.
AI.method: Define FSD as a normalized, permutation-tested synchronisation metric for Fourier circuits; evaluate across nine modular addition configurations with multiple primes and seeds; compare to an excluded loss baseline; perform causal experiments by forking training at FSD-ceiling with varying weight decay; conduct architecture ablations (attention-only, MLP-only, single-layer).
AI.result: FSD synchronises 500-3,000 steps before grokking across all configurations (mean lead +1,722 steps; p≈0.004). It precedes the excluded loss baseline, providing the earliest predictor. Forcing training to fork at the FSD ceiling and varying lambda yields strictly monotone earlier grokking with Delta_t ~ C/λ, replicated across primes (R^2≈1.0, 0.99). Attention-only models show strong FSD precursor; MLP-only do not grok; single-layer FSD lags, indicating multi-block circuit property is essential.
AI.conclusion: FSD is a robust, early, causally linked predictor of grokking in modular arithmetic transformers, revealing a regularisation-driven inter-phase gap as a key mechanism; grokking requires multi-block Fourier circuits; controlling weight decay modulates grokking timing predictably.

title: Point-Wise Geometry-Aware Transformer for Partial-to-Full Point Cloud Registration in Computer-Assisted Surgery
summary: Partial-to-full registration remains challenging due to varying overlap ratios, fluctuating point densities, and the presence of noise. While transformers have shown strong potential for point cloud processing, prior methods typically confine them to global context aggregation, overlooking fine-grained local geometry crucial for accurate correspondence. We propose \emph{GAPR-Net}, a learning-based point cloud registration framework with a coarse-to-fine architecture that combines convolution and transformer modules, in which local and global information is fused between the partial and full point clouds using a cross-attention mechanism. To achieve this, a transformation-invariant point-wise geometric feature representation is proposed, which can robustly capture relative geometric features for individual points with respect to their neighboring points. To evaluate the effectiveness of the proposed approach, experiments are conducted on four geometrically distinct bones, including the tibia, femur, pelvis, and thoracic cartilage. The overall registration recall reaches 94.2\%, the method results in a low RMSE of 1.992 mm and $R^2$ values of 0.908 and 0.974 for rotation and translation, respectively. The results demonstrate that the proposed method effectively addresses the partial-to-full point cloud registration problem. The proposed method enables highly accurate 3D point cloud registration using partial observation, providing a critical foundation for precise surgical navigation and robotic interventions in computer-assisted surgery. The code will be accessed after the double-blind review process.

AI.tldr: GAPR-Net introduces a coarse-to-fine registration framework that fuses local geometric cues and global context via cross-attention to improve partial-to-full point cloud alignment, supported by a transformation-invariant point-wise feature representation.
AI.motivation: Partial-to-full registration is hindered by varying overlap, density, and noise; transformers excel at global context but often miss fine-grained local geometry; a robust local geometry-aware correspondence mechanism is needed.
AI.method: A hybrid coarse-to-fine architecture that combines convolution and transformer modules; cross-attention links partial and full clouds; proposes a transformation-invariant point-wise geometric feature representation that captures relative geometry to neighboring points.
AI.result: Evaluated on four bones (tibia, femur, pelvis, thoracic cartilage); registration recall 94.2%; RMSE 1.992 mm; R^2 for rotation 0.908 and translation 0.974; demonstrates effective partial-to-full registration.
AI.conclusion: Demonstrates effective partial-to-full registration; supports precise surgical navigation and robotic interventions; code to be released after double-blind review.

title: IterCAD: An Iterative Multimodal Agent for Visually-Grounded CAD Generation and Editing
summary: Computer-Aided Design is pivotal in modern manufacturing, yet existing automated methods predominantly rely on open-loop, one-shot generation, creating a mismatch with iterative real-world practices. In this paper, we present IterCAD, a unified multimodal agent framework for closed-loop, interactive CAD generation and editing. We formulate the task as a multi-turn interaction between a multimodal agent and an executable CAD sandbox, covering three tasks: Drawing-to-Code, Text-to-Code, and Interactive Editing. To support this, we develop a data synthesis pipeline incorporating advanced industrial manufacturing features to generate standard-compliant multi-view engineering drawings, complex code-editing tasks, and high-fidelity interaction trajectories. We optimize the agent via progressive SFT followed by geometry-aware reinforcement learning with viable-prefix masking to enhance code executability and geometric fidelity. Finally, we introduce the IterCAD-Bench evaluation suite and propose the Chamfer Distance Tolerance-Recall (CD-TR) curve alongside its AUC-TR metric, establishing a survivor-bias-free standard that unifies code validity and geometric precision. Extensive experiments demonstrate that IterCAD achieves highly competitive performance across multiple benchmarks, significantly outperforming existing approaches in both code executability and geometric precision, while exhibiting superior capabilities in closed-loop iterative refinement.

AI.tldr: IterCAD introduces a closed-loop, multimodal agent for CAD generation/editing, integrating data synthesis, progressive SFT, and geometry-aware RL; introduces IterCAD-Bench and CD-TR metrics; shows improvements over baselines in code executability and geometric fidelity and iterative refinement.
AI.motivation: Tackling the gap between prevailing open-loop, one-shot CAD generation and real-world iterative workflows; aims to enable interactive, multi-turn CAD design with executable code.
AI.method: A multi-turn agent–sandbox loop for Drawing-to-Code, Text-to-Code, and Interactive Editing; data synthesis pipeline producing standard-compliant multi-view drawings, complex code-editing tasks, and high-fidelity interaction trajectories; training via progressive supervised fine-tuning followed by geometry-aware reinforcement learning using viable-prefix masking; evaluation with IterCAD-Bench and Chamfer Distance Tolerance-Recall (CD-TR) curve and AUC-TR metric.
AI.result: IterCAD achieves competitive performance across benchmarks, significantly outperforming existing approaches in code executability and geometric precision, with strong capabilities in closed-loop iterative refinement.
AI.conclusion: Introduces a unified framework for interactive CAD generation, plus new evaluation metrics (CD-TR/AUC-TR) and an evaluation suite (IterCAD-Bench); demonstrates improved code–geometry alignment and practical iterative capabilities for CAD automation.

title: Predicting Cognitive Load from Speech and Interaction Dynamics in Dyadic Conversations
summary: Estimating cognitive load from speech has largely been studied in controlled laboratory settings, with limited understanding of its reliability in natural collaborative conversations. We investigate whether speech and interaction dynamics predict perceived cognitive load during dyadic conversations. We analyze audio from 53 dyads performing nine collaborative tasks and extract static acoustic, dynamic, and interaction features to train a two-head Gated Recurrent Unit encoder to predict cognitive load scores. Results show conversational interaction provides useful signals for predicting cognitive load related to time pressure, mental work, effort, and task performance. Temporal demand is associated with turn-taking dynamics such as overlap and speaker switch, while mental demand is linked to imbalanced participation between speakers. These findings highlight the importance of task structure and conversational interaction for modeling cognitive load in natural collaborative settings.

AI.tldr: Natural dyadic speech signals can predict perceived cognitive load; temporal demand relates to turn-taking (overlap, switches) and mental demand to participation imbalance.
AI.motivation: Assess whether cognitive load can be inferred from real-world conversational speech and identify which acoustic and interaction cues drive predictions.
AI.method: analyzed audio from 53 dyads across nine collaborative tasks; extracted static acoustic, dynamic, and interaction features; used a two-head GRU encoder to predict cognitive load scores.
AI.result: Interaction dynamics provide useful signals for predicting cognitive load; temporal demand links to overlap and speaker switches, while mental demand links to imbalanced participation; highlights the role of task structure and conversational interaction in natural settings.
AI.conclusion: Modeling cognitive load in natural collaborative settings benefits from incorporating conversational interaction cues and task-structure signals to improve prediction and understanding.

title: Budget-Constrained Step-Level Diffusion Caching
summary: Step-level caching accelerates diffusion models by exploiting temporal redundancy across denoising steps. Existing methods make per-step cache decisions using threshold-based heuristics, without directly optimizing for final output quality. As a result, their inference latency varies across inputs and is difficult to control at deployment. In this work, we propose BudCache, which inverts this formulation: rather than letting per-step error thresholds dictate the runtime cost, we fix the compute budget in advance and search for the cache policy that best preserves the final output. To tackle the combinatorial complexity of step selection, we combine Simulated Annealing with deterministic Hill Climbing. This offline search identifies high-quality cache policies within minutes and introduces no online search or thresholding overhead during inference. When the compute budget is very tight, we further introduce cache-aware schedule alignment, which adapts the time discretization to the selected cache policy to reduce cache-induced trajectory mismatch. Experiments on FLUX.1-dev and Wan2.1 show that BudCache achieves better generation quality than heuristic caching baselines under the same inference budgets. Code is available at https://github.com/Westlake-AGI-Lab/BudCache

AI.tldr: BudCache reframes step-level caching as a fixed-budget optimization problem, using offline search (simulated annealing + hill climbing) to find cache policies that preserve final output quality, with cache-aware schedule alignment to reduce trajectory mismatch; shows improved generation quality vs heuristic baselines under the same budgets.
AI.motivation: Existing per-step caching uses threshold-based heuristics to decide which denoising steps to cache, leading to variable latency and non-optimal final output. A fixed-budget, offline-optimized policy aims for predictable latency and improved quality.
AI.method: Offline search over step-level caching policies with simulated annealing and deterministic hill climbing to maximize final output quality under a fixed compute budget; no online search or thresholding during inference. When budgets are extremely tight, apply cache-aware schedule alignment to tailor time discretization to the policy.
AI.result: BudCache achieves better generation quality than heuristic caching baselines under the same inference budgets on FLUX.1-dev and Wan2.1; code available at the provided URL.
AI.conclusion: Reframing caching as a fixed-budget optimization and using offline SA+HC search yields high-quality policies with no online overhead, and cache-aware scheduling further reduces trajectory mismatch, making the approach practical for deployment.

title: A Quantitative Experimental Repeated Measures Study of Training Dynamics in a Small Llama Style Language Model Under a Compute-Aware Token Budget
summary: This study examines training dynamics in a small Llama-style language model trained under a fixed, compute-constrained token budget. Rather than evaluating efficiency solely through endpoint performance, the study uses a quantitative experimental repeated measures design to analyze how validation loss, validation perplexity, rolling volatility, backslide behavior, spike behavior, and between-seed variability change across token-based training intervals. Six independent training runs were conducted on a 4.26-million-parameter model using the TinyStories corpus, CPU-based full-precision training, and a target budget of approximately 20 million cumulative training tokens. Metrics were collected across 21 intervals, producing 126 seed-by-interval observations. Repeated measures ANOVA showed statistically significant interval effects for validation loss, validation perplexity, and rolling volatility. Descriptive trajectories revealed rapid early improvement followed by non-monotonic degradation during later training intervals. Mean validation loss decreased from 8.3552 at initialization to 2.7996 near 4 million tokens, but increased to 3.9010 by the final checkpoint. Validation perplexity followed the same pattern, falling sharply early in training before rising later. Derived telemetry further showed recurrent validation-loss backslides and no interval-summary evidence of a stable phase under the predefined criteria. These findings suggest that compute-aware language model evaluation should examine training trajectories rather than endpoint metrics alone. In constrained compute settings, additional token exposure may increase computational cost without producing proportional generalization gains, and interval-level telemetry can reveal instability, regression, and diminishing returns that final metrics may obscure.

AI.tldr: A small Llama-style model trained under a fixed compute budget shows non-monotonic training dynamics; interval-level metrics reveal significant changes, backslides, and volatility that endpoint metrics miss, advocating trajectory-aware evaluation in constrained settings.
AI.motivation: To understand how compute-constrained training dynamics unfold over token intervals and across seeds, and to assess whether endpoint performance adequately reflects generalization under fixed compute budgets.
AI.method: Six independent runs on a 4.26M-parameter model trained on TinyStories with CPU full-precision, targeting ~20M cumulative tokens. Data collected across 21 intervals (126 seed-interval observations) for validation loss, validation perplexity, rolling volatility, backslide/spike behavior, and inter-seed variability. Repeated measures ANOVA used to assess interval effects.
AI.result: Statistically significant interval effects for validation loss, perplexity, and rolling volatility. Trajectories show rapid early improvement followed by non-monotonic degradation. Mean validation loss fell from 8.3552 to 2.7996 around 4M tokens, then rose to 3.9010 at final checkpoint. Validation perplexity exhibited a similar pattern. Telemetry indicated recurrent validation-loss backslides and no interval-based stable phase per predefined criteria.
AI.conclusion: In compute-constrained LM training, evaluating trajectory dynamics is essential; endpoint metrics can mask instability and diminishing returns. Additional token exposure may incur higher compute cost without proportional generalization gains; interval telemetry can reveal instability that final metrics obscure.

title: EPM-JEPA: Operator-Side Experience Modulation in JEPA-Family World Models
comment: 16 pages, 5 figures, 9 tables, 5 code listings. Pre-registered experimental study with mechanism analysis
summary: JEPA-family world models use a static predictor whose weights do not adapt when test-time dynamics diverge from training. We compare two mechanisms for incorporating accumulated experience into a JEPA predictor under distribution shift: operand-side injection, where a compressed experience representation is added as a residual to the predictor's hidden state (EI-JEPA), and operator-side modulation, where the same representation generates low-rank weight deltas via LoRA applied to the predictor's weights (EPM-JEPA). On a pre-registered comparison (Moving MNIST, gravity shift), EPM-JEPA (D_shift^{n=50} = 0.7848 +/- 0.0078, three seeds) differs from EI-JEPA (0.8238) by delta = 4.74% - Outcome C: a null result - by our stated criterion, a valid outcome. As a secondary, non-pre-registered observation, EPM-JEPA improves 1.90% over a no-memory baseline (0.8000), consistently across seeds, while EI-JEPA underperforms the baseline, indicating the benefit is specific to weight-level modulation. Our primary contribution is a mechanism analysis: the D_shift^{n=50} trajectory reflects three independent dynamical processes - buffer cycling, EMA target drift, and an intrinsic LoRA settling transient of +0.021 - rather than convergence to equilibrium. These findings motivate PEM-JEPA, a physics-grounded successor addressing this dynamical-peak limitation.

AI.tldr: Two JEPA-memory mechanisms are compared under distribution shift: EI-JEPA (residual cue) and EPM-JEPA (LoRA-based weight modulation). Main task shows EPM-JEPA underperforms EI-JEPA on Moving MNIST gravity shift (D_shift^50 about 0.7848 vs 0.8238), yielding a ~4.7% degradation, yet EPM-JEPA improves over a no-memory baseline; EI-JEPA worsens relative to baseline. A mechanism analysis attributes the observed trajectory to three dynamical processes (buffer cycling, EMA target drift, LoRA settling transient), not convergence. This motivates PEM-JEPA, a physics-grounded successor that aims to address dynamical-peak limitations.
AI.motivation: Address how to integrate accumulated experience into JEPA predictors under distribution shift, comparing operand-side residual augmentation versus operator-side weight modulation to understand their effects and dynamics.
AI.method: Pre-registered comparison on Moving MNIST with gravity shift (n=50, three seeds) comparing EI-JEPA (residual) and EPM-JEPA (LoRA-based modulation). Reported D_shift^n and trajectories; secondary non-pre-registered observation across seeds showing memory-based gains vs no-memory baseline. Mechanism analysis decomposing the D_shift^50 trajectory into buffer cycling, EMA drift, and LoRA settling transient (+0.021). Proposes PEM-JEPA as successor.
AI.result: EPM-JEPA underperforms EI-JEPA on the primary metric under the tested shift but beats a no-memory baseline by ~1.9%; EI-JEPA underperforms the baseline. The D_shift^50 trajectory is explained by three independent processes rather than convergence. A physics-grounded path (PEM-JEPA) is proposed to address dynamical-peak limitations.
