AI.conclusion: Memory mechanisms for JEPA models yield mixed outcomes under distribution shift; weight-modulation via LoRA produces a distinct dynamical signature and may require physics-grounded design (PEM-JEPA) to address dynamical peaks and target drift.

title: MiniMax Sparse Attention
summary: Ultra-long-context capability is becoming indispensable for frontier LLMs: agentic workflows, repository-scale code reasoning, and persistent memory all require the model to jointly attend over hundreds of thousands to millions of tokens, yet the quadratic cost of softmax attention makes this untenable at deployment scale. We introduce MiniMax Sparse Attention (MSA), a blockwise sparse attention built upon Grouped Query Attention (GQA). A lightweight Index Branch scores key-value blocks and independently selects a Top-k subset for each GQA group, enabling group-specific sparse retrieval while maintaining efficient block-level execution; the Main Branch then performs exact block-sparse attention over only the selected blocks. Designed around a principle of simplicity and scalability, MSA is deliberately streamlined, making it straightforward to deploy efficiently across a broad range of GPUs. To translate sparsity into practical speedups, we co-design MSA with a GPU execution path that uses exp-free Top-k selection and KV-outer sparse attention to improve tensor-core utilization under block-granular access. On a 109B-parameter model with native multimodal training, MSA performs on par with GQA while reducing per-token attention compute by 28.4x at 1M context. Paired with our co-designed kernel, MSA achieves 14.2x prefill and 7.6x decoding wall-clock speedups on H800. Our inference kernel is available at: https://github.com/MiniMax-AI/MSA. A production-grade natively multimodal model powered by MSA has been publicly released at: https://huggingface.co/MiniMaxAI/MiniMax-M3.

AI.tldr: MiniMax Sparse Attention (MSA) enables ultra-long-context LLMs with blockwise sparse attention built on Grouped Query Attention (GQA), achieving substantial speedups while preserving performance.
AI.motivation: To overcome the quadratic cost of softmax attention for extremely long contexts (hundreds of thousands to millions of tokens) and enable practical, scalable long-context models.
AI.method: MSA uses a two-branch design: an Index Branch scores key-value blocks and selects a Top-k subset for each GQA group; a Main Branch performs exact block-sparse attention over the selected blocks. It employs exp-free Top-k selection and KV-outer sparse attention and is coupled with a GPU kernel optimized for block-granular access.
AI.result: On a 109B-parameter model with native multimodal training, MSA matches GQA performance while reducing per-token attention compute by 28.4x at 1M context; with the co-designed kernel, 14.2x prefill and 7.6x decoding wall-clock speedups on H800. Code and a production-grade multimodal model have been released publicly.
AI.conclusion: MSA is a simple, scalable, and production-friendly sparse attention mechanism that delivers large practical speedups for ultra-long-context models and is readily deployable on standard GPU infrastructures.

title: Exposure Bias as Epistemic Underidentification in Recursive Forecasting
summary: Recursive multi-step forecasting is usually framed as distribution shift: models are trained on observed histories but deployed on their own predictions. We show this framing is incomplete by proving that, under partial observability or state truncation, recursive rollout is also an epistemic underidentification problem. Even with deterministic latent dynamics, one-step Bayes supervision identifies behavior only on observed contexts and need not identify the deployed recursive predictor once rollout queries self-generated induced states whose correct local targets are not determined by numeric state alone. We formalize this with induced states $Z$ and provenance variables $P$, and derive a decomposition of induced-state error into teacher-forcing/rollout mismatch, representation--class approximation, and provenance information gaps. Empirically, we show that rollout enters a distinct induced-state regime, that fixed induced states define a distinct local corrective task, and that closed-loop gains arise not only from local adaptation but also from changing the induced states visited during rollout. Using a simple binary provenance encoding, provenance-aware correction can further improve performance, though gains are conditional rather than uniform. These results recast exposure bias as reasoning under self-induced epistemic uncertainty.

AI.tldr: Frames recursive multi-step forecasting as an epistemic underidentification problem under partial observability, introducing induced states Z and provenance P; decomposes induced-state error into teacher-forcing/rollout mismatch, representation–class approximation, and provenance gaps; shows rollout creates distinct induced-state regimes and that provenance-aware corrections can help; reframes exposure bias as self-induced epistemic uncertainty.
AI.motivation: To extend understanding of rollout-based forecasting beyond distribution shift by capturing how partial observability and latent state truncation create epistemic uncertainty that affects recursive predictors.
AI.method: Theoretical formalism introducing induced states Z and provenance variables P, with an error decomposition and empirical validation using simple binary provenance encoding to demonstrate regime shifts, state-visit dynamics, and local corrective tasks under rollout.
AI.result: Identification of distinct induced-state regimes during rollout, a separate local corrective task linked to fixed induced states, and gains from both local adaptation and changes in induced-state visitation; provenance-aware correction provides conditional improvements; reframing exposure bias as self-induced epistemic uncertainty.
AI.conclusion: Exposure bias in recursive forecasting should be viewed as reasoning under self-induced epistemic uncertainty due to partial observability and induced-state dynamics, not merely distribution shift; provenance and induced-state considerations offer new diagnostic and corrective avenues.

title: Measurement-Calibrated Multi-Camera Fusion for Vision-Based Indoor Localization
comment: This paper has been accepted for presentation at the IEEE 22st International Conference on Automation Science and Engineering (CASE 2026)
summary:
Indoor vision-based localization systems are affected by detection noise, occlusions, and limited camera coverage, leading to uncertainty at multiple stages of the pipeline. While multi-camera data fusion is widely used to mitigate these issues, it is typically treated as a black-box component and evaluated solely end-to-end, obscuring its mechanistic contributions. To address this gap, this work investigates whether explicitly characterizing single-camera localization errors can be leveraged to calibrate and optimize multi-camera data fusion.
  We introduce a measurement-calibrated fusion approach that integrates component-wise error quantification, specifically isolating homography calibration, human detection, and motion tracking. A component-wise evaluation is conducted to quantify error contributions from homography calibration, human detection, and motion tracking.
  Experimental results show that data fusion improves localization accuracy compared to single-camera baselines. While measurement-calibrated fusion provides only limited improvement in absolute accuracy over standard fusion, it substantially reduces trajectory variance and improves motion smoothness, which are critical for applications requiring stable and continuous motion estimates. These results highlight the value of explicit error characterization when designing data fusion strategies for vision-based indoor positioning systems.

AI.tldr: Measurement-calibrated fusion for multi-camera indoor localization; exploits component-wise error quantification (homography, detection, tracking) to reduce trajectory variance and improve motion smoothness, though absolute accuracy gains over standard fusion are modest.
AI.motivation: Address the opacity of multi-camera fusion when treated as a black box by explicitly characterizing per-component localization errors to guide calibration and fusion design.
AI.method: Propose a measurement-calibrated fusion framework that quantifies errors for homography calibration, human detection, and motion tracking; perform component-wise evaluation to attribute error contributions and integrate these measurements into fusion.
AI.result: Fusion improves localization over single-camera baselines. The measurement-calibrated approach yields limited improvement in absolute accuracy over standard fusion but significantly reduces trajectory variance and enhances motion smoothness.
AI.conclusion: Explicit error characterization at the component level is valuable for designing robust multi-camera data fusion in vision-based indoor positioning systems, enabling more stable, continuous estimates even when absolute accuracy gains are small.

title: Neuro-Symbolic Agents for Regulated Process Automation: Challenges and Research Agenda
comment: Accepted as a poster in NILA Workshop @ IJCAI-ECAI 2026
summary: LLM-based agents are entering regulated industries where they automate judgment intensive quality management processes. We argue that symbolic structures already embedded in these domains, including regulations, typed process models, and compliance constraints, should be treated not merely as external monitoring mechanisms but as core architectural components that shape the agent's decision-making and behavior. We propose compliance-by-construction as a complementary paradigm to guardrail-based monitoring: a structural foundation that prevents control-flow violations, while guardrails remain essential for catching semantic errors. We identify a structured set of neuro-symbolic research challenges on foundational and capability level and show that addressing them jointly enables compliance-by-construction. We call on the neuro-symbolic community to engage with regulated process automation as a high impact research domain.

AI.tldr: Embed symbolic domain constraints into LLM agents as a structural foundation for compliance-by-construction, complementing guardrails; identifies neuro-symbolic challenges and advocates community engagement in regulated process automation.
AI.motivation: Regulated industries require reliable, auditable, judgment-intensive quality management. Guardrails alone cannot prevent control-flow violations; symbolic structures (regulations, process models, constraints) can serve as architectural constraints shaping decision-making.
AI.method: Propose compliance-by-construction as a design paradigm that integrates symbolic domain knowledge into the agent's core architecture; delineate a structured agenda of neuro-symbolic research challenges at foundational and capability levels; argue that joint progress enables the approach; advocate community engagement.
AI.result: Identification of a structured set of neuro-symbolic challenges; demonstration that addressing them jointly enables compliance-by-construction; establishes a research agenda.
AI.conclusion: Calls on the neuro-symbolic community to treat regulated process automation as a high-impact research domain and pursue compliance-by-construction.

title: DeepJEB++: Foundation Model-Driven Large-Scale 3D Engineering Dataset via 2D Latent Space Augmentation
comment: 16 pages, 14 figures. Submitted to ASME Journal of Mechanical Design
summary: Data-driven engineering design is constrained by the lack of large-scale 3D datasets that pair geometry with physics-based performance labels. In particular, existing 3D data augmentation techniques have limitations in preserving subtle and diverse geometric variations, and it remains difficult to automate the subsequent simulation-labeling process, where boundary conditions vary depending on the generated geometry. We present DeepJEB++, a foundation-model-driven data-augmentation framework that expands a small seed set of jet engine brackets into a large, simulation-labeled 3D dataset under constrained resources. Our key idea is to augment in the data-rich 2D latent space, then transfer to 3D. In Stage 1, we fine-tune a pretrained 2D latent diffusion model on multi-view renders and synthesize novel views by latent interpolation, retaining manufacturable designs through a vision-language-model (VLM) quality filter. In Stage 2, the validated images are lifted to 3D meshes by a domain-adapted generative foundation model. In Stage 3, an automated pipeline recognizes the load and bolt interfaces on each mesh and assigns finite-element labels -- mass, stress, and displacement -- without manual intervention. We assess augmentation quality along three intrinsic axes: manufacturability, label fidelity against the SimJEB ground truth, and distributional consistency. Starting from fewer than 400 seed designs, DeepJEB++ yields 15,360 simulation-labeled 3D brackets -- a 40x expansion -- using a single GPU per stage. The dataset will be made publicly available to support reproducible engineering-AI research.

AI.tldr: Foundation-model-driven data augmentation (DeepJEB++) expands a small seed set of jet-engine bracket designs into a large, simulation-labeled 3D dataset by augmenting in 2D latent space and transferring to 3D, enabling automated finite-element labeling; achieves ~40x expansion from <400 seeds using a three-stage pipeline; dataset will be public.
AI.motivation: Overcome the scarcity of large, labeled 3D datasets and the limitations of existing 3D augmentation, especially when boundary conditions vary; automate simulation-labeling pipeline to keep up with geometry diversity under constrained compute.
AI.method: Stage 1: fine-tune a pretrained 2D latent diffusion model on multi-view renders; synthesize novel views via latent interpolation; apply a vision-language-model filter to retain manufacturable designs. Stage 2: lift validated images to 3D meshes with a domain-adapted generative foundation model. Stage 3: automated pipeline detects load/bolt interfaces and assigns finite-element labels (mass, stress, displacement) without manual intervention.
AI.result: From fewer than 400 seed designs, DeepJEB++ yields 15,360 simulation-labeled 3D brackets (≈40x expansion) using a single GPU per stage; assessment along manufacturability, label fidelity vs SimJEB ground truth, and distributional consistency.
AI.conclusion: Publicly release the dataset to support reproducible engineering-AI research; demonstrates effective, scalable augmentation for 3D geometry with physics labels via a multi-stage foundation-model pipeline.

title: Optimizing Appliance Scheduling for Solar Energy Management Using Metaheuristic Algorithms
comment: 9 pages; full results and methodology for poster paper accepted to GECCO 2026
summary: Renewable energy is essential for meeting future energy demands; however, solar energy generation, which occurs only during daylight hours often does not align with household consumption patterns. Appliances such as cookers, washing machines, and dryers are typically operated according to user preferred schedules rather than solar energy availability, creating a scheduling optimization problem. The objective is to determine optimal appliance start times to maximize renewable energy utilization while minimizing user inconvenience and adhering to system constraints. This paper presents a metaheuristic approach using Iterated Local Search (ILS) and Simulated Annealing (SA) to optimize appliance start times, while considering appliance operating durations, power consumption, inverter limit, battery state of charge constraints, and solar generation forecasts. Unlike most existing work, the scheduling is extended beyond a single day to accommodate unfinished tasks from previous days (spillover), ensuring operational continuity and enabling sequential operation across multiple days. Experimental results show that the sequential multi-day scheduling framework effectively manages system constraints while ensuring user convenience under exclusive solar generation. These findings also open opportunities for future research on multi-objective trade-offs between investment in equipment of various sizes, return on that investment, and user satisfaction.

AI.tldr: A sequential multi-day appliance scheduling framework that uses a hybrid metaheuristic (Iterated Local Search + Simulated Annealing) to optimize appliance start times for maximizing solar energy utilization while minimizing user inconvenience, incorporating spillover across days, inverter and battery constraints, and solar forecasts; validated experimentally with evidence of feasible, user-friendly operation under solar-only generation and highlighting multi-objective trade-offs for future investment decisions.
AI.motivation: Address the mismatch between solar generation and household demand, by enabling flexible appliance scheduling that extends across days (spillover) to maintain continuity and system feasibility, and to explore the impact of equipment sizing and investment on user satisfaction.
AI.method: Formulate a multi-day optimization problem for appliance start times (considering operating durations, power consumption, inverter rating, battery state of charge, and forecasted solar generation). Solve with a hybrid metaheuristic combining Iterated Local Search (ILS) and Simulated Annealing (SA), with spillover allowing unfinished tasks to roll into subsequent days. Evaluate via experiments demonstrating constraint handling and user convenience under exclusive solar generation.
AI.result: The sequential multi-day scheduling framework effectively manages system constraints (inverter, battery SOC) and maintains user convenience while operating under solar-only generation, demonstrating feasibility of cross-day continuity and improved renewable utilization.
AI.conclusion: The approach enables sequential operation across multiple days and supports further exploration of multi-objective trade-offs among system sizing, investment return, and user satisfaction; highlights potential extensions to include economic/robust optimization and scalability considerations.

title: Reliability of Probabilistic Emulation of Physical Systems
summary: Two dominant approaches have emerged for generating probabilistic forecasts of physical systems: generative models, such as diffusion or flow matching; and ensembles of deterministic models with stochasticity injected, trained using the continuous ranked probability score (CRPS) loss. While both approaches have demonstrated strong predictive accuracy, the reliability of their uncertainties has not been systematically assessed. We address this gap by developing a framework to evaluate both approaches across diverse 2D spatiotemporal physical systems, under matched model size and computational budget. We assess the reliability of probabilistic emulation by inspecting the empirical coverage of predictive intervals, while also considering accuracy and computational efficiency metrics. CRPS-trained ensembles typically achieve more reliable uncertainties on both single-step prediction and autoregressive rollouts, demonstrating better coverage than the standard alternative of training generative models in a latent space. Moreover, the CRPS approach offers significantly faster inference. When generative models are trained in ambient rather than a compressed latent space, which is often infeasible for high-dimensional problems, they exhibit comparable coverage to CRPS-trained ensembles, though with substantially larger inference latency. In contrast, when CRPS-trained ensembles are trained in latent space they do not show a marked degradation in coverage with respect to ambient space. Both generative models and CRPS-trained ensembles demonstrate good predictive accuracy. To facilitate future research and application, we release AutoCast, a modular framework implementing both generative models and CRPS-trained ensembles, alongside AutoSim, a flexible dataset generation package for rapid prototyping.

AI.tldr: Systematic comparison of probabilistic forecasting methods for 2D spatiotemporal physics shows CRPS-trained ensembles provide more reliable uncertainty estimates and faster inference than generative models; ambient-space generative models are competitive in coverage but slower; latent-space generative models and CRPS ensembles offer different trade-offs; AutoCast and AutoSim released.
AI.motivation: Uncertainty reliability of probabilistic forecasters has not been systematically evaluated across major approaches under equal resource constraints; need to quantify calibration (coverage) and efficiency.
AI.method: Benchmark framework evaluating two families—generative models (diffusion/flow matching) and CRPS-trained ensembles—on diverse 2D spatiotemporal systems with matched model size and compute. Assess predictive-interval coverage, accuracy, and speed for single-step and autoregressive rollouts; compare ambient vs latent-space training for generative models; release AutoCast and AutoSim.
AI.result: CRPS-trained ensembles yield more reliable uncertainties with better empirical coverage for both single-step and autoregressive predictions, outperforming latent-space generative-model baselines. Ambient-space generative models achieve comparable coverage to CRPS ensembles but with substantially higher inference latency; CRPS ensembles trained in latent space retain coverage with less latency. Both approaches achieve good predictive accuracy.
AI.conclusion: For calibrated probabilistic forecasts under fixed computational budgets, CRPS-trained ensembles currently provide more reliable uncertainty quantification than generative models, though latent-space generative models offer latency benefits. The released AutoCast/AutoSim frameworks will facilitate further exploration and adoption.

title: What's Old is New Again: Classical Dimensionality Reduction for Efficient Saliency-Guided Biometric Attack Detection
comment: 16 pages (8 main, 2 references, 6 appendix), 4 figures (3 main, 1 appendix), 13 tables (3 main, 10 appendix)
summary: Saliency-guided training is a paradigm in visual recognition that encourages models to focus on the most relevant image regions during learning. While its application in biometric presentation attack detection (PAD) has shown strong benefits in robustness and generalization, adoption is often limited by the high cost, domain specificity, and limited scalability of existing saliency acquisition methods, such as human annotations over a limited dataset. We present a novel, cost-efficient, and highly-scalable approach to saliency acquisition using maps inspired by classical dimensionality reduction techniques: PCA and LDA. Our proposed methods generate saliency maps directly from raw training data, requiring no human annotation nor domain knowledge. We contextualize the effectiveness of these saliency sources in three saliency-explored domains (iris PAD, synthetic face detection, fingerprint PAD) and demonstrate its scalability in two saliency-novel domains (fingerprint vein PAD and ID card PAD). Across all domains tested, models trained using dimensionality reduction-sourced saliency maps exceed baseline and sometimes SOTA saliency methods without any resource investment or domain-specific tooling. Our findings overcome an important yet unaddressed barrier to saliency-guided training for biometric attack detection and beyond.

AI.tldr: Dimensionality-reduction-based saliency maps (PCA/LDA) enable scalable, annotation-free saliency-guided training for biometric PAD across multiple modalities, outperforming baselines and sometimes SOTA without domain-specific tooling.
AI.motivation: Bridge the cost, domain-specificity, and scalability limitations of existing saliency methods in biometric PAD by generating saliency maps directly from data without human annotations, to improve robustness and generalization.
AI.method: Compute saliency maps from raw training data using PCA- and LDA-inspired maps. No human annotation or domain knowledge required. Apply to iris PAD, synthetic face detection, and fingerprint PAD (and test scalability in fingerprint vein PAD and ID card PAD). Integrate these maps into the training loss/architecture to guide model attention.
AI.result: Models trained with reduction-based saliency sources exceed baseline and sometimes state-of-the-art saliency methods across tested domains, with no extra resource investment or domain-specific tooling.
AI.conclusion: Dimensionality reduction-based saliency provides a cost-effective, scalable alternative that facilitates broader adoption of saliency-guided training in biometric attack detection and related fields.

title: Evaluation Sovereignty in Metadata-Driven Classification: A Multi-Track Framework for Weakly Supervised Information Systems
summary:
Evaluation in machine learning is typically treated as a neutral measurement process. However, in operational information systems, evaluation outcomes are often conditioned by the processes used to generate labels. This paper does not seek to improve classification performance. Instead, it examines the validity of performance measurement under differing label-authority regimes. This issue is particularly relevant in large-scale metadata-driven systems, where labels are often incomplete, inconsistent, or weakly supervised.
  We introduce evaluation sovereignty, defined as the degree to which performance metrics are independent of label authority and supervision regime, and propose a multi-track evaluation framework that systematically varies training and evaluation label sources. Using hierarchical multi-label classification on large-scale scientific metadata, we demonstrate that models exhibiting strong performance under operational ("silver") evaluation degrade substantially under independent ("gold") evaluation, particularly for fine-grained classification. For example, Micro-F1 decreases from approximately 0.54 to 0.03. Notably, ranking-based metrics remain above baseline, revealing a divergence between latent model signal and classification validity.
  These findings suggest that commonly reported performance metrics may reflect alignment with labeling processes rather than true predictive capability. We therefore reconceptualize evaluation validity as a system-level property shaped by label governance and provide a practical methodology for auditing intelligent systems operating under weak supervision.

AI.tldr: Evaluation sovereignty reveals that performance metrics can be driven by label-authority regimes; a multi-track framework shows strong silver performance can collapse under gold evaluation; ranking metrics persist; highlights mismatch between model signals and true predictive validity.
AI.motivation: In large-scale metadata systems with incomplete/inconsistent/weak supervision, evaluation outcomes depend on label governance; need to audit evaluation validity.
AI.method: Propose evaluation sovereignty; multi-track evaluation varying training and evaluation label sources; apply to hierarchical multi-label classification on large-scale scientific metadata.
AI.result: Under gold evaluation Micro-F1 drops from ~0.54 to ~0.03; ranking metrics stay above baseline; shows divergence between latent signal and validity.
AI.conclusion: Evaluation validity is a system-level property influenced by label governance; offers methodology to audit datasets and systems under weak supervision.

title: scLLM-DSC: LLM-Knowledge Enhanced Cross-Modal Deep Structural Clustering for Single-Cell RNA Sequencing
summary: Clustering is fundamental to scRNA-seq analysis, serving as a cornerstone for identifying cell populations and resolving tissue heterogeneity. However, existing methods focus on mining numerical statistical patterns, suffering from semantic agnosticism by neglecting the intrinsic biological functions encoded by genes. While Large Language Models (LLMs) offer promising semantic capabilities, their direct adaptation to cell clustering is hindered by the structural mismatch between generative pre-training objectives and discriminative downstream tasks. To bridge this gap, we propose scLLM-DSC, a novel LLM-Knowledge Enhanced Cross-Modal Deep Structural Clustering framework. Diverging from data-driven paradigms, scLLM-DSC establishes a semantically-grounded representation by synergizing two views: a Knowledge-Driven Semantic View derived from NCBI gene priors and contextualized Cell2Sentence embeddings, and a Structure-Aware Topological View extracted via a graph-guided encoder. Crucially, we introduce a cross-modal contrastive alignment mechanism to enforce consistency between biological semantics and transcriptomic features within a unified latent space. Extensive benchmarks demonstrate that scLLM-DSC significantly outperforms eleven state-of-the-art baselines in clustering accuracy.

AI.tldr: Presents scLLM-DSC, a cross-modal, knowledge-enhanced clustering framework for scRNA-seq that fuses semantic gene knowledge with transcriptomic structure via an LLM-informed semantic view and a graph-based topological view, aligned by cross-modal contrastive learning, yielding superior clustering accuracy over baselines.
AI.motivation: Current scRNA-seq clustering relies on numerical statistics and often ignores gene-level biological semantics. LLMs offer semantic understanding but mismatch with discriminative clustering tasks; there is a need to unify semantic gene knowledge with transcriptomic structure for improved cluster discovery.
AI.method: Construct two views: (1) Knowledge-Driven Semantic View from NCBI gene priors plus contextualized Cell2Sentence embeddings; (2) Structure-Aware Topological View via a graph-guided encoder. Apply a cross-modal contrastive alignment to enforce consistency between semantic representations and transcriptomic features within a shared latent space.
AI.result: On extensive benchmarking, scLLM-DSC significantly outperforms eleven state-of-the-art baselines in clustering accuracy.
AI.conclusion: The framework establishes a semantically grounded, cross-modal clustering paradigm that integrates biological gene semantics with transcriptomic topology, achieving superior clustering and offering a robust direction for semantically informed scRNA-seq analysis.

title: Edit the Bits, Diff the Codes: Bitwise Residual Editing for Visual Autoregressive Models
summary: Text-guided image editing with visual autoregressive (VAR) generators requires controlling both what the model samples and where the sampled change is written back into the image code. Existing VAR editors mainly operate on token streams, features, or flat next-token logits, leaving two native structures of bitwise-residual VAR models underused: the per-bit Bernoulli prediction head and the additive multi-scale residual code field from which the image is assembled. We propose BitResEdit, a training-free editor for bitwise-residual VAR generators such as Infinity. BitEdit performs source-negative guidance by tilting the post-CFG per-bit log-odds along a source--target contrast computed on a shared edited prefix, then projects each update into a closed-form Bernoulli-KL trust region around the clean CFG sampler. ResEdit converts the sampled bits into per-scale continuous-code residuals, gates them with a localization mask, and re-injects them through the generator's native sum-of-scales. Together they couple decision-time bit guidance with combination-time code composition, so masked-out latent features are preserved exactly by code arithmetic while localized, scale-aware edits are applied inside the target region. On PIE-Bench with Infinity-2B, BitResEdit attains the strongest text alignment among same-backbone VAR editors, improving CLIP on the edited region by +1.07 over the strongest prior editor while keeping background preservation competitive with it. Ablations show BitEdit and ResEdit play complementary roles in target alignment and background preservation.

AI.tldr: A training-free editor for bitwise-residual VAR generators (BitResEdit) that combines per-bit log-odds guidance (BitEdit) with per-scale residual code reinjection (ResEdit) to achieve strong text alignment and good background preservation; evaluated on PIE-Bench with Infinity-2B, outperforming prior editors in target region CLIP scores (+1.07) and maintaining background quality.
AI.motivation: Existing VAR editors typically operate on token streams, features, or flat logits and underutilize native bitwise-residual VAR structures (per-bit Bernoulli heads and multi-scale residual fields). There is a need to control both what is edited and where in the latent code while preserving unedited regions.
AI.method: BitEdit: source-negative guidance by tilting per-bit log-odds along a source–target contrast on a shared edited prefix; projects updates into a Bernoulli-KL trust region around the clean CFG sampler. ResEdit: converts sampled bits to per-scale continuous-code residuals, applies a localization mask, and reinjects them via the generator’s sum-of-scales. The two modules couple decision-time bit guidance with code composition to preserve masked features and apply localized edits inside the target region.
AI.result: On PIE-Bench with Infinity-2B, BitResEdit achieves the strongest text alignment among same-backbone VAR editors, with a CLIP improvement of +1.07 on the edited region over the strongest prior editor; background preservation is competitive. Ablations indicate BitEdit and ResEdit play complementary roles in target alignment and background preservation.
AI.conclusion: BitResEdit demonstrates a training-free, bitwise-residual editing framework that effectively jointly optimizes target alignment and background preservation, leveraging native VAR structures to deliver robust, localized text-guided edits.

title: Why Sampling Is Not Choosing: Intentionality, Agency, and Moral Responsibility in Large Language Models
summary: Recent advances in large language models (LLMs) have prompted claims that such systems exhibit agency or qualify as moral agents. This paper argues that these attributions are misguided. We maintain that moral responsibility requires commitment-bearing agency grounded in intrinsic intentionality and self-attributed action, and that such agency constitutes the form of free will relevant to responsibility. Although LLMs generate coherent and normatively evaluable outputs, their operation is fully characterized by probabilistic input-output mappings learned from data. Their apparent intentionality is derived rather than intrinsic, and their outputs are neither owned as commitments nor guided by reasons. Variability introduced by stochastic sampling does not amount to choice or authorship. We address objections from the intentional stance, functionalism, compatibilism, and the presence of moral reasoning in model outputs, arguing that none suffice to establish genuine agency.

AI.tldr: The abstract argues that LLMs simulate agency but do not possess genuine commitment-bearing, intrinsically intentional agency required for moral responsibility; hence they are not moral agents.
AI.motivation: Clarify the limits of attributing agency and responsibility to LLMs and defend a robust requirement for responsibility that LLMs fail to meet.
AI.method: Conceptual analysis of agency, intentionality, and responsibility; critique of objections (intentional stance, functionalism, compatibilism, moral reasoning in outputs) using normative criteria.
AI.result: LLMs' outputs are probabilistic input-output mappings; apparent intentionality is derivative; no ownership of commitments or reasons; stochasticity ≠ genuine choice; thus no genuine agency or responsibility.
AI.conclusion: Avoid attributing moral agency to LLMs; responsibility requires commitment-bearing agency grounded in intrinsic intentionality; future work should distinguish surface competence from true agency and address governance.

title: CausalMoE: A Billion-Scale Multimodal Foundation Model for Granger Causal Discovery with Pattern-Routed Heterogeneous Experts
summary: Granger Causal Discovery (GCD) is fundamental for analyzing temporal dependencies in complex systems. However, existing neural GCD methods predominantly rely on a "one-size-fits-all" paradigm, struggling to capture distribution shifts and dynamic regime changes inherent in real-world time series. This often leads to entangled representations and spurious causal graphs. In this paper, we propose CausalMoE, a billion-scale multimodal Granger causal foundation model that explicitly models patch-level heterogeneity. CausalMoE introduces a Pattern-Routed Mixture of Heterogeneous Experts, which dynamically identifies latent temporal patterns and routes patches to specialized domain experts, effectively decoupling regime-specific mechanisms from shared dynamics. To ensure interpretable graph recovery, we design a Causality-Aware Self-Attention mechanism operating across variables, yielding sparse Granger causal graphs via proximal optimization. Furthermore, CausalMoE is the first to integrate LLMs and VLMs to align numerical signals with textual and visual priors, regularizing causal estimation in complex scenarios. Extensive experiments demonstrate that CausalMoE establishes a new state-of-the-art on fully supervised benchmarks, while effectively generalizing to few-shot settings where traditional methods fail.

AI.tldr: CausalMoE is a billion-scale multimodal foundation model for Granger causal discovery that uses Pattern-Routed Mixture of Heterogeneous Experts to capture patch-level regime heterogeneity, a causality-aware self-attention to produce sparse graphs, and multimodal alignment with LLMs/VLMs to regularize estimation; it achieves state-of-the-art results and strong few-shot generalization.
AI.motivation: Neural GCD methods struggle with distribution shifts and regime changes, leading to entangled representations and spurious causal graphs. A scalable, interpretable, and robust approach is needed that can decouple regime-specific mechanisms from shared dynamics and leverage multimodal priors.
AI.method: Introduce Pattern-Routed Mixture of Heterogeneous Experts (CausalMoE) to dynamically route patches to specialized domain experts based on latent temporal patterns. Implement Causality-Aware Self-Attention across variables with proximal optimization to enforce sparse Granger graphs. Integrate LLMs and VLMs to align numerical signals with textual/visual priors for regularization.
AI.result: Extensive experiments show CausalMoE achieves state-of-the-art performance on fully supervised benchmarks and generalizes to few-shot settings where traditional methods fail.
AI.conclusion: CausalMoE effectively handles heterogeneity in temporal causal discovery, improving interpretability and robustness through a combination of mixture-of-experts, causality-aware attention, and multimodal priors, with strong empirical performance.

title: Contrast-Informed Augmentation and Domain-Adversarial Training for Adult-to-Neonatal MR Reconstruction Generalization
summary: Purpose: To investigate whether contrast-informed data augmentation and domain-adversarial training improve the adult-to-neonatal generalization of the E2E-VarNet. Methods: Three training regimes were investigated: (1) adult-only training with unaugmented adult data, (2) mixed training with paired unaugmented and neonatal-informed augmented adult data, and (3) mixed training with a domain-adversarial objective. Models were trained on retrospectively undersampled multi-coil adult T2-weighted brain MR data and evaluated on neonatal and adult test data at acceleration factors $R=4$ and $R=8$ using quantitative metrics and qualitative evaluation. Feature analyses assessed whether domain-adversarial training altered the latent representations of unaugmented adult, augmented adult, and neonatal test samples. Results: Mixed training (Mixed) and mixed domain-adversarial training (Mixed-DAT) outperformed unaugmented adult-only training (Unaug-Only) when evaluated on neonatal data. At R=4, Mixed-DAT achieved the best performance (SSIM = 0.924 +/- 0.027, PSNR = 33.98 +/- 1.15 dB). At R=8, Mixed-DAT performed best when measured using SSIM (0.848 +/- 0.031 vs. 0.766 +/- 0.037 for Unaug-Only and 0.814 +/- 0.035 for Mixed) and Mixed performed best when measured using PSNR (29.56 +/- 0.83 dB vs. 26.26 +/- 0.78 dB for Unaug-Only and 29.43 +/- 0.83 dB for Mixed-DAT). Qualitative assessment of t-SNE plots suggested that Mixed-DAT increased the overlap among the latent representations of the unaugmented adult, augmented adult, and neonatal test data. Conclusion: Contrast-informed augmentation and domain-adversarial training improved adult-to-neonatal generalization of deep learning-based MR reconstruction. These findings suggest that contrast-informed data augmentation combined with adversarial training may improve robustness to domain shift in undersampled neonatal MR reconstruction.

AI.tldr: Contrast-informed augmentation plus domain-adversarial training enhances adult-to-neonatal generalization in E2E-VarNet MR reconstruction under undersampling.
AI.motivation: Domain shift between adult and neonatal brain MR images degrades reconstruction performance; improving generalization is critical for robust neonatal MR reconstruction from adult-trained models.
AI.method: Three training regimes were evaluated: (1) Unaug-Only (adult data, no augmentation); (2) Mixed (augmented adult data paired with unaugmented adult data and neonatal information); (3) Mixed-DAT (same as Mixed with a domain-adversarial objective). Models trained on retrospectively undersampled multi-coil adult T2-weighted brain MR data; evaluated on neonatal and adult test data at acceleration factors R=4 and R=8 using SSIM and PSNR. Latent representations analyzed with t-SNE to assess domain overlap.
AI.result: Mixed and Mixed-DAT outperformed Unaug-Only on neonatal data. At R=4, Mixed-DAT achieved SSIM 0.924±0.027 and PSNR 33.98±1.15 dB. At R=8, Mixed-DAT best SSIM 0.848±0.031 (vs. 0.766±0.037 for Unaug-Only and 0.814±0.035 for Mixed); Mixed best PSNR 29.56±0.83 dB (vs. 26.26±0.78 for Unaug-Only and 29.43±0.83 for Mixed-DAT). t-SNE suggested greater overlap of latent spaces among unaugmented adult, augmented adult, and neonatal data with Mixed-DAT.
AI.conclusion: Contrast-informed data augmentation combined with domain-adversarial training improves adult-to-neonatal generalization in deep learning-based MR reconstruction, suggesting enhanced robustness to domain shift in undersampled neonatal MR reconstruction.

title: CloudCons: A Comprehensive End-to-End Benchmark for Cloud Resource Consolidation
summary: Driven by conservative over-provisioning to guarantee service reliability, resource utilization in cloud data centers remains at low levels. To mitigate this, the forecast-then-optimize paradigm has emerged to optimize consolidation by anticipating future demands. While emerging time series foundation models promise to enhance this paradigm through zero-shot generalization, existing benchmarks focus solely on prediction error metrics. The actual decision utility of these advanced models remains unverified, rendering their practical value for downstream tasks uncertain. To bridge this gap, we propose CloudCons, a comprehensive end-to-end benchmark designed to evaluate forecasting models within the specific context of cloud resource consolidation. We build high-quality datasets that cover diverse workloads from Huawei Cloud, Microsoft Azure, and Google Borg, capturing distinct service characteristics ranging from synchronized diurnal rhythms to stochastic, pulse-like bursts and high-frequency noise. We conduct an extensive evaluation of statistical, deep learning, and foundation models. Our experiments reveal a pivotal finding: while foundation models demonstrate superior zero-shot forecasting accuracy, this advantage does not inherently translate into better decision utility. Of practical significance, we systematically analyze how the selection of predictive quantiles acts as a critical lever. We provide actionable guidelines for calibrating these selections to balance the trade-off between resource efficiency and service reliability, offering vital insights for real-world deployment decisions.

AI.tldr: CloudCons is an end-to-end benchmark for evaluating forecasting models in cloud resource consolidation, revealing that foundation models, while strong in zero-shot forecasting, do not inherently improve decision utility; it highlights predictive quantile calibration as a key lever and provides deployment guidelines based on diverse datasets from major cloud providers.
AI.motivation: Existing benchmarking focuses on prediction error without assessing downstream decision utility. There is a need for an end-to-end evaluation framework to understand how forecast quality translates into consolidation decisions and service reliability.
AI.method: Assemble high-quality, diverse datasets from Huawei Cloud, Microsoft Azure, and Google Borg capturing varied workload characteristics. Evaluate statistical, deep learning, and foundation models for forecasting. Assess both forecast accuracy and downstream decision utility, analyze the role of predictive quantiles, and derive practical calibration guidelines for deployment.
AI.result: Foundation models achieve superior zero-shot forecasting accuracy but this does not automatically translate into better decision utility. The choice of predictive quantiles critically affects the trade-off between resource efficiency and service reliability. The study offers actionable guidelines for calibrating quantiles and demonstrates the value of end-to-end benchmarking for real-world deployment.
AI.conclusion: End-to-end benchmarks like CloudCons are essential to translate forecasting advances into practical gains for cloud consolidation. Calibrating predictive quantiles is a key lever for balancing efficiency and reliability, and foundation-model forecasting performance should be evaluated by its impact on downstream decisions.

title: TWLA: Achieving Ternary Weights and Low-Bit Activations for LLMs via Post-Training Quantization
summary: Large language models (LLMs) exhibit exceptional general language processing capabilities, but their memory and compute costs hinder deployment. Ternarization has emerged as a promising compression technique, offering significant reductions in model size and inference complexity. However, existing methods struggle with heavy-tailed activation distributions and therefore keep activations in high precision, fundamentally limiting end-to-end inference acceleration. To overcome this limitation, we propose TWLA, a post-training quantization (PTQ) framework that achieves 1.58-bit weight compression and 4-bit activation quantization while maintaining high accuracy. TWLA comprises three components: (1) Euclidean-to-Manifold Asymmetric Ternary Quantizer (E2M-ATQ) minimizes layer-output error under weight ternarization via a two-stage optimization from Euclidean initialization to manifold relocation; (2) Kronecker Orthogonal Tri-Modal Shaping (KOTMS) applies a Kronecker-structured orthogonal rotation to reshape weights into ternary-friendly tri-modal distributions, while the shared rotation statistically suppresses activation outliers; and (3) Inter-Layer Aware Activation Mixed Precision (ILA-AMP) explicitly introduces adjacent-layer second-order interaction costs in bit allocation and jointly optimizes for the layer-wise disparity of activation quantization gains induced by the shared orthogonal transform, preventing cascades triggered by a few weak layers. Extensive experiments demonstrate that TWLA maintains high accuracy under W1.58A4, while delivering significant inference acceleration. The code is available at <https://github.com/Kishon-zzx/TWLA>.

AI.tldr: TWLA is a post-training quantization framework that compresses weights to 1.58 bits and activations to 4 bits using E2M-ATQ, KOTMS, and ILA-AMP, achieving end-to-end LLM inference acceleration with minimal accuracy loss.
AI.motivation: To reduce memory and compute costs of large language models while overcoming heavy-tailed activation distributions that hinder naive PTQ, enabling practical end-to-end acceleration.
AI.method: 1) E2M-ATQ: a two-stage Euclidean-to-manifold optimization for weight ternarization that minimizes layer-output error; 2) KOTMS: Kronecker-structured orthogonal rotation that reshapes weights into tri-modal distributions to suppress activation outliers; 3) ILA-AMP: Inter-Layer Aware Activation Mixed Precision that allocates bits considering adjacent-layer interactions to prevent cascades.
AI.result: Empirical results show high accuracy under W1.58A4 with substantial inference acceleration; code available at the provided URL.
AI.conclusion: TWLA offers a cohesive PTQ pipeline addressing heavy-tailed activations and inter-layer dynamics, enabling accurate, end-to-end accelerated LLM inference with relatively low-bit representations.

title: EvTexture++: Event-Driven Texture Enhancement for Video Super-Resolution
comment: IEEE TPAMI 2026. Extended version of arXiv:2406.13457 (ICML 2024). Project page: https://dachunkai.github.io/evtexture-project-page/
summary: Event-based vision has drawn increasing attention owing to its distinctive properties, including ultra-high temporal resolution and extreme dynamic range. Recent works have introduced it to video super-resolution (VSR) to enhance flow estimation and temporal alignment. In contrast, this paper shifts the focus of event signals from motion refinement to texture enhancement in VSR. We propose EvTexture++, the first event-driven framework dedicated to texture enhancement in VSR. It leverages high-frequency spatiotemporal details from events to improve texture recovery. EvTexture++ incorporates a customized texture enhancement branch, along with an iterative texture enhancement module that progressively exploits high-temporal-resolution event information for texture restoration. This enables gradual refinement of texture regions across iterations, yielding more accurate and detailed high-resolution outputs. Besides intra-frame texture recovery, large motions could degrade inter-frame temporal consistency, particularly in texture regions, leading to texture flickering. To mitigate this, we further exploit the continuous-time motion cues of events to enhance temporal consistency, introducing a temporal texture alignment module that estimates event-guided texture-aware flow for precise inter-frame texture alignment. Moreover, EvTexture++ is designed as a plug-and-play tool to flexibly boost the performance of existing VSR models. Experiments on five datasets demonstrate that EvTexture++ achieves state-of-the-art performance. When integrated into recent VSR models, it yields significant improvements, with gains of up to 1.55 dB in PSNR on the texture-rich Vid4 dataset. Code: https://github.com/DachunKai/EvTexture.

AI.tldr: EvTexture++ is the first event-driven VSR framework focused on texture enhancement, featuring a texture enhancement branch with an iterative module and a temporal texture alignment module using event-guided flow to improve inter-frame consistency; plug-and-play with existing VSR models, achieving state-of-the-art results on five datasets (up to 1.55 dB PSNR gain on Vid4).
AI.motivation: Event data inherently contains high-frequency texture information; however, existing VSR methods relying on motion refinement may underutilize texture cues. The work aims to shift emphasis to texture restoration and stabilize texture across time by exploiting continuous-time event cues.
AI.method: Proposes EvTexture++ with (1) a dedicated texture enhancement branch, (2) an iterative texture enhancement module that progressively leverages high-temporal-resolution events for texture restoration, and (3) a temporal texture alignment module that computes event-guided texture-aware flow for inter-frame alignment. It is designed as a plug-and-play module to boost existing VSR models.
AI.result: Extensive experiments on five datasets show state-of-the-art performance; integration into recent VSR models yields substantial improvements, including up to 1.55 dB PSNR gain on the texture-rich Vid4 dataset. Code is released.
AI.conclusion: Demonstrates the effectiveness of exploiting event-driven high-frequency texture cues and continuous-time motion information for texture enhancement and temporal consistency in VSR, while offering a practical plug-and-play tool for boosting existing models.

title: Uncertainty-Aware Hybrid Retrieval for Long-Document RAG
summary: Retrieval augmented generation (RAG) depends critically on the quality and granularity of retrieved evidence. Large retrieval units preserve context but often introduce irrelevant content, which can dilute answer bearing evidence and worsen long context utilization. Fine-grained units are more compact, but they may be difficult to retrieve reliably because short chunks can lack semantic, lexical, or bridging cues needed to match the query. We propose Uncertainty-aware Multi-Granularity RAG (UMG-RAG), a training-free hybrid retrieval framework that treats chunk granularity as query-specific reliability estimation. Instead of training a new retriever or modifying the generator, UMG-RAG uses existing dense and sparse retrievers as complementary experts across multiple chunk granularities. For each query, it converts each expert-granularity score list into an evidence distribution, estimates reliability from distribution entropy, and fuses candidates according to query-specific semantic, lexical, and granularity confidence. We further introduce UMGP-RAG, a parent promotion variant that uses fine-grained hits to locate relevant evidence while returning broader non-redundant parent chunks for local coherence. Experiments on question answering benchmarks show that uncertainty-aware fusion and parent promotion improve generation quality while maintaining a lightweight, plug-and-play retrieval pipeline.

AI.tldr: A training-free, uncertainty-aware multi-granularity RAG framework (UMG-RAG) that fuses dense and sparse retriever outputs across multiple chunk granularities using entropy-based reliability, plus a parent-promotion variant UMGP-RAG for better coherence, showing QA gains with a lightweight pipeline.
AI.motivation: RAG performance hinges on evidence granularity; large chunks preserve context but add noise; small chunks improve precision but are hard to retrieve; need a way to combine granularities without retraining.
AI.method: Use existing dense and sparse retrievers as experts across chunk granularities; convert each expert's scores into an evidence distribution; estimate reliability via entropy; fuse candidates using query-specific semantic, lexical, and granularity confidence; introduce UMGP-RAG that uses fine-grained hits to locate evidence and returns broader parent chunks for coherence; training-free, no retriever/generator changes.
AI.result: On QA benchmarks, uncertainty-aware fusion and parent promotion improve generation quality while keeping a lightweight plug-and-play retrieval pipeline.
AI.conclusion: Uncertainty-aware multi-granularity fusion with a parent-promotion variant provides better QA generation performance without additional retraining, highlighting practical advantages of hybrid retrieval across granularities.

title: A green solvent screening tool for emerging materials via uncertainty aware, transformer enhanced transfer learning
summary: Accurate prediction of solubility remains a central challenge across materials science and sustainable chemistry. In particular due to emerging technologies like organic and hybrid photovoltaics, batteries, and catalysis, solvent usage is expected to increase significantly within the coming years. Therefore, substituting solvents with greener alternatives is vital. This is where machine learning can have substantial impact. However, the limited data on critical parameters of solubility significantly constraints machine learning efficacy. In this work, we transfer a pre-trained foundational model on QM9 targets to our application with minimal data requirements. Additionally, the pipeline integrates uncertainty quantification, allowing the user to gauge the confidence of the predictions. As baseline, we succeed in predicting the Hansen solubility parameters and Dielectric Constant for which extensive databases exist. Importantly, we achieve high model performance on additional targets, such as Gutmann Donor and Acceptor numbers, where the available data is extremely limited. Overall, we augment data on solubility descriptors by orders of magnitude with high quality predictions. For effective dissemination, we deploy easy-to-use, easily integrateable with high throughput labs, customizable tool for ranking and screening possible solvent substitutes. Finally, we rediscovered known green solvent alternatives and proposed new candidates proving its relevance for finding eco-friendly solvents.

AI.tldr: Transfer learning from a QM9 foundation model enables solubility descriptor predictions with minimal data, incorporating uncertainty quantification; yields accurate Hansen parameters and dielectric constant, and performs well on limited Gutmann donor/acceptor numbers, enabling high-throughput greener solvent screening.
AI.motivation: Solubility prediction is critical for material/green chemistry but data scarcity on key parameters limits ML; need scalable methods to identify eco-friendly solvents for growing use in photovoltaics, batteries, catalysis.
AI.method: Fine-tune a pre-trained QM9-based foundation model on solubility descriptors with small datasets; integrate uncertainty quantification; evaluate on Hansen solubility parameters, dielectric constant, Gutmann donor/acceptor numbers; deploy a high-throughput screening pipeline.
AI.result: High accuracy on Hansen parameters and dielectric constant; successful prediction for donor/acceptor numbers with scarce data; data augmentation by orders of magnitude via model predictions; rediscovered known green solvents and proposed new candidates.
AI.conclusion: Foundation-model transfer with uncertainty quantification enables accurate, scalable solubility descriptor predictions and practical solvent screening, supporting greener solvent discovery with limited data.

title: Towards Effective Waste Segmentation for Automated Waste Recycling in Cluttered Background
summary: Rapid expansion of urban areas and population growth is causing an immense increase in waste production, which demands the need for efficient and automated waste management. In this scenario, automated waste recycling (AWR) using deep learning methods can assist humans in optimal waste management. Recent deep learning approaches for AWR provide promising waste segmentation performance, however, these methods rely on large backbone networks that are inefficient for AWR systems and suffer from performance deterioration in cluttered scenes. To this end, an optimal waste segmentation network is introduced which effectively utilizes the spatial domain to capture localized structural dependencies and the spectral domain to efficiently extract global contextual relationships. This cascaded design allows the network to progressively leverage both local and global representations across complementary domains to highlight the semantic information necessary for effective segmentation of various waste objects. Furthermore, auxiliary feature enhancement module (AFEM) is introduced to enhance the target objects' boundaries and blob amplification for better segmentation in cluttered scenarios. Extensive experimentation on ZeroWaste-aug, ZeroWaste-f and SpectralWaste datasets reveals the merits of the proposed method.

AI.tldr: A cascaded dual-domain waste segmentation network combining spatial and spectral analysis with an auxiliary feature enhancement module to improve automated waste recycling segmentation, achieving robust performance in cluttered scenes on multiple datasets.
AI.motivation: The rapid growth of urban waste generation demands efficient, accurate, and computationally viable waste segmentation for automated waste recycling; existing deep learning models rely on large backbones and degrade in cluttered scenes.
AI.method: Introduce an optimal waste segmentation network with a cascaded design that leverages the spatial domain for localized structure and the spectral domain for global contextual relationships, and incorporate an auxiliary feature enhancement module (AFEM) to sharpen boundaries and amplify blobs. Evaluate on ZeroWaste-aug, ZeroWaste-f, and SpectralWaste datasets.
AI.result: Demonstrates improved segmentation performance and robustness in cluttered environments; AFEM enhances boundary delineation and blob amplification, contributing to better segmentation of diverse waste objects across datasets.
AI.conclusion: The proposed cascaded spatial-spectral network with AFEM provides an effective, efficient solution for automated waste recycling segmentation, validated across multiple datasets and ready for deployment considerations.

title: Is It You or Your Environment? A Bayesian Inference Framework for Genomically-Anchored Personalized Physiological Interpretation
comment: 24 pages, 8 figures, 3 tables. Conceptual framework paper
summary: Personalized health AI systems face a fundamental cold-start problem: machine learning models for physiological interpretation require weeks of individual behavioral data before they can distinguish constitutional variation from environmentally driven deviation. We propose a solution grounded in causal inference and Bayesian prior design. An individual's genomic profile serves as an exogenous genetic anchor -- a domain-informed, personalized prior that is fixed at conception, immune to reverse causation, and available before a single behavioral observation is collected. The anchor initializes a Bayesian belief state over an individual's physiological set point G-hat = mu + sum(beta_i * g_i), where beta_i are GWAS-derived effect sizes and g_i are risk-allele counts. Each incoming physiological measurement P produces a non-constitutional deviation delta = P - G-hat that separates the signal attributable to environment and state from the constitutionally fixed baseline. As behavioral data accrue, the prior decays according to G-hat_t = w(t)*G-hat_genomic + [1-w(t)]*P-bar_t, transitioning from genome-dominated to empirical-baseline-dominated inference. The same observed HRV of 55 ms generates a suppression hypothesis for a person whose prior predicts 80 ms, and an enhancement hypothesis for a person whose prior predicts 30 ms -- a reversal impossible without a personalized anchor. We develop this architecture across six physiological domains, grading genomic priors by evidence strength, distinguishing robustly replicated anchors (FTO, FADS1/2, FKBP5) from contested candidate genes (SLC6A4, MAOA, DRD2). We address the inference boundary between association, Mendelian randomization, and individual token causation, and define four constraints for deployment: evidence-graded priors, dynamic decay, ancestry-matched effect sizes, and attribution rather than deterministic output.

AI.tldr: A causal Bayesian framework uses genomic priors as fixed anchors to bootstrap personalized physiological inference, decaying to empirical baselines as data accrue; it distinguishes constitutional baseline from environment/state, applies across six domains, and defines deployment constraints (graded priors, decay, ancestry-matched effect sizes, attribution).
AI.motivation: Address the cold-start problem in personalized health AI by incorporating a fixed, exogenous genetic anchor that informs the individual physiological baseline prior before any behavioral data are observed.
AI.method: Define G-hat_genomic as mu + sum(beta_i * g_i) from GWAS-derived effect sizes; compute delta = P - G-hat; update posterior/decode as G-hat_t = w(t)*G-hat_genomic + [1-w(t)]*P-bar_t; apply across six domains; grade anchors by evidence strength (robust genes vs contested ones); discuss inference boundaries (association, Mendelian randomization, individual causation) and impose four deployment constraints (evidence-graded priors, dynamic decay, ancestry-matched effects, attribution rather than deterministic output).
AI.result: Proposes a coherent architecture to separate constitutional baseline from environmental/state-driven variation; uses a concrete HRV example to illustrate how a fixed prior can yield opposite interpretations for the same observation; demonstrates cross-domain application and a ranking of genomic priors by replicability; outlines an inference framework with explicit boundaries and constraints.
AI.conclusion: The framework offers a principled approach to cold-start personalized inference, leveraging genomic anchors with dynamic decay and explicit attribution, while acknowledging limitations around effect-size transfer, ancestry matching, and the need for careful deployment criteria.

title: Limits of spectral learning under noise
summary: Learning functional relationships from noisy data is a central problem in scientific inference. Spectral methods approximate unknown functions by expanding them in a basis and estimating the corresponding coefficients from data, but the stability of these coefficients under noise remains poorly understood. Here we study supervised regression with additive label noise using sparse spectral representations across multiple bases and dimensions. We show that noise induces a predictable drift in the learned coefficient vector whose magnitude depends on the effective number of active spectral modes. After whitening the empirical feature geometry, we derive a closed-form expression for the overlap between noisy and noiseless coefficient vectors, revealing a universal degradation curve governed by a single intrinsic noise scale. Numerical experiments across Fourier, Legendre, Bessel, and Haar bases confirm the theoretical prediction. The results demonstrate that spectral learning exhibits a fundamental noise threshold beyond which coefficient estimates become unstable, placing intrinsic limits on recovering functional structure from noisy data.

AI.tldr: Spectral learning with noisy labels shows a predictable drift in coefficient estimates across multiple spectral bases; after feature whitening, the overlap between noisy and noiseless coefficients follows a universal degradation curve controlled by an intrinsic noise scale, implying a fundamental noise threshold for stable functional recovery.
AI.motivation: To understand how additive label noise affects coefficient stability in sparse spectral representations, and to identify intrinsic limits to recovering function structure from noisy data.
AI.method: Consider supervised regression with additive label noise using sparse representations across Fourier, Legendre, Bessel, and Haar bases in multiple dimensions. Lead steps include whitening the empirical feature geometry, deriving a closed-form expression for the overlap between noisy and noiseless coefficient vectors, and validating the theory with numerical experiments.
AI.result: Derivation of a closed-form overlap between noisy and noiseless coefficient vectors showing a universal degradation curve dictated by a single intrinsic noise scale. Empirical confirmation across multiple bases (Fourier, Legendre, Bessel, Haar) and dimensions, revealing a noise threshold beyond which coefficient estimates become unstable.
AI.conclusion: Spectral learning exhibits intrinsic noise limits that cap the recoverable functional structure, introducing a universal threshold for stable coefficient estimation and highlighting the importance of basis choice and feature whitening.

title: Revisiting Vehicle Color Recognition in Long-Tailed Surveillance Scenarios
comment: Accepted for presentation at the 2026 International Conference on Pattern Recognition (ICPR) - V3SC Workshop
summary: Vehicle color recognition is an important cue for vehicle identification in surveillance systems, especially when license plates are illegible due to low resolution, occlusion, motion blur, or poor illumination. However, real-world vehicle color distributions are highly imbalanced, making overall accuracy insufficient to assess performance on rare but operationally relevant colors. This paper presents a comprehensive study of vehicle color recognition under severe class imbalance using UFPR-VeSV, a challenging real-world surveillance dataset. We investigate synthetic minority-class augmentation through two off-the-shelf generative strategies: text-conditioned image generation with RunDiffusion/JuggernautXL and image-conditioned color editing with Gemini 2.0 Flash. The curated synthetic data are combined with modern visual representations, loss reweighting, learning-rate scheduling, color-safe augmentation, foreground-aware preprocessing, and ensemble fusion. The bestperforming approach achieves 94.6% micro accuracy and 79.7% macro accuracy, improving macro accuracy by 8.2 percentage points over recent literature. A manual error analysis further shows that many remaining failures are visually ambiguous even for human annotators, highlighting the practical limits of color-based vehicle identification in unconstrained surveillance imagery. The generated images and source code are publicly available at https://github.com/viniciusorru/vcr-synthetic

AI.tldr: Under highly imbalanced vehicle color data, the paper uses synthetic minority augmentation (RunDiffusion/JuggernautXL and Gemini 2.0 Flash) with reweighting, scheduling, augmentation, preprocessing, and ensembling on UFPR-VeSV to boost macro-level performance; achieves 79.7% macro and 94.6% micro, with macro gains of 8.2 points; residual errors are largely visually ambiguous.
AI.motivation: Imbalanced color distributions hinder reliable color-based vehicle identification; assess whether synthetic data and training strategies can improve macro-level performance on real-world surveillance data.
AI.method: Two synthetic augmentation strategies (text-conditioned generation via RunDiffusion/JuggernautXL; image-conditioned color editing via Gemini 2.0 Flash) combined with modern learning tricks (loss reweighting, LR scheduling, color-safe augmentation, foreground-aware preprocessing) and ensemble fusion.
AI.result: Best approach: micro 94.6%, macro 79.7%; macro improvement +8.2 pp vs recent literature.
AI.conclusion: Despite improvements, many errors are visually ambiguous to humans, indicating practical limits of color-based vehicle identification in unconstrained surveillance; data and code released.

title: A Three-Layer Framework for AI in Scientific Discovery
summary: Current discussions of AI in scientific discovery are often dominated by two visible capabilities: search over existing knowledge and execution through optimization, simulation, and automation. Both are important, but neither fully captures the central act of discovery: the formation and evolution of models. This paper proposes a three-layer view of AI in discovery. Layer 1 is search and retrieval by large language models. Layer 2, as the main innovation of this paper, is model formation through qualitative reasoning: the capacity to recognize when a current framework is structurally inadequate and to understand the problem within a broader representational space, not through trial and error, but through structural insight into what is missing and where it can be found. Layer 3 is execution, optimization, and refinement. The main claim is that Layer 2 is both the most important and the least developed. Search without model formation remains confined to inherited frameworks, while execution without conceptual revision only amplifies an existing formulation. We illustrate Layer 2 reasoning through three case studies: S. S. Chern's intrinsic proof of the Gauss-Bonnet theorem, the resolution of the Nesterov Accelerated Gradient convergence problem via Lyapunov functions, and the autonomous disproof of the Erdos unit distance conjecture by OpenAI in 2026. Each case exhibits the same structural signature: a framework that had become inadequate, a missing conceptual object, and a resolution found in an unexpected neighboring field.

AI.tldr: Proposes a three-layer framework for AI-driven scientific discovery. Layer 2 (model formation via qualitative reasoning) is the central, least-developed pillar, enabling genuine discovery beyond search and execution. Three case studies illustrate the pattern: inadequate frameworks, missing conceptual objects, and cross-domain resolution.
AI.motivation: Current AI discourse emphasizes search over existing knowledge and automated execution, but neither captures the core act of discovery: forming and evolving models. There is a need for a robust account of model-formation to realize autonomous scientific discovery.
AI.method: Introduce a three-layer model of AI in discovery (Layer 1: search/retrieval with LLMs; Layer 2: model formation through qualitative reasoning; Layer 3: execution/optimization/refinement). Illustrate Layer 2 with three case studies that share a structural signature: a once-adequate framework becomes inadequate, a missing conceptual object is identified, and resolution occurs via cross-domain insight.
AI.result: Argues that Layer 2 is the most important yet underdeveloped layer for AI-driven discovery and that progress in AI discovery requires advancing model-formation capabilities beyond search and optimization. The three case studies exemplify the same structural pattern.
AI.conclusion: To advance AI-enabled discovery, invest in developing model-formation capabilities that enable recognition of structural inadequacies and exploratory navigation of broader representational spaces, bridging disciplines to find missing conceptual objects.

title: Emotional regulation improves deep learning-based image classification
summary: Emotion significantly influences cognition, enhancing memory and learning under certain conditions. Drawing on this principle, emotion-augmented deep learning investigates how affective states can improve neural network architectures and learning paradigms, achieving better generalization than non-emotional models. However, existing methods often rely solely on objective neurophysiological factors, neglecting the role of subjectivity in emotion. To bridge this gap, the present study introduces Emotional Regulation, a novel framework for modeling emotion in deep learning through artificial subjective experience. The method employs pre-training based on affective stimuli, balancing non-emotional and emotionally-influenced responses in downstream task optimization. Extensive experimentation was conducted in image classification, pre-training ResNet and ViT architectures on four emotional datasets, using CIFAR-10 and -100 as target benchmarks. Results reveal improvements over the aforementioned backbones, providing evidence of Emotional Regulation as a promising method for defining emotion-augmented deep learning through artificial subjective experience. Furthermore, the proposed approach overcomes the related work in image classification based on CIFAR, revealing Emotional Regulation as the new state-of-the-art in emotion-augmented deep learning for large-scale vision datasets. The study also enforces evidence of the impact of affective states in improving machine learning tasks' optimization, encouraging further investigation on emotion-inspired architectures.

AI.tldr: Introduces Emotional Regulation, a framework for modeling emotion in deep learning via artificial subjective experience; uses affective-stimulus pre-training to balance emotional and non-emotional downstream optimization; demonstrates improvements on image classification (ResNet, ViT) across four emotional datasets with CIFAR-10/100, claiming state-of-the-art in emotion-augmented DL for large-scale vision tasks.
AI.motivation: Emotion influences cognition and learning; existing emotion-aware DL often relies on objective neurophysiological signals and neglects subjectivity; bridging subjectivity can enhance generalization and optimization in ML models.
AI.method: Pre-train DL models on affective stimuli to instantiate artificial subjective experience; balance non-emotional and emotionally-influenced responses during downstream task optimization; evaluate on image classification using ResNet and ViT architectures; train on four emotional datasets and benchmark on CIFAR-10/100.
AI.result: Emotional Regulation yields improvements over baseline backbones; provides evidence that affective states can improve ML task optimization; claims state-of-the-art performance for emotion-augmented learning on large-scale vision datasets (CIFAR) and shows positive impact across architectures and datasets.
AI.conclusion: Emotion-inspired architectures with artificial subjective experience are a promising direction for emotion-augmented DL; the approach motivates broader exploration of affective states in ML and how subjectivity can be modeled to enhance learning and generalization.

title: Surflo: Consistent 3D Surface Flow Model with Global State
summary: Geometry is invariant to viewpoint, which makes any collection of images a redundant encoding of a single 3D state. Existing feed-forward reconstruction models fail to exploit this: per-view methods emit overlapping, unaligned pointmaps that grow linearly with input count, while global-latent methods commit to a fixed, low-resolution output. We introduce Surflo, which compresses a variable number of unposed RGB views into K latent tokens-one global state-and decodes oriented 3D surface points by independently transporting them from noise onto the surface via flow matching. This frees the output from any fixed grid or token budget: the same latent yields from a few thousand to a million points in a single forward pass. To suppress the local inconsistencies inherent to independent per-point decoding, an inference-time guidance term correlates nearby points by injecting a photometric gradient during ODE integration. Surflo matches or surpasses feed-forward baselines on surface metrics, runs an order of magnitude faster than optimization-based methods that require hundreds of views, and is the only feed-forward approach to combine a global latent with arbitrary-resolution decoding.

AI.tldr: Surflo is a feed-forward framework that reconstructs oriented 3D surface points from a variable number of unposed RGB views using a single global latent, decode via flow matching, and employs an inference-time photometric gradient to encourage local consistency, achieving arbitrary-resolution outputs efficiently.
AI.motivation: Geometry is viewpoint-invariant, causing redundancy across views. Per-view reconstructions produce unaligned, grid-like outputs, while global-latent approaches restrict output resolution. There is a need for a scalable, fast, and flexible method that handles varying view counts without fixed output budgets.
AI.method: Surflo compresses a variable number of unposed RGB views into K latent tokens (one global state) and decodes oriented 3D surface points by transporting them from noise to the surface via flow matching. Output has no fixed grid or token budget, enabling scaling from thousands to millions of points.
