To mitigate per-point decoding artifacts, an inference-time guidance term introduces a photometric gradient during ODE integration to correlate nearby points.
AI.result: Surflo matches or surpasses feed-forward baselines on surface metrics. It runs an order of magnitude faster than optimization-based methods that require hundreds of views. It is the first feed-forward approach to combine a global latent with arbitrary-resolution decoding.
AI.conclusion: Surflo demonstrates that a global latent paired with scalable, per-instance decoding can effectively reconstruct 3D surfaces from variable numbers of views, offering accurate, fast reconstructions without fixed output constraints and enabling efficient processing across wide output resolutions.

title: Multiagent Protocols with Aggregated Confidence Signals
comment: 22 pages and 5 figures, 9 pages and 2 figures before the appendix
summary: Confidence is used for reliability, oversight, and a range of downstream decision tasks in Natural Language Processing (NLP), yet no existing method produces or evaluates a confidence for the output of a multiagent system. Prior work uses confidence within multiagent debate (MAD) to weight messages, trigger debate, or calibrate individual agents, but it never aggregates these into a single confidence for the system itself. We introduce three protocols that produce a final answer along with a single aggregated confidence by first transforming raw confidence signals to make them comparable across models, then combining them via soft voting or a probability fusion we call Bayesian fusion. This aggregated confidence is substantially more discriminative (AUARC) than that of the best single agent or the standard debate baselines, while correctness (F1-score) stays stable and recovers the losses MAD incurs on more ambiguous tasks. Analyzing two estimators, sequence probability and self-report, alongside parametric and non-parametric calibrators, we find that calibration improves F1 for both estimators while AUARC is less reliant on it. We evaluate six homogeneous and heterogeneous debating pairs per benchmark, across five benchmarks and four task types, spanning a range of model capabilities and sizes.

AI.tldr: Three protocols produce a single, aggregated system-level confidence for multiagent debates by transforming heterogeneous confidence signals to a common scale and fusing them with soft voting or Bayesian fusion. This yields a more discriminative AUARC confidence than baselines while preserving F1, and calibration improves F1 for two estimators; AUARC is relatively calibration-tolerant across varied benchmarks and tasks.
AI.motivation: Address the lack of a dedicated, comparable system-level confidence for multiagent outputs, enabling reliable decision-making and robust downstream tasks.
AI.method: Introduce three protocols that (i) transform raw agent confidences to comparable scales, then (ii) fuse them via soft voting or a Bayesian fusion mechanism to produce a final answer with an aggregated confidence. Evaluate two estimators (sequence probability and self-report) with parametric and non-parametric calibrators, across six debating pairs, five benchmarks, and four tasks.
AI.result: The aggregated confidence (AUARC) is substantially more discriminative than the best single agent or standard baselines, while F1 remains stable and recovers MAD losses on more ambiguous tasks. Calibration improves F1 for both estimators; AUARC is less dependent on calibration.
AI.conclusion: System-level aggregated confidence for multiagent debates is effective and robust across model types and task domains; Bayesian fusion offers a calibration-friendly path to reliable confidence, with practical implications for reliability and decision tasks in NLP ensembles.

title: World Tracing: Generative Pixel-Aligned Geometry Beyond the Visible
summary: Image-to-3D methods often trade off faithfulness and completeness: depth estimators are anchored to input pixels but stop at the visible surface, while image-to-3D models generate complete shapes that are often misaligned with the input. We introduce World Tracing, a generative pixel-aligned geometry representation that predicts 3D points aligned with observed pixels while completing geometry beyond the visible surface. For each input pixel, World Tracing predicts an ordered stack of camera-space 3D points, where the first layer represents the visible surface and subsequent layers represent front-to-back intersections with occluded surfaces. We instantiate this representation with a world-tracing diffusion transformer, WT-DiT, which treats multiple geometry layers as separate denoising tokens coupled through factorized and global attention. WT-DiT is trained with pixel-space flow matching and a mixed noise schedule that balances visible-surface reconstruction with occluded-geometry generation. World Tracing achieves strong performance on visible-surface reconstruction and complete geometry generation across object, scene, and dynamic benchmarks, outperforming both depth predictors and image-to-3D generators. It also preserves 2D-to-3D correspondence, enabling text-driven 3D scene editing, geometry-conditioned novel-view video synthesis, and training-free integration with textured-mesh generators.

AI.tldr: Introduces World Tracing (WT), a pixel-aligned, multi-layer 3D representation that stacks camera-space points per pixel to model both visible surfaces and occluded geometry; instantiated by WT-DiT, a diffusion transformer leveraging layer-wise tokens; trained with pixel-space flow matching and a mixed-noise schedule; yields accurate visible reconstruction and complete geometry, enabling 2D-3D correspondence and editing/video synthesis applications.
AI.motivation: To overcome the trade-off between depth predictors that faithfully reconstruct visible surfaces but are incomplete, and image-to-3D generators that produce complete shapes but misalign with input pixels.
AI.method: Per-pixel, an ordered stack of camera-space 3D points is predicted: the first layer corresponds to the visible surface, and subsequent layers capture front-to-back intersections with occluded surfaces. The model uses a world-tracing diffusion transformer (WT-DiT) that treats multiple geometry layers as separate denoising tokens coupled via factorized and global attention. Training uses pixel-space flow matching and a mixed noise schedule balancing visible-surface reconstruction with occluded-geometry generation.
AI.result: World Tracing achieves strong performance on visible-surface reconstruction and complete geometry generation across object, scene, and dynamic benchmarks, outperforming both depth predictors and image-to-3D generators. It preserves 2D-to-3D correspondence, enabling text-driven 3D scene editing, geometry-conditioned novel-view video synthesis, and training-free integration with textured-mesh generators.
AI.conclusion: The approach delivers faithful, pixel-aligned multi-layer geometry that combines accurate visible-surface reconstruction with plausible occluded geometry, enabling versatile downstream tasks such as editing and video synthesis while remaining compatible with existing mesh/texturing pipelines.

title: Reward Modeling for Multi-Agent Orchestration
summary: Multi-Agent Systems (MAS) built on Large Language Models (LLMs) require effective orchestration to coordinate specialized agents, yet training such orchestrators is hindered by limited supervision and high computational cost. We propose Orchestration Reward Modeling (OrchRM), a self-supervised framework for evaluating orchestration quality without human annotations. OrchRM leverages intermediate artifacts from multi-agent executions to construct win-lose pairs for Bradley-Terry reward model training. Unlike existing MAS test-time scaling and orchestrator training frameworks that rely on costly sub-agent rollouts, OrchRM operates directly at the orchestration level, enabling efficient and high-performing reward-guided orchestrator training and MAS test-time scaling. OrchRM improves training efficiency by up to 10x in token usage while improving MAS test-time scaling performance by up to 8% in accuracy. These gains consistently transfer across multiple domains, including mathematical reasoning, web-based question answering, and multi-hop reasoning, demonstrating orchestration-level reward modeling as a scalable direction for robust multi-agent orchestration. Code will be available at https://github.com/Wang-ML-Lab/OrchRM.

AI.tldr: OrchRM is a self-supervised method for training and scaling orchestration in LLM-based MAS by learning orchestration-level rewards via Bradley-Terry models using artifacts from multi-agent runs, achieving up to 10x token efficiency and up to 8% accuracy gains across domains.
AI.motivation: To address limited supervision and high computational cost in training orchestrators for MAS built on LLMs, and to enable scalable, test-time orchestration without costly sub-agent rollouts.
AI.method: Construct win-lose pairs from intermediate artifacts of multi-agent executions; train a Bradley-Terry reward model at the orchestration level; use this reward model for supervising orchestrator training and test-time scaling; operate without human annotations; demonstrate cross-domain gains.
AI.result: Training efficiency up to 10x in token usage; test-time scaling accuracy improvements up to 8%; gains transfer across math reasoning, web QA, multi-hop reasoning; code release planned.
AI.conclusion: Orchestration-level reward modeling is a scalable, effective direction for robust MAS orchestration, enabling efficient, reward-guided orchestrator training and test-time scaling without heavy sub-agent rollouts.

title: Authority, Truth, and Citation Bias: A Large-Scale Multi-Domain Benchmark for Studying Epistemic Susceptibility in Large Language Models
comment: 10 pages, 5 figures. Accepted to AI4GOOD and EIML at ICML 2026
summary: Large language models are increasingly deployed in citation-augmented settings, yet the effect of citation presence on model behavior independent of factual content remains poorly understood. We introduce AuthorityBench, a 220,564-prompt multi-domain benchmark that isolates how citation-based authority signals influence epistemic behavior in LLMs. The benchmark uses a fully balanced 2x2 factorial design crossing claim veracity with citation veracity, the first to do so, across four domains (general knowledge, science, law, and medicine), with controlled variation over 40 prompt templates, four venue prestige tiers, and a country-coded author name dataset. Evaluating seven models on 12 structured research questions, we find that citation presence, whether real or fabricated, consistently increases hallucination rates relative to a no-citation baseline. The effect is strongest when fabricated citations accompany true claims, raising hallucination rates by 3 to 22 percentage points and reaching 35 to 77% in the general knowledge domain, while legal claims are comparatively robust and venue prestige and author demographics show negligible impact. All datasets and evaluation code are available at: https://github.com/floating-reeds/AuthorityBench

AI.tldr: AuthorityBench reveals that citation signals, even when fabricated, systematically increase LLM hallucinations; the effect is domain-variant, strongest when fabricated citations accompany true claims, with general knowledge worst (35–77% hallucinations); legal content is more robust; venue prestige and author demographics have little impact.
AI.motivation: To understand how citation-based authority cues influence LLM epistemic behavior beyond factual content, addressing a gap where citations may affect belief formation independent of truth.
AI.method: A 2x2 factorial design across four domains (general knowledge, science, law, medicine) crossing claim veracity with citation veracity, using 40 prompt templates, four venue prestige tiers, and a country-coded author name dataset. Evaluated seven models on 12 research questions.
AI.result: Citation presence (real or fabricated) increases hallucination rates relative to a no-citation baseline. Fabricated citations with true claims yield the largest boost (3–22 percentage points; 35–77% hallucinations in general knowledge). Legal claims are comparatively robust; venue prestige and author demographics show negligible impact. All data and code are publicly available.
AI.conclusion: Citation-based authority signals bias LLM outputs toward hallucinations, especially when citations are fabricated. Users should be cautious about relying on citations for truth-value and should employ retrieval-augmented or verified-citation strategies, with careful evaluation across domains to avoid domain-specific vulnerabilities.

title: Flex4DHuman: Flexible Multi-view Video Diffusion for 4D Human Reconstruction
comment: 18 pages, 8 figures. Code, and multi-view caption dataset available
summary: We present Flex4DHuman, a multi-view video diffusion model that transforms a monocular or sparse multi-view video of a dynamic subject into synchronized dense multi-view videos using only relative camera-pose conditioning. Unlike prior human-centric methods that rely on skeletons, depth maps, normals, or rendered target-view geometry, Flex4DHuman requires no explicit geometry priors and instead conditions generation through relative camera-pose positional encoding. The generated videos can be directly ingested by downstream reconstruction pipelines to create dynamic 4D Gaussian splats. Built on the Wan 2.1 1.3B text-to-video model, Flex4DHuman preserves the backbone architecture and encodes camera and view information through a five-axis positional encoding that extends spatio-temporal RoPE with view indices and continuous SE(3) relative camera geometry. A three-stage curriculum progressively trains the model for pose following, flexible reference-to-target view generation, and temporal rollout. To support temporal rollout, we train with clean historical target-view tokens. We also add multi-view captions to enable test-time text control. Combined with an off-the-shelf 4D Gaussian Splatting stage, our framework lifts monocular static-camera videos into dynamic 4D Gaussian splats. Experiments on DNA-Rendering and ActorsHQ show that Flex4DHuman surpasses prior state-of-the-art methods, while the same formulation generalizes to animal categories after mixed human-animal training. These capabilities make Flex4DHuman a practical step toward scalable 4D content creation from casual monocular videos for simulation, gaming, AR/VR, and video re-shooting.

AI.tldr: A diffusion-based framework (Flex4DHuman) converts monocular/sparse multi-view video into synchronized dense multi-view video using relative camera pose encoding, without explicit geometry priors, enabling downstream 4D Gaussian splatting and surpassing prior SOTA on DNA-Rendering/ActorsHQ with generalization to animals.
AI.motivation: To enable scalable 4D content creation from casual monocular videos by eliminating reliance on explicit geometry priors (skeletons, depth, normals, target-view geometry) and leveraging relative camera-pose conditioning for multi-view consistency.
AI.method: Extend Wan 2.1 1.3B text-to-video model with five-axis relative camera-geometry encoding (spatio-temporal RoPE plus view indices and SE(3) geometry). Train via a three-stage curriculum: pose following, reference-to-target view generation, temporal rollout. Use clean historical target-view tokens for rollout; add multi-view captions for test-time text control. Output is compatible with an off-the-shelf 4D Gaussian Splatting stage to produce dynamic 4D splats.
AI.result: Outperforms prior state-of-the-art on DNA-Rendering and ActorsHQ. Generalizes to animals after mixed human–animal training. Enables practical scalable 4D content creation from casual monocular videos for downstream simulation, gaming, AR/VR, and video re-shooting.
AI.conclusion: Flex4DHuman provides a practical, geometry-free pathway to scalable 4D content from casual videos, by leveraging relative pose conditioning and curriculum-based training, and integrating with 4D Gaussian Splatting for downstream reconstruction.

title: EpiBench: Verifiable Evaluation of AI Agents on Epigenomics Analysis
summary: We introduce EpiBench, a verifiable benchmark for short-horizon epigenomics analysis. EpiBench evaluates whether agents can make well-defined analysis decisions from realistic workflow states and return deterministically gradable answers. The benchmark includes 106 evaluations across CUT\&Tag/CUT\&RUN, ATAC-seq, ChIP-seq, and DNA methylation workflows. Across 5,088 valid trajectories from 16 model-harness pairs, no system passed a majority of attempts: GPT-5.5 / Pi led at 45.0\% (143/318 attempts; 95\% confidence interval (CI), 36.3--53.7), followed by GPT-5.5 / OpenAI Codex at 39.9\% (127/318 attempts; 95\% CI, 31.6--48.3). Claude Opus 4.8 Max / Pi and GPT-5.4 / Pi each passed 39.0\% (124/318 attempts; 95\% CI, 30.2--47.8 and 31.0--47.0, respectively). Performance varies across assay types, and many failed runs still contain parts of the correct answer. Agents often found the right files and computed useful intermediate results, but failed when the task required deeper, assay-specific scientific judgment.

AI.tldr: EpiBench is a verifiable short-horizon epigenomics benchmark; current LLMs struggle to pass a majority of tasks, achieving only about 40–45% success across attempts, indicating partial progress but limited deep domain judgment.
AI.motivation: There is a need for objective, reproducible assessment of AI agents' ability to make well-defined, gradable analysis decisions in epigenomics workflows, capturing both decision quality and deterministic outputs.
AI.method: Design includes 106 evaluations across CUT&Tag/CUT&RUN, ATAC-seq, ChIP-seq, and DNA methylation workflows, totaling 5,088 valid trajectories from 16 model–harness pairs. Tasks require decisions from realistic workflow states with deterministically gradable answers. Performance is reported with pass rates and 95% CIs, and analysis notes assay-type variance and partial information in failed runs.
AI.result: No system passed a majority of attempts. GPT-5.5 / Pi led at 45.0% (143/318 attempts; 95% CI, 36.3–53.7), followed by GPT-5.5 / OpenAI Codex at 39.9% (127/318; 95% CI, 31.6–48.3). Claude Opus 4.8 Max / Pi and GPT-5.4 / Pi each passed 39.0% (124/318; 95% CI, 30.2–47.8 and 31.0–47.0). Performance varies across assay types; many failed runs contain parts of the correct answer. Agents often find correct files and compute useful intermediate results, but struggle with deeper, assay-specific scientific judgment.
AI.conclusion: The results indicate limited ability of current agents to consistently perform complete, assay-specific analyses; while partial information and intermediate results are obtainable, higher-level domain reasoning and reproducible decision-making remain challenging, underscoring the need for improved alignment and benchmark design.

title: Disparate Impact in Synthetic Data Generation
summary: We revisit the fairness notion of disparate impact for synthetic data generation (SDG), that assesses whether the utility of generated records is the same across sensitive groups. Our approach departs from existing work on fair SDG, that address the problem of correcting for undue biases in the observed distribution, hence redefining SDG as learning a distribution that is not that of the real data. By contrast, non-disparate impact is notably achieved when the synthetic and real distributions are the same. We expose reasons why SDG may fail to reach that solution and discuss why approximation and estimation errors occur and can be disparate across groups. We notably look into the expressive power of SDG methods relative to distribution complexity, sampling errors due to group proportions, and estimation errors induced by differential privacy mechanisms. We illustrate cases of disparate impact on both artificial and real-world data, focusing on SDG methods that rely on probabilistic graphical models. We also introduce a strategy of learning group-wise SDG models and illustrate how it can improve both the overall utility and its parity in many settings.

AI.tldr: This paper analyzes disparate impact in synthetic data generation (SDG), arguing that achieving identical real and synthetic distributions (non-disparate impact) is often difficult; it critiques existing fair-SDG approaches, identifies error sources (approximation, estimation, sampling, DP), uses probabilistic graphical models to illustrate disparities, and proposes group-wise SDG modeling to improve utility and parity.
AI.motivation: Understand why SDG may exhibit disparate impact and how to mitigate by analyzing fundamental sources of error and the expressive power of models; evaluate how DP and group proportions affect fairness.
AI.method: Theoretical and empirical analysis focusing on probabilistic graphical models; examines distribution complexity, sampling errors, DP-induced estimation errors; presents artificial and real-world case studies; proposes learning group-wise SDG models.
AI.result: Demonstrates cases of disparate impact in SDG, highlights factors contributing to asymmetries across groups, and shows that group-wise SDG models can improve both overall utility and fairness parity in many settings.
AI.conclusion: Group-wise SDG modeling can mitigate disparate impact; fairness in SDG is contingent on the ability to control estimation and sampling errors and the expressive power of the model; achieving non-disparate impact by matching distributions is challenging in practice.

title: SpatialClaw: Rethinking Action Interface for Agentic Spatial Reasoning
summary: Spatial reasoning, the ability to determine where objects are, how they relate, and how they move in 3D, remains a fundamental challenge for vision-language models (VLMs). Tool-augmented agents attempt to address this by augmenting VLMs with specialist perception modules, yet their effectiveness is bounded by the action interface through which those tools are invoked. In this work, we study how the design of this interface shapes the agent's capacity for open-ended spatial reasoning. Existing spatial agents either employ single-pass code execution, which commits to a full analysis strategy before any intermediate result is observed, or rely on a structured tool-call interface that often offers less flexibility for freely composing operations or tailoring the analysis to each task. Both designs offer limited flexibility for open-ended, complex 3D/4D spatial reasoning. We therefore propose SpatialClaw, a training-free framework for spatial reasoning that adopts code as the action interface. SpatialClaw maintains a stateful Python kernel pre-loaded with input frames and a suite of perception and geometry primitives, letting a VLM-backed agent write one executable cell per step conditioned on all prior outputs, enabling the agent to flexibly compose and manipulate perception results and adapt its analysis to both intermediate text and visual observations and the demands of each problem. Evaluated across 20 spatial reasoning benchmarks spanning a broad range of static and dynamic 3D/4D spatial reasoning tasks, SpatialClaw achieves 59.9% average accuracy, outperforming the recent spatial agent by +11.2 points, with consistent gains across six VLM backbones from two model families without any benchmark- or model-specific adaptation.

AI.tldr: SpatialClaw introduces a code-based action interface for VLM-enabled spatial reasoning, enabling iterative, flexible analysis via a stateful Python kernel; it achieves strong, training-free generalization across 20 static/dynamic 3D/4D benchmarks, outperforming existing spatial agents.
AI.motivation: Current spatial agents are constrained by single-pass code execution or structured tool-call interfaces, which limit open-ended, complex 3D/4D spatial reasoning. A flexible, programmable action interface could enhance adaptability to diverse tasks and observations.
AI.method: SpatialClaw maintains a stateful Python kernel pre-loaded with input frames and perception/geometry primitives. A VLM-backed agent writes one executable cell per step, conditioned on all prior outputs, permitting composition and manipulation of results and adaptation to intermediate text/visual observations.
AI.result: Achieved 59.9% average accuracy across 20 spatial reasoning benchmarks, outperforming a recent spatial agent by 11.2 points. Gains are consistent across six VLM backbones from two model families, with no benchmark- or model-specific adaptation.
AI.conclusion: Using code as the action interface yields flexible, open-ended spatial reasoning for VLM agents, delivering robust, generalizable improvements without task-specific training.

title: Multi-Agent Reinforcement Learning from Delayed Marketplace Feedback for Objective-Weight Adaptation in Three-Sided Dispatch
comment: Accepted at ICML 2026 Workshop on Reinforcement Learning from World Feedback (RLxF)
summary: Dispatch in three-sided marketplaces provides a natural setting for reinforcement learning from world feedback: decisions are evaluated by delayed operational outcomes such as delivery speed, courier utilization, and merchant congestion. We present a deployed reinforcement learning system at DoorDash that adapts dispatch objective weights in a large-scale food-delivery marketplace using delayed signals. Rather than replacing the combinatorial assignment optimizer, a store-level policy learned from logged marketplace data selects a discrete multiplier that shifts the dispatch optimizer's tradeoff between delivery quality and batching efficiency. This interface enables offline policy learning under noisy, delayed, and coupled feedback while preserving production feasibility constraints and operational safeguards. We train a shared value function using centralized offline data and decentralized store-level execution, with Double Q-learning targets and a conservative regularizer to reduce out-of-distribution value overestimation. In a production switchback experiment, the offline-trained policy increases batching and reduces courier-side time costs without degrading customer-facing delivery quality. Results illustrate how world feedback from a live economic and logistics system can be used to safely adapt decision policies online.

AI.tldr: Deploys a store-level RL policy to adapt dispatch objective weights in a three-sided marketplace (DoorDash) using offline data and centralized learning with Double Q-learning, implemented with a multiplier to the dispatch optimizer to balance delivery quality and batching efficiency; demonstrated in production with a switchback that improves batching without hurting customer delivery quality.
AI.motivation: Enable safe and data-efficient learning from world feedback in a large-scale, delayed, noisy marketplace while preserving feasibility constraints and production safeguards.
AI.method: Offline-constrained RL: train a shared value function with centralized offline data, then deploy a discrete multiplier at each store to shift the dispatch optimizer's trade-off between quality and batching. Uses Double Q-learning targets and a conservative regularizer to mitigate out-of-distribution value overestimation; centralized training with decentralized execution and safeguards.
AI.result: In a production switchback experiment, the offline-trained policy increases batching and reduces courier-side time costs without degrading customer-facing delivery quality.
AI.conclusion: World feedback from a live economic and logistics system can be safely used to adapt decision policies online; the proposed interface enables offline policy learning under noisy, delayed, and coupled feedback while preserving production feasibility.

title: Demystifying Hidden-State Recurrence: Switchable Latent Reasoning with On-Policy Reinforcement Learning
summary: Latent chain-of-thought compresses reasoning by replacing visible reasoning traces with continuous hidden-state recurrence, but existing formulations are difficult to optimize with standard on-policy reinforcement learning (RL) and hard to interpret causally. Our key insight is that a single pair of explicit boundary tokens can address both issues at once: discrete entry and exit anchors make the latent block compatible with standard on-policy RL, and the same anchors offer a natural foothold for mechanistic analysis. Motivated by this, we propose SWITCH, a switchable latent reasoning framework. The model emits <swi> to enter latent mode and </swi> to exit. Because the boundaries are ordinary discrete tokens, the GRPO policy ratio is well-defined at every decision point. The same anchors also expose the latent steps to direct probing and causal intervention. We train the model with a visible-to-latent curriculum and a Switch-GRPO objective that propagates gradients through recurrent latent computation. SWITCH consistently outperforms prior hidden-state-recurrence latent reasoning approaches at similar scale. Mechanistic analysis through the boundary tokens further reveals three findings: (i) <swi> is a sharply localised, learned switching policy rather than a stylistic artefact; (ii) the latent step it opens performs problem-specific, causally important computation rather than acting as an inert placeholder; and (iii) that computation is concentrated at a single hidden-state transition on entry. Together, these results show that hidden-state-recurrence latent reasoning is both RL-trainable and open to direct mechanistic analysis, including of how on-policy RL itself improves the model from the inside.

AI.tldr: Switchable latent reasoning (SWITCH) uses explicit boundary tokens to enable on-policy RL training and mechanistic analysis of latent computation; it achieves stronger performance and provides causal interpretability.
AI.motivation: To address optimization difficulty and interpretability of latent chain-of-thought methods based on hidden-state recurrence; want RL-compatible training and causal probes.
AI.method: Introduce discrete boundary tokens <swi> and </swi> to demarcate entry/exit of latent mode; use a Switch-GRPO objective; apply visible-to-latent curriculum; ensure policy ratio well-defined; analyze boundaries for mechanistic insights.
AI.result: SWITCH outperforms prior hidden-state-recurrence methods at similar scales; boundary tokens yield localized switching; latent computation is causally important and concentrated at the entry transition; enables mechanistic analysis of RL-driven improvements.
AI.conclusion: Hidden-state-recurrence latent reasoning can be trained with on-policy RL and subjected to mechanistic analysis; boundary tokens provide a practical and interpretable framework for latent reasoning.

title: RepWAM: World Action Modeling with Representation Visual-Action Tokenizers
summary: This work presents RepWAM, a representation-centric world action model (WAM) built on representation visual-action tokenizers. Existing WAMs typically inherit reconstruction-oriented video tokenizers from pretrained video generation models. Although these tokenizers preserve visual fidelity, pixel reconstruction alone provides limited guidance for learning instruction-following dynamics that connect future prediction with robot control. To address this, we explore a semantic visual-action latent space for representation-centric world action modeling. Specifically, we train a representation visual-action tokenizer that maps visual inputs into aligned visual and latent action tokens. We then pretrain our WAM to jointly model future visual states and the latent actions that connect them under language instructions, followed by adaptation to real robot trajectories for closed-loop manipulation. Experiments on real-world manipulation tasks and simulation benchmarks show that RepWAM delivers strong performance across diverse manipulation settings, while ablations highlight the value of semantic visual-action tokenization over reconstruction-oriented alternatives. These results establish representation visual-action tokenization as a promising foundation for world action models and a step toward generalist robot policies. Code and weights will be available at https://github.com/wdrink/RepWAM.

AI.tldr: RepWAM proposes a representation-centric world action model using a semantic visual-action tokenizer, enabling instruction-grounded future-state prediction and closed-loop control, outperforming reconstruction-based tokenizers.
AI.motivation: Pixel-reconstruction tokenizers in world action models (WAMs) preserve visuals but provide weak guidance for instruction-following dynamics. A semantic, aligned visual-action representation could enable more effective learning of control from language instructions.
AI.method: Train a representation visual-action tokenizer that maps visual inputs to aligned visual and latent action tokens. Pretrain a WAM to jointly model future visual states and the latent actions that connect them under language instructions, then adapt to real robot trajectories for closed-loop manipulation.
AI.result: Demonstrates strong performance on real-world manipulation tasks and simulation benchmarks; ablations indicate semantic visual-action tokenization yields benefits over reconstruction-oriented alternatives.
AI.conclusion: Semantic visual-action tokenization is a promising foundation for world action models and advances toward generalist robot policies; code and weights will be released.

title: Reasoning as Pattern Matching: Shared Mechanisms in Human and LLM Everyday Reasoning
comment: 13 pages main text, 51 pages supplementary text
summary: When large language models (LLMs) fail to generalize or make haphazard errors in reasoning, it is often taken as evidence that LLMs are not truly reasoning, but rather performing a kind of pattern matching. The implication is that people's behavior does not exhibit the same types of failures because human reasoning uses principled and abstract world models. We evaluate human participants and 25 LLMs on their ability to engage in common-sense reasoning about a variety of everyday situations and observe similar patterns of errors in both people and models. We then identify the set of attention heads driving LLM responses and find that these heads implement a form of pattern-matching. These attention heads allow us to predict seemingly inexplicable reasoning errors in people caused by ostensibly irrelevant prompt details. Taken together, our results suggest that everyday causal reasoning in people and LLMs is more consistent with a form of pattern-matching than with abstract world models.

AI.tldr: The paper questions whether LLM reasoning reflects true abstraction or pattern-matching by comparing humans and 25 LLMs on common-sense tasks, identifying attention-heads that implement pattern-matching, and showing that prompt details can predict human errors. It concludes that everyday causal reasoning in both humans and LLMs aligns with pattern-matching rather than abstract world models.
AI.motivation: To test the claim that LLM failures are due to pattern-matching rather than genuine reasoning, and to compare this with human reasoning across stimuli to see if humans show similar error patterns.
AI.method: Evaluate humans and 25 LLMs on common-sense reasoning across various everyday scenarios; analyze attention heads in LLMs to identify ones driving responses; examine whether seemingly irrelevant prompt details predict errors in both humans and models.
AI.result: Both humans and models exhibit similar error patterns. A subset of attention heads in LLMs implements pattern-matching and can predict seemingly inexplicable errors in humans caused by irrelevant prompt details.
AI.conclusion: Everyday causal reasoning in people and LLMs appears more consistent with pattern-matching than with abstract world models, challenging the view that LLMs perform robust, abstract reasoning.

title: MP3: Multi-Period Pattern Pre-training forSpatio-Temporal Forecasting
summary: Spatio-Temporal forecasting is crucial in diverse fields, such as transportation, climate, and energy. Urban spatio-temporal data exhibits temporal mirage: similar short-window inputs have divergent future trends, and vice versa. Existing spatio-temporal graph neural networks (STGNNs) cannot effectively identify such mirages. We argue that the core reason lies in the short-window inputs that have incomplete period observation, heterogeneous global spatial correlation, and cross-period superposition causality. To bridge this gap, we develop a novel Multi- Period Pattern Pre-training (MP3), a plug-and-play pre-training plugin for distinguishing temporal mirages. MP3 presents two core innovations: (1) The multi-period pattern learning is designed to learn multi-period patterns from long time series. Specifically, multi-period temporal modeling leverages edge convolution to identify different multi-period patterns. Multi-period spatial modeling uses a bottleneck project and a global memory bank to capture heterogeneous global spatial relations efficiently. Cross-period pattern interaction employs a causality-enhanced Transformer to capture dependencies across different period patterns. (2) This plugin can seamlessly integrate into existing STGNN backbones to strengthen their forecasting performance. The experiment on five STGNN baselines across five real-world datasets (including a large-scale dataset CA) verify the effectiveness, superior scalability and strong adaptability of MP3, which brings consistent and robust performance improvements across all evaluated baselines. On average, MP3 reduces the MAE 4.7% and the RMSE 5.0%. The code can be available at https://github.com/YAN-outlook/MP3.

AI.tldr: MP3 is a plug-and-play pre-training module that learns multi-period patterns to improve STGNN forecasting by addressing temporal mirage via multi-period temporal modeling, multi-period spatial modeling, and cross-period causality-driven interaction; it improves MAE by 4.7% and RMSE by 5.0% across five baselines and five datasets.
AI.motivation: Urban spatio-temporal data suffer from temporal mirage where short-window inputs mislead future trends due to incomplete period observation, heterogeneous spatial relations, and cross-period superposition causality; existing STGNNs struggle with this.
AI.method: Two core innovations: (1) Multi-period pattern learning: temporal modeling via edge convolution to capture multiple periodicities; spatial modeling via bottleneck projection plus a global memory bank; cross-period interaction via a causality-enhanced Transformer. (2) Plugin integration into existing STGNN backbones. Training uses long time-series for pattern learning; evaluation on multiple baselines/datasets; code release.
AI.result: Consistent improvements in MAE (-4.7%) and RMSE (-5.0%) on average; robust performance across baselines and five real-world datasets including a large CA dataset; demonstrates scalability and adaptability; code available.
AI.conclusion: MP3 provides a versatile pre-training plugin that strengthens STGNNs by explicitly modeling multiple-period patterns and cross-period causality, with empirical evidence of broad efficacy and practicality.

title: Modality Forcing for Scalable Spatial Generation
summary: Text-to-image (T2I) models contain rich spatial priors. Synthesizing photorealistic, cluttered scenes requires an understanding of geometry, including perspective and relative scale. Prior works adapt T2I models to leverage this prior for depth prediction, but they require dense depth data and involve complex recipes. We propose Modality Forcing, a simple, scalable post-training recipe for joint image-depth generation using a single DiT trained on sparse depth data. Modality Forcing enables conditional and joint generation of image and depth in any permutation by assigning separate noise levels per modality. Per-modality decoders let us train on sparse, real-world depth and achieve strong, generalizable depth prediction. We further show that Modality Forcing inherits the scalability of T2I pre-training: by training a set of T2I models from scratch (370M to 3.3B parameters), we find that larger models trained on more image data produce more accurate depth. Our strongest model is competitive with state-of-the-art monocular depth estimators and reduces AbsRel by 57% relative to existing joint image-depth generative models. These results provide strong evidence that image generation is a scalable pre-training objective for spatial perception. https://modality-forcing.github.io/

AI.tldr: A simple, scalable post-training recipe (Modality Forcing) enables joint image-depth generation from a single diffusion image model trained on sparse depth, using per-modality noise levels and decoders; larger T2I models with more image data improve depth accuracy, achieving competitive monocular depth and strong error reductions.
AI.motivation: To leverage the rich spatial priors learned by text-to-image models for geometry-aware scene generation without relying on dense depth data or complex pipelines; aim for scalable, post-training joint image-depth generation that generalizes to real-world sparse-depth data.
AI.method: Modality Forcing: a post-training recipe that uses a single DiT (diffusion image model) trained on sparse depth data. Introduces per-modality noise levels and per-modality decoders to support conditional and joint generation of image and depth in any modality permutation. Trains from scratch across large model scales (370M–3.3B params) using sparse real-world depth data, demonstrating that image pre-training scales depth accuracy.
AI.result: Achieves competitive performance with state-of-the-art monocular depth estimators; reduces AbsRel by 57% relative to existing joint image-depth generative models; demonstrates strong generalization to real-world depth and confirms scalability of image generation as a pre-training objective for spatial perception.
AI.conclusion: Image generation serves as a scalable pre-training objective for spatial perception; Modality Forcing provides a simple, scalable approach to joint image-depth generation that benefits from larger T2I models and more image data.

title: AgentBeats: Agentifying Agent Assessment for Openness, Standardization, and Reproducibility
summary:
Agent systems are advancing quickly across domains, but their evaluation remains fragmented. Most benchmarks rely on fixed, LLM-centric harnesses that require heavy integration, create test-production mismatch, and limit fair comparison across diverse agent designs. The root problem is the lack of an open, agent-agnostic assessment interface. We advocate Agentified Agent Assessment (AAA), where evaluation is performed by judge agents and all participants interact through standardized protocols: A2A for task management and MCP for tool access. Conventional benchmarking defines two separate interfaces, one for the benchmark and one for the agent, while AAA only needs one; this yields a generic, unified framework that separates assessment logic from agent implementation and enables reproducible, interoperable, and multi-agent evaluation. We further introduce AgentBeats as a concrete realization of AAA: we identify five practical operation modes that make standardized assessment compatible with real-world constraints on openness, privacy, and reproducibility.
  To evaluate our design at scale, we conduct two studies: a five-month open competition that drew 298 judge agents across 12 categories together with 467 subject agents from independent participants, showing that AAA applies across a heterogeneous range of benchmarks; and a case study on coding agents that confirms agentified evaluation preserves fidelity with the public record while surfacing previously missing head-to-head results, yielding research insights about agent design. Combining a community-scale field study and a controlled coding case study, we verify that AAA delivers coverage, practicality, and fidelity across heterogeneous scenarios at scale. Together, AAA and AgentBeats offer a clear path toward open, standardized, and reproducible agent assessment.

AI.tldr: AAA standardizes agent evaluation via judge agents and unified interfaces (A2A, MCP), with AgentBeats enabling practical, open, reproducible benchmarking across heterogeneous tasks.
AI.motivation: Fragmented benchmarks, heavy integration, test-production mismatch, need open agent-agnostic assessment.
AI.method: Introduce judge-based assessment; standardized protocols A2A (task management) and MCP (tool access); single interface; AgentBeats modes; field studies including a 5-month open competition and a coding case study.
AI.result: Field study: 298 judge agents across 12 categories; 467 subject agents; demonstrates applicability across benchmarks. Case study: coding agents; preserved fidelity with public record; surfaced new head-to-head results; delivers insights.
AI.conclusion: AAA + AgentBeats enables open, standardized, reproducible, and interoperable multi-agent evaluation across diverse scenarios at scale.

title: Select and Improve: Understanding the Mechanics of Post-Training for Reasoning
summary: Reinforcement learning has rapidly emerged as a key component in the training of reasoning and coding models, yet it remains poorly understood from a mechanistic perspective. We study how and through what underlying processes capabilities are acquired or enhanced via reinforcement learning post-training. Our analysis, based on controlled math reasoning experiments with Qwen-2.5-1.5B, reveals two core mechanisms: strategy selection and strategy improvement. Our results highlight the role of SFT data and reinforcement learning data in activating these mechanisms, in particular showing how supervising the model on diverse reasoning strategies can enable strategy selection and how increasing difficulty in reinforcement learning data can enable strategy improvement. Taken together, our results provide mechanistic insight into RL training and suggest practical interventions to continue scaling reasoning capabilities.

AI.tldr: The abstract identifies two mechanistic pathways by which RL training enhances reasoning in language models: strategy selection (enabled by diverse supervised data) and strategy improvement (enabled by more challenging RL data). It emphasizes the distinct roles of supervised fine-tuning data and RL data in activating these mechanisms and proposes interventions to scale reasoning capabilities.
AI.motivation: To uncover the mechanistic processes by which reinforcement learning post-training shapes reasoning and coding capabilities, beyond performance gains.
AI.method: Controlled math-reasoning experiments on Qwen-2.5-1.5B to observe how SFT data and RL data influence two proposed mechanisms (strategy selection and strategy improvement). The study manipulates data diversity and task difficulty to trace causal effects.
AI.result: Identification of two core mechanisms: strategy selection and strategy improvement. SFT data activates strategy selection by exposing diverse reasoning strategies; higher-difficulty RL data activates strategy improvement by pushing the model to enhance its reasoning under harder conditions. The work provides mechanistic insight into RL training and suggests practical interventions to scale reasoning.
AI.conclusion: RL training can be understood through the lens of strategy selection and strategy improvement. Effective scaling of reasoning capabilities may require tailoring SFT data to diversify reasoning strategies and increasing RL data difficulty to promote improvements in strategy execution.

title: InterleaveThinker: Reinforcing Agentic Interleaved Generation
summary: Recent image generators have demonstrated impressive photorealism and instruction-following capabilities in single-image generation and editing. However, constrained by their architectures, they cannot achieve interleaved generation (text-image sequence), which has crucial applications in visual narratives, guidance, and embodied manipulation. Even the latest open-source Unified Multimodal Models (UMMs) exhibit limited performance in this regard. In this paper, we introduce InterleaveThinker, the first multi-agent pipeline designed to endow any existing image generator with interleaved generation capabilities. Specifically, we employ a planner agent to organize the image-text input sequence, instructing the image generator on the required execution at each step. Subsequently, we introduce a critic agent to evaluate the generator's outputs, identify samples that deviate from the planned instructions, and refine the instructions for regeneration. To implement this pipeline, we construct the Interleave-Planner-SFT-80k and Interleave-Critic-SFT-112k to perform a format cold-start. Then we develop Interleave-Critic-RL-13k to reinforce the step-wise instruction correction capability within a generation trajectory using GRPO. Since a single interleaved generation trajectory may involve over 25 generator calls, optimizing the entire trajectory is computationally impractical. Therefore, we propose accuracy reward and step-wise reward, allowing single-step RL to effectively guide the entire generation trajectory. The results show that InterleaveThinker improves performance across various image generators. On interleaved generation benchmarks, it achieves performance comparable to Nano Banana and GPT-5. Surprisingly, it also significantly enhances the base model on reasoning-based benchmarks; for example, on 4-step FLUX.2-klein, we observe substantial gains on WISE and RISE.

AI.tldr: InterleaveThinker enables interleaved image–text generation for any image generator via a two-agent loop: a planner that sequences inputs and a critic that detects errors and refines instructions. Trained with cold-start SFT stages (80k/112k) and RL (GRPO) to optimize stepwise guidance, using single-step rewards to approximate long trajectories.
AI.motivation: Current image generators and unified multimodal models struggle to perform interleaved, multi-step generation required for visual narratives and embodied tasks. A planner–critic workflow could coordinate planning, execution, and correction across generations.
AI.method: A multi-agent pipeline: a planner to organize the input sequence; a critic to evaluate outputs against the plan and refine instructions. Training includes Interleave-Planner-SFT-80k and Interleave-Critic-SFT-112k for cold-start, and Interleave-Critic-RL-13k with GRPO for reinforcement learning. To handle long trajectories (often >25 generator calls), they use accuracy and step-wise rewards for single-step RL guiding the full trajectory.
AI.result: Improved performance across various image generators on interleaved-generation benchmarks, achieving results comparable to Nano Banana and GPT-5. Also notable gains on reasoning benchmarks (e.g., 4-step FLUX.2-klein with WISE and RISE).
AI.conclusion: A generalizable approach to empower existing image generators with interleaved generation through planner–critic dynamics and lightweight, single-step RL; demonstrates broad gains on generation and reasoning benchmarks, suggesting substantial potential for multi-step visual tasks.

title: Beyond Runtime Enforcement: Shield Synthesis as Defensibility Analysis for Adversarial Networks
comment: 26 pages, 7 figures, 7 tables. Under review at JAIR. Code: https://github.com/AchrafHsain7/Bastion
summary:
Shielded reinforcement learning is typically presented as a runtime safety mechanism that compiles temporal-logic specifications into automata restricting an agent's actions. We argue this is the wrong product. The same automata-theoretic machinery -- specification compilation, product game construction, attractor computation, and winning-region extraction -- is better read as a design-time analytical instrument whose outputs are structural insights about a system rather than runtime constraints on a deployed agent.
  We instantiate this through a constrained two-player safety game for network defense. The two specifications are enforced asymmetrically: the defender specification defines the unsafe region of the game, whereas the attacker specification restricts the adversary's legal actions during attractor computation. Solving the game yields a defensibility verdict -- a formal certificate that a topology-specification pair is or is not defensible -- with the associated winning region and shield.
  Beyond the binary verdict, we derive topology-level metrics from the attractor structure and combine them with post-convergence behavior from shield-constrained adversarial multi-agent reinforcement learning. Together these form a defensibility fingerprint capturing both a network's formal safety properties and its operational behavior under adaptive play.
  A what-if analysis shows that formal defensibility and operational effectiveness capture distinct aspects of security: small architectural changes can produce large shifts in operational outcomes while leaving formal safety margins nearly unchanged. Shield synthesis is thus most valuable not as a deployment mechanism for safe agents, but as a framework for answering architectural questions about whether, where, and how a system can be defended. The defensibility verdict is the output, not the safe policy.

AI.tldr: Reframe shielded RL as a design-time instrument using automata-theoretic methods to derive defensibility verdicts and operational fingerprints, rather than as a runtime safety mechanism.
AI.motivation: To address the gap between formal safety margins and operational performance in shielded RL; formal tooling should inform architecture, not constrain deployed agents.
AI.method: Model the defense as a constrained two-player safety game with asymmetric specs (defender defines unsafe region, attacker constrains actions during attractor computation); solve for winning region, defensibility verdict, and shield; extract topology-level metrics from attractors; combine with shield-constrained adversarial MARL to produce a defensibility fingerprint; perform what-if analyses.
AI.result: The framework outputs a defensibility verdict, the winning region, and a shield; it yields topology-level metrics and a defensibility fingerprint that links formal safety properties with operational behavior; shows decoupling of formal safety margins from post-convergence performance.
AI.conclusion: Defensibility verdicts are the primary outputs guiding architectural decisions; shield synthesis serves as a tool for evaluating how, where, and how to defend a system rather than a deployment mechanism for safe policies.

title: MiniPIC: Flexible Position-Independent Caching in <100LOC
summary: Retrieval-augmented and agentic workloads repeatedly prefill recurring predictable structured inputs (which we call "spans") such as documents and code files. Yet, prefix caching in engines such as vLLM cannot reuse their KV entries unless they share identical prefixes with another request, while Position-Independent Caching (PIC) implementations within production-grade inference servers typically either require substantial server code changes or keep KV state outside the server, incurring host-to-device transfer overhead. We present Minimalistic PIC (MiniPIC): a minimal, flexible and fast vLLM design built from two ingredients: positional-encoding-free KV cache and user-controlled cache-reuse primitives. MiniPIC stores unrotated K vectors in the KV cache, applies RoPE to K tiles inside attention using per-request logical positions, and exposes three user-facing and token-level primitives: block-aligned padding, span separator (SSep), and prompt depend (PDep), that modify hashing behavior and effective block-level causal attention structure. With fewer than 100 lines of core-engine changes plus a custom attention backend, these primitives are sufficient to realize multiple PIC methods, including Block-Attention, EPIC, and Prompt Cache, within the same running vLLM instance, while natively integrating with KV cache CPU offload implementations. On 2WikiMultihopQA, MiniPIC with interleaved scheduling improves prefill throughput by 49% over baseline vLLM, reduces cached-span time-to-first-token by up to two orders of magnitude, preserves the linear prefill scaling of uncached spans, and incurs only 5.7% worst-case overhead.

AI.tldr: Minimalistic Position-Independent Cache (MiniPIC) for vLLM that enables PIC methods with a lightweight design.
AI.motivation: Existing prefix caching in vLLM misses reuse opportunities unless prefixes match exactly; PIC deployments usually require invasive changes or off-server KV state, incurring overhead.
AI.method: Introduce MiniPIC: store unrotated K vectors in KV cache, apply RoPE to K tiles inside attention using per-request positions, and expose three user primitives—block-aligned padding, span separator (SSep), and prompt depend (PDep)—that modify hashing and causal structure. Small (<100 lines) core-engine changes plus a custom attention backend to realize Block-Attention, EPIC, and Prompt Cache within one vLLM, with native KV cache CPU offload integration.
AI.result: On 2WikiMultihopQA with interleaved scheduling, MiniPIC improves prefill throughput by 49% vs baseline vLLM, reduces cached-span time-to-first-token by up to 100x, preserves linear prefill scaling for uncached spans, and incurs at most 5.7% worst-case overhead.
AI.conclusion: MiniPIC provides a lightweight, flexible, and fast PIC integration in vLLM that supports multiple caching strategies within a single instance, delivering substantial throughput and latency benefits with minimal code changes.

title: Before You Think: System 0, AI-Mediated Cognition and Cognitive Colonization
summary: This paper examines three recent frameworks for understanding the cognitive and epistemic consequences of artificial intelligence: Tri-System Theory, Thinkframes, and System 0. It argues that while the first two capture important dimensions of AI's influence on individual reasoning and collective epistemic practices, System 0 occupies a theoretically distinctive position that neither can fully replicate. The paper introduces the concept of cognitive colonization, according to which AI systems can embed external interests within the architecture of the self in ways that are difficult for users to perceive. Because such systems are already widely deployed, understanding these invisible forms of influence is an urgent philosophical and practical task.

AI.tldr: The paper analyzes Tri-System Theory, Thinkframes, and System 0 to map AI's cognitive and epistemic effects, highlighting System 0's unique position and introducing cognitive colonization as a hidden influence of AI on the self, urging attention to these invisible forces given widespread deployment.
AI.motivation: to clarify how AI shapes individual reasoning and collective epistemic practices, and to identify a theoretically distinctive framework (System 0) not captured by existing approaches.
AI.method: theoretical-conceptual comparison of three frameworks; critical synthesis; introduction of the concept cognitive colonization; normative and practical discussion.
AI.result: System 0 offers a distinctive perspective not fully captured by Tri-System Theory or Thinkframes; cognitive colonization is a mechanism by which AI embeds external interests within the architecture of the self; implications for theory and practice include heightened awareness of invisible influences and the need for ethical safeguards.
AI.conclusion: Because AI systems are widely deployed, understanding invisible forms of influence is urgent for philosophy and practice, informing epistemic standards, user autonomy, and governance.

title: When Does Routing Become Interpretable? Causal Probes on Block Attention Residuals
summary: Block Attention Residuals (Block AttnRes) by replace fixed additive residuals with a learned softmax over earlier depth-source representations, surfacing cross-layer routing as an inspectable tensor in the forward pass. This is a tempting interpretability target: information flow normally inferred indirectly is now directly observable. We ask whether such exposure suffices for mechanistic interpretation. We probe two same-scale ($0.6$B) Block AttnRes checkpoints under identical routing-ablation interventions: a vanilla Qwen3 inference-wrapped through a deterministic recency-bias schedule that the codebase admits as a routing-equivalent loading path, and a Block AttnRes Qwen3 trained from scratch with routing as part of optimisation. The wrapped baseline's routing weights are content-independent and reproduce the schedule's analytic prediction. The trained AttnRes checkpoint instead exhibits three localised routing motifs: an embedding-source pathway through early-layer MLP, a current-state pathway through early-layer attention and MLP, and an older-history pathway through late-layer attention. Beyond this stratification, we find a sharp dissociation between average routing mass and causal importance: in both sublayers, the largest mass slice is not the largest causal contribution, and one source family carries appreciable mass with no detectable causal role under intervention. Architectural exposure of routing is therefore necessary but not sufficient for mechanistic interpretation: structured depth routing emerges only when routing has been part of training, and even then, descriptive routing summaries should be treated as candidate hypotheses to be tested by causal interventions, not as evidence of mechanism in their own right.

AI.tldr: Exposes cross-layer routing in Block AttnRes but shows that interpretability is not automatically mechanistic; exposure is necessary but not sufficient for causal understanding.
AI.motivation: Assess whether making routing weights observable in Block AttnRes suffices for mechanistic interpretation of information flow in deep nets, by comparing two routing-ablated checkpoints under controlled interventions.
AI.method: Compare two ~0.6B Block AttnRes Qwen3 checkpoints under identical routing ablations: (1) a vanilla Qwen3 wrapped in a deterministic recency-bias routing schedule (routing-equivalent path); (2) a Qwen3 trained from scratch with routing included in optimization. Analyze routing weights, localised motifs (embedding-source, current-state, older-history), and causal impact via interventions; examine mass vs causal contribution.
AI.result: Found three localised routing motifs: embedding-source through early-layer MLP, current-state through early-layer attention/MLP, and older-history through late-layer attention. Observed dissociation between routing mass and causal importance: largest mass slice not necessarily largest causal contributor; a source family can have substantial mass yet yield no causal effect under intervention. Exposure of routing is necessary but not sufficient for mechanistic interpretation; structured depth routing emerges only when routing is part of training, and even then, routing summaries should be treated as candidate hypotheses to be tested causally, not definitive evidence of mechanism.
AI.conclusion: Architectural exposure of routing enables inspection but does not guarantee mechanistic understanding. Mechanistic interpretation requires causal testing of routing hypotheses; interpretability analyses should be framed as hypothesis generation rather than proof of underlying mechanisms.

title: EurekAgent: Agent Environment Engineering is All You Need For Autonomous Scientific Discovery
summary: LLM-based agents have shown increasing potential in automating scientific discovery. Given an optimizable metric and an execution environment, they can propose, validate, and iterate scientific solutions, and have produced results that outperform human-designed approaches. As model capabilities continue to improve, we argue that the bottleneck for autonomous scientific discovery is shifting from prescribing agent workflows to designing agent environments: the resources, constraints, and interfaces that shape agent behavior. We frame this as environment engineering: building environments that amplify productive behaviors, such as open-ended exploration, systematic artifact management, and inter-agent collaboration, while suppressing harmful behaviors, such as reward hacking and high-friction human oversight. We present EurekAgent, an environment-engineered agent system for metric-driven autonomous scientific discovery. EurekAgent engineers the environment along four dimensions: permissions engineering for bounded agent execution and isolated evaluation; artifact engineering for filesystem and Git-based collaboration; budget engineering for budget-aware exploration; and human-in-the-loop engineering for easy human supervision and intervention. EurekAgent sets new state-of-the-art results on multiple mathematics, kernel engineering, and machine learning tasks, including new state-of-the-art 26-circle packing results discovered with less than $11 in total API cost. We open-source our code and results, and call for environment engineering as a core research direction for developing reliable autonomous research agents.

AI.tldr: Shifts the bottleneck of autonomous scientific discovery from agent workflows to designing productive agent environments; introduces EurekAgent, an environment-engineered system with four dimensions that achieves state-of-the-art results (e.g., 26-circle packing under $11 API cost) and is open-sourced; advocates environment engineering as core research.
AI.motivation: To improve reliability and productivity of autonomous scientific discovery by shaping agent behavior via carefully designed environments (permissions, artifacts, budgets, and human-in-the-loop) instead of relying solely on prescribed workflows.
AI.method: Proposes EurekAgent, an environment-engineered agent system that optimizes four dimensions: (1) permissions engineering for bounded execution and isolated evaluation; (2) artifact engineering for filesystem and Git-based collaboration; (3) budget engineering for budget-aware exploration; (4) human-in-the-loop engineering for easy supervision/intervention. Uses metric-driven exploration and demonstrates across mathematics, kernel engineering, and ML tasks, including 26-circle packing with low API cost; open-sources code and results.
AI.result: Achieves new state-of-the-art results on multiple mathematics, kernel engineering, and machine learning tasks; reports a 26-circle packing solution discovered with total API cost under $11; code and results are open-source.
AI.conclusion: Environment engineering should be a central research direction for developing reliable autonomous research agents; EurekAgent exemplifies this approach and invites broader adoption.

title: Detecting Explanatory Insufficiency in Learned Representations: A Framework for Representational Vigilance
comment: 22 pages, 1 figure. Conceptual framework for representation diagnostics in machine learning
summary: Learned representations are central to modern machine learning and are commonly evaluated through predictive performance, robustness, uncertainty estimation, or generalization. However, a learned representation may remain operationally successful while progressively failing to organize persistent residual structures that are not fully captured by conventional evaluation metrics. This article introduces VER, the Vigilant Evaluator of Representations, a conceptual framework for monitoring representational adequacy in learned representations. VER does not propose a new learning algorithm, loss function, or model architecture. Instead, it formalizes a diagnostic process through which persistent residual structures may be identified, analyzed, and interpreted as potential indicators of explanatory insufficiency. The framework distinguishes representational inadequacy from ordinary prediction error, uncertainty, noise, and distribution shift. It introduces a monitoring sequence based on representation identification, explanatory-domain delimitation, residual-structure detection, explanatory-resistance evaluation, and vigilance signaling. VER is intended as a contribution to representation diagnostics in machine learning. Its objective is not to replace existing evaluation methods but to complement them by treating representational adequacy as an explicit object of inquiry. A path toward empirical evaluation through representational-vigilance benchmarks is also outlined.

AI.tldr: Proposes VER, a non-empirical diagnostic framework to monitor representational adequacy by identifying persistent residual structure, distinguishing it from ordinary errors, and outlining a diagnostic workflow for future benchmarks.
AI.motivation: Addresses the potential mismatch between strong predictive performance and underlying inadequacy in representations. Seeks a formal diagnostic process to reveal explanatory insufficiency and guide improvement.
AI.method: Conceptual framework with a five-step monitoring sequence: representation identification, explanatory-domain delimitation, residual-structure detection, explanatory-resistance evaluation, and vigilance signaling; no new learning algorithm, loss, or architecture.
AI.result: No empirical results presented; provides a theoretical construct and workflow. Outlines a path toward empirical evaluation through representational-vigilance benchmarks.
AI.conclusion: VER complements existing evaluation methods by treating representational adequacy as an explicit object of inquiry and guiding future diagnostics and benchmark development.

title: Agents-K1: Towards Agent-native Knowledge Orchestration
summary: Current LLM-based research agents have advanced through agent orchestration, yet largely overlook scientific knowledge orchestration. Existing works often reduce papers to abstracts, surface mentions, and flat \texttt{cites} edges, omitting key entities, claims, evidence, mechanisms, and method lineages essential for scientific reasoning. To this end, we introduce \textbf{Agents-K1}, an end-to-end knowledge orchestration pipeline that converts raw documents into agent-native scientific knowledge graphs. Agents-K1 integrates three components under a unifying theoretical foundation: a multimodal parser whose five-module schema captures entities, multimodal evidence, citations, and typed inter-entity relations across the full paper rather than abstracts alone; a 4B information-extraction backbone trained with GRPO under a rule-based reward; and a graphanything CLI, a tri-source agent interface that unifies web search, multimodal graph retrieval, and cross-document traversal. On top of this, we process 2.46 million scientific papers across six subjects to produce \textbf{Scholar-KG}, of which we release a one-million-paper subset, and the full Scholar-KG is accessible via the SCP link below. The same pipeline can be extended to general-domain corpora and to schema-conformant data synthesis. Extensive experiments demonstrate that Agents-K1 achieves superior performance in scientific information extraction, knowledge graph construction, and multi-hop scientific reasoning.

AI.tldr: Agents-K1 is an end-to-end pipeline that converts full scientific papers into a structured knowledge graph (Scholar-KG) using a five-module multimodal parser, a 4B-scale information-extraction backbone trained with GRPO under a rule-based reward, and a graphanything CLI, producing a large-scale, cross-domain KG and enabling improved scientific reasoning; a 1-million-paper subset is publicly released.
AI.motivation: Addresses the gap in scientific knowledge orchestration within LLM-based research agents. Existing approaches primarily reduce papers to abstracts, surface mentions, and flat citations, missing core entities, claims, evidence, mechanisms, and method lineages necessary for robust scientific reasoning across documents.
AI.method: Proposes Agents-K1 with three components under a unified theory: (1) a multimodal parser with a five-module schema capturing entities, multimodal evidence, citations, and typed inter-entity relations across the full paper; (2) a 4B information-extraction backbone trained with GRPO under a rule-based reward; and (3) a graphanything CLI that unifies web search, multimodal graph retrieval, and cross-document traversal. The pipeline processes 2.46 million papers across six subjects to produce Scholar-KG, including a released one-million-paper subset; the full Scholar-KG is accessible via SCP.
AI.result: Empirical results claim superior performance in scientific information extraction, knowledge graph construction, and multi-hop scientific reasoning. The framework yields Scholar-KG, a large-scale, cross-domain knowledge graph, with extensibility to general-domain corpora and schema-conformant data synthesis.
AI.conclusion: Agents-K1 demonstrates the feasibility and effectiveness of end-to-end knowledge orchestration for scientific reasoning, enabling richer representations of papers and cross-document inference; the dataset (Scholar-KG) can catalyze downstream research and potential generalization to other domains; full access via SCP is provided.

title: Getting Better at Working With You: Compiling User Corrections into Runtime Enforcement for Coding Agents
summary: Interactive LLM agents are becoming part of daily work, but they do not reliably become easier to work with over time: a correction remembered in one session may still be violated in the next. We study this gap between preference access and preference compliance. In tasks derived from anonymized real-user friction cases, Mem0 memory still leaves 57.5% of applicable preference checks violated. We introduce Test-time Rule Acquisition and Compiled Enforcement (TRACE), a drop-in skill-layer pipeline for coding-agent runtimes that mines user corrections, rewrites them as atomic rules, and compiles them into runtime checks that must pass before an agent completes future tasks. Unlike runtime checks written ahead of time by developers, TRACE skills come from the user's own chat corrections. We evaluate TRACE with simulated user-in-the-loop experiments on ClawArena coding-agent tasks and MemoryArena-derived memory-intensive tasks. On ClawArena, TRACE reduces held-out preference violation from 100.0% to 37.6% on in-distribution tasks and from 100.0% to 2.0% on out-of-distribution tasks. On MemoryArena-derived tasks, TRACE reduces in-distribution violation from 100.0% to 60.5% while matching or exceeding the strongest memory baseline on task pass. These results suggest that compiling corrections into runtime enforcement can address a repeated-friction failure mode that memory alone does not reliably solve, reducing the need for users to restate the same correction across future sessions. Experiment code is available at https://github.com/YujunZhou/TRACE_exp, and the deployable skill is available at https://github.com/YujunZhou/tellonce.

AI.tldr: TRACE turns user chat corrections into atomic runtime rules and compiles them as checks, reducing persistent preference violations in coding-agent tasks.
AI.motivation: Address the gap where user corrections remembered in one session are not reliably enforced in future sessions; current memory alone often fails to prevent repeated friction.
AI.method: A drop-in skill-layer pipeline that mines user corrections from chat, rewrites them as atomic rules, and compiles them into runtime checks that must pass before completing future tasks. Evaluated with simulated user-in-the-loop experiments on ClawArena and MemoryArena tasks.
AI.result: On ClawArena: in-distribution violations dropped from 100% to 37.6% and out-of-distribution to 2.0%. On MemoryArena: in-distribution violations reduced to 60.5%, with task pass matching/exceeding the strongest memory baseline. Code and deployable skill released for replication.
AI.conclusion: Compiling user-derived corrections into runtime enforcement can address a persistent friction not solved by memory alone, reducing the need to re-state corrections across sessions and improving reliability of preference compliance.

title: Automated reproducibility assessments in the social and behavioral sciences using large language models
summary: Reproducibility in the social and behavioral sciences is typically evaluated by independent researchers who reanalyze the original data to assess whether the published findings can be recovered. However, such approaches are resource-intensive and difficult to scale. Here, we show that large language models (LLMs) can automate reproducibility assessments. Using N=76 published studies with predefined claims from the behavioral and social sciences, we compare LLM-generated analysis with the original findings and human reanalysis. For 7 studies, the LLM could not produce a viable effect size estimate. For the remaining studies, our LLM pipeline recovered the original effect sizes in 41% of studies using a +/-0.05 tolerance in Cohen's d. Further, our LLM pipeline reached the same qualitative conclusion as the original study in 96% of cases, where conclusions indicate whether the reanalysis supports the original claim. For comparison, human reanalysts recovered the original effect sizes in 34% of studies and reached the same qualitative conclusion in 74% of cases. Together, these results show that LLMs can serve as a scalable tool for automated reproducibility assessment and provide a foundation for systematic auditing of empirical results in the social and behavioral sciences.

AI.tldr: LLMs can automate reproducibility assessments in social/behavioral sciences, with 41% recovery of original effect sizes within a small tolerance and 96% qualitative agreement, offering a scalable alternative to manual reanalysis (vs humans: 34% and 74%).
AI.motivation: Reproducibility evaluation is resource-intensive; a scalable automation method could enable systematic auditing of empirical results in the social/behavioral sciences.
AI.method: Applied an LLM pipeline to 76 published studies with predefined claims, comparing LLM-generated analyses to original findings and to human reanalyses. For 7 studies, the LLM could not produce a viable effect size; for the rest, within +/-0.05 Cohen's d tolerance, the LLM recovered original effect sizes in 41% of cases; the LLM reached the same qualitative conclusion as the original study in 96% of cases. By comparison, human reanalysts recovered original effect sizes in 34% and reached the same qualitative conclusion in 74%.
AI.result: LLMs demonstrated potential as scalable tools for automated reproducibility assessment, achieving high qualitative agreement and substantial but lower quantitative replication relative to human reanalysis in this sample.
AI.conclusion: LLMs can serve as a scalable foundation for automated reproducibility auditing in the social and behavioral sciences, enabling broader audits of empirical results.

title: Loss-Shift Transfer via Bayes Quotients
summary: Transfer learning is usually studied as a consequence of distribution shift. This paper identifies an orthogonal failure mode in which the data distribution is fixed and the loss changes. This setting is called \emph{loss shift}. A loss determines which information in \(X\) is Bayes-relevant, and two losses may therefore require different representations even under the same joint law \(P(X,Y)\).
