The idea is formalized using Bayes quotients, which allow losses to be ordered by refinement. In the Bayes-quotient formulation, strict refinement gives an immediate qualitative obstruction. A source-minimal representation for a coarser loss is insufficient for a strictly finer target loss. For finite-output log loss, this obstruction becomes an exact quantitative identity. The excess risk is the conditional information about \(Y\) discarded by the representation. Experiments in controlled, learned, synthetic-image, and real-image settings show the predicted effect, i.e., classification-equivalent representations can have different optimal log-loss performance under a fixed data distribution.

AI.tldr: Loss shift: even with a fixed joint distribution P(X,Y), optimal representations depend on the chosen loss. The paper uses Bayes quotients to order losses by refinement, showing that a source-minimal representation for a coarse loss may be insufficient for a finer loss; for finite-output log loss, the obstruction is exact and the excess risk equals the information about Y discarded by the representation (I(Y; X|Z)). Experiments across controlled, learned, and real-image settings validate that class-equivalent representations can have different log-loss performance under the same distribution.
AI.motivation: To identify and formalize an orthogonal failure mode to distribution shift in representation learning: loss choice can drive different Bayes-relevant information and representations even when P(X,Y) is fixed.
AI.method: Formalize loss refinement via Bayes quotients; derive when a source-minimal representation suffices for a coarse loss and when it fails for a finer loss; prove exact identity for finite-output log loss; quantify excess risk as the conditional information about Y discarded by the representation; empirical validation across controlled, synthetic-image, and real-image experiments.
AI.result: Establishes a qualitative obstruction via strict refinement and, for log loss, a quantitative identity: the excess risk equals I(Y; X|Z). Demonstrates that two representations can be classification-equivalent yet differ in optimal log-loss under a fixed data distribution.
AI.conclusion: Loss-aware representation learning is essential: even with the same joint distribution, representations optimized for a coarse loss may be suboptimal for finer losses. Bayes quotients offer a principled framework to analyze when and how representations must change with the target loss, and experimental results corroborate the theoretical predictions.

title: The Geometry of Phase Transitions in Generative Dynamics via Projection Caustics
summary: Continuous-state generative samplers, including diffusion and flow-matching models, evolve through continuous reverse-time dynamics, yet their samples often undergo abrupt qualitative changes: trajectories commit to modes, semantic alternatives collapse, and small perturbations in narrow time windows can produce large downstream effects. This paper develops a geometric account of such phase-transition-like behaviour. We view denoising as gradient descent on a free energy landscape and show that sharp transitions arise near projection caustics, where the nearest-point projection onto the data support ceases to be unique. Motivated by this perspective, we introduce the Critical Boundary Detector (CBD), as practical diagnostics for score-direction instability. Across toy models, standard diffusion models, and latent text-to-image diffusion models, CBD localises mode commitment, predicts intervention-sensitive windows, and supports targeted control in geometrically sensitive regions. Our results connect geometry of data and dynamics of diffusion generation.

AI.tldr: Geometric explanation for abrupt, phase-transition-like changes in continuous-state samplers (diffusion/flow models) via projection caustics; introduces Critical Boundary Detector (CBD) to diagnose score-direction instability and enable mode localization, intervention timing, and geometry-aware control.
AI.motivation: Explain why diffusion-based samplers exhibit sharp qualitative transitions despite smooth dynamics, by connecting data geometry with the dynamics of denoising.
AI.method: Interpret denoising as gradient descent on a free-energy landscape; analyze projection caustics where nearest-point projections onto data support become non-unique; define CBD as a practical diagnostic for score-direction instability; validate across toy models, standard diffusion models, and latent text-to-image diffusion models.
AI.result: CBD localizes mode commitment, predicts intervention-sensitive windows, and enables targeted control in geometrically sensitive regions; establishes a link between data geometry and diffusion dynamics, with practical diagnostics and control implications.
AI.conclusion: Geometry of data spaces governs diffusion dynamics; CBD provides a practical tool to diagnose and exploit score-direction instability and phase-transition-like behavior in continuous-state generative models.

title: WHAR Arena: Benchmarking the State of the Art in Efficient Wearable Human Activity Recognition
summary: Deep learning has become the dominant paradigm in Wearable Human Activity Recognition (WHAR), yet progress is obscured by a comparability crisis. Results are often reported using inconsistent datasets, custom data processing, and varying evaluation protocols, making state-of-the-art claims fragile. We address this with a large-scale, open-source benchmark that integrates 30 diverse datasets under standardized processing, unified model interfaces, and a shared cross-subject evaluation protocol. Evaluating 17 representative architectures across 4760 training runs, we jointly measure predictive performance alongside on-device latency, peak memory, and model size on an Android reference device. Our results reveal that the WHAR state of the art is distributed rather than dominated by a single architecture. While CNN-HAR achieves the highest mean macro-F1, top-performing models cluster tightly, indicating contemporary architectures have converged near a predictive performance ceiling. When accounting for deployment efficiency, compact neural models, such as TinierHAR, and classical Random Forests define the practically relevant Pareto frontier, whereas larger recurrent and hybrid models incur high hardware costs without corresponding performance gains. Consequently, while predictive performance has plateaued, substantial potential for future progress remains in optimizing deployment efficiency and improving adaptation to domain shifts. We release our full framework to support transparent reuse and extension.

AI.tldr: A large, open WHAR benchmark across 30 datasets shows no single architecture dominates; accuracy has plateaued while deployment efficiency drives practical performance; simple/compact models and Random Forests form the Pareto frontier when accounting for resources; an open framework is released for reproducibility.
AI.motivation: Address the WHAR comparability crisis due to inconsistent datasets, data processing, and evaluation protocols; enable fair, cross-architecture comparisons with standardized benchmarks and deployment metrics.
AI.method: Assemble 30 diverse WHAR datasets with standardized preprocessing and unified model interfaces; employ a shared cross-subject evaluation protocol; assess 17 architectures across 4760 training runs; measure predictive performance (macro-F1) and on-device metrics (latency, peak memory, model size) on an Android reference device.
AI.result: The state of the art in WHAR is distributed rather than dominated by a single architecture. CNN-HAR achieves the highest mean macro-F1, but top models cluster closely, signaling a predictive ceiling. When including deployment efficiency, compact models (e.g., TinierHAR) and Random Forests define the practical Pareto frontier; larger recurrent/hybrid models incur higher hardware costs without performance gains. Predictive performance appears plateaued, but there remains substantial room to improve deployment efficiency and domain adaptation. An open framework for transparent reuse is released.
AI.conclusion: Future progress likely lies in deployment-efficiency optimization and domain-shift adaptation, facilitated by community-wide standardized benchmarks and openly available tooling to enable reproducible, comparable evaluations.

title: Understanding helpfulness and harmless tension in reward models
comment: The source code used in this study is publicly available at: https://github.com/EshaanT/RM-alignment\_tension
summary: Reward models are a key component of reinforcement learning from human feedback (RLHF), aligning language models toward both helpful and harmless behaviour. However, the internal mechanisms underlying these objectives and their conflicts remain poorly understood. We study alignment tension in reward models trained under helpfulness-only, harmlessness-only, and mixed-objective settings. We find that mixed-objective models often underperform single-objective models, indicating interference between objectives. Using activation-based methods, we identify neurons associated with each objective and study their functional roles via targeted ablations. We find that these neurons causally support their corresponding objectives while often negatively affecting the opposing one. We find that a substantial proportion of neurons are shared between helpfulness and harmlessness, and that these shared neurons exert a disproportionate influence on model behaviour, contributing to alignment tension. Additionally, our results provide insights and mechanistic interpretation into how alignment objectives are represented in reward models and why multi-objective alignment remains challenging, motivating future work on disentangled and controllable alignment methods.

AI.tldr: Multi-objective reward models in RLHF show objective interference; shared and distinct neurons underlie alignment tension; targeted ablations reveal causal roles that often trade off objectives; disentangled alignment needed.
AI.motivation: To understand how helpful and harmless objectives are represented and conflict in reward models, explaining underperformance of mixed objectives and guiding future methods.
AI.method: Activation-based neuron identification for each objective; ablation to test causal roles; comparisons across helpfulness-only, harmlessness-only, and mixed-objective models; analysis of shared neurons and their influence.
AI.result: Mixed-objective models underperform single-objective models due to interference; neurons tied to one objective can negatively affect the other; a substantial portion of neurons are shared between helpfulness and harmlessness and these shared neurons disproportionately shape behavior.
AI.conclusion: Provides mechanistic view of alignment representations; explains why multi-objective alignment is challenging; motivates disentangled and controllable alignment approaches.

title: From Uncertain Judgments to Calibrated Rankings: Conformal Elo Estimation for LLM Evaluation
summary: Evaluating new large language models typically requires costly human annotation campaigns at scale. LLM-as-a-judge offers a cheaper alternative, but judge scores carry systematic errors - such as position bias, self-preference, or intransitivity - that can strongly miscalibrate the resulting rankings. We quantify the resulting judge-human disagreement at two complementary levels. At the local level, we estimate per-battle uncertainty from the judge's own score differences by propagating calibrated win probabilities rather than hard labels into the Bradley-Terry procedure. This alone provides a drastic improvement to Elo estimation accuracy, bringing LLM-derived ratings within 17.9 Elo MAE of human-derived ones when averaged over 55 held-out models on LMArena. At the global level, we apply split conformal prediction to the residual gap between LLM-derived and human-derived Elo ratings across held-out models, producing prediction intervals with distribution-free marginal coverage guarantees that account for irreducible LLM-human disagreement. Together, these two layers yield a low-cost evaluation tool that provides developers with calibrated Elo estimates and honest uncertainty bounds, without access to large-scale human annotations.To facilitate reproducibility, we release our code at https://github.com/kargibora/SoftElo .

AI.tldr: A cost-effective evaluation framework for large language models using LLMs as judges. It reduces systematic judgment bias with calibrated win-probabilities in a Bradley–Terry model for local Elo estimation and applies split conformal prediction on residuals for global uncertainty intervals, achieving near-human Elo accuracy and distribution-free uncertainty guarantees without large-scale human annotations.
AI.motivation: To enable scalable, low-cost evaluation of large language models while mitigating judgment biases (position bias, self-preference, intransitivity) that arise when using LLM-based judges, and to provide honest uncertainty estimates.
AI.method: 1) Local layer: propagate calibrated win probabilities (instead of hard labels) into a Bradley–Terry framework to estimate per-battle uncertainty and improve Elo estimation accuracy. 2) Global layer: use split conformal prediction on the gap between LLM-derived and human-derived Elo ratings across held-out models to generate distribution-free prediction intervals with marginal coverage guarantees.
AI.result: Elo MAE on held-out models (55 models on LMArena) improved to within 17.9 points of human-derived ratings. The approach yields calibrated Elo estimates and honest uncertainty bounds, with a practical code release for reproducibility.
AI.conclusion: The two-layer approach provides a low-cost, reliable evaluation tool that reduces reliance on expensive human annotations while delivering calibrated scores and valid uncertainty intervals, facilitating scalable LLM evaluation.

title: Distributional Loss for Robust Classification
summary: This paper proposes a novel loss concept for supervised classification tasks. Rather than enforcing a direct mapping from each input sample to a single assigned label, we define an optimization objective over all classifier outputs as a bimodal Gaussian distribution. This softer target formulation implicitly captures class ambiguity, mitigates overfitting, and encourages the learning of more robust decision boundaries, all without requiring additional label information. Experimental results demonstrate consistent improvements in robustness, with particularly pronounced gains in low-data regimes, while requiring only minimal modifications to standard training pipelines.

AI.tldr: Introduce a soft target loss using a bimodal Gaussian over classifier outputs to encode class ambiguity, improving robustness and low-data performance with minimal training changes.
AI.motivation: Hard, one-hot targets can cause overfitting and brittle decision boundaries; leveraging target uncertainty could improve robustness without additional labels.
AI.method: Define the training objective as fitting classifier outputs to a bimodal Gaussian target distribution (soft targets) over all class scores, integrating into standard training without extra labels.
AI.result: Empirical results show consistent robustness improvements, especially in low-data regimes, with only minor modifications to standard training pipelines.
AI.conclusion: A soft, bimodal target loss mitigates overfitting and yields robust decision boundaries under data scarcity, without requiring extra labeling effort.

title: Towards More General Control of Diffusion Models Using Jeffrey Guidance
summary: A key strength of diffusion models lies in their flexibility, since their outputs can be controlled at sampling time through guidance. However, beyond simple cases such as conditional sampling, the target distribution is often left implicit, defined only through a sampling rule or a heuristic energy function. To address this, we propose Jeffrey guidance, a principled framework that extends diffusion-model control to applications beyond what standard guidance can express. It leverages Jeffrey's rule of conditioning to update marginal distributions towards a prescribed target, preserving the conditional structure and minimally perturbing the joint distribution. We first demonstrate Jeffrey guidance by targeting a prescribed embedding distribution. With Inception embeddings as the target, this leads to substantial reductions in FID on both CIFAR-10 and FFHQ. We further apply Jeffrey guidance to fairness on CelebA-HQ, updating an unconditional diffusion model to enforce independence between attributes.

AI.tldr: Jeffrey guidance generalizes diffusion-model control by using Jeffrey's rule to update marginals toward a prescribed target, preserving conditional structure and minimally perturbing the joint distribution, enabling targets beyond standard conditioning.
AI.motivation: Standard diffusion guidance handles only simple conditioning; there is a need for a principled method to steer the joint distribution toward specific target marginals (e.g., embedding distributions, fairness constraints) while maintaining conditional dependencies.
AI.method: Apply Jeffrey's rule of conditioning to update marginal distributions toward a prescribed target marginal, preserving the conditional structure and minimizing joint perturbation. Demonstrations include (1) targeting an embedding distribution (Inception embeddings) to improve image realism (FID) on CIFAR-10 and FFHQ; (2) enforcing attribute independence (fairness) on CelebA-HQ by updating an unconditional diffusion model.
AI.result: Achieved substantial reductions in FID on CIFAR-10 and FFHQ for embedding-targeted generation; demonstrated enforcement of independence between attributes for fairness on CelebA-HQ.
AI.conclusion: Jeffrey guidance provides a principled extension of diffusion-model control, enabling explicit targeting of marginal distributions beyond traditional conditioning, with demonstrated gains in sample quality and controllability (e.g., embedding alignment and fairness constraints).

title: ReSET: Accurate Latency-Critical NVFP4 Reasoning via Step-Aware Temperature Scaling
summary: Large reasoning models (LRMs) improve complex problem-solving by generating long intermediate reasoning traces, but this substantially increases inference costs. NVFP4 inference offers a promising approach to reduce both computational and memory costs through hardware-supported low-precision execution. However, directly applying NVFP4 to LRMs introduces two practical limitations: reasoning accuracy degrades under quantization, and existing NVFP4 kernels do not fully realize latency benefits in small-batch autoregressive decoding. In this work, we analyze the effect of NVFP4 quantization on token-level uncertainty during reasoning. We show that quantization increases incorrect sampling at low-entropy symbolic tokens, while causing over-concentration on a small set of tokens in high-uncertainty reasoning steps. Based on this observation, we propose \textbf{ReSET}, a reasoning-step entropy-based temperature-scaling method that estimates step-level uncertainty online and adapts the decoding temperature using both token-level and step-level entropy signals. To address the latency gap, we further design a CUDA-core small-$M$ NVFP4 kernel for latency-critical autoregressive decoding. Across reasoning benchmarks and model scales, ReSET improves NVFP4 reasoning accuracy by up to $\sim\!$2 points over the NVFP4 baseline. Our CUDA-core small-$M$ kernel further improves latency-critical decoding, delivering up to $2.5\!\times$ kernel-level speedup over NVFP4 vLLM and approximately $2\!\times$ end-to-end decoding speedup over BF16. Code is available at https://github.com/aiha-lab/ReSET.

AI.tldr: Proposes ReSET for NVFP4-quantized large reasoning models, combining online step-level uncertainty estimation with entropy-based temperature scaling and a small-M CUDA kernel to boost latency in autoregressive decoding; achieves up to ~2-point accuracy gains and up to 2.5× kernel speedups, with code released.
AI.motivation: LRMs require long reasoning traces, driving high compute/memory costs. NVFP4 offers low-precision inference but quantization harms accuracy, and existing kernels do not deliver latency benefits for small-batch autoregressive decoding.
AI.method: Quantify token-level uncertainty under NVFP4; introduce ReSET that online-estimates step-level uncertainty and adapts decoding temperature using token-level and step-level entropy signals; develop a CUDA-core small-M NVFP4 kernel to reduce latency in autoregressive decoding.
AI.result: ReSET improves NVFP4 reasoning accuracy by up to ~2 points over the NVFP4 baseline. The specialized CUDA-core small-M kernel yields up to 2.5× kernel-level speedup over NVFP4 vLLM and ~2× end-to-end speedup over BF16, across reasoning benchmarks and model scales. Code is released.
AI.conclusion: Entropy-aware, online adaptation combined with hardware-optimized kernels can mitigate accuracy loss from quantization and unlock latency benefits for small-batch autoregressive decoding in large reasoning models.

title: VideoMDM: Towards 3D Human Motion Generation From 2D Supervision
summary: We introduce VideoMDM, a diffusion-based framework that trains 3D human motion priors directly from accurate 2D poses extracted from monocular videos, without any 3D ground truth. A pretrained 2D-to-3D lifter provides approximate 3D pose sequences that serve as a noisy teacher: these are diffused, denoised by the model in 3D, and supervised in 2D by reprojecting the prediction and comparing against accurate keypoints. We show that, under mild assumptions, a depth-weighted 2D reprojection loss is equivalent in expectation to direct 3D supervision, and we adapt standard 3D motion regularizers - velocity consistency and over-parameterized representation alignment - to this 2D setting. Unlike methods that lift 2D to 3D only at inference, VideoMDM learns a coherent 3D motion manifold during training. On HumanML3D it nearly closes the gap to fully 3D-supervised MDM (FID 0.88 vs 0.54); On real video datasets Fit3D and NBA the method learns to generate motions consistently preferred by humans, with strong quantitative results.

AI.tldr: A diffusion-based framework learns 3D human motion priors from accurate 2D poses without 3D ground truth, using a noisy 3D teacher from a 2D-to-3D lifter and depth-weighted 2D reprojection loss to train a 3D motion manifold.
AI.motivation: Enable high-quality 3D motion priors from monocular videos without costly 3D supervision, and close the gap between 2D-to-3D lifting during training and inference.
AI.method: Diffusion-based model that denoises 3D poses starting from lifter-provided noisy 3D poses; supervision via reprojected 2D keypoints; depth-weighted 2D reprojection loss; adapts 3D regularizers (velocity consistency, representation alignment) to 2D supervision; learns a coherent 3D motion manifold during training.
AI.result: On HumanML3D, it nearly closes the gap to fully 3D-supervised MDM (FID 0.88 vs 0.54); on Fit3D and NBA, the generated motions are preferred by humans with strong quantitative support.
AI.conclusion: VideoMDM demonstrates that 3D motion priors can be learned from 2D data alone when combined with a diffusion framework and a depth-weighted 2D supervision signal, achieving competitive or superior results to fully 3D-supervised baselines and producing coherent motion manifolds.

title: Decoding Insect Song: A Multitask Semisupervised Orthoptera Bioacoustic Classifier
comment: ICML 2026 Workshop on Machine Learning for Audio
summary: Passive acoustic monitoring holds great promise for ecological inference, yet existing automated tools are typically narrowly trained and non-transferable. We address these limitations with PULSE, a semi-supervised, multi-task framework for Orthoptera bioacoustics, combining weakly-supervised species classification, self-supervised learning on unlabelled field audio, and knowledge distillation from a general-purpose bioacoustic model. Our domain-adapted specialist model outperforms a state-of-the-art general model across all metrics (macro F1: 0.21 vs. 0.07; AUC: 0.74 vs. 0.45; AP: 0.32 vs. 0.19), with active learning further raising F1 to 0.34 and AUC to 0.84. Beyond classification, the learned embeddings encode ecologically meaningful structure, exposed through an interactive visualisation tool for ecological discovery.

AI.tldr: PULSE is a semi-supervised, multi-task framework for Orthoptera bioacoustics that combines weakly supervised classification, self-supervised learning on unlabeled field audio, and knowledge distillation from a general bioacoustic model; it yields a domain-adapted specialist model that outperforms a general model, with active learning further boosting performance and embeddings enabling ecological discovery.
AI.motivation: Passive acoustic monitoring suffers from narrowly trained, non-transferable models. There is a need for data-efficient, transferable methods that leverage unlabeled data and domain-specific adaptation to improve Orthoptera bioacoustics classification and ecological insights.
AI.method: A semi-supervised, multi-task approach: weakly supervised species classification, self-supervised learning on unlabeled field audio, and knowledge distillation from a general-purpose bioacoustic model. Includes a domain-adapted specialist model and an active learning component; results include learned embeddings and an interactive visualization tool for ecological discovery.
AI.result: The domain-adapted specialist model outperforms the general model across all metrics: macro F1=0.21 vs 0.07; AUC=0.74 vs 0.45; AP=0.32 vs 0.19. Active learning further improves performance: F1=0.34 and AUC=0.84. Embeddings capture ecologically meaningful structure, exposed via an interactive visualization tool.
AI.conclusion: A transferable, data-efficient semi-supervised, multi-task framework (PULSE) improves Orthoptera bioacoustic classification over general models, with active learning and meaningful embeddings enabling ecological discovery and visualization.

title: Reinforcement Learning for Neural Model Editing
summary: Editing pretrained neural networks requires specialized algorithms tailored to specific objectives. Designing such algorithms is often time-consuming and demands significant effort. We present an exploratory framework that formulates neural model editing as a reinforcement learning problem, where agents modify models using reward feedback. We introduce two environments: MaskWorld, where agents scale weights multiplicatively, and ShiftWorld, where agents apply additive weight updates. The reward function combines a utility-preservation objective with a task-specific editing objective, enabling agents to learn targeted modifications while maintaining overall model performance. We evaluate the framework on bias mitigation in text classification and machine unlearning in image classification, both of which traditionally rely on specialized algorithms. Our results show that the learned policies reduce forget set accuracy to nearly 0% while preserving over 90% retain set accuracy on the unlearning task. In the bias mitigation setting, the learned policies improve bias-related performance by more than 5% while maintaining general classification utility. Our findings show that neural model editing can be cast as a reinforcement learning problem, allowing editing policies to be learned from reward feedback rather than manually engineered for each task.

AI.tldr: An RL-based framework for neural model editing with two environments (MaskWorld and ShiftWorld); learns policies to edit weights to satisfy an editing objective while preserving utility; demonstrates on text bias mitigation and image unlearning with strong performance.
AI.motivation: Editing pretrained neural networks is time-consuming and task-specific; aim to generalize editing via reinforcement learning and reward feedback.
AI.method: Formulate as RL; define two environments: MaskWorld (multiplicative weight scaling), ShiftWorld (additive weight updates); reward combines utility preservation and task-specific editing objective; train policies; evaluate on bias mitigation and unlearning.
AI.result: Forget set accuracy reduced to nearly 0%; retain set accuracy > 90% for unlearning; bias mitigation performance improved >5%; maintain general utility.
AI.conclusion: Neural model editing can be cast as RL; policies can be learned from reward feedback rather than hand-engineered; framework generalizable to tasks requiring targeted edits.

title: To GAN or Not To GAN: Segmentation Analysis on Mars DEM
summary: To better understand Martian Surface, which is needed to enable Rovers navigate Mars with ease, it is necessary to be able to determine the location of mounds. Detecting and studying these morphologies can also help us find evidence of extraterrestrial life, in this case, more specifically, water or signs of life conducive environments. Detection of mounds was done by manually mapping morphological parameters onto Digital Elevation Models. This paper solves the problem by automatically detecting and or predicting mounds on Mars using Neural Network based Semantic Segmentation methodologies. This is done by using supervised semantic segmentation model and generative adversarial approach. A comparison of the approaches shows that adding extra artificially generated data did not improve the result.

AI.tldr: Automatic mound detection on Mars using neural network semantic segmentation; compares supervised segmentation with a GAN-based data augmentation approach; artificial data did not improve results.
AI.motivation: Understanding Martian surface features (mounds) is essential for rover navigation and for seeking signs of water/life; manual mapping on DEMs is labor-intensive.
AI.method: Employ neural network-based semantic segmentation; compare a supervised segmentation model with a GAN-driven data augmentation approach.
AI.result: Both approaches explored; adding artificially generated data did not improve results; no clear metric provided in abstract.
AI.conclusion: Semantic segmentation enables automatic mound detection; GAN data augmentation did not yield gains in this study; future work could test alternative augmentation or models.

title: Extracting Governing Equations from Latent Dynamics via Multi-View Contrastive Learning
summary: Identifying latent dynamical systems from noisy, high-dimensional measurements is a central problem at the intersection of representation learning, system identification, and scientific discovery. We present DYSCO, a multi-view temporal contrastive learning algorithm that jointly recovers latent trajectories and the governing dynamics from such observations, by leveraging multiple independent noisy views of the same underlying process to disentangle signal from noise. By parameterizing the dynamics in a structured functional basis, our framework further enables symbolic recovery of the governing equations within an affine gauge. We offer theoretical guarantees for strong identification up to an affine indeterminacy, extending prior identifiability results to the realistic setting of noisy nonlinear observations. Empirically, we demonstrate accurate recovery of both latent trajectories and flow fields across a diverse set of dynamical regimes (e.g., chaotic, oscillatory, and metastable) under both Gaussian and Poisson observation noise, the latter being particularly relevant for neural recordings.

AI.tldr: DYSCO is a multi-view temporal contrastive learning framework that recovers latent trajectories and governing dynamics from noisy, high-dimensional observations; it supports symbolic recovery of dynamics in an affine gauge, with identifiability guarantees up to affine indeterminacy; empirically robust across regimes and noise types including Gaussian and Poisson.
AI.motivation: Identify latent dynamical systems from noisy, high-dimensional measurements; leverage multiple independent views to separate signal from noise and enable data-driven discovery of governing equations.
AI.method: Proposes DYSCO: multi-view temporal contrastive learning; learn latent trajectories and flow fields; parameterize dynamics in a structured functional basis; compute symbolic recovery under an affine gauge; provide identifiability theory.
AI.result: Theoretical guarantees for strong identification up to affine indeterminacy under noisy nonlinear observations; empirical success in recovering latent trajectories and flow fields across chaotic, oscillatory, and metastable dynamics; robustness to Gaussian and Poisson noise (Poisson relevant for neural data).
AI.conclusion: DYSCO enables accurate latent dynamics recovery and symbolic equation discovery in realistic noisy settings, extending identifiability to nonlinear observations and leveraging multi-view data for improved denoising.

title: Different Layers, Different Manifolds: Module-Wise Weight-Space Geometry in Transformer Optimization
comment: Accepted at WSS @ ICML 2026, code is available at https://github.com/kiratoyoshihara/module-wise-manifold-muon
summary: Weight-space geometry plays a central role in neural network optimization, yet manifold constraints are often applied uniformly across all weight matrices. In this work, we ask whether different transformer modules prefer different manifold geometries. We study Manifold Muon for GPT-2 pretraining and compare layer-wise assignments of Stiefel and DGram constraints across attention and MLP blocks. Our results show a clear asymmetry: constraining attention layers with Stiefel geometry while assigning DGram geometry to MLP layers gives the best performance among the tested configurations, whereas the inverted assignment and all-DGram configuration become unstable under the shared hyperparameter setting. We trace this failure to singular value growth in DGram-constrained attention weights, which can amplify attention logits and induce softmax saturation. These findings suggest that symmetry-aware and geometry-aware optimization for transformers should be module-specific rather than uniform.

AI.tldr: Transformer modules benefit from heterogeneous weight-space constraints; Stiefel geometry for attention and DGram geometry for MLPs yields best performance, while uniform or inverted constraints cause instability due to singular value growth and softmax saturation.
AI.motivation: To determine whether different transformer components prefer different manifold geometries, challenging the idea of uniform weight-space constraints across a model.
AI.method: Apply Manifold Muon with layer-wise manifold constraints (Stiefel vs DGram) across GPT-2 pretraining; compare across attention and MLP blocks; test configurations under a shared hyperparameter setting; diagnose instability via singular value growth in DGram-constrained attention weights and softmax saturation.
AI.result: Clear asymmetry: Stiefel constraints on attention layers and DGram constraints on MLP layers provide the best performance among tested configurations. Inverted assignment and all-DGram configurations are unstable under the shared hyperparameters. Singlular value growth in DGram-constrained attention weights amplifies logits, leading to softmax saturation.
AI.conclusion: Optimization for transformers should be geometry-aware and module-specific rather than applying the same geometry uniformly across all layers.

title: Once-for-All: Scalable Simultaneous Forecasting via Equilibrium State Estimation
summary: We introduce Equilibrium State Estimation (ESE), a novel paradigm for simultaneous prediction, where multiple interacting systems require separate yet coordinated forecasts. Such scenarios often arise in real-world settings such as economics and healthcare modeling. Unlike existing approaches that predict one system at a time, ESE forecasts all systems in a single pass. It first estimates the equilibrium state across systems, then generates holistic forecasts based on the difference between the current state and the estimated equilibrium. Extensive experiments on synthetic and real-world datasets, including currency exchange and COVID-19 spread modeling, demonstrate that ESE is at least as accurate as state-of-the-art (SOTA) methods while being significantly faster. In addition, ESE integrates seamlessly with conventional predictors, combining their accuracy with its exceptional efficiency and delivering a 10-70x speedup. With linear-time complexity, ESE scales far better than SOTA methods as the number of systems increases. Moreover, it remains accurate under diverse perturbations, establishing ESE as a fast, generalizable, robust, and scalable multi-prediction method.

AI.tldr: ESE is a fast, scalable multi-system forecasting framework that estimates a shared equilibrium state to yield simultaneous predictions, achieving comparable accuracy to SOTA with 10-70x speedups and linear-time scalability.
AI.motivation: In real-world settings with multiple interacting systems (e.g., economics, healthcare), predictions must be coordinated. Traditional methods predict one system at a time, which is inefficient and may miss cross-system dynamics.
AI.method: Estimate a common equilibrium state across all systems, then forecast based on the difference between the current state and the equilibrium. All systems are predicted in a single pass with linear-time complexity, and ESE can integrate with conventional predictors to boost accuracy while enhancing efficiency.
AI.result: Empirical evaluation on synthetic and real-world datasets (currency exchange, COVID-19 spread) shows ESE is at least as accurate as state-of-the-art methods while offering substantial speedups. When combined with standard predictors, it yields 10–70x faster overall performance. ESE scales linearly with the number of systems and remains accurate under diverse perturbations.
AI.conclusion: ESE is a fast, generalizable, robust, and scalable multi-prediction framework. It achieves competitive accuracy with substantial speedups, maintains performance under perturbations, and scales gracefully with more systems, making it a practical drop-in for multi-system forecasting.

title: Clipping Makes Distributed and Federated Asynchronous SGD Robust to Stragglers
summary: In modern machine learning, parallelization of training is an important strategy for increasing scale. Asynchronous stochastic gradient descent (ASGD), which maximizes the utilization of available hardware by avoiding waiting for slow workers. However, with constant step sizes, the convergence of ASGD is nonetheless affected negatively by slow workers due to large delays in updates. At the same time, it has been empirically observed in asynchronous training of deep learning models that gradient clipping "stabilizes" training. In this work, we provide a theoretical justification for this behavior, as we show that clipping removes the dependence of the maximum delay in the oracle complexity. We employ a sub-Weibull model of gradient noise which generalizes sub-Gaussian and sub-exponential distributions to more heavy-tailed distributions, motivated by empirical observations in deep learning. We show convergence in expectation, and the first time in asynchronous optimization, convergence with high probability.

AI.tldr: Clipping stabilizes asynchronous SGD under heavy-tailed gradient noise by decoupling convergence from maximum delay, with convergence in expectation and, for the first time in asynchronous optimization, convergence with high probability.
AI.motivation: To explain why gradient clipping stabilizes ASGD in practice and to extend convergence guarantees to heavy-tailed gradient noise, addressing delays from slow workers.
AI.method: Theoretical analysis using a sub-Weibull gradient-noise model; analyzes oracle complexity and delay effects; demonstrates that gradient clipping removes dependence on the maximum delay; proves convergence in expectation and, for the first time in asynchronous optimization, convergence with high probability.
AI.result: Clipping eliminates the dependence of oracle complexity on the maximum delay; establishes convergence in expectation and establishes high-probability convergence in asynchronous optimization under sub-Weibull noise.
AI.conclusion: Gradient clipping stabilizes ASGD by removing delay-based bottlenecks in convergence, providing rigorous convergence guarantees under heavier-tailed gradient noise models and corroborating empirical observations in deep learning.

title: Quantizing Time-Series Models As Dynamical Systems: Trajectory-Based Quantization Sensitivity Score
comment: ICML 2026, Workshop on Forecasting as a New Frontier of Intelligence
summary: We introduce the Trajectory-based Quantization Sensitivity Score (TQS), a metric that reframes post-training quantization (PTQ) through the lens of dynamical-systems stability. By modeling the network's rollout as a discrete-time dynamical system, TQS characterizes how quantization-induced errors propagate and amplify over the rollout horizon. Unlike conventional PTQ methods, where sensitivity analysis is often coupled to the quantization procedure, TQS enables a priori sensitivity estimation decoupled from quantizer selection and bit-width assignment. This separation allows for quantization budget planning even for black-box or compiled networks with fused operators. Building on this, we present TQS-PTQ, a flexible mixed-precision framework that requires no calibration data or costly second-order approximations. Our experiments show that a dynamical-systems perspective provides a robust, high-performing pathway for low-precision deployment in resource-constrained settings.

AI.tldr: Introduces Trajectory-based Quantization Sensitivity Score (TQS), a dynamical-systems-based metric for post-training quantization (PTQ) that decouples sensitivity estimation from quantizer design, enabling budget planning and a calibration-free mixed-precision framework (TQS-PTQ) with robust performance in resource-constrained settings.
AI.motivation: To overcome entanglement between sensitivity analysis and the quantization procedure in PTQ, enabling a priori sensitivity estimation and budget planning, even for black-box or fused-operator networks.
AI.method: Model the network rollout as a discrete-time dynamical system, analyze propagation/amplification of quantization errors to define TQS, and develop TQS-PTQ, a flexible mixed-precision framework that requires no calibration data or second-order approximations.
AI.result: Empirical results indicate that viewing PTQ through a dynamical-systems lens yields a robust, high-performing approach for low-precision deployment in resource-constrained environments.
AI.conclusion: TQS provides a priori, decoupled sensitivity estimates from quantizer design, enabling budget planning and enabling a calibration-free mixed-precision PTQ via TQS-PTQ, with strong practical performance.

title: Rarity-Gated Context Conditioning for Offline Imitation Learning-Based Maritime Anomaly Detection
summary: Contextual anomaly detection aims to identify abnormal behavior conditional on context variables, but practical deployments often face highly imbalanced context distributions where rare regimes can be critical information. Under such frequency bias, context-conditioned models can produce unstable decisions and excessive false alarms in rare contexts. We propose Rarity-Gated Feature-wise Linear Modulation (RGFiLM), a rarity-aware conditioning module that combines feature-wise modulation (i.e., context-conditioned scaling and shifting of hidden features) with a gate controlled by a data-driven rarity score. The rarity score is estimated from the empirical distribution of context variables and regulates how strongly context modulates intermediate representations: the gate becomes more decisive under rare contexts while remaining conservative under frequent contexts. We evaluate RGFiLM on maritime trajectory anomaly detection using AIS motion sequences with ERA5 environmental context in an environment-sensitive detour scenario. When instantiated in a sequential anomaly scoring pipeline, RGFiLM achieves the best mean F1--False Positive Rate (FPR) trade-off among the compared context-agnostic and context-conditioned methods. These results suggest that explicitly accounting for context rarity is an effective approach for reducing false alarms in context-sensitive anomaly detection.

AI.tldr: Rarity-aware RGFiLM modulates context features using a rarity-driven gate to reduce false alarms in contextual anomaly detection.
AI.motivation: Contextual anomaly detection suffers from frequency bias in rare contexts, causing unstable decisions and high false positives; a mechanism is needed to adapt conditioning strength to context rarity.
AI.method: Introduce RGFiLM: feature-wise linear modulation gated by a data-driven rarity score derived from context distributions; apply in a sequential anomaly scoring pipeline for maritime AIS with ERA5 environmental context in an environment-sensitive detour scenario.
AI.result: RGFiLM achieves the best mean F1–False Positive Rate trade-off among context-agnostic and context-conditioned baselines in the evaluated setup.
AI.conclusion: Modeling context rarity explicitly improves robustness of context-conditioned anomaly detectors, reducing false alarms in imbalanced contexts; suggests broader applicability to context-sensitive tasks.

title: Navigating the Safety-Fidelity Trade-off: Massive-Variate Time Series Forecasting for Power Systems via Probabilistic Scenarios
summary: Probabilistic forecasting models are increasingly deployed on multivariate systems with distinct channel physics and operational constraints, but existing benchmarks evaluate neither property at scale. Public canonical multivariate benchmarks cap out at 2,000 channels, while power-system benchmarks either lack temporal structure or probabilistic evaluation. We introduce PowerPhase, a probabilistic forecasting benchmark built on six transmission grids ranging from 2,000 to 36,964 jointly forecasted channels, more than an order of magnitude beyond popular canonical multivariate benchmarks. Each target trajectory is the output of an AC power-flow solve, and PowerPhase ships with constraint-aware metrics, including Safety_mBrier, NECV, and CVaR-alpha, that complement CRPS and Distortion. Across eight baselines and three seeds, distributional accuracy and constraint satisfaction rank models differently, a trade-off we term safety-fidelity. We further propose PowerForge, a scenario-based quantile forecaster with type-specific decoding heads and a causal bridge between variable groups, which achieves the best average rank on every grid.

AI.tldr: Introduces PowerPhase, a scalable probabilistic forecasting benchmark for large-scale multivariate power-grid systems (2k–36.9k channels) with constraint-aware metrics, plus PowerForge, a scenario-based quantile forecaster with causal variable-group bridging that achieves top average rank across grids.
AI.motivation: Existing multivariate probabilistic benchmarks either cap channels or lack temporal structure or probabilistic evaluation; need scalable, physics-consistent benchmarks and methods to assess safety and fidelity in large power systems.
AI.method: PowerPhase constructs six transmission-grid benchmarks (2k–36.9k channels) with targets from AC power-flow solves; includes constraint-aware metrics (Safety_mBrier, NECV, CVaR-alpha) alongside CRPS and Distortion; introduces PowerForge, a scenario-based quantile forecaster with type-specific decoding heads and a causal bridge between variable groups.
AI.result: With eight baselines and three seeds, distributional accuracy and constraint satisfaction rankings differ across models, revealing a safety-fidelity trade-off; PowerForge achieves the best average rank on every grid.
AI.conclusion: The work provides a scalable, physics-informed probabilistic forecasting benchmark for large multivariate power systems and demonstrates a specialized forecaster (PowerForge) that consistently performs best, highlighting the trade-off between safety (constraint satisfaction) and fidelity (distributional accuracy).

title: Enhanced Low-Density Region Exploration in Classifier-Guided Diffusion Models Through Modified Reverse Diffusion Sampling
summary: Diffusion models have emerged as state-of-the-art generative models for high-fidelity image synthesis, particularly in their classifier-free guided and classifier-guided forms. However, standard classifier guidance concentrates probability mass around high-density class mean, leading to poor coverage of rare samples in the tails of the class-conditional distributions. Recent work on diffusion-based tail sampling mitigates this by training an additional low-density-seeking classifier with a synthetic-vs-real discriminator, at the cost of additional networks and training. In parallel, a number of samplers and distillation techniques accelerate or refine diffusion sampling, but do not explicitly address long-tail coverage. We propose a purely sampling-time, density-aware extension of classifier-guided conditional diffusion model that targets low-density regions without any additional training. We have applied guidance at noisy images not on predicted noise like most diffusion models. Starting from a pretrained conditional diffusion model and classifier on ImageNet, we modify the guided reverse dynamics by steering trajectories toward low-confidence regions via the modified classifier gradient, and at each time step, we also guide the sampling process toward the predicted real image. 1st guidance helps explore low-probability samples, and 2nd guidance helps to generate samples to be close to the real data manifold. The proposed sampler consistently improves ADM model recall at 64x64 resolution while maintaining a comparable FID, and with a 256x256 ADM model, we showed the results visually with different combinations of both guidance. We also showed that standard ADM classifier guidance, combined with predicted real image guidance, helps generate high perceptual quality samples with a 256x256 ADM model on ImageNet.

AI.tldr: A sampling-time, density-aware extension of classifier-guided diffusion that targets low-density regions to improve long-tail coverage without extra training; it uses two gradient-guided terms during reverse diffusion—one to explore low-density regions and one to stay near the predicted real image—evaluated on ADM models with ImageNet, yielding improved recall at 64x64 while maintaining FID and showing qualitative gains at 256x256.
AI.motivation: Standard classifier guidance concentrates probability mass around high-density class means, underrepresenting tail samples. Tail-sampling methods often require training an extra low-density classifier, adding complexity. A training-free, sampling-time solution is desirable to improve long-tail coverage without extra networks.
AI.method: Starting from a pretrained conditional diffusion model and classifier on ImageNet, apply two guidance terms during the reverse diffusion over noisy images (not predicted noise). First, steer trajectories toward low-confidence regions using a modified classifier gradient to explore tails. Second, guide toward the predicted real image at each step to stay on or near the data manifold. This is implemented as a purely sampling-time extension without additional training. Evaluated on ADM models at 64×64 and 256×256 resolutions, testing combinations of both guidance terms.
AI.result: The sampler consistently improves ADM recall at 64×64 while maintaining comparable FID. For 256×256, visual results are shown for different guidance combinations, and standard ADM classifier guidance with predicted real image guidance yields high perceptual quality samples on ImageNet.
AI.conclusion: A density-aware, sampling-time augmentation of classifier-guided diffusion can enhance tail coverage and realism without extra training, balancing long-tail exploration with data-manifold alignment and preserving overall sample quality.

title: Positional Encoding in the Context of Memristor-Based Analog Computation for Automatic Speech Recognition
summary: Memristors provide a new chance for resource-efficient computation of neural models for natural language processing by enabling analog execution of vector-matrix-multiplication. Yet, computations on these devices are currently subject to larger distortion, both in weight programming and execution. In this work, we identify large output values of transformed positional encodings to cause major degradation within analog-to-digital conversion (ADC) as part of memristor-based computation. By adjusting the proportion of weight and precision bits of the ADC of specific memristor layers, we reduce the degradation of the execution by ~50% relative, while keeping the estimated energy consumption stable. Additionally, we investigate scenarios where the ADC cannot be modified. In that case the degradation can be reduced by ~30% relative after removing encoding-related linear transformations.

AI.tldr: Adaptive ADC bit-precision/weight allocation in memristor-based NLP accelerators reduces degradation from large transformed positional encodings by ~50%, with energy largely unchanged; if ADC cannot be modified, removing encoding-related linear transformations can still reduce degradation by ~30%.
AI.motivation: Enable resource-efficient, analog computation of neural NLP models using memristors while mitigating accuracy loss from ADC and weight programming distortions; large output values from transformed positional encodings amplify ADC degradation.
AI.method: Tune the proportion of weight bits and ADC precision bits for specific memristor layers; analyze scenarios where ADC modification is not possible by removing encoding-related linear transformations.
AI.result: Degradation in analog execution is reduced by about 50% when ADC bit-configuration is adjusted; energy consumption remains stable. If ADC cannot be modified, degradation is reduced by about 30% after removing encoding-related linear transformations.
AI.conclusion: Adaptive ADC- and encoding-management strategies can significantly mitigate analog computation degradation in memristor-based NLP accelerators, enabling more reliable energy-efficient processing.

title: Hölder++: Improving the Quality-Coherence Trade-off in Multimodal VAEs
summary: Existing approaches for multimodal variational autoencoders (VAEs) face a trade-off between generative quality and coherence-i.e., they struggle to generate realistic and diverse samples that, at the same time, are semantically consistent across modalities. A recent work shows that using a simple approximation to Hölder pooling as an aggregation method improves coherence over the SOTA MMVAE+, despite assuming a single shared representation across all modalities. Yet, it slightly compromises sample diversity. Inspired by this insight, we propose Hölder++, a novel multimodal VAE that improves the generative quality-coherence trade-off through: (i) the first implementation of Hölder pooling without any approximation for multimodal VAEs; (ii) an extended architecture that models distinct shared and private (i.e., modality-specific) representations (Hölder+); and (iii) hierarchical inference that further enhances the disentanglement between the shared and private representations (Hölder++). Our experiments corroborate that Hölder++ consistently improves the generative quality-coherence trade-off, yields more structured latent spaces, and learns shared representations that are informative for downstream tasks.

AI.tldr: Hölder++ improves multimodal VAE performance by adopting exact Hölder pooling, introducing distinct shared and private latent representations, and applying hierarchical inference to better disentangle factors, yielding better generative quality-coherence trade-off and more informative latent spaces.
AI.motivation: Current MMVA methods struggle to simultaneously achieve high generative quality and cross-modal coherence. An approximate Hölder pooling helped coherence but reduced diversity; a more principled pooling and latent structure could balance these goals while improving interpretability.
AI.method: Introduce Hölder++: (i) exact Hölder pooling for multimodal VAEs (no approximation); (ii) an architecture (Hölder+) with separate shared and modality-specific private latent representations; (iii) hierarchical inference to further disentangle shared vs private factors and improve inference quality.
AI.result: Empirical results show Hölder++ consistently improves the generative quality-coherence trade-off, yields more structured latent spaces, and learns shared representations that are more informative for downstream tasks.
AI.conclusion: Exact Hölder pooling combined with a shared/private latent factorization and hierarchical inference yields a robust improvement in MMVAE performance, suggesting principled pooling and disentangled latent structure are key for balancing realism and cross-modal coherence.

title: Accelerating Speculative Diffusions via Block Verification
summary: Speculative decoding speeds up LLM inference by using a draft model to generate tokens, with an acceptance-rejection scheme that ensures that the output matches the target distribution. Adapting this to continuous diffusions is difficult because speculative sampling requires drawing from a residual distribution. While straightforward in discrete spaces, efficiently sampling this residual in continuous space is non-trivial. Consequently, existing diffusion adaptations either use computationally inefficient sampling techniques or rely on an alternative scheme. In this work, we introduce a novel scheme that efficiently implements the original speculative sampling mechanism for diffusion models. Our approach offers a critical advantage over current methods: it enables us to adapt block verification from LLMs to diffusions -- which provably improves the acceptance rate of drafts. Furthermore, we formalize and analyze the Free Drafter, a heuristic self-speculative drafter for diffusions that requires no training. By enabling block verification, our Free Drafter yields up to a 6.3% speedup over existing speculative methods with no additional training and negligible overhead beyond the existing parallel verification pass.

AI.tldr: Speculative decoding is extended to diffusion models via a novel, training-free scheme and block verification, achieving up to 6.3% speedup with negligible overhead.
AI.motivation: Speed up diffusion-based generation by leveraging speculative sampling; but continuous sampling of residuals is non-trivial, hindering prior attempts.
AI.method: Introduce an efficient speculative sampling scheme for diffusion models; adapt LLM block verification to improve acceptance; formalize Free Drafter, a training-free heuristic self-speculative drafter; relies on parallel verification.
AI.result: Demonstrates up to 6.3% speedup over existing speculative methods without extra training; negligible overhead beyond the parallel verification pass.
AI.conclusion: The scheme enables practical, training-free speculative decoding for diffusion models, with block verification increasing draft acceptance and Free Drafter offering a simple, effective heuristic.

title: How Much Memory Do We Need? Adaptive Memory Gate for Neural Operators
summary: Neural operators have emerged as a powerful data-driven approach for solving time-dependent PDEs. Among recent advances, memory-augmented neural operators explicitly incorporate past states and have achieved remarkable performance under low-resolution observation settings. However, existing approaches apply a fixed memory weight regardless of observation conditions, such as resolution or physical parameters, limiting their adaptability. Our preliminary experiments reveal that optimal memory weight varies with resolution and viscosity, implying that a fixed memory weight cannot simultaneously optimize performance across diverse settings. We propose AMGFNO, which dynamically modulates memory weight through a learnable gate. On the Kuramoto-Sivashinsky and Burgers' equations, AMGFNO achieves 55-79% nRMSE reduction over at low resolution, with the learned gate value automatically decreasing from $\bar{g} \approx 0.7$ to near-zero as resolution increases.

AI.tldr: AMGFNO introduces a learnable gate to dynamically modulate memory weight in memory-augmented neural operators, enabling better performance across resolutions; achieves substantial nRMSE reductions at low resolution on KS and Burgers' equations, with the gate value decreasing with higher resolution.
AI.motivation: Fixed memory weights in memory-augmented neural operators hinder adaptability to varying observation conditions (e.g., resolution, viscosity). A dynamic mechanism is needed to adjust memory influence.
AI.method: Propose AMGFNO with a learnable gate that modulates memory weight. Evaluate on Kuramoto-Sivashinsky and Burgers' equations, reporting nRMSE improvements and gate dynamics (approximately ḡ ≈ 0.7 at low res, approaching zero as resolution increases).
AI.result: AMGFNO achieves 55–79% reduction in nRMSE over low-resolution baselines. The gate value automatically decreases from ~0.7 to near-zero as resolution increases, indicating adaptive memory usage.
AI.conclusion: Dynamic memory modulation via a learnable gate improves adaptability and performance of memory-augmented neural operators across varying resolutions and viscosity; the gating mechanism tunes memory influence in response to observation conditions.

title: Clustering Node Attributed Networks with Graph Neural Networks and Self Learning
summary: Graph clustering - partitioning the node set of a graph into disjoint subsets that reflect some latent information - is a fundamental problem as it finds applications in a myriad of different scenarios. While this classic problem has been tackled for decades by different communities, a recent variation of the problem driven by real data considers the scenario where nodes have attributes that are also informative. This has triggered novel methods that simultaneously leverage network information (edges) and node information (attributed) in the design of novel clustering algorithms. This work proposes a novel framework that builds on prior works that have applied graph neural networks (GNN) to graph clustering. The proposed framework operates in rounds of self learning in a fully unsupervised setting. In each round, a GNN generates representations for nodes that are used to cluster the nodes. This clustering influences the graph used to generate the node representation in the next round. Moreover, a context graph built in each round using the original graph is used to generate the node representations. Empirical results show that the proposed methodology extracts information from both network edges and node attributes in synthetic data, outperforming algorithms focused solely on the network or attributes when neither are very informative. Multiple rounds of learning also improve the performance and always outperforms a long single round of training (i.e., classic GNN graph clustering). When considering real datasets, empirical results indicate that the proposed methodology is competitive to state-of-the-art methods when cluster sizes are balanced.

AI.tldr: An unsupervised, multi-round GNN-based framework for attributed graph clustering that alternates representation learning and clustering, using both edges and node attributes, with a context graph per round; shows advantages on synthetic data and competitive real-data performance when cluster sizes are balanced.
AI.motivation: To improve graph clustering by leveraging both network structure and node attributes within an unsupervised, iterative learning framework, addressing limitations of single-round GNN clustering and attribute- or edge-only methods.
AI.method: In each round, a GNN learns node representations from the current graph and a context graph built from the original graph; nodes are clustered based on these representations; clustering outcomes influence the graph used in the next round, creating a self-learning loop with multiple rounds.
AI.result: On synthetic data, the method effectively uses both edges and attributes to outperform edge-only or attribute-only baselines when neither is highly informative; multiple rounds yield improvements and beat a single long training; on real data, performance is competitive with state-of-the-art when cluster sizes are balanced.
AI.conclusion: The framework demonstrates that iterative, unsupervised GNN-based clustering can robustly fuse structural and attribute information, yielding gains over single-round methods and maintaining competitive performance on real datasets under balanced cluster settings.

title: Uncertainty Estimation for Molecular Diffusion Models
summary: Diffusion models have seen wide adoption for 3D molecular generation, yet they offer no principled signal of when a generated molecule is likely to be of low quality. We propose a post-hoc method for estimating per-sample uncertainty in pretrained molecular diffusion models. Building on a Laplace approximation of the denoising network, we measure the variability of the noise prediction across the generation trajectory. Empirically, we show that the resulting uncertainty score is informative of sample quality, exhibiting a negative correlation with established sample-level quality metrics. We further study how the proposed uncertainty score can be used to filter generated samples, improving model performance via test-time scaling.

AI.tldr: Post-hoc uncertainty estimation for 3D molecular diffusion models using a Laplace-approximated denoiser; uncertainty correlates with sample quality and enables test-time filtering to boost performance.
AI.motivation: Lack of principled signals to gauge when a generated molecule is of low quality in diffusion-based 3D molecular generation.
AI.method: Apply Laplace approximation to the denoising network; quantify per-sample noise-prediction variability along generation trajectory to obtain an uncertainty score.
AI.result: Uncertainty score correlates negatively with established sample quality metrics, providing informative signal for filtering; test-time scaling with the score improves model performance.
AI.conclusion: Per-sample uncertainty can be estimated post-hoc in pretrained molecular diffusion models and effectively used to filter samples, enhancing downstream quality via test-time adjustments.

title: MaxProof: Scaling Mathematical Proof with Generative-Verifier RL and Population-Level Test-Time Scaling
summary: We present MaxProof, a population-level test-time scaling framework for competition-level mathematical proof in the MiniMax-M3 series. M3 first trains three proof-oriented capabilities -- proof generation, proof verification, and critique-conditioned proof repair -- using a defense-in-depth generative verifier engineered for low false-positive rate. These capabilities are merged into a single released M3 model. At test time, MaxProof treats the model as a generator, verifier, refiner, and ranker, searches over a population of candidate proofs, and returns one final proof through tournament selection. With MaxProof test-time scaling, the M3 model reaches 35/42 on IMO 2025 and 36/42 on USAMO 2026, exceeding the human gold-medal threshold on both.

AI.tldr: MaxProof enables population-level, test-time optimization to achieve competition-level mathematical proofs with the M3 model, surpassing gold-medal thresholds on IMO 2025 and USAMO 2026.
AI.motivation: Address the challenge of generating reliable, competition-grade mathematical proofs with AI by combining generation, verification, and critique-driven repair in a single model and leveraging population-based search at test time.
AI.method: Train three proof-oriented capabilities within M3 (proof generation, proof verification, critique-conditioned proof repair) using a defense-in-depth generative verifier with low false-positive rate; merge into a single M3 model. At test time, MaxProof uses the model as generator, verifier, refiner, and ranker to search over a population of candidate proofs and selects a final proof via tournament selection.
AI.result: With MaxProof, M3 achieves 35/42 on IMO 2025 and 36/42 on USAMO 2026, exceeding the human gold-medal threshold on both competitions.
AI.conclusion: The approach demonstrates that population-based, test-time exploration combined with multi-capability models can reach or exceed human performance on mathematical competitions, highlighting a path toward scalable AI-assisted mathematical reasoning—while prompting considerations of generalization, reliability, and study of failure modes.

title: SupraBench: A Benchmark for Supramolecular Chemistry
summary: Supramolecular chemistry, which includes the study of non-covalent host-guest assemblies, has advanced various applications. However, designing host-guest systems remains time-consuming, requiring days of dry-lab verification per candidate pair. Although LLMs have emerged as a fast alternative with strong performance on molecular binding tasks, no benchmark currently systematically evaluates LLMs for host-guest reasoning across fundamental supramolecular chemistry tasks, e.g., binding affinity prediction. To this end, we collaborate with domain experts to release the first Supramolecular Benchmark, called SupraBench, to evaluate LLMs in chemistry reasoning. Specifically, we design four fundamental tasks, i.e., binding affinity prediction, top-binder selection, solvent identification, and host-guest description, plus an auxiliary vision-based task for molecular identification. We also release SupraPMC, a curated 16M-token corpus of Supramolecular chemistry articles distilled from Europe PMC, to support the adaptation to the supramolecular domain. We benchmark a broad range of open and proprietary LLMs and find that LLMs leave substantial headroom across all tasks. Domain adaptation pretraining over SupraPMC transfers cleanly to in-distribution regression but trades off against strict letter-format output. Moreover, the difficulty profile differs sharply across task families, revealing distinct failure modes that indicate specific gaps in current supramolecular chemistry reasoning. Our source codes and benchmark datasets are available at https://github.com/Tianyi-Billy-Ma/SupraBench.

AI.tldr: SupraBench is the first benchmark suite evaluating LLMs on supramolecular chemistry reasoning across four tasks (binding affinity, top-binder, solvent identification, host-guest description) plus a vision-based identification task; SupraPMC is a 16M-token domain corpus to enable adaptation. Results show substantial headroom for LLMs, domain adaptation improves in-distribution regression but hurts strict output formatting, and task difficulty profiles reveal distinct failure modes; code and data are public.
AI.motivation: Fill the gap of a systematic, domain-specific benchmark for LLMs in supramolecular host-guest reasoning, enabling rapid assessment and targeted improvements in chemistry reasoning tasks.
AI.method: Design four fundamental host-guest tasks (binding affinity prediction, top-binder selection, solvent identification, host-guest description) plus a vision-based molecule ID task; curate SupraPMC (16M tokens from Europe PMC) for domain adaptation; benchmark a range of open/proprietary LLMs; assess domain-adaptation pretraining effects; analyze task-specific failure modes.
AI.result: LLMs show substantial headroom across all tasks; domain-adaptation pretraining over SupraPMC transfers to in-distribution regression but reduces strictly formatted output; difficulty varies across task families with distinct failure modes; public release of SupraBench and SupraPMC enables community benchmarking and improvement.
AI.conclusion: The SupraBench suite and SupraPMC corpus provide a valuable foundation for advancing LLMs in supramolecular chemistry reasoning. Domain adaptation offers gains in certain tasks, but attention is needed to maintain output formatting and address task-specific weaknesses; code and data are publicly available for replication and extension.

title: CRAFTIIF: Cross-Resolution Analytic Four-Type Interpretable Isolation Forest for Multivariate Time Series Anomaly Detection
comment: 14 pages, 4 figures, 2 appendices. Submitted to IEEE Transactions on Knowledge and Data Engineering (TKDE). Code: https://github.com/smitswil/craftiif
summary: Anomaly detection in multivariate time series is challenged by four structurally distinct anomaly types -- point (isolated spikes), distributional (level shifts), temporal (rhythm changes), and collective (inter-sensor correlation breakdowns) -- each requiring different feature representations. Most unsupervised methods target only one or two types and provide limited interpretability. We present CRAFTIIF (Cross-Resolution Analytic Four-Type Interpretable Isolation Forest), a fully unsupervised framework targeting all four types without dataset-specific tuning. CRAFTIIF generates K=500 random analytic wavelet feature draws across four families (Morlet, DOG, Haar, Coiflet), each targeting a specific anomaly type, feeding five structured Isolation Forests -- one per type plus a meta-IF for compound anomalies. An adaptive Otsu/MAD threshold calibrates detection automatically across anomaly rates from 0.1% to 69.2%. Because each IF is trained exclusively on type-specific features, branch firing provides direct anomaly-type attribution by construction, without post-hoc explanation. Evaluated on all 19 datasets of the mTSBench benchmark (Zhou et al., TMLR 2026), CRAFTIIF achieves mean F1=0.228 (all 19 datasets) and F1=0.322 (13 detectable datasets), ranking first among all 25 evaluated methods on VUS-PR (0.463 vs. previous best 0.329, +40.7%). A diagnostic framework -- oracle F1, detectability limits, and branch separation ratios -- identifies 6 of 19 datasets as fundamentally undetectable by any unsupervised method. Ablation over 11 conditions confirms adaptive thresholding (+38% F1), four-branch structure (+20%), and meta-IF (+23%) are each essential. Code: https://github.com/smitswil/craftiif

AI.tldr: Unsupervised cross-resolution detector CRAFTIIF handles four anomaly types in multivariate time series with four type-specific feature-IFs and a meta-IF; adaptive thresholding enables automatic detection and branch-based attribution yields direct anomaly-type labels, achieving top performance on mTSBench.
AI.motivation: Existing unsupervised anomaly detectors typically target one or two anomaly types and offer limited interpretability; a unified, interpretable approach covering all four types is needed for robust, dataset-agnostic detection.
AI.method: Generate 500 random analytic wavelet features from Morlet, DOG, Haar, Coiflet to target four anomaly types. Train five Isolation Forests (one per type plus a meta-IF for compound anomalies). Use adaptive Otsu/MAD thresholding to calibrate detection across a wide range of anomaly rates (0.1%–69.2%). Branch firing directly indicates anomaly type without post-hoc explanation.
AI.result: On mTSBench (19 datasets), mean F1=0.228 (all datasets) and F1=0.322 on 13 detectable datasets; ranking first on VUS-PR (0.463 vs 0.329; +40.7%). Ablation shows adaptive thresholding (+38% F1), four-branch structure (+20%), and meta-IF (+23%) are essential. Six datasets are fundamentally undetectable by any unsupervised method. Code: https://github.com/smitswil/craftiif
AI.conclusion: CRAFTIIF provides a universal, unsupervised, interpretable framework for four anomaly types with strong empirical performance and built-in type attribution, with design choices validated by ablation.

title: A2D2: Fine-Tuning Any-Length Discrete Diffusion for Adaptive Decoding
summary: Discrete diffusion models offer a simple and stable likelihood-based framework for sequence generation, recently extended to any-length settings via token insertion. Principled reward-guided fine-tuning for any-length discrete diffusion, however, remains largely unexplored. We introduce Fine-Tuning Any-Length Discrete Diffusion for Adaptive Decoding (A2D2), a unified framework for reward-guided fine-tuning of any-length discrete diffusion models via joint optimization of the insertion and unmasking policies together with a quality-based inference schedule. We derive the Radon-Nikodym derivative for the joint insertion-unmasking path measures, enabling theoretically guaranteed convergence to the intractable reward-tilted sequence distribution without requiring target samples. Building on this, we establish unmasking and insertion quality as tractable approaches for minimizing decoding error and introduce the Adaptive Joint Decoding (AJD) loss, which provably yields the optimal path measure that generates the reward-tilted distribution. Empirically, A2D2 improves reward optimization while enhancing generation flexibility and accuracy over prior fixed-length fine-tuning and inference-time guidance methods.

AI.tldr: Introduces A2D2, a unified reward-guided fine-tuning framework for any-length discrete diffusion with joint insertion/unmasking optimization and adaptive decoding; derives Radon-Nikodym-based convergence to the reward-tilted distribution without target samples; proposes Adaptive Joint Decoding (AJD) loss; shows empirical gains over prior methods.
AI.motivation: Principled reward-guided fine-tuning for any-length discrete diffusion is underexplored. Enabling flexible sequence generation via insertion and unmasking requires a tractable, convergent optimization framework that does not rely on target samples.
AI.method: Derive the Radon-Nikodym derivative for joint insertion-unmasking path measures; formulate tractable quality objectives for unmasking and insertion; introduce the Adaptive Joint Decoding (AJD) loss that yields the optimal path measure for the reward-tilted distribution; jointly optimize insertion/unmasking policies with a quality-based inference schedule.
AI.result: Empirically, A2D2 improves reward optimization and enhances generation flexibility and accuracy compared with prior fixed-length fine-tuning and inference-time guidance methods.
AI.conclusion: The framework provides theoretical convergence guarantees to the reward-tilted distribution and offers a practical, flexible approach for any-length discrete diffusion via joint insertion/unmasking optimization and adaptive decoding.

title: Adjusted Cup-Product Neural Layer
summary: Many important observables in physics and geometry are cup products of cochains. The adjusted cup product neural layer has been introduced in this paper. It is a neural primitive that hard wires the cup product with an adjustment term from higher gauge theory. This creates a readout that is gauge invariant by design. Their main theoretical result shows that on a closed cycle the output relies entirely on the adjustment coefficient. Setting this coefficient to zero removes the output completely regardless of other parameters. Thus the adjustment is the only source of gauge invariant signal. They prove this observable is a nonzero quadratic form and is exactly invariant under one and two gauge transformations.
