AI.tldr: An adjusted cup-product neural layer is proposed, injecting a higher gauge theory adjustment to create a gauge-invariant observable. On closed cycles, the output depends solely on the adjustment coefficient; setting it to zero nullifies the signal. The observable is a nonzero quadratic form invariant under one and two gauge transformations.
AI.motivation: To realize gauge-invariant observables in neural networks by integrating cup products with a higher gauge-theory adjustment, isolating the invariant signal to a tunable coefficient.
AI.method: Hard-wire the cup product with an adjustment term from higher gauge theory to form a neural primitive; derive theoretical results showing dependence on the adjustment coefficient, nonzero quadratic form, and exact invariance under one and two gauge transformations.
AI.result: On closed cycles, the output collapses to a function of the adjustment coefficient; a zero coefficient yields zero output; the observable is a nonzero quadratic form and is exactly invariant under one and two gauge transformations.
AI.conclusion: The adjustment term is the sole source of gauge-invariant signal; the observable's invariance properties are rigorously established, suggesting a principled route to gauge-invariant neural representations using higher gauge-theory concepts.

title: Existence Precedes Value: Joint Modeling of Observational Existence and Evolving States in Time Series Forecasting
summary: Real-world time series are often highly incomplete and irregular due to sensor dormancy, transmission delays, and event-driven sampling, making reliable forecasting fundamentally challenging. Existing methods have evolved from impute-then-forecast pipelines to continuous-time models such as Neural ODEs and continuous-time graph networks. While these approaches improve the modeling of historical irregularity, they still rely on an implicit oracle assumption at inference time: the timestamps of future valid observations are presumed to be known in advance. This assumption limits practical relevance, since in many real systems the more fundamental question is not only what the future value will be, but also whether a valid observation will occur at all. In this paper, we propose Timeflies, a unified framework that reformulates forecasting as a joint problem of future observability inference and value estimation. To explicitly model the interaction between observation dynamics and state evolution, Timeflies adopts an observation stream and a value stream, coupled through three dedicated modules for reliability-aware embedding, observation-guided dependency modeling, and joint prediction. We further construct Shadow, a benchmark that combines natural missingness from public datasets with real-world industrial data, and introduce the Observation-Value Joint Entropy (OVJE) metric to comprehensively evaluate this coupled predictability. Extensive experiments show that Timeflies consistently outperforms existing methods, highlighting the importance of explicitly modeling future observability in time series forecasting with missing values. Code and dataset are available in https://github.com/ant-intl/Timeflies.

AI.tldr: Timeflies jointly models future observability and value estimation for irregular, missing time series, introducing an observation–value dual-stream architecture with reliability-aware embedding, observation-guided dependency modeling, and joint prediction; it is evaluated on the Shadow benchmark with the OVJE metric, showing strong empirical gains and releasing code/dataset.
AI.motivation: Real-world time series are often incomplete and irregular due to sensor dormancy, transmission delays, and event-driven sampling. Existing impute-then-forecast or continuous-time models assume known future observation timestamps, an unrealistically strong oracle, hindering practical forecasting. There is a need to explicitly model future observability.
AI.method: Timeflies introduces an observation stream and a value stream coupled by three modules: 1) reliability-aware embedding to capture observation quality, 2) observation-guided dependency modeling to couple observation dynamics with state evolution, and 3) joint prediction to estimate future values while inferring observability. The framework explicitly models the interaction between when observations occur and the latent state to forecast.
AI.result: Empirical evaluations show Timeflies consistently outperforms existing methods for forecasting with missing data, validating the importance of modeling future observability. The paper also presents Shadow, a benchmark combining public natural missingness with real-world industrial data, and the OVJE metric for evaluating coupled observability and value predictability; code and dataset are released.
AI.conclusion: Explicitly modeling future observability is crucial for practical forecasting with missing values. Timeflies provides a principled framework and accompanying benchmark suite (Shadow, OVJE), enabling more reliable predictions in real-world, irregular time series.

title: Learning with Simulators: No Regret in a Computationally Bounded World
summary: Understanding the minimal assumptions necessary for generalization is the fundamental question in learning theory. Unfortunately, most results rely heavily on independence (or some proxy thereof) of the data-generating process, while results for strongly dependent data are far more limited. Towards addressing this gap, we introduce the framework of simulatable processes, where the learner has access to a simulator that approximates the distribution generating the data (which may be an arbitrarily complex and dependent process). Surprisingly, given access to such a simulator, we show that we can recover the same learning guarantees as in the classical setting with independent data, namely, error bounds that depend on the VC dimension. Further, we use this framework to study the power of conditional sampling and show strict statistical and computational advantages in this setting. As a highlight of our framework, we exhibit a single algorithm that simultaneously learns any given VC class under all processes samplable in bounded polynomial time, with regret controlled by the time-bounded Kolmogorov complexity of the process. This provides a significant conceptual broadening of the classical PAC model.

AI.tldr: Introduces simulatable processes, a simulator-access framework that preserves VC-dimension generalization guarantees for dependent data, and presents a universal algorithm with performance tied to time-bounded Kolmogorov complexity; extends the PAC model via conditional sampling and poly-time simulability.
AI.motivation: To close the gap between theory for independent data and strongly dependent data by enabling learning with guarantees when a data-generating process can be approximated by a simulator.
AI.method: Define simulatable processes and analyze learning with access to their simulators; prove that VC-dimension-based error bounds from classical PAC hold; study conditional sampling; construct a single algorithm that learns any VC class under all processes simulable in bounded polynomial time; relate regret to time-bounded Kolmogorov complexity.
AI.result: Error bounds identical to the i.i.d. case depending on VC dimension; conditional sampling yields statistical/computational advantages; a universal learner exists for all poly-time simulable processes with regret governed by time-bounded Kolmogorov complexity.
AI.conclusion: This framework broadens the PAC paradigm to dependent data settings via simulators, highlighting a strong link between computational resources and statistical guarantees.

title: Simplex-Constrained Sparse Bagging: Transitioning from Uniform Priors to Sparse Posteriors in Ensemble Learning
summary: We present Simplex-Constrained Sparse Bagging (SCSB), a mathematically rigorous framework for post-training compression and probability calibration of bootstrap-based bagging ensembles. Standard bagging ensembles (such as Random Forests, Bagged SVMs, and Bagged Neural Networks) assign uniform voting power to all constituent estimators. However, this naive uniform prior ignores the varying local competence of base estimators and contributes to model overconfidence. We formulate ensemble pruning and calibration as a joint optimization problem over the probability simplex by minimizing the Out-Of-Bag (OOB) loss. To induce sparsity, we address the theoretical "L1-simplex paradox" -- the mathematical reality that the L1 norm is constant on the simplex and fails to prune -- by introducing a concave quadratic penalty. SCSB is model-agnostic and achieves up to 96% ensemble compression, yielding linear inference speedups and superior probability calibration (lowered Expected Calibration Error) while preserving or enhancing generalization accuracy.

AI.tldr: A principled, model-agnostic framework for post-training compression and probability calibration of bootstrap ensembles by learning sparse, simplex-constrained weights that minimize OOB loss, addressing the L1-simplex paradox with a concave quadratic penalty.
AI.motivation: Standard bagging treats all base estimators equally, ignoring local competence and contributing to overconfidence. There is a need for principled pruning (sparsity) and calibration within a unified optimization on the probability simplex.
AI.method: Formulate joint optimization over the probability simplex to reweight base estimators via OOB loss minimization. Introduce a concave quadratic penalty to overcome the L1-simplex paradox and induce sparsity. The approach is model-agnostic and operates as post-training compression and calibration of bootstrap ensembles.
AI.result: Achieves up to 96% ensemble compression, leading to linear inference speedups and improved probability calibration (lowered Expected Calibration Error) while preserving or enhancing generalization accuracy.
AI.conclusion: SCSB provides a rigorous, model-agnostic framework for compressing and calibrating bagging ensembles through simplex-constrained sparse optimization, effectively pruning weak estimators and improving calibration without sacrificing performance.

title: Beyond the Commitment Boundary: Probing Epiphenomenal Chain-of-Thought in Large Reasoning Models
summary: Chain-of-thought (CoT) reasoning is the dominant paradigm for inference-time scaling in language models, yet the causal influence of individual steps on the final answer poorly understood. We estimate each step's causal importance via early exit and use this measure to study how answers form across the reasoning traces of several model families. Across diverse tasks, we find that reasoning typically crosses a \emph{commitment boundary} -- a sharp transition from transient intermediate guesses to a stable, high-confidence answer. This transition often happens in a single step, well before the model's reasoning block ends, and is followed by \emph{epiphenomenal} CoT steps that leave the final answer probability unaltered. Using attention probes, we show that answer-formation stages can be linearly decoded from intermediate reasoning steps with high accuracy and generalize robustly to unseen reasoning tasks. We exploit this signal to early-exit reasoning blocks at the commitment boundary, reducing the length of CoTs up to 55\% on average with negligible impact on model performance.

AI.tldr: CoT steps show a commitment boundary where a stable, high-confidence answer forms; many subsequent steps are epiphenomenal. Early-exiting at this boundary can cut CoT length by up to 55% with minimal performance loss, and answer formation can be decoded from intermediate reasoning.
AI.motivation: Clarify the causal influence of individual chain-of-thought steps on final answers and improve the efficiency of CoT reasoning.
AI.method: Estimate causal importance via early exit at each step; analyze across model families and tasks; use attention probes to assess linear decodability of answer formation from intermediate steps; identify the commitment boundary; evaluate early exit.
AI.result: Commitment boundary often occurs within a single step; many later CoT steps do not affect final answer; attention probes show linear decodability; early-exit reduces CoT length by up to 55% with negligible impact on performance; generalizes to unseen tasks.
AI.conclusion: CoT reasoning includes a pivotal transition point; beyond it, steps are largely epiphenomenal; leveraging this enables efficient CoT with minimal accuracy loss; findings generalize across tasks and models.

title: The Stable Recovery Manifold: Geometric Principles Governing Recoverability in Continual Learning
summary: Catastrophic forgetting is often viewed as the destruction of previously learned knowledge during sequential learning. Building on the Accessibility Collapse framework, we investigate the geometric structure of recoverability in continual learning. Using Split CIFAR-100 and a sequentially trained ResNet-18, we analyze recoverability, representational drift, and recovery complexity across ten tasks. We introduce Recovery Subspace Dimensionality (k_t), a measure of the minimum number of singular directions required to preserve 90 percent of full probe performance. Contrary to our Recoverability Diffusion hypothesis, recovery dimensionality remains stable throughout training (mean k_t = 8.0) despite substantial representational drift. Principal-angle drift strongly predicts recoverability (r = -0.862), and a simple geometric model explains 82.2 percent of recoverability variance. These findings support the Stable Recovery Manifold hypothesis, suggesting that forgotten knowledge remains compactly decodable despite representational reorganization. The results indicate that catastrophic forgetting is primarily an accessibility and manifold-alignment problem rather than information destruction.

AI.tldr: Recovery subspace dimensionality is stable across sequential learning; catastrophic forgetting stems from accessibility and manifold alignment rather than information loss.
AI.motivation: To understand the geometry of recoverability in continual learning under the Accessibility Collapse framework by analyzing how representational drift affects recoverability in a ten-task Split CIFAR-100 setup with a ResNet-18.
AI.method: Train a ResNet-18 sequentially on ten tasks from Split CIFAR-100. Define Recovery Subspace Dimensionality kt as the minimum number of singular directions needed to preserve 90% of full probe performance. Assess recoverability, representational drift, and recovery complexity over time; analyze principal-angle drift and fit a geometric model to explain recoverability variance.
AI.result: kt is stable across training (mean kt = 8.0) despite substantial representational drift. Principal-angle drift strongly predicts recoverability (r = -0.862). The geometric model explains 82.2% of recoverability variance.
AI.conclusion: The findings support the Stable Recovery Manifold hypothesis: forgotten knowledge remains compactly decodable despite representational reorganization, indicating catastrophic forgetting is primarily an accessibility/manifold-alignment issue rather than destruction of information.

title: Dense Supervision, Sparse Updates: On the Sparsity and Geometry of On-Policy Distillation
summary: On-policy distillation (\textsc{OPD}) has recently become a prominent post-training recipe as it combines two desirable ingredients: on-policy student trajectories and dense teacher supervision, yet how this hybrid changes a model's parameters remains unclear. Across several language and vision-language model pairs and use cases, our analysis yields two main findings. On sparsity, \textsc{OPD}-style updates are small and coordinate-sparse. They are distributed across layers and are usually FFN-heavy. This sparse structure is operationally useful: training only the discovered subnetwork recovers nearly the same performance as full \textsc{OPD}. However, the sparsity-inducing SGD optimizer underperforms AdamW in our optimizer ablation, likely because dense teacher supervision preserves heterogeneous coordinate-wise gradient scales where AdamW's adaptive scaling remains useful. On geometry, the updates are numerically full-rank but spectrally concentrated; they lie mostly away from the principal singular subspaces of the source weights and fall disproportionately on coordinates where the source weights are close to zero. These findings suggest that dense teacher supervision does not turn \textsc{OPD} into ordinary dense parameter rewriting; instead, \textsc{OPD} retains important geometric signatures of on-policy post-training.

AI.tldr: OPD produces sparse, coordinate-wise updates that are FFN-dominated and distributed across layers; training only the discovered subnetwork achieves near-full OPD performance. Dense teacher supervision favors AdamW over sparse SGD for optimization. Updates are full-rank yet spectrally concentrated, avoiding the main subspaces of source weights and concentrating on near-zero weights. Overall, OPD preserves geometric signatures of on-policy post-training rather than rewriting dense parameters.
AI.motivation: To understand how on-policy distillation (OPD) reshapes model parameters and geometry, elucidating sparsity patterns, optimizer behavior, and spectral properties across different model families.
AI.method: Empirical analysis across multiple language and vision-language model pairs. Investigated update sparsity (layer distribution, FFN bias), performed subnetwork ablation to assess performance retention, compared optimizers (sparse SGD vs AdamW), and conducted spectral analysis of update directions relative to source weight subspaces.
AI.result: Updates are twofold: (1) sparse, coordinate-wise updates distributed across layers and FFN-dominant; subnetwork training recovers nearly full OPD performance. (2) Sparse SGD underperforms AdamW in the presence of dense teacher supervision. Updates are numerically full-rank but spectrally concentrated, lying away from principal singular subspaces and focusing on coordinates where source weights are near zero.
AI.conclusion: OPD does not merely rewrite dense parameters; it preserves key geometric signatures of on-policy post-training. The updates exhibit structured sparsity and a distinct spectral profile, informing optimizer choice and the interpretation of OPD’s effect on model parameters.

title: Understanding Truncated Positional Encodings for Graph Neural Networks
summary: Positional encodings (PEs) enhance the power of graph neural networks (GNNs), both theoretically and empirically. Two of the most popular families of PEs - spectral (e.g., Laplacian eigenspaces, effective resistance) and walk-based (polynomials of the adjacency matrix) - are theoretically equivalent in expressive power, with expressivity between the 1-WL and 3-WL tests. However, this equivalence assumes the GNN uses the "complete" version of these PEs, which requires $O(n^3)$ time and space complexity. Instead, practitioners commonly use truncated variants of these encodings, such as the first $k$ eigenspaces or powers of the adjacency matrix. However, the theoretical properties of these truncated PEs are unknown. In this work, we initiate the study of these truncated PEs. Theoretically, we show that, under truncation, several families of PEs are fundamentally different in expressive power. As a corollary, we show that truncated spectral PEs are no longer stronger than the 1-WL test. We also study a family of spectral PEs, the $k$-harmonic distances, to highlight the differences in expressive power of even closely related truncated PEs. Finally, we experimentally show that a mix of truncated PEs is preferable to any single family on real-world datasets.

AI.tldr: Truncated (practical) spectral and walk-based positional encodings are no longer equivalent in expressive power; truncation weakens their strength, with spectral truncation not surpassing 1-WL. Combining multiple truncated PEs improves performance empirically.
AI.motivation: Bridging theory and practice: while complete PEs yield equivalence between spectral and walk-based families, this only holds with O(n^3) resources. In practice, truncated encodings are used, but their theoretical properties are unknown. The work analyzes the expressive power of truncated PEs and guides PE design.
AI.method: Theoretically analyze truncated spectral and walk-based PEs; compare their expressivity relative to WL tests; examine a family of spectral PEs (k-harmonic distances) to illustrate nuanced differences; conduct empirical experiments combining multiple truncated PEs on real-world datasets.
AI.result: Under truncation, the previously observed equivalence between PE families dissolves; several PE families become expressively distinct; truncated spectral PEs are not stronger than 1-WL; k-harmonic distances reveal subtler distinctions among truncated PEs; empirically, mixing truncated PEs outperforms any single-family approach on real data.
AI.conclusion: Practical GNN PE design should embrace a mixture of truncated encodings to capture complementary information; relying on a single truncated family is suboptimal; theoretical guarantees available for complete encodings do not extend to their truncated counterparts.
